title: Multivariate diagonal coinvariant spaces for complex reflection groups
overall_summary: Bergeron studies diagonal coinvariant spaces C_W^(r) and their harmonic models H_W^(r) for finite complex reflection groups W acting on r sets of n variables. The main structural point is that the multigraded Hilbert series is the GL_r-character of the quotient, hence has a Schur expansion with nonnegative multiplicities, and these multiplicities can be organized in a universal way independent of the number r of variable sets. For W = S_n, the paper refines this to a universal Schur-positive expansion of the graded Frobenius characteristic, separating the q-variable Schur functions from the S_n-irreducible Schur functions in auxiliary w-variables. For the original problem, this paper is mainly foundational: it justifies interpreting c_λ(n) as stable GL_m multiplicities, gives degree and length bounds on possible λ, and provides specializations and small-n data that any proposed hook-shape formula must satisfy; it does not itself give a hook-shape combinatorial interpretation.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 2 GL_r-character realization of the Hilbert series
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let W be a rank n finite complex reflection group, let R_n^(r) = C[x_{ij}: 1 <= i <= r, 1 <= j <= n] be the polynomial ring in r sets of n variables, and write X for the r by n matrix of variables. W acts diagonally by f(X) -> f(Xw), where w is viewed as an n by n matrix. Let I_W^(r) be the ideal generated by all W-invariant polynomials with zero constant term, and let C_W^(r) = R_n^(r)/I_W^(r). The natural left action of GL_r on X, given by f(X) -> f(MX), commutes with the diagonal W-action and preserves I_W^(r). Therefore C_W^(r) is a polynomial GL_r-module. Its GL_r-character, obtained by taking the trace of f(X) -> f(QX) for Q = diag(q_1,...,q_r), is exactly the multigraded Hilbert series of C_W^(r). Consequently this Hilbert series has an expansion as a nonnegative integer linear combination of Schur polynomials s_μ(q_1,...,q_r), where the coefficients are GL_r irreducible multiplicities.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The two matrix actions commute because M(Xw) = (MX)w. If f is W-invariant, then the polynomial X -> f(MX) is again W-invariant, since f(MXw) = f((MX)w) = f(MX); constant terms are also preserved. Hence the GL_r action stabilizes the invariant-generated ideal and descends to the quotient C_W^(r). The diagonal matrix Q acts on a monomial X^A by multiplication by q_1^{d_1}...q_r^{d_r}, where (d_1,...,d_r) is the row-degree of the monomial. Taking the trace over each multihomogeneous quotient component therefore gives exactly the Hilbert series. Since polynomial representations of GL_r decompose into irreducibles and the irreducible characters are Schur polynomials, the Hilbert series has a Schur expansion with nonnegative integer coefficients.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.1 (diagonal harmonics as the orthogonal complement of the invariant ideal)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the same W and R_n^(r), define a scalar product on polynomials by <f(X), g(X)> = f(∂X)g(X)|_{X=0}, where f(∂X) is obtained from f by replacing each variable x_{ij} with the partial derivative ∂/∂x_{ij}. Define H_W^(r) to be the space of diagonal harmonic polynomials, i.e. the set of polynomial solutions g(X) of f(∂X)g(X) = 0 for every f(X) in I_W^(r). Then H_W^(r) = (I_W^(r))^⊥ with respect to this scalar product. In particular, H_W^(r) is a canonical representative space for the quotient C_W^(r), and C_W^(r) and H_W^(r) have the same multigraded Hilbert series.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: One direction is immediate from the definition: if g is annihilated by h(∂X) for every h in I_W^(r), then <h,g> = h(∂X)g|_{X=0} = 0 for every h in I_W^(r), so g lies in the orthogonal complement. Conversely, suppose g lies in (I_W^(r))^⊥. If for some f in I_W^(r) the polynomial f(∂X)g(X) were nonzero, choose a leading monomial c X^A in it with c nonzero. Since I_W^(r) is an ideal, X^A f(X) also lies in I_W^(r). But <X^A f, g> is obtained by applying X^A(∂X) to f(∂X)g and evaluating at 0, which extracts a nonzero scalar multiple of c, contradicting orthogonality. Thus f(∂X)g = 0 for all f in I_W^(r), so g is harmonic. The quotient and harmonic space are identified because the scalar product makes the harmonic space a graded complement to the ideal.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 2 stability under adding a set of variables and bounded total degree
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The harmonic spaces form an increasing chain H_W^(1) ⊆ H_W^(2) ⊆ ... ⊆ H_W^(r) ⊆ ..., where H_W^(r-1) is embedded in H_W^(r) by viewing a polynomial in r-1 rows of variables as a polynomial independent of the rth row. Their Hilbert series satisfy H_W^(r-1)(q_1,...,q_{r-1}) = H_W^(r)(q_1,...,q_{r-1},0). The paper also states, by adapting an argument of Garsia and Haiman for r = 2, that the total degree of elements of H_W^(r) is bounded by the maximal total degree occurring in H_W^(1), namely the degree of the Jacobian of W. Hence only finitely many q-Schur functions can occur in the universal expansion as the number of variable sets varies.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The inclusion H_W^(r-1) ⊆ H_W^(r) follows by regarding a harmonic polynomial in the first r-1 rows as independent of the new variables. Differential operators involving derivatives in the new row act trivially on such a polynomial, while the remaining parts reduce to operators from the smaller diagonal invariant ideal. Setting q_r = 0 in the Hilbert series of H_W^(r) exactly keeps the components of degree 0 in the rth row, which are the embedded copy of H_W^(r-1). The total-degree bound is not reproved in detail in the paper; it is cited as an adaptation of the standard diagonal-harmonic degree-bound argument. Together, specialization compatibility and a uniform degree bound allow the Schur expansions for all r to be viewed as specializations of one finite universal symmetric function.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.1 (universal Schur-positive Hilbert series)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the rank n complex reflection groups W = G(ρ,1,n) considered in the theorem, where ρ denotes the group parameter to avoid confusion with the number r of variable sets, the Hilbert series of the diagonal coinvariant space C_W^(r) in r sets of variables has an expansion C_W^(r)(q_1,...,q_r) = Σ_μ c_μ s_μ(q_1,...,q_r), where every c_μ is a nonnegative integer independent of r. The partitions μ that can occur have at most n parts. Their total degree is bounded by the degree of the Jacobian of W; in the displayed bound of the paper for this family, this is written as 0 <= |μ| <= n(ρ n + ρ - 2)/2. For W = S_n = G(1,1,n), this degree bound becomes |μ| <= binom(n,2).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The Schur-positive expansion follows from the GL_r-character realization: C_W^(r), equivalently H_W^(r), is a finite-dimensional polynomial GL_r-module, and Schur functions are irreducible GL_r characters. The coefficients are multiplicities, hence nonnegative integers. Independence of r follows from the inclusions H_W^(r-1) ⊆ H_W^(r), the specialization identity obtained by setting q_r = 0, and the uniform total-degree bound: for a fixed partition μ, once r is large enough to contain μ, its multiplicity cannot keep changing without violating these compatible specializations. The remaining restriction, that μ has at most n parts, is proved by bounding the possible GL_r-types by those appearing in the ambient polynomial ring R_n^(r). The paper invokes a plethystic symmetric-function calculation, writing an ambient character in terms of H(q) = Σ_{k>=0} h_k(q) = ∏_{i=1}^r (1-q_i)^(-1), and uses classical symmetric-function facts to conclude that only Schur functions indexed by partitions of length at most n appear. Since the coinvariant/harmonic space is a GL_r subquotient of the ambient polynomial ring, no other q-Schur types can occur.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.2 (universal Frobenius expansion for S_n)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let W = S_n and let H_n(w;q) be the graded Frobenius characteristic of the diagonal harmonic space H_n in r sets of variables: H_n(w;q) = Σ_{d in N^r} q^d F_{H_{n;d}}(w), where F_{H_{n;d}}(w) is the Frobenius characteristic of the S_n-module in multidegree d, expressed in auxiliary variables w. Then H_n(w;q) has a universal expansion H_n(w;q) = Σ_{λ ⊢ n} (Σ_μ b_{λ,μ} s_μ(q)) S_λ(w), where S_λ(w) is the Schur function corresponding to the S_n irreducible indexed by λ, s_μ(q) is a Schur function in the grading variables q_1,...,q_r, and b_{λ,μ} are nonnegative integers independent of r. The partitions μ have at most n parts and satisfy 0 <= |μ| <= binom(n,2).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The GL_r action on the variables commutes not only with the diagonal W-action but also with the S_n action when W = S_n. Therefore each S_n-isotypic component of H_n is a GL_r-submodule. Decompose H_n simultaneously as an S_n-module and as a GL_r-module: for each S_n irreducible S_λ(w), the corresponding multiplicity space carries a polynomial GL_r representation, and its character decomposes Schur-positively as Σ_μ b_{λ,μ} s_μ(q). The coefficients are multiplicities and hence nonnegative integers. Stability in r and the q-degree bound are inherited from the universal Hilbert-series argument; for S_n the maximal total degree in the classical coinvariant ring is binom(n,2), the degree of the Vandermonde/Jacobian. The length bound ℓ(μ) <= n again comes from the ambient polynomial ring GL_r-type bound.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Equations (22) and (29) (one-variable specializations)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: When the universal Frobenius characteristic for S_n is specialized by setting q_1 = t and q_i = 0 for i >= 2, the paper records the classical formula H_n(w;t) = Σ_{λ ⊢ n} [n choose λ]_t m_λ(w), where m_λ(w) is a monomial symmetric function and [n choose λ]_t = n!_t/(λ_1!_t ... λ_k!_t), with n!_t = (1+t)(1+t+t^2)...(1+t+...+t^{n-1}). More generally, for a finite complex reflection group W with degrees d_1,...,d_n, the one-set Hilbert series is the Poincare polynomial H_W(t) = Σ_{w in W} t^{ℓ(w)} = ∏_{i=1}^n (t^{d_i}-1)/(t-1). For the symmetric group, ℓ(w) is the number of inversions of the permutation w.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Setting all but one grading variable equal to zero reduces diagonal coinvariants to the classical one-set coinvariant theory. The product formula for H_W(t) is the standard Chevalley-Shephard-Todd/Steinberg Poincare polynomial for the coinvariant algebra of a reflection group. In type A, the graded Frobenius characteristic of the coinvariant ring can be computed by standard symmetric-function methods and gives the t-multinomial expansion in the monomial basis. The paper uses this also to observe that if a universal Hilbert series were h-positive, then because h_μ(t) specializes to t^{|μ|} in one variable, the h-basis coefficients would refine the length distribution of W; for S_n this is the inversion-number distribution.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 4 low-degree comparison with polynomial and invariant rings
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For W = S_n, the paper compares the full polynomial ring R_n with H_n ⊗ R_n^{S_n}, where R_n^{S_n} is the ring of diagonal S_n-invariants. It records the plethystic formulas R_n(w;q) = h_n[w H(q)] and R_n^{S_n}(q) = h_n[H(q)], where H(q) = Σ_{k>=0} h_k(q) = ∏_{i=1}^r (1-q_i)^(-1). It then gives the approximation H_n(w;q) ≃_n h_n[w H(q)]/h_n[H(q)], stated as experimentally valid for all terms of q-degree at most n. Equivalently, using Cauchy identities, the right side expands as Σ_{λ ⊢ n} (h_λ[H(q)]/h_n[H(q)]) m_λ(w), and also as a Schur-basis analogue. For the underlying Hilbert series the corresponding approximation begins H(q)^n/h_n[H(q)] = 1 + (n-1)h_1 + (n-2)h_2 + binom(n-1,2)h_1^2 + (n-1)(n-3)h_1 h_2 + (n-2)h_3 + binom(n-1,3)h_1^3 + ... .
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For r = 1, the classical coinvariant theorem says that the polynomial ring is a free module over symmetric polynomials, yielding an exact factorization of the graded Frobenius characteristic. For r >= 2 the diagonal invariant ring does not make R_n free, but the paper states that low-degree homogeneous components still compare with H_n ⊗ R_n^{S_n}; a full explanation would require a free resolution, which is left for future work. The formulas R_n(w;q) = h_n[wH(q)] and R_n^{S_n}(q) = h_n[H(q)] come from Cauchy identities and plethystic notation. Dividing the polynomial-ring character by the invariant-ring Hilbert series gives the proposed low-degree approximation, and expanding it in the h or Schur bases gives explicit polynomial-in-n predictions for low-degree coefficients.

proof_techniques.name: Interpret multigraded Hilbert coefficients as GL_r multiplicities
proof_techniques.description: The paper’s central move is to use the left GL_r action on the r rows of variables. Because this action commutes with the diagonal W-action and preserves the invariant-generated ideal, the coinvariant quotient is a polynomial GL_r-module. The multigraded Hilbert series is then not just a generating function but the GL_r-character of this module, so its Schur expansion is the irreducible decomposition of that GL_r-module.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For hook-shaped λ, c_λ(n) can be attacked as the multiplicity of the irreducible GL_m representation of highest weight λ inside the diagonal coinvariant/harmonic space. This suggests looking for a combinatorial basis of GL_m-highest weight vectors of hook weight.

proof_techniques.name: Replace the quotient by diagonal harmonic representatives
proof_techniques.description: Instead of working directly in the quotient R_n^(r)/I_W^(r), the paper uses the orthogonal complement H_W^(r) = (I_W^(r))^⊥ under the differential-operator scalar product. Membership in H_W^(r) is characterized by explicit partial differential equations f(∂X)g = 0 for invariant generators f. This model preserves all graded and representation-theoretic information while giving concrete polynomial representatives.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A hook coefficient could be counted by constructing harmonic polynomials satisfying highest-weight conditions for GL_m. The harmonic model may make such objects more explicit than quotient classes.

proof_techniques.name: Stabilize in the number of variable sets by specialization q_r = 0
proof_techniques.description: The inclusions H_W^(r-1) ⊆ H_W^(r) and the identity H_W^(r-1)(q_1,...,q_{r-1}) = H_W^(r)(q_1,...,q_{r-1},0) let the paper compare all numbers of variable sets at once. A uniform total-degree bound, coming from the one-set Jacobian degree, prevents infinitely many new Schur types from appearing as r grows. Together these facts produce a single universal symmetric function whose specialization to r variables is the r-set Hilbert series.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is the formal justification for treating c_λ(n) as independent of m. For a fixed hook λ, one may choose m at least the length of λ and compute in that many sets without losing information.

proof_techniques.name: Use ambient polynomial-ring GL_r type bounds
proof_techniques.description: The coinvariant/harmonic space is a GL_r subquotient of the full polynomial ring in r by n variables. The paper uses plethystic symmetric-function calculations for the ambient character to conclude that only q-Schur functions indexed by partitions with at most n parts can occur. This is a representation-theoretic analogue of the fact that the polynomial ring is built from n copies of the defining GL_r representation.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For hooks λ = (a,1^b), this immediately rules out b+1 > n. It also suggests using Cauchy decompositions or semistandard tableaux with bounded length when searching for a hook formula.

proof_techniques.name: Refine by S_n-isotypic components using Frobenius characteristics
proof_techniques.description: For W = S_n, the GL_r action commutes with the S_n action, so the paper decomposes diagonal harmonics simultaneously by S_n irreducibles and GL_r irreducibles. The result is a two-level Schur expansion: S_λ(w) records the S_n type, while s_μ(q) records the multigrading/GL_r type. The coefficient b_{λ,μ} is the multiplicity of GL_r type μ inside the S_n-isotypic component λ.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A formula for c_μ(n) can potentially be refined by first finding b_{λ,μ} and then summing over S_n dimensions or extracting the monomial coefficient corresponding to the underlying Hilbert series. Existing combinatorics for the S_n Frobenius characteristic may therefore be relevant to hook μ.

proof_techniques.name: Check universal coefficients through specializations
proof_techniques.description: The paper emphasizes several specializations: one variable q_1=t recovers the classical coinvariant Poincare polynomial and graded Frobenius characteristic; setting q_i=1 gives dimension polynomials involving evaluations of Schur functions; for r=2 the diagonal coinvariant Frobenius characteristic is tied to the nabla operator and q,t-Catalan theory. These specializations collapse or partially collapse the universal Schur expansion and give strong numerical constraints.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Any proposed combinatorial interpretation of hook c_λ(n) should specialize correctly to the one-variable inversion distribution and to known r=2 diagonal coinvariant data. Evaluating hook Schur functions at 1^m can also convert a hook formula into testable dimension identities.

proof_techniques.name: Identify highest-weight vectors explicitly in examples
proof_techniques.description: In the proof of the explicit rank-one and rank-two formulas, the paper constructs bases of diagonal harmonics and then identifies the highest-weight vectors responsible for each Schur summand. For the dihedral case, most one-row Schur terms come from classical harmonic polynomials, while the exceptional s_11 component is represented by polynomials x_i y_j - x_j y_i. This illustrates how a Schur expansion can be proven by writing concrete harmonic highest-weight generators.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Hook Schur functions are naturally highest-weight representations with exterior-type components in the leg. The example suggests looking for determinant or alternating-row expressions analogous to x_i y_j - x_j y_i to realize hook highest weights in type A diagonal harmonics.

proof_techniques.name: Low-degree quotient approximation by polynomial-ring character divided by invariant-ring Hilbert series
proof_techniques.description: Section 4 uses the exact freeness picture for r=1 as a guide for r>=2. Although R_n is not free over diagonal invariants for multiple variable sets, the paper proposes that low-degree pieces behave as if R_n ≈ H_n ⊗ R_n^{S_n}, giving the approximation H_n(w;q) ≃ h_n[wH(q)]/h_n[H(q)] in small q-degree. Expanding this quotient gives explicit polynomial-in-n formulas for low-degree h-coefficients and, via Cauchy identities, for low-degree Frobenius components.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For hooks of small total size relative to n, this approximation may provide candidate formulas or consistency checks. It is especially useful for generating low-degree data before seeking a full all-degree combinatorial interpretation.

other_useful_info: The paper gives explicit universal Hilbert-series data for S_n for n <= 5 in the h-basis. In particular H_1(q)=1, H_2(q)=1+h_1, H_3(q)=1+2h_1+h_2+h_11+h_3, and H_4(q)=1+3h_1+2h_2+3h_11+2h_3+3h_21+h_111+h_4+4h_31+2h_5+h_41+h_6; the longer H_5(q) expansion is displayed in equation (15). It also lists universal Frobenius Schur expansions for H_3, H_4, and H_5; for example H_3(w;q)=S_3(w)+(s_2+s_1)S_21(w)+(s_3+s_11)S_111(w). The paper notes that the coefficient of m_{1^n}(w) in the monomial-basis Frobenius expansion is the Hilbert series of the underlying representation. It observes h-positivity for the computed cases n <= 5 but reports that h-positivity fails in general, with a degree-9 term of H_6(q) containing a negative -h_9 contribution; Schur positivity, coming from GL_r representation theory, is the robust positivity statement. The paper cites Haglund-Haiman-Loehr-Remmel-Ulyanov for a combinatorial formula for the r=2 diagonal coinvariant character and Haiman’s Hilbert-scheme results, and it records the relation H_n(w;q,t)=∇(e_{n+1}(w)) as a constraint in the two-variable case. It also records dimension specializations for small n; at one set they give n!, at two sets they give (n+1)^{n-1}, and for three sets the paper points to conjectural/generalized Tamari connections in related work.
why_relevant: Establishes the universal Schur expansion viewpoint for multivariate diagonal coinvariant Hilbert series and explains why coefficients are independent of the number of variable sets. This is foundational for the coefficients c_lambda(n) appearing in the problem.

title: A Proof of the Shuffle Conjecture
overall_summary: Carlsson and Mellit prove the Haglund-Haiman-Loehr-Remmel-Ulyanov shuffle conjecture and the stronger Haglund-Morse-Zabrocki compositional shuffle conjecture. In the paper's conventions, the main theorem identifies ∇ C_{α_1}⋯C_{α_k}(1) with a parking-function generating function over Dyck paths with prescribed touch composition α, and summing over α gives the parking-function formula for the Frobenius character of the diagonal coinvariant algebra in two sets of variables. The proof replaces the area/dinv model by a bounce/area-prime model, introduces symmetric characteristic functions of Dyck paths closely related to LLT polynomials, derives raising and lowering operators on spaces V_k = Sym[X][y_1,...,y_k], and then constructs an operator algebra with an antilinear involution extending ∇ composed with conjugation. For the original hook-coefficient problem, the most relevant contribution is the proven combinatorial control of the diagonal coinvariant Frobenius series by parking functions and Dyck-path/LLT recursions, which can serve as input for Schur coefficient extraction or for comparison with later Delta/Theta/superspace formulas.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 7.5, with the ordinary shuffle conjecture as a corollary
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Use the paper's sign conventions. Let α = (α_1,...,α_k) be a composition of n. Let C_r be the plethystic operator on Sym[X] defined by (C_r F)[X] = - q^{1-r} F[X + (q^{-1}-1) z^{-1}] Exp[zX] |_{z^r}, and let C_α = C_{α_1}⋯C_{α_k}. Let ∇ be the modified Macdonald eigenoperator defined by ∇ H̃_μ = (-1)^{|μ|} q^{n(μ')} t^{n(μ)} H̃_μ. For a Dyck path π of length n, let area(π) be the number of cells between π and the diagonal, let touch(π) be the composition recording gaps between consecutive diagonal touch points, and define word parking functions WP_π as labelings w = (w_1,...,w_n) by positive integers on the cells immediately east of north steps, strictly decreasing when two such cells lie in the same column. Let Dinv(π) = {(j,j') : j < j' and a_j = a_{j'}} ∪ {(j,j') : j' < j and a_{j'} = a_j + 1}, where a_j is the area sequence, and let dinv(π,w) count those pairs (j,j') in Dinv(π) with w_j > w_{j'}. Define D_α(X;q,t) = ∑_{touch(π)=α} t^{area(π)} ∑_{w in WP_π} q^{dinv(π,w)} x_{w_1}⋯x_{w_n}. Then ∇ C_α(1) = D_α(X;q,t). Since Haglund-Morse-Zabrocki had proved ∑_{α ⊨ n} C_α(1) = (-1)^n e_n in these conventions, summing over all compositions α of n gives the shuffle formula (-1)^n ∇ e_n = ∑_{|π|=n} t^{area(π)} ∑_{w in WP_π} q^{dinv(π,w)} x_w.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is assembled from two independently constructed recursions. First, Theorem 4.11 constructs, for each composition α of length k, an element N_α in V_k = Sym[X][y_1,...,y_k] such that D_α = d_-^k N_α, where d_- is a lowering operator. The recursion for N_α is obtained purely from Dyck-path combinatorics after moving from the original area/dinv statistics to bounce/area-prime statistics. Second, Theorem 7.4 constructs an antilinear involution N on V_* = ⊕_k V_k that extends ∇ composed with the conjugation bar-omega on V_0 = Sym[X], commutes with d_-, and satisfies N(y^α) = q^{|α|-k} N_α for y^α = y_1^{α_1-1}⋯y_k^{α_k-1}. Hence N_α = N(q^{|α|-k} y^α), where antilinearity accounts for the q power. Therefore D_α = d_-^k N(q^{|α|-k} y^α) = N(q^{|α|-k} d_-^k y^α). The definition of d_- gives d_-^k(y^α) = (-1)^k B_α(1), where B_r is the companion Hall-Littlewood creation operator. The paper's B/C sign conventions imply q^{|α|-k} (-1)^k B_α(1) = bar-omega C_α(1). Thus D_α = N bar-omega C_α(1) = ∇ C_α(1).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Propositions 2.2 and 2.4, area/dinv to bounce/area-prime reformulation with touch composition
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Given a Dyck path π of length n with area sequence a(π), let (x_j,j) be the cell immediately east of the j-th north step. Sort these cells in reading order, meaning by increasing a_j and breaking ties by increasing j, and let σ_j be the position of (x_j,j) in this order. There is a unique Dyck path π' with Area(π') = {(σ_j,σ_{j'}) : (j,j') in Dinv(π)}. Under this bijection, dinv(π) = area(π'), and area(π) = bounce(π'), where bounce is defined by the usual bounce path. The paper defines a refined touch statistic touch'(π') by writing π' = N^l E ~tπ and setting t_i = bounce(N^{i+1} E N^{l-i} E ~tπ) for 0 ≤ i ≤ l, then touch'(π') = (t_0-t_1,...,t_{l-1}-t_l); Proposition 2.2 states that touch'(π') = touch(π). If labels are transported by w'_{σ_j} = w_j, then dinv(π,w) becomes inv(π',w'), the number of cells (i,j) under π' with w'_i > w'_j, and the parking-function column condition becomes the condition w'_i > w'_j for every corner (i,j) of π'. Consequently D_α(X;q,t) also equals ∑_{touch'(π)=α} t^{bounce(π)} ∑_{w in WP'_π} q^{inv(π,w)} x_w, where WP'_π consists of all positive labelings satisfying the corner inequalities.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The reading-order permutation σ is chosen so that, for each original north-step cell, its attacking cells in the dinv relation are exactly the later cells in reading order before the position where the cell one row higher would be inserted. This makes σ(Dinv(π)) the area set of a Dyck path π'. The bounce sequence b of π' satisfies b_{σ_j} = a_j, because the first index of each bounce block is determined by the first later nonattack; therefore bounce(π') = ∑ b_i = ∑ a_j = area(π). To compare touch compositions, split π at its i-th diagonal touch point (x,x), form a new Dyck path ˆπ by taking a north step, then the upper part of π, then an east step, then the lower part, and observe that the area of ˆπ is area(π)+n-x. Under the bijection this ˆπ maps to N^{i+1} E N^{l-i} E ~tπ, so the differences of the bounce values t_i recover the gaps between original touch points. For labels, σ transports dinv inequalities to ordinary inversions below π'. The only remaining parking-function restrictions come from consecutive north-step cells in the same column, and these are exactly the corners of π'.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Propositions 3.2, 3.3, 3.4, and 3.5, simple characteristic functions of Dyck paths
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a Dyck path π of length n, define its simple characteristic function χ(π) = ∑_{w in Z_{>0}^n} q^{inv(π,w)} x_{w_1}⋯x_{w_n}, where inv(π,w) counts pairs (i,j) in Area(π), with i < j, such that w_i > w_j. Proposition 3.2 states that χ(π) is symmetric in the variables x_1,x_2,.... If π^{op} is the reversed Dyck path obtained by reversing steps and exchanging north and east, Proposition 3.3 states χ(π) = χ(π^{op}). If bar-omega is the antilinear involution sending q to q^{-1} and X to -X, Proposition 3.4 states bar-omega χ(π) = (-1)^{|π|} q^{-area(π)} χ(π). Proposition 3.5 gives the plethystic no-attack specialization χ(π)[(q-1)X] = (q-1)^{|π|} ∑_{w no attack} q^{inv(π,w)} x_w, where no attack means w_i ≠ w_j whenever (i,j) is a cell below π.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For symmetry, encode a Dyck path by its attack set R ⊂ {(i,j):1≤i<j≤n}, which satisfies the interval closure property that if i<j<k and (i,k) is in R then both (i,j) and (j,k) are in R. To prove invariance under swapping adjacent variables x_i and x_{i+1}, fix the positions S occupied by labels i and i+1 and the labels outside S. The contribution factors as a monomial in outside variables times a two-variable characteristic function χ(R_S,|S|)(x_i,x_{i+1}). Induct on |R|. Choose a maximal attack pair (a,b); removing it gives R'. The difference χ(R,k)-χ(R',k) receives contributions only from words with w_a=2 and w_b=1, and the contribution factors as (q-1)x_1x_2 χ(R'_S,k-2), symmetric by induction. The reversal identity follows by reversing the order of components in the monomial-coefficient interpretation. For the bar-omega and no-attack identities, expand χ(π) in Gessel quasisymmetric functions Q_{n,Des(σ^{-1})} via standardization of words. Then use the standard superization formula for Q-functions over the ordered alphabet 1 < ̅1 < 2 < ̅2 < ⋯. Setting X=0,Y=-X gives the non-strict inversion form and, after reversing label order, Proposition 3.4. Setting X=qX,Y=X and applying the sign-flipping involution from Haglund-Haiman-Loehr cancels all terms with equal absolute values on attacking positions, leaving exactly no-attack words and producing the factor (q-1)^n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.7 and equation (3.7), weighted characteristic functions and corner flipping
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a Dyck path π, let c(π) be its set of corners, meaning cells above the path whose southern and eastern neighbors are below the path. Given a weight function wt:c(π)→R, define χ(π,wt) = ∑_{w in Z_{>0}^n} q^{inv(π,w)} (∏_{(i,j) in c(π), w_i ≤ w_j} wt(i,j)) x_w. Then χ(π,wt) is symmetric in the x variables. More precisely, if (i,j) is a corner of π, wt_1 is obtained from wt by setting wt(i,j)=1, π' is the Dyck path obtained by flipping this corner inside out, and wt_2 is the induced weight on π' that agrees with wt on common corners and is 1 on new corners, then χ(π,wt) = (q wt(i,j)-1)/(q-1) χ(π,wt_1) + (1-wt(i,j))/(q-1) χ(π',wt_2). In particular, for wt=0 one obtains the inclusion-exclusion formula χ(π,0) = (1-q)^{-|c(π)|} ∑_{S⊂c(π)} (-1)^{|S|} χ(π_S,1), where π_S is obtained by flipping all corners in S.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Group both sides of the corner recurrence by the same label vector w. The only local distinction is whether w_i > w_j or w_i ≤ w_j at the chosen corner. In the first case the corner contributes q on the flipped path side and no weight on the original weighted side; in the second case it contributes the scalar wt(i,j). A direct two-case calculation shows that the displayed linear combination reproduces exactly the original coefficient. Iterating the recurrence over all corners expresses χ(π,wt) as a linear combination of unweighted χ(ρ,1)=χ(ρ), which are symmetric by Proposition 3.2. Setting wt identically zero yields the explicit alternating corner-flip formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.4 and Corollary 4.6, raising/lowering operator formula for characteristic functions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let V_k = Sym[X][y_1,...,y_k]. Define T_i = Δ^*_{y_i,y_{i+1}} on V_k by Δ^*_{uv}P = ((q-1)uP(u,v)+(v-qu)P(v,u))/(v-u). Define d_+:V_k→V_{k+1} by (d_+F)[X] = T_1 T_2⋯T_k (F[X+(q-1)y_{k+1}]), and define d_-:V_k→V_{k-1} by (d_-F)[X] = - F[X-(q-1)y_k] Exp[-y_k^{-1}X] |_{y_k^{-1}}. If π is a Dyck path of length n and ε_1⋯ε_{2n} is its step word read from southwest to northeast, with east step encoded by + and north step encoded by -, then χ(π) = d_{ε_1}⋯d_{ε_{2n}}(1) in V_0 = Sym[X]. Moreover, χ(π,0), the characteristic function imposing corner inequalities, is computed by starting from 1, following the path from right to left, applying (q-1)^{-1}[d_-,d_+] at each corner, and applying d_- or d_+ for a north or east step not belonging to a corner.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof first extends χ from complete Dyck paths to partial Dyck paths π in D_{k,n}, running from (0,k) to (n,n). For a tuple σ of distinct special labels, define χ'_σ(π) as the no-attack generating function in which the first k positions are forced to have labels σ_1,...,σ_k. After substituting special labels as y variables and ordinary labels as x variables, normalize by χ_σ(π)[X] = (y_1⋯y_k)^{-1}(q-1)^{|π|} χ'_σ(π)[X/(q-1)]. This lies in V_k, using the no-attack plethystic identity. For an east step, inserting a new special label k+1 at the front gives χ_σ(Eπ)=q^k χ_k(π)[X+(q-1)y_{k+1}] for σ=(k+1,1,...,k). Reordering σ to the identity is controlled by Proposition 4.8: swapping adjacent special labels m,m+1 applies Δ_{z_m,z_{m+1}}, so the product of Demazure-Lusztig operators gives exactly d_+. For a north step, one sums over all possible values of the removed special label. Expanding χ_k(π) uniquely as ∑_{i≥1} y_k^i g_i[X+y_k] and repeatedly applying swap operators gives explicit functions f_{i,r}; Proposition 4.10 proves f_{i,r} = (h_i[(1-q)X_r]-h_i[(1-q)X_{r-1}])/(1-q). Summing over r gives (1-q)^{-1}h_i[(1-q)(X+y_k)], which is precisely the coefficient-extraction formula defining d_-. The corner version follows by combining Theorem 4.4 with the corner-flip inclusion-exclusion formula, since flipping a corner replaces d_-d_+ by d_+d_- and hence produces the commutator.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.11, recursion for the compositional parking-function sums D_α
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a composition α of length ℓ, let D_α(X;q,t) be the compositional parking-function sum. Then D_α = d_-^ℓ(N_α), where N_α belongs to V_ℓ and is defined recursively as follows. For the empty composition, N_empty = 1. If α is any composition, then N_{[1,α]} = d_+ N_α. If a > 1 and aα denotes the composition obtained by prefixing a to α, then N_{aα} = t^{a-1}/(q-1) [d_-,d_+] ∑_{β ⊨ a-1} d_-^{ℓ(β)-1} N_{αβ}, where β ranges over all compositions of a-1, ℓ(β) is its length, and αβ denotes concatenation.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Work on the bounce/area-prime side of Proposition 2.4. Let D_k^0 be the set of partial Dyck paths in D_k that begin with an east step, with the empty path included for k=0. Define χ^0 on these paths by χ^0(empty)=1, χ^0(Eπ)=d_+χ^0(π), and χ^0(EN^iπ)=(q-1)^{-1}[d_-,d_+]d_-^{i-1}χ^0(π). Corollary 4.6 implies that if a full path π with touch'(π)=α has the form π=N^ℓ ~tπ with ~tπ in D_ℓ^0, then χ(π,0)=d_-^ℓχ^0(~tπ). Hence D_α equals d_-^ℓ times the t^{bounce}-weighted sum of χ^0 over such ~tπ; call that weighted sum N'_α. It remains to show N'_α obeys the displayed recursion. For a composition α of length ℓ and 0≤r≤ℓ, define γ_{α,r}(π)=N^{r+1} E N^{ℓ-r} ~tπ. The bounce changes by adding ∑_{i>r} α_i, and the refined touch composition becomes (1+∑_{i>r}α_i, α_1,...,α_r). Every nonempty Dyck path is uniquely obtained in this way from a smaller path. Translating this unique decomposition through the definition of χ^0 gives exactly the recursion above, so N'_α=N_α.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.14, evaluation identity underlying the Catalan special case
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let π be a Dyck path of size N ending with k east steps, and write its step word as ε_1⋯ε_{2N-k} followed by k plus signs. For any f in V_k, one has (d_{ε_1}⋯d_{ε_{2N-k}} f)[-1] = (-1)^N q^{area(π)} f[-q^k] evaluated at y_i=q^{i-1}. Here the bracket notation is plethystic substitution in the symmetric-function alphabet X. The paper notes that this recovers the Garsia-Xin-Zabrocki recursions for the Catalan case of the compositional shuffle conjecture.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Define ρ_k(f)=f[-q^k]|_{y_i=q^{i-1}}. Induct on the number 2N-k of non-final steps. If the last processed step is an east step, then ρ_{k+1}(d_+f)=ρ_{k+1}(f[X+(q-1)y_{k+1}])=ρ_k(f), using ρ_{k+1}T_i=ρ_{k+1} and y_{k+1}=q^k. This corresponds to no change in the relevant Dyck path area. If the last processed step is a north step, apply the explicit coefficient formula for d_- to f y_{k+1}^a, with f homogeneous of symmetric degree d, obtaining ρ_k(d_-(f y_{k+1}^a)) = - q^{d+1-(k-1)a}ρ_k(f). The exponent matches the area change when removing the north step. The base case is immediate.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.4, the commutator d_-d_+ - d_+d_- realizes multiplication by y_k
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For F in V_k, the operators d_+, d_-, and T_i satisfy (d_-d_+ - d_+d_-)F = (q-1) T_1T_2⋯T_{k-1}(-y_k F). Moreover multiplication by y_i is related to multiplication by y_{i+1} through y_i = q^{-1} T_i y_{i+1} T_i. Thus the polynomial y variables can be recovered internally from the raising/lowering and Hecke operators.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Introduce a twisted Sym[X]-action on V_k by (F*G)[X]=F[X+(q-1)(y_1+⋯+y_k)]G. Both d_+ and d_- commute with this twisted action, so it suffices to verify the first identity on polynomials in the y variables. After factoring T_1⋯T_{k-1}, the needed identity reduces to d_-T_kF - (d_-F)[X+(q-1)y_k] = (1-q)y_kF. Since the variables y_1,...,y_{k-1} play no role, it is enough to take F=y_k^i. A direct calculation using T_k(y_k^i)=y_{k+1}^i+(1-q)∑_{j=1}^i y_k^j y_{k+1}^{i-j} and the coefficient formula for d_- gives (1-q)y_k^{i+1}. The second identity follows directly from the defining formula for T_i as the Demazure-Lusztig operator.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.2 and Lemma 5.6, Dyck path algebra action and PBW-type basis
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Define the Dyck path algebra A over Q(q,t) as the path algebra with vertices indexed by k≥0, arrows d_+:k→k+1 and d_-:k+1→k, loops T_1,...,T_{k-1} at vertex k, and relations: (T_i-1)(T_i+q)=0, braid and distant-commutation relations among the T_i, T_i d_- = d_- T_i, d_+ T_i = T_{i+1}d_+, T_1 d_+^2=d_+^2, d_-^2 T_{k-1}=d_-^2, d_-(d_+d_- - d_-d_+)T_{k-1}=q(d_+d_- - d_-d_+)d_- for k≥2, and T_1(d_+d_- - d_-d_+)d_+ = q d_+(d_+d_- - d_-d_+). The operators d_± and T_i on V_* = ⊕_k V_k define a representation of A. The map A e_0 → V_* sending e_0 to 1 is an isomorphism. More explicitly, elements d_-^m y_1^{a_1}⋯y_{k+m}^{a_{k+m}} d_+^{k+m} e_0 with a_{k+1}≥a_{k+2}≥⋯≥a_{k+m} form a basis of A e_0, and their images form a basis of V_*; the symmetric-function factor is a product B_{a_{k+1}+1}⋯B_{a_{k+m}+1}(1), a Hall-Littlewood basis element up to scalar.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The representation relations are checked from the Demazure-Lusztig identities for T_i and from the definitions of d_±. The nontrivial d_-^2T_{k-1}=d_-^2 relation reduces to the antisymmetry in the indices a,b of qB_{a+2}B_{b+1}-B_{a+1}B_{b+2}, using a Hall-Littlewood operator identity from Haglund-Morse-Zabrocki. The commutator relations use Lemma 5.4 to rewrite d_+d_- - d_-d_+ as y_k times Hecke operators. For spanning, Lemma 5.5 shows that the internally defined y_i commute appropriately with T_j and d_- and have a controlled commutation with d_+. Reduction rules move all d_- to the left, all d_+ to the right, and all T_i away from the cyclic vector e_0. The relation d_-^2T_{k-1}=d_-^2 gives straightening relations that reorder the exponents a_{k+1},...,a_{k+m} into weakly decreasing order. Finally, the image of a basis element is (-1)^m y_1^{a_1}⋯y_k^{a_k} B_{a_{k+1}+1}⋯B_{a_{k+m}+1}(1); because the latter B-products with weakly decreasing shifted exponents form a Hall-Littlewood basis of Sym[X], these images are linearly independent and span.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 6.1, conjugate action of A*
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A* be the algebra A with q replaced by q^{-1}; write its generators as d_+^*, d_-^*, T_i^*, e_i^*. On V_* there is an action of A* given by T_i^*=T_i^{-1}, d_-^*=d_-, e_i^*=e_i, and, for F in V_k, (d_+^*F)[X] = γ F[X+(q-1)y_{k+1}], where γ sends y_i to y_{i+1} for i=1,...,k and sends y_{k+1} to t y_1. If z_i denotes the A*-analogue of y_i, this action satisfies the additional relations z_{i+1}d_+ = d_+z_i, y_{i+1}d_+^* = d_+^*y_i, z_1d_+ = -y_1d_+^* t q^{k+1} at vertex k, and d_+^* d_+^m(1)=d_+^{m+1}(1) for every m≥0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The easy Hecke relations follow directly from the definition of d_+^* and the braid relations for T_i^{-1}. The harder relations are checked using a family of twisted multiplications indexed by m, namely F *_m G = F[X+(q-1)(t y_1+⋯+t y_m+y_{m+1}+⋯+y_k)]G. The identities d_+^*(F*_mG)=F*_{m+1}d_+^*G and d_-(F*_mG)=F*_m d_-G reduce many operator relations to checks on pure y monomials. The relation d_-(d_+^*d_- - d_-d_+^*)T_{k-1}^{-1}=q^{-1}(d_+^*d_- - d_-d_+^*)d_- is reduced to an expression involving B_aB_b that is antisymmetric in a,b. The relation T_1^{-1}(d_+^*d_- - d_-d_+^*)d_+^* = q^{-1}d_+^*(d_+^*d_- - d_-d_+^*) is reduced to a one-variable complete symmetric function identity. The z_i d_+ relations follow by comparing how z_1d_+ and y_1d_+^* commute with twisted multiplication and y variables, then checking on 1; the remaining cases follow by applying T-operators.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 6.6, recursions for monomials y^α under the conjugate action
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a composition α=(α_1,...,α_k), set y^α = y_1^{α_1-1}⋯y_k^{α_k-1} in V_k. Then the monomials y^α satisfy recursions dual to those defining N_α. First, y^{(1,α)} = d_+^* y^α. Second, for a>1, y^{(a,α)} = t^{1-a}/(q-1) (d_+^*d_- - d_-d_+^*) ∑_{β ⊨ a-1} q^{1-ℓ(β)} d_-^{ℓ(β)-1} y^{(α,β)}, where β ranges over compositions of a-1 and (α,β) denotes concatenation.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The first identity is immediate from the cyclic definition of d_+^*. For the second, use the commutation (d_-d_+^* - d_+^*d_-)y_i = y_{i+1}(d_-d_+^* - d_+^*d_-) to reduce to a universal identity involving only the last y variable and the first y variable after applying d_+^*. Group the sum over β by b=β_1-1 and use the Hall-Littlewood creation identity h_n[q^{-1}X] = ∑_{β ⊨ n} q^{-ℓ(β)}(-1)^{ℓ(β)}B_β(1), obtained from the Haglund-Morse-Zabrocki identity for the C operators by applying bar-omega. The right side becomes (d_+^*d_- - d_-d_+^*)∑_{b=0}^{a-1} y_k^b h_{a-b-1}[q^{-1}X], which is evaluated by commuting the translation operator Γ_+(t(q-1)y_1) past B operators. The only remaining assertion is ∑_{b=0}^m B_b h_{m-b}[q^{-1}X]=0 for m>0; this follows again from the same Hall-Littlewood identity together with B_0 C_m = q C_m B_0 and B_0(1)=1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorems 7.3 and 7.4, quotient algebra and involution extending ∇ bar-omega
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let ~A be the quotient of the free product of A and A* by the relations d_-^*=d_-, T_i^*=T_i^{-1}, e_i^*=e_i, z_{i+1}d_+=d_+z_i, y_{i+1}d_+^*=d_+^*y_i, and z_1d_+=-y_1d_+^*tq^{k+1}. The operators on V_* define an action of ~A, and the kernel of the natural map ~A e_0 → V_* sending f e_0 to f(1) is I e_0, where I is the ideal generated by d_+^* d_+^m - d_+^{m+1} for all m≥0. Thus V_* ≅ ~A e_0 / I e_0. Consequently there is a unique antilinear degree-preserving automorphism N:V_*→V_* satisfying N(1)=1, N T_i = T_i^{-1}N, N d_- = d_-N, N d_+ = d_+^*N, and N y_i = z_i N. This N is an involution. For every composition α, N(y^α)=q^{∑(α_i-1)}N_α. On V_0=Sym[X], N equals ∇ bar-omega, where bar-omega sends q,t,X to q^{-1},t^{-1},-X.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Theorem 6.1 supplies the ~A action and the relations d_+^*d_+^m(1)=d_+^{m+1}(1), so ~A e_0/I e_0 maps onto V_*. The inclusion A e_0→~A e_0/I e_0 maps isomorphically to V_* by Theorem 5.2. To prove it is also surjective inside the quotient, filter by total degree in d_+,d_+^*,d_-. Inductively reduce any d_+^*F, with F already in A e_0, using the PBW form of Lemma 5.6: if F=d_+^m(1) then the ideal relation replaces d_+^*F by d_+^{m+1}(1); if F=y_iG then d_+^*F=y_{i+1}d_+^*G; if F=d_-G then commute d_+^* past d_- at the cost of z_i terms, and move those z_i past d_+ or d_- using the defining ~A relations until the induction hypothesis applies. The defining relations of ~A are symmetric under the antilinear involution that swaps A and A*, sends q,t to q^{-1},t^{-1}, and exchanges y_i with z_i; this involution preserves I and hence descends to N on V_*. Applying N to the recursions of Proposition 6.6 gives exactly the recursions defining N_α in Theorem 4.11, with the stated q factor. Finally, on V_0 define D_1F[X]=F[X+(1-q)(1-t)u^{-1}]Exp[-uX]|_{u^1} and D_1^*F[X]=F[X-(1-q^{-1})(1-t^{-1})u^{-1}]Exp[uX]|_{u^1}. The identities D_1=-d_-d_+^*, e_1=d_-d_+, and bar-omega D_1=D_1^*bar-omega imply that N bar-omega has the same commutation relations with e_1 and D_1^* as ∇. A result of Garsia-Haiman-Tesler says these relations characterize ∇ because all symmetric functions are generated from 1 by repeated application of e_1 and D_1^*. Hence N=∇bar-omega on V_0.

proof_techniques.name: Compositional refinement by touch points
proof_techniques.description: Instead of proving the shuffle formula only after summing over all Dyck paths of size n, the paper refines the statement by the touch composition α, which records the distances between consecutive contacts of the path with the diagonal. This produces smaller objects D_α and algebraic inputs C_α(1), and the global identity follows from the known decomposition ∑_{α⊨n}C_α(1)=(-1)^n e_n.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For hook Schur coefficients, a comparable refinement may isolate subfamilies whose contributions satisfy tractable recursions before summation. Touch composition is a natural statistic to try whenever parking-function formulas for diagonal coinvariants appear.

proof_techniques.name: Reading-order area/dinv to bounce/area-prime bijection
proof_techniques.description: The paper constructs the Haglund-Loehr area/dinv-to-bounce/area-prime bijection by sorting the north-step cells of a Dyck path in reading order and using the resulting permutation σ to send the dinv attack set to an area set. This converts dinv into ordinary inversions below a new path, converts area into bounce, and translates the parking-function column condition into inequalities at corners of the new path.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This change of statistics can turn dinv-type conditions into local corner conditions, which are easier to impose by inclusion-exclusion or operator commutators. If hook coefficients are expressed through parking functions, the bounce-side model may be the more usable one.

proof_techniques.name: Dyck-path characteristic functions as symmetric/LLT-type functions
proof_techniques.description: For a Dyck path π, the generating function χ(π)=∑ q^{inv}x^w over all labelings is proved symmetric by an attack-set induction, and its plethystic specializations are controlled by superization of Gessel quasisymmetric functions. The paper also notes that these χ functions and their corner-restricted variants are instances of path symmetric functions and LLT polynomials.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Schur coefficients, especially hook coefficients, can often be attacked by expanding or specializing symmetric functions. Identifying parking-function summands with LLT-type objects gives access to known Schur-positivity and coefficient-extraction tools.

proof_techniques.name: Corner-flipping inclusion-exclusion
proof_techniques.description: The weighted characteristic function χ(π,wt) tracks failures or satisfactions of inequalities at corners. A two-case local identity expresses χ(π,wt) as a linear combination of the characteristic function with the corner weight removed and the characteristic function of the path with that corner flipped. Setting all weights to zero gives an explicit alternating sum over all subsets of corners.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a hook coefficient formula requires enforcing strict inequalities or excluding equal labels, this provides a systematic way to replace constrained sums by unconstrained symmetric functions indexed by modified paths.

proof_techniques.name: Partial Dyck paths and raising/lowering operators
proof_techniques.description: The proof embeds complete Dyck paths into a larger state space of partial Dyck paths D_k and represents adding an east or north step by explicit operators d_+ and d_- on V_k=Sym[X][y_1,...,y_k]. The extra y variables record special labels at the boundary, and Demazure-Lusztig operators T_i account for reordering these special labels.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Universal Schur coefficients independent of the number of grading variables often suggest a hidden stable state space. A V_k-type construction may provide finite recursive control over coefficient families such as hooks.

proof_techniques.name: Operator-algebra presentation with PBW basis
proof_techniques.description: The paper packages d_± and T_i into a Dyck path algebra A and proves that A e_0 is isomorphic to V_* via a PBW-like basis d_-^m y^a d_+^{k+m}e_0. This establishes that the operator recursions have no hidden relations beyond the stated algebraic ones and connects the symmetric-function part to Hall-Littlewood creation operators.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For coefficient extraction problems, a PBW basis can turn identities in diagonal coinvariant rings into explicit normal forms. Hook shapes might correspond to restricted normal-form patterns or to particular Hall-Littlewood components.

proof_techniques.name: Antilinear involution extending ∇
proof_techniques.description: The central algebraic step is to construct a conjugate action A* and a larger quotient algebra ~A whose relations are invariant under q,t inversion and swapping A with A*. This descends to an antilinear involution N on V_*; on V_0 it is identified with ∇bar-omega by commutation relations with e_1 and D_1^*.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: When a combinatorial recursion is available but its relation to diagonal coinvariants is unclear, constructing an involution or symmetry that matches ∇ can certify that the recursion computes the desired coinvariant object.

proof_techniques.name: Specialization identities for scalar coefficients
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 4.14 evaluates strings of d_± operators at X=-1 and y_i=q^{i-1}, producing a closed expression involving q^{area(π)}. This is how the paper connects its refined recursion to earlier Catalan-special-case recursions.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Hook Schur coefficients may be accessible through specializations, scalar products, or finite-alphabet evaluations. The proposition is a model for converting operator recursions into explicit graded enumerators.

other_useful_info: The paper states as background Haiman's theorem that the Frobenius character of the diagonal coinvariant algebra in n pairs of variables is FR_n(X;q,t)=(-1)^n∇e_n in the paper's conventions; combined with Theorem 7.5 this is the proven parking-function Frobenius formula. The paper's sign conventions differ from some standard sources: its ∇ includes a factor (-1)^{|μ|} on H̃_μ and its C_r differs from Haglund-Morse-Zabrocki by (-1)^r, so external formulas must be sign-adjusted. Remark 3.6 identifies χ(π',0) with Haglund's path symmetric functions F_π(X;q) and χ(π',1) with an LLT polynomial of a disjoint union of single boxes determined by the area sequence. Example 3.10 rewrites the Haglund-Haiman-Loehr formula for modified Macdonald polynomials as a weighted characteristic function χ(π_μ,wt_μ), with weights q^{arm}t^{-1-leg}. The proof explicitly recovers the Garsia-Xin-Zabrocki Catalan recursion as a special case, and the introduction points to later connections with rational shuffle conjectures, DAHA, Hilbert schemes, affine Springer fibers, and knot invariants.
why_relevant: Proves the compositional shuffle conjecture, thereby validating the main parking-function formula for the diagonal coinvariant algebra. This supplies proven combinatorial control over the nabla side that underlies later Delta, Theta, and superspace formulas.

title: The Delta Conjecture
overall_summary: The paper formulates the Delta Conjecture, giving two conjectural labeled-Dyck-path formulas, the rise version and the valley version, for the symmetric function Δ′_{e_k} e_n defined through modified Macdonald eigenoperators. It develops several equivalent combinatorial models for these formulas, including decorated labeled Dyck paths, leaning stacks, densely labeled Dyck paths, and ordered multiset partitions, and proves a number of specializations: q=0, t=0, q=t=1, t=1/q, and the k=1 rise case. The main proof tools are plethystic Macdonald identities, coefficient extraction by decorating Dyck-path features, bijections to ordered set partitions, LLT polynomial Schur expansions, and reciprocity identities for Delta operators. For the original hook-coefficient problem, the most relevant pieces are the hook scalar-product identity Proposition 7.1, the Delta reciprocity Lemma 6.1, the explicit k=1 Schur expansion supported on two-column shapes, and the ordered-partition interpretations of Delta specializations.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Conjecture 1.1, Delta Conjecture, stated but not proved in full
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let n>k≥0. Let LD_n be the set of labeled Dyck paths of order n, where labels are positive integers increasing up columns. For P∈LD_n, let area(P)=∑_i a_i(P), where a_i(P) is the number of full squares between row i of the path and the diagonal, let dinv(P)=∑_i d_i(P), where d_i(P) counts primary and secondary diagonal inversions beginning in row i, let x^P be the product of variables indexed by the labels of P, let Val(P) be the set of contractible valleys of P, and let Δ′_{e_k} be the Macdonald eigenoperator defined by Δ′_{e_k} H̃_μ = e_k[B_μ(q,t)-1] H̃_μ, where B_μ(q,t)=∑_{c∈μ} q^{a′(c)} t^{ℓ′(c)}. The conjecture asserts that Δ′_{e_k} e_n equals both of the following symmetric series: ∑_{P∈LD_n} q^{dinv(P)} t^{area(P)} ∏_{i: a_i(P)>a_{i-1}(P)} (1+z t^{-a_i(P)}) x^P, taking the coefficient of z^{n-k-1}, and ∑_{P∈LD_n} q^{dinv(P)} t^{area(P)} ∏_{i∈Val(P)} (1+z q^{-d_i(P)-1}) x^P, again taking the coefficient of z^{n-k-1}. Equivalently, replacing Δ′_{e_k} e_n by Δ_{e_k} e_n, multiplying the two right-hand sides by 1+z, and taking the coefficient of z^{n-k} gives an equivalent formulation.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper does not prove the conjecture in full. It establishes notation and then proves several equivalent formulations and special cases. The rise formula is later related to LLT polynomials through leaning stacks, which proves its symmetry and enables the k=1 case. The q=0, t=0, and M_{1^n} specializations are related to ordered set partitions. The t=1/q specialization is attacked from the symmetric-function side using a plethystic formula for Δ_f e_n. The k=n-1 case is identified with the Shuffle Conjecture, proved independently by Carlsson and Mellit.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.1, decorated Dyck path reformulation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For n>k≥0, define Rise_{n,k}(x;q,t) and Val_{n,k}(x;q,t) to be the two right-hand sides in the Delta Conjecture, respectively. For P∈LD_n, let Rise(P) be the set of double-rise rows, Fall(P) the set of double-fall columns, and Val(P) the set of contractible valleys. Let LDRise_{n,r}, LDFall_{n,r}, and LDVal_{n,r} be the sets of pairs (P,R), (P,F), and (P,V), where R⊆Rise(P), F⊆Fall(P), V⊆Val(P), and the selected set has size r. Define area⁻(P,R)=∑_{i∉R} a_i(P), define the analogous column version area⁻(P,F)=∑_{i∉F} c_i(P), and define dinv⁻(P,V)=∑_{i∉V} d_i(P)-|V|. Then Rise_{n,k}(x;q,t)=∑_{(P,R)∈LDRise_{n,n-k-1}} q^{dinv(P)} t^{area⁻(P,R)} x^P = ∑_{(P,F)∈LDFall_{n,n-k-1}} q^{dinv(P)} t^{area⁻(P,F)} x^P, and Val_{n,k}(x;q,t)=∑_{(P,V)∈LDVal_{n,n-k-1}} q^{dinv⁻(P,V)} t^{area(P)} x^P.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Expanding the product ∏(1+z t^{-a_i(P)}) in the rise formula and taking the coefficient of z^{n-k-1} is exactly the same as choosing n-k-1 double rises R, and the factor t^{-∑_{i∈R}a_i(P)} converts area(P) into area⁻(P,R). The same argument with the valley product chooses a subset V of contractible valleys and converts q^{dinv(P)} into q^{∑_{i∉V}d_i(P)-|V|}. The paper also explains why dinv⁻ is nonnegative: every decorated contractible valley v has at least one diagonal inversion of the form (i,v) with i not itself selected as a valley, using either the immediately preceding row when a_{v-1}=a_v, or a carefully chosen row at the same area together with the next rise when a_{v-1}>a_v. The equality between the rise-decorated and fall-decorated forms follows from the standard matching between double rises and double falls in a Dyck path, equivalently matching parentheses in the Dyck word.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.2, leaning-stack bijections
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix n>k≥0. A leaning stack S∈Stack_{n,k} is a sequence of n unit boxes in which exactly k boxes are diagonally northeast of the box below and the remaining adjacencies are vertical. Let LD(S) be the labeled paths from (0,0) to (k+1,n) staying weakly left of S, with labels increasing in columns, and let LDStack_{n,k}=⋃_{S∈Stack_{n,k}} LD(S). For P∈LD(S), define area(P) as the number of squares between P and S; define hdinv(P) using the height values h_i of rows and the usual primary and secondary inversion rules; define wdinv(P) using the area values a_i but allowing inversions to begin only in rows belonging to Diag(S), the set consisting of row 1 and rows where the stack moves diagonally, and subtracting n-k-1. Then there are bijections φ_{n,k}: LDFall_{n,n-k-1} → LDStack_{n,k} and ψ_{n,k}: LDVal_{n,n-k-1} → LDStack_{n,k} preserving labels and satisfying area(φ(P,F))=area⁻(P,F), hdinv(φ(P,F))=dinv(P), area(ψ(P,V))=area(P), and wdinv(ψ(P,V))=dinv⁻(P,V). Consequently Rise_{n,k}(x;q,t)=∑_{P∈LDStack_{n,k}} q^{hdinv(P)} t^{area(P)} x^P and Val_{n,k}(x;q,t)=∑_{P∈LDStack_{n,k}} q^{wdinv(P)} t^{area(P)} x^P.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For φ, start with the completely diagonal stack associated to an ordinary Dyck path. For each decorated double fall column j, remove the east step in column j+1 and move the corresponding stack square one unit left. The inverse pushes every vertical stack square one unit right and reinserts the corresponding east step. This removal accounts exactly for the column contribution subtracted in area⁻, and the new height statistic h_i agrees with the original area statistic a_i, so hdinv becomes the original dinv. For ψ, start again from the completely diagonal stack. For each decorated contractible valley row i, remove the east step immediately preceding the north step in row i and move the stack square in that row left. The inverse pushes vertical stack squares back and reinserts the missing east steps before those rows. This construction preserves the row area values and converts the restriction on inversion-start rows in wdinv into the exclusion of selected valley rows in dinv⁻, including the subtraction by |V|=n-k-1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.3, densely labeled Dyck path reformulation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For n>k≥0, let LDDense_{n,k} be the set of densely labeled Dyck paths built from an ordinary Dyck path D of order k+1 by labeling every square weakly above the diagonal whose northwest corner meets D. Squares adjacent to north steps are north squares and the others are east squares. Labels are finite sets of positive integers, no north square may receive the empty set, labels in north squares increase up each column in the sense that every entry below is smaller than every entry above, and the total number of label entries is n. Define area(P) by summing the diagonal distance of each label entry. Define wdinv(P) by counting pairs of entries (r,s) with r minimal in its square and r's square strictly west of s's square, with either r<s and equal area or r>s and area(r)=area(s)+1, then subtracting the number of entries in east squares. There is a bijection θ_{n,k}: LDStack_{n,k} → LDDense_{n,k} preserving area and wdinv. Hence Val_{n,k}(x;q,t)=∑_{P∈LDDense_{n,k}} q^{wdinv(P)} t^{area(P)} x^P.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The bijection θ contracts every north step of a leaning-stack path whose row contains a vertical stack adjacency. When a north step is contracted, its label is merged into the label set of the corresponding remaining square. This produces exactly the allowed set-labeled north and east squares of a densely labeled Dyck path. The inverse separates a set-labeled square according to the vertical stack adjacencies and expands the contracted north steps. Since contraction does not change the diagonal distance of any label entry, area is preserved. The definition of wdinv in the dense model was chosen so that the same inversion pairs counted in the stack model are counted after contraction, with the subtraction for east-square entries matching the subtraction in wdinv for vertical stack adjacencies.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.4, q=t=1 formula
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For integers 1≤k≤n, with X=x_1+x_2+⋯ and plethystic notation, the specialization of the rise side satisfies Rise_{n,k}(x;1,1)+Rise_{n,k-1}(x;1,1) = 1/(k+1) · binom(n,k) · coefficient of u^n in (∑_{i≥0} e_i u^i)^{k+1} = 1/(k+1) · binom(n,k) · e_n[(k+1)X]. The surrounding text notes that this proves the q=t=1 case of the Delta Conjecture.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: At q=t=1, adding the two adjacent rise expressions corresponds to ∑_{P∈LD_n} (1+z)^{|Rise(P)|+1} x^P and taking the coefficient of z^{n-k}. Classify a Dyck path by its vertical-run length partition λ⊢n, with c_i runs of length i and ℓ(λ) total runs. Such a path has n-ℓ(λ) double rises. The number of Dyck paths with these run multiplicities is 1/(n+1) times the multinomial coefficient binom(n+1; c_1,c_2,…,c_n,n-ℓ(λ)+1), as cited from earlier work. Labeling each vertical run increasingly contributes e_λ. Summing over λ and extracting the coefficient of z^{n-k} gives the coefficient of u^n in (∑e_i u^i)^{k+1} with the prefactor 1/(k+1) binom(n,k). The plethystic identity with e_n[(k+1)X] follows from Cauchy's formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.1, M_{1^n} coefficients at q=0, t=0, and related specializations
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For n>k≥0, the coefficient of the monomial quasisymmetric function M_{1^n} is the same in each of the following expressions: Rise_{n,k}(x;q,0), Rise_{n,k}(x;0,q), Val_{n,k}(x;q,0), Δ′_{e_k} e_n evaluated at t=0, and Δ′_{e_k} e_n evaluated at q=0 and then t=q. The coefficient of M_{1^n} in a symmetric function of degree n is equivalently the scalar product with p_{1^n}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper combines a result of Wilson with the ordered-partition interpretations in Proposition 4.1. Wilson's result states that the M_{1^n} coefficient of Δ′_{e_k}e_n at t=0 and at q=0,t=q is ∑_{π∈OP_{n,k+1}} q^{inv(π)}, where OP_{n,k+1} is the set of ordered set partitions of {1,…,n} into k+1 blocks. Proposition 4.1 identifies the M_{1^n} coefficients of the three combinatorial expressions with the distributions of dinv, maj, and inv on the same ordered set partitions. The known equidistribution of these statistics on ordered set partitions, from Remmel-Wilson and related work, then gives equality of all listed coefficients.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.1, ordered multiset partition interpretations
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let α=(α_1,…,α_r) be a composition, and let OP_{α,k+1} be the ordered multiset partitions of the multiset {1^{α_1},2^{α_2},…,r^{α_r}} into k+1 ordered blocks. For π∈OP_{α,k+1}, define inv(π) as the number of pairs a>b such that a lies in a block strictly to the left of b's block and b is minimal in its block. Define dinv(π) by writing π_i^h for the h-th smallest element of block i, starting at h=0, and counting primary triples (h,i,j) with i<j and π_i^h>π_j^h and secondary triples (h,i,j) with i<j and π_i^h<π_j^{h+1}. Define maj(π) by writing every block in decreasing order to obtain a word σ, defining w_0=0 and w_i=w_{i-1}+1 if σ_i is minimal in its block and w_i=w_{i-1} otherwise, and summing w_i over descents σ_i>σ_{i+1}. Define minimaj(π) by cyclically ordering blocks from right to left so as to minimize the major index, as described in the paper, and taking the major index of the resulting word. Then Rise_{n,k}(x;q,0)|_{M_α}=∑_{π∈OP_{α,k+1}} q^{dinv(π)}, Rise_{n,k}(x;0,q)|_{M_α}=∑_{π∈OP_{α,k+1}} q^{maj(π)}, Val_{n,k}(x;q,0)|_{M_α}=∑_{π∈OP_{α,k+1}} q^{inv(π)}, and Val_{n,k}(x;0,q)|_{M_α}=∑_{π∈OP_{α,k+1}} q^{minimaj(π)}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Rise_{n,k}(x;q,0), use the leaning-stack formula. Setting t=0 restricts to area-zero paths. Reading the columns from right to left gives the ordered blocks of a multiset partition, and the height-based hdinv statistic becomes the ordered-partition dinv statistic. For Rise_{n,k}(x;0,q), use the double-rise-decorated model and restrict to dinv-zero labeled paths. Such paths have weakly increasing area sequence, with strict area increase exactly controlled by adjacent label increases. Reading labels from top to bottom gives a multiset word, and each decorated rise joins two adjacent entries into one block; under this bijection the area statistic becomes maj. For Val_{n,k}(x;q,0), use the densely labeled model. Setting t=0 forces area zero, so the underlying Dyck path is the diagonal path (NE)^{k+1}; label sets read from right to left become blocks, and dense wdinv becomes inv. For Val_{n,k}(x;0,q), the appendix constructs a bijection γ_{α,k} from ordered multiset partitions to dense paths with wdinv=0. It forms the minimaj word τ of π, splits τ into increasing runs from right to left, and inserts the i-th run into squares of area i; the construction is invertible, and the sum of areas of entries is exactly maj(τ)=minimaj(π).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.2, equidistribution of ordered multiset partition statistics
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every composition α and every k, the four generating functions over ordered multiset partitions OP_{α,k+1} agree: ∑_{π∈OP_{α,k+1}} q^{minimaj(π)} = ∑_{π∈OP_{α,k+1}} q^{dinv(π)} = ∑_{π∈OP_{α,k+1}} q^{maj(π)} = ∑_{π∈OP_{α,k+1}} q^{inv(π)}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper reports that this statement was conjectured in an earlier preprint and had since been proved by Brendon Rhoades. No proof is included in this paper. Its role here is to explain why the missing Val_{n,k}(x;0,q) specialization is expected to match the other ordered-partition specializations, even though minimaj behaves differently from inv, maj, and dinv in the direct arguments.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.1, plethystic formula at t=1/q
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f be a homogeneous symmetric function of degree k. For every n, with [r]_q=1+q+⋯+q^{r-1} and plethystic notation, Δ_f e_n evaluated at t=1/q is f[[n]_q] · e_n[X[k+1]_q] divided by q^{k(n-1)}[k+1]_q. In formula form: Δ_f e_n|_{t=1/q} = f[[n]_q] e_n[X[k+1]_q] / (q^{k(n-1)}[k+1]_q). In particular, taking f=e_k gives Δ_{e_k} e_n|_{t=1/q} = q^{binom(k,2)-k(n-1)} [n choose k]_q e_n[X[k+1]_q]/[k+1]_q.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof begins with the specialization of modified Macdonald polynomials at t=1/q: H̃_μ[X;q,1/q] is a scalar multiple of s_μ[X/(1-q)]. Cauchy's formula gives e_n[X]=∑_{μ⊢n} s_{μ′}[X/(1-q)] s_μ[1-q]. The specialization s_μ[1-q] vanishes unless μ is a hook μ=(n-r,1^r), and in that case it equals (−q)^r(1-q). For the conjugate hook μ′=(r+1,1^{n-r-1}), the Macdonald eigenalphabet satisfies B_{μ′}(q,1/q)=q^{-(n-r-1)}[n]_q, so f[B_{μ′}(q,1/q)]=q^{-k(n-r-1)}f[[n]_q]. Applying Δ_f to the hook expansion of e_n therefore gives a sum over r with weights (−q^{k+1})^r(1-q^{k+1}), up to the factor f[[n]_q]/(q^{k(n-1)}[k+1]_q). A second application of Cauchy's formula identifies this sum with e_n[X[k+1]_q]. The e_k specialization uses e_k[[n]_q]=q^{binom(k,2)}[n choose k]_q.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 5.1, Schur positivity at t=1/q
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For integers n and k, the symmetric function q^{k(n-1)-binom(k,2)} Δ_{e_k} e_n evaluated at t=1/q is Schur positive.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Theorem 5.1 reduces the statement to showing Schur positivity of ([n choose k]_q/[k+1]_q) e_n[X[k+1]_q]. Let d=gcd(k+1,n). A theorem of Garsia, Leven, Wallach, and Xin states that ([d]_q/[k+1]_q) e_n[X[k+1]_q] is Schur positive. Thus it remains to prove that [n choose k]_q/[d]_q is a polynomial with nonnegative coefficients. Polynomiality is checked cyclotomically using the q-Lucas theorem: if Φ_p(q) divides [d]_q, then p divides n and k+1, hence n≡0 mod p and k≠0 mod p; q-Lucas gives [n choose k]_q≡0 mod Φ_p(q). Nonnegativity of the quotient follows from the cited proposition of Garsia-Leven-Wallach-Xin together with the known unimodality and positivity of q-binomial coefficients.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.1, Delta reciprocity identity from Haglund 2004
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For positive integers d and n and for every symmetric function f∈Λ^{(n)} of degree n, the Hall scalar products satisfy ⟨Δ_{e_{d-1}} e_n, f⟩ = ⟨Δ_{ω f} e_d, s_d⟩, where ω is the standard involution on symmetric functions and s_d is the one-row Schur function of degree d.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this as Corollary 2 of Haglund 2004 and does not reprove it. In Section 6 it is used with d=2 and f=s_λ to convert the Schur coefficient ⟨Δ_{e_1}e_n,s_λ⟩ into the much smaller calculation ⟨Δ_{s_{λ′}}e_2,s_2⟩. This reduction is the key step allowing an explicit Schur expansion for Δ_{e_1}e_n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 6.1, symmetric-function side of the k=1 rise case
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every positive integer n, Δ_{e_1} e_n = ∑_{m=0}^{⌊n/2⌋} s_{2^m,1^{n-2m}} ∑_{p=m}^{n-m} [p]_{q,t}, where [p]_{q,t}=∑_{i=0}^{p-1} q^i t^{p-i-1}, and s_{2^m,1^{n-2m}} denotes the Schur function indexed by the partition with m parts equal to 2 and n-2m parts equal to 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Take the coefficient of s_λ by applying Lemma 6.1 with d=2: ⟨Δ_{e_1}e_n,s_λ⟩=⟨Δ_{s_{λ′}}e_2,s_2⟩. Expand e_2 in the modified Macdonald basis as e_2=(H̃_{1,1}-H̃_2)/(t-q). Applying Δ_{s_{λ′}} gives (s_{λ′}[1+t]H̃_{1,1}-s_{λ′}[1+q]H̃_2)/(t-q). Taking the scalar product with s_2 yields (s_{λ′}[1+t]-s_{λ′}[1+q])/(t-q). The principal specialization s_{λ′}[1+u] is zero unless λ′ has at most two rows, so only λ′=(n-m,m), equivalently λ=(2^m,1^{n-2m}), can occur. For this two-row shape, semistandard tableaux with entries 1 and 2 show s_{n-m,m}[1+u]=∑_{p=m}^{n-m} u^p. Therefore the coefficient is ∑_{p=m}^{n-m}(t^p-q^p)/(t-q)=∑_{p=m}^{n-m}[p]_{q,t}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.2, Schur coefficients of two-column LLT polynomials
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S∈Stack_{n,1} be a leaning stack with one diagonal adjacency, and let D be an unlabeled path in D(S). Define LLT_{S,D}(x;q)=∑_{P∈LD(S), D(P)=D} q^{hdinv(P)} x^P. The coefficient of s_λ in LLT_{S,D}(x;q) is ∑_P q^{hdinv(P)}, where the sum is over labelings P of D with x^P=∏_i x_i^{λ_i} whose reading word is Yamanouchi. The reading word is obtained by reading labels from largest height h_i to smallest, and within a fixed height from right to left.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this result from Carré-Leclerc and van Leeuwen. It identifies the fixed-stack, fixed-path generating functions with special LLT polynomials and uses the known Schur expansion of these two-column LLT polynomials. In the subsequent proof, the Yamanouchi condition is translated into a simple condition on first and second occurrences of labels, enabling an explicit enumeration.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 6.2, combinatorial side of the k=1 rise case
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every positive integer n, Rise_{n,0}(x;q,t)+Rise_{n,1}(x;q,t) = ∑_{m=0}^{⌊n/2⌋} s_{2^m,1^{n-2m}} ∑_{p=m}^{n-m} [p]_{q,t}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The term Rise_{n,0} is s_{1^n}, giving the m=p=0 contribution. For Rise_{n,1}, use the leaning-stack interpretation: Rise_{n,1}=∑_{S∈Stack_{n,1}}∑_{D∈D(S)} t^{area(D)} LLT_{S,D}(x;q). Lemma 6.2 reduces the Schur coefficient to counting Yamanouchi labelings. For a coefficient of s_{2^m,1^{n-2m}}, each label occurs at most twice, so encode a labeling by an XY diagram: scanning from bottom to top and left to right, write X for a first occurrence and Y for a second occurrence. The Yamanouchi condition is equivalent to every prefix having at least as many X's as Y's, and column-increase forces all Y's to occur in the right column with no Y above an X. The paper classifies all possible XY diagrams into Type I and Type II. Type I diagrams have a rows of left-only X's, then b rows XY, then c rows XX, then d rows of single X's all on one side. Type II diagrams have a rows of left-only X's, b≥1 rows XY, then c′ right-only Y rows with 1≤c′≤a, then d′ right-only X rows. The area is a, and hdinv counts pairs consisting of a northwest-left square over a right square or an XX row. Fixing area j, m Y's, and n-m X's, Type I contributes q^i for i=m-1,…,n-m-j-1, with the evident m=0 variant, while Type II contributes q^i for i=max(0,m-j-1),…,m-2. Together the coefficient of t^j s_{2^m,1^{n-2m}} is ∑_{i=max(0,m-j-1)}^{n-m-j-1} q^i, which is exactly the coefficient of t^j in ∑_{p=m}^{n-m}[p]_{q,t}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 6.1, proof of the rise version for k=1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every positive integer n, Δ_{e_1} e_n = Rise_{n,0}(x;q,t)+Rise_{n,1}(x;q,t) = ∑_{m=0}^{⌊n/2⌋} s_{2^m,1^{n-2m}} ∑_{p=m}^{n-m} [p]_{q,t}. This verifies the rise version of the Delta Conjecture for k=1, using the equivalent Δ_{e_k} formulation.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Proposition 6.1 computes the Schur expansion of the symmetric-function side Δ_{e_1}e_n using the Delta reciprocity identity and a direct degree-two Macdonald calculation. Proposition 6.2 computes the same Schur expansion for the combinatorial rise side by decomposing into two-column LLT polynomials and counting Yamanouchi labelings via XY diagrams. Since both sides have identical Schur expansions, they are equal. The equivalence between Δ_{e_1} and Δ′_{e_1}+Δ′_{e_0} matches the sum Rise_{n,1}+Rise_{n,0}, so this proves the k=1 rise case of the Delta Conjecture.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 7.1, hook scalar-product identity
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let m and k be positive integers with the hook shape s_{k+1,1^{m-k-1}} defined, let f∈Λ^{(m)} be a homogeneous symmetric function of degree m, and let Γ be any operator diagonal in the modified Macdonald basis, Γ H̃_μ = g_μ H̃_μ with g_μ∈Q(q,t). Then ⟨Γ ∇ f, s_{k+1,1^{m-k-1}}⟩ = ⟨Γ Δ′_{e_{m-k-1}} f, e_m⟩.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the modified Macdonald scalar-product identity, cited from Macdonald, that ⟨H̃_μ, s_{k+1,1^{m-k-1}}⟩ = e_{m-k-1}[B_μ(q,t)-1]. Also use that ⟨H̃_μ,s_m⟩=1 and, equivalently through the ∇ eigenvalue T_μ, ⟨H̃_μ,e_m⟩=T_μ. Expanding f in the H̃_μ basis, the left side contributes for each μ the product g_μ · T_μ · e_{m-k-1}[B_μ-1] times the coefficient of H̃_μ in f. On the right side, Δ′_{e_{m-k-1}} supplies the same e_{m-k-1}[B_μ-1] factor, Γ supplies g_μ, and pairing with e_m supplies T_μ. Term-by-term equality follows.

proof_techniques.name: Coefficient extraction by decorating Dyck-path features
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly turns a product of factors such as ∏(1+z t^{-a_i}) or ∏(1+z q^{-d_i-1}) into a model of decorated Dyck paths. Extracting a coefficient of z is reinterpreted as choosing a fixed number of rises, falls, or contractible valleys, and the negative powers inside the product are absorbed into modified statistics area⁻ or dinv⁻. This makes the expression a manifestly nonnegative weighted sum over decorated objects once the nonnegativity of dinv⁻ is checked combinatorially.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Hook Schur coefficients often arise from adding one fermionic or exterior choice to a multigraded Hilbert series; this technique suggests encoding hook parameters as decorations on a base Dyck-path or parking-function model and then reading the coefficient as an ordinary positive enumeration.

proof_techniques.name: Leaning-stack common model for rise and valley formulas
proof_techniques.description: The paper constructs leaning stacks to put the rise/fall-decorated and valley-decorated formulas on the same underlying set LDStack_{n,k}. The two bijections φ and ψ alter the boundary stack rather than the labels: decorated falls remove east steps and convert the usual dinv into hdinv, while decorated valleys remove preceding east steps and convert dinv⁻ into wdinv. This gives a unified comparison framework and immediately proves identities such as Rise_{n,k}(x;1,t)=Val_{n,k}(x;1,t).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If hook coefficients can be represented by two apparently different statistics, a stack or boundary-deformation model may identify them by moving the decoration into the shape of the ambient path.

proof_techniques.name: Dense-label contraction of stack paths
proof_techniques.description: The dense labeled path model contracts certain north steps of a leaning-stack path and combines labels into set labels on north and east squares. The statistic wdinv is redefined in terms of minimal entries in squares and a correction for entries in east squares, exactly preserving the stack statistic. This is particularly effective at t=0, where only area-zero dense paths remain and the model collapses to ordered set partitions.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Set-labeled or partially contracted models are useful when Schur or hook coefficients require grouping labels into blocks; this is a natural bridge from diagonal-coinvariant-type objects to ordered set partitions.

proof_techniques.name: Specialization to ordered multiset partitions
proof_techniques.description: At q=0 or t=0, the Dyck-path models simplify drastically: either only area-zero paths or only dinv-zero paths contribute. The paper turns these surviving objects into ordered multiset partitions and identifies the remaining weights with inv, dinv, maj, or minimaj. This gives explicit finite combinatorial interpretations of several Delta specializations and connects algebraic Macdonald-operator expressions to classical Euler-Mahonian statistics.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If hook coefficients of the multigraded coinvariant Hilbert series can be related to a Delta specialization, this gives a plausible target interpretation: ordered multiset partitions with one of the four statistics inv, dinv, maj, or minimaj.

proof_techniques.name: Plethystic t=1/q reduction using hook Macdonald specializations
proof_techniques.description: At t=1/q, the expansion of e_n in the modified Macdonald basis collapses, through Cauchy's formula, to a sum over hook partitions only because s_μ[1-q] vanishes unless μ is a hook. The Delta eigenvalue on a conjugate hook becomes a simple principal specialization f[[n]_q] times a power of q. A second Cauchy identity then produces the compact formula Δ_f e_n|_{t=1/q}=f[[n]_q]e_n[X[k+1]_q]/(q^{k(n-1)}[k+1]_q).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem asks specifically about hook-shaped Schur coefficients in grading variables; the mechanism here shows how hook restrictions can arise from principal specializations and may be useful for deriving or checking hook formulas after a q,t specialization.

proof_techniques.name: Delta reciprocity to reduce Schur coefficients to small degree
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 6.1 converts ⟨Δ_{e_{d-1}}e_n,f⟩ into ⟨Δ_{ω f}e_d,s_d⟩. For d=2 this turns all Schur coefficients of Δ_{e_1}e_n into a calculation involving e_2, whose modified Macdonald expansion is explicit.
