The resulting coefficient is a difference quotient of principal specializations s_{λ′}[1+t] and s_{λ′}[1+q], which is then evaluated by elementary semistandard-tableau counting.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For hook coefficient extraction, a reciprocity identity can trade a large-n coefficient problem for a low-degree Macdonald calculation, especially when the hook parameter corresponds to d=2 or another small exterior degree.

proof_techniques.name: LLT decomposition and Yamanouchi-word Schur extraction
proof_techniques.description: For a fixed leaning stack and underlying path, the generating function over all labelings is an LLT polynomial. Known Schur expansion rules for two-column LLT polynomials identify the coefficient of s_λ with labelings whose reading word is Yamanouchi. The paper then classifies such labelings using XY diagrams recording first and second occurrences of labels, leading to an explicit Schur expansion in the k=1 case.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a candidate Hilbert-series model is naturally an LLT sum, hook or near-hook Schur coefficients can be extracted by imposing Yamanouchi conditions and counting the resulting constrained labelings.

proof_techniques.name: Hook scalar-product transfer via Δ′
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 7.1 shows that pairing Γ∇f with a hook Schur function s_{k+1,1^{m-k-1}} is equivalent to pairing ΓΔ′_{e_{m-k-1}}f with e_m, for any Macdonald-diagonal operator Γ. The proof is termwise in the modified Macdonald basis and uses the identity ⟨H̃_μ,s_{hook}⟩=e_{m-k-1}[B_μ-1]. This transforms a hook Schur coefficient into an e_m coefficient of a Delta-prime expression.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is directly relevant to hook coefficients: it suggests replacing a hook Schur coefficient in a Frobenius or Hilbert series by a Delta-prime operator acting on a simpler symmetric function, where existing Dyck-path or ordered-partition formulas may apply.

proof_techniques.name: Cyclotomic q-Lucas divisibility for positivity
proof_techniques.description: In proving Schur positivity at t=1/q, the paper separates a Schur-positive plethystic factor from a scalar q-binomial quotient. Polynomiality of the quotient is checked by showing every cyclotomic factor of [gcd(k+1,n)]_q divides [n choose k]_q, using the q-Lucas theorem. Nonnegativity is then obtained from unimodality and a cited positivity proposition.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a hook-coefficient formula contains rational q-binomial prefactors, the same q-Lucas divisibility argument can certify that the prefactor is actually a nonnegative polynomial.

other_useful_info: Section 7 contains conjectural extensions that may be relevant to hook-coefficient interpretations. The 4-variable Catalan conjectures define Cat_n(q,t,z,w) and Cat′_n(q,t,z,w) by simultaneously selecting valley-type and rise-type features of Dyck paths, and conjecture that the z^k w^ℓ coefficient equals both ⟨Δ_{h_k}∇e_{n-k}, s_{ℓ+1,1^{n-k-ℓ-1}}⟩ and ⟨Δ_{h_k}Δ′_{e_{n-k-ℓ-1}}e_{n-k}, e_{n-k}⟩, with refinements by touch points and compositions. Zabrocki is cited as having proved the Cat′ side of the compositional 4-variable Catalan conjecture after the first draft. Section 7.2 defines partially labeled Dyck paths LDPart_{n,ℓ}: Dyck paths of order n+ℓ with n positive labels increasing in columns and ℓ unlabeled north steps, all unlabeled steps required to be valleys; area is ordinary area and dinv is computed after giving every unlabeled row label 0. Conjecture 7.4 states Δ_{h_ℓ}Δ′_{e_{n-k-1}}e_n = ∑_{P∈LDPart_{n,ℓ}} q^{dinv(P)}t^{area(P)}x^P ∏_{i:a_i>a_{i-1}}(1+z t^{-a_i})|_{z^k}; Conjecture 7.5 states Δ_{h_ℓ}∇E_{n,r} = ∑_{P∈LDPart_{n,ℓ}, touch(P)=r} q^{dinv(P)}t^{area(P)}x^P. The paper also notes an alternate dinv′ statistic on partially labeled Dyck paths that computations suggest can replace dinv in these conjectures. Figure 3 records proved cases known at the time: p_{1^n} scalar products at q=0 and t=0 for both sides through Wilson and Remmel-Wilson, a q=1 rise-side result, t=1/q scalar products with e_{n-d}h_d and h_{n-d}h_d from Wilson, and the k=1 rise version proved in Section 6. The paper points out that Rise_{n,k}(x;q,t) is symmetric because of its LLT decomposition, while symmetry of Val_{n,k}(x;q,t) was left as an open problem except in special cases such as Val_{n,k}(x;q,0) and Val_{n,k}(x;1,t)=Rise_{n,k}(x;1,t).
why_relevant: Introduces the Delta conjecture, a major generalization of the shuffle theorem whose specializations govern ordered set partitions and superspace coinvariant Frobenius series. The one-boson/one-fermion superspace formulas used for hook coefficients are closely tied to Delta-operator specializations.

title: Ordered Set Partitions, Generalized Coinvariant Algebras, and the Delta Conjecture
overall_summary: The paper defines generalized coinvariant algebras R_{n,k} = Q[x1,...,xn]/<x1^k,...,xn^k,e_n,e_{n-1},...,e_{n-k+1}> for 1 ≤ k ≤ n and proves that their algebraic and representation-theoretic structure is governed by ordered set partitions of [n] with k blocks. Its main results give the Hilbert series, ungraded S_n-module structure, standard monomial bases, Gröbner bases, generalized Artin and Garsia-Stanton bases, and a full graded Frobenius formula. The graded Frobenius character is identified with the t = 0 specialization of the combinatorial side of the Delta Conjecture, after applying q-reversal and the involution ω, and it has explicit Schur and dual Hall-Littlewood expansions. For the original hook-coefficient problem, the most directly useful outputs are the Schur expansion in terms of standard Young tableaux and q-binomials, the ordered multiset partition/Gessel fundamental expansions, and the repeated use of exterior/antisymmetrization operators e_j^⊥, which are naturally connected to hook-shaped Schur phenomena.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 2.1, Equations (2.8)-(2.9): Mahonian statistics on ordered set partitions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let OP_{n,k} be the set of ordered set partitions σ = (B1 | ... | Bk) of [n] into k ordered blocks. For σ in OP_{n,k}, inv(σ) is the number of pairs i < j such that i is the minimum element of some block B_m, j lies in a block B_l, and l < m. Define coinv(σ) = (n-k)(k-1) + binom(k,2) - inv(σ). The paper also uses a major-index statistic maj(σ), defined by viewing σ as an ascent-starred permutation, and its complement comaj(σ) = (n-k)(k-1) + binom(k,2) - maj(σ). If Stir_q(n,k) is defined recursively by Stir_q(n,k) = Stir_q(n-1,k-1) + [k]_q Stir_q(n-1,k), with Stir_q(1,1)=1 and Stir_q(1,k)=0 for k>1, then the cited results of Steingrímsson and Remmel-Wilson give ∑_{σ in OP_{n,k}} q^{inv(σ)} = ∑_{σ in OP_{n,k}} q^{maj(σ)} = [k]!_q Stir_q(n,k). Consequently ∑_{σ in OP_{n,k}} q^{coinv(σ)} = ∑_{σ in OP_{n,k}} q^{comaj(σ)} = rev_q([k]!_q Stir_q(n,k)), where rev_q reverses a polynomial in q.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper recalls these identities rather than reproving the equidistribution from scratch. The complementary formulas follow immediately from the explicit maximum value (n-k)(k-1)+binom(k,2) of inv and maj on OP_{n,k}. Later in the paper, the authors also use a direct insertion viewpoint: an ordered set partition of size n can be built from one of size n-1 either by inserting n into an existing block, called star insertion, or by inserting n as a singleton block, called bar insertion. These insertions change coinv by controlled amounts, giving the recursion P_{n,k}(q) = [k]_q(P_{n-1,k}(q)+q^{n-k}P_{n-1,k-1}(q)) for the coinv generating function, matching the q-Stirling recursion after multiplication by [k]!_q.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Equations (2.19)-(2.23): Delta-specialization and ordered multiset partition expansions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Risen_{n,k}(x;q,t) and Val_{n,k}(x;q,t) denote the two combinatorial sides of the Delta Conjecture in the paper's indexing convention. Prior work cited in the paper gives Risen_{n,k}(x;q,0) = Risen_{n,k}(x;0,q) = Val_{n,k}(x;q,0) = Val_{n,k}(x;0,q); call this common symmetric function C_{n,k}(x;q). Let D_{n,k}(x;q) = rev_q(ω C_{n,k}(x;q)), where ω is the usual involution on symmetric functions sending s_λ to s_{λ'} and rev_q reverses the q-polynomial. For a weak composition γ of n, let OP_{γ,k} be the set of ordered multiset partitions of content γ with k blocks. Then C_{n,k}(x;q) can be written in any of the four forms ∑_{|γ|=n} ∑_{μ in OP_{γ,k}} q^{inv(μ)} x^γ, ∑ q^{maj(μ)} x^γ, ∑ q^{dinv(μ)} x^γ, or ∑ q^{minimaj(μ)} x^γ, using the corresponding ordered multiset partition statistics. Equivalently, if F_{n,D}(x) is Gessel's fundamental quasisymmetric function, rword(σ) is the reading word of an ordered set partition σ, revword(σ) is its reverse reading word, and iDes(π) is the descent set of π^{-1}, then D_{n,k}(x;q) = ∑_{σ in OP_{n,k}} q^{coinv(σ)} F_{n,iDes(revword(σ))}(x).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The four ordered multiset partition formulas are quoted from Haglund-Remmel-Wilson, Wilson, and Rhoades. For the fundamental quasisymmetric expansion, group ordered multiset partitions by the standardization of their reading word. The reading word is chosen so that inversion pairs are preserved under standardization, hence Val_{n,k}(x;q,0) = ∑_{σ in OP_{n,k}} q^{inv(σ)} F_{n,iDes(rword(σ))}(x). The standard identity for the involution ω on a symmetric function expressed in Gessel fundamentals sends the summand indexed by a word to the summand indexed by the reversed word. Finally q-reversal changes inv into coinv because coinv is the complement of inv with respect to the common maximum.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.1: elementary-homogeneous vanishing identity
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let k ≤ n, let α1,...,αk be distinct rational numbers, and let β1,...,βn be rational numbers whose set of values is exactly {α1,...,αk}. Then for every integer r with n-k+1 ≤ r ≤ n, one has ∑_{j=0}^{r} (-1)^j e_{r-j}(β1,...,βn) h_j(α1,...,αk) = 0, where e_d and h_d are elementary and complete homogeneous symmetric polynomials.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The left-hand side is the coefficient of t^r in the rational function ∏_{i=1}^n(1+tβ_i) / ∏_{j=1}^k(1+tα_j). Because every α_j appears among the β_i, every denominator factor cancels a numerator factor. The resulting expression is a polynomial in t of degree n-k. Therefore all coefficients of t^r for r > n-k vanish, which is exactly the stated range.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.4: skip-composition Demazure identity
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix k ≤ n and a subset S = {s1 < ... < s_{n-k+1}} of [n]. Define the skip composition γ(S) = (γ1,...,γn) by γ_i = 0 if i is not in S and γ_{s_j} = s_j - j + 1; equivalently the skip monomial is x(S) = x_{s1}^{s1} x_{s2}^{s2-1} ... x_{s_{n-k+1}}^{s_{n-k+1}-(n-k)}. Let γ(S)^* denote reversal of the composition, and let overline{γ(S)^*} be obtained by subtracting 1 from every positive part of γ(S)^*. Let κ_δ(x1,...,xn) be the Demazure character indexed by the weak composition δ. Then κ_{γ(S)^*}(x1,...,xn) = ∑_λ (-1)^{|λ|} κ_{overline{γ(S)^*}-λ}(x1,...,xn) e_{n-k+1+|λ|}(x1,...,xn), where the sum is over all left-leaning collections λ of cells in the skyline diagram of overline{γ(S)^*}. Reversing variables gives κ_{γ(S)^*}(xn,...,x1) = ∑_λ (-1)^{|λ|} κ_{overline{γ(S)^*}-λ}(xn,...,x1) e_{n-k+1+|λ|}(x1,...,xn).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof encodes the right-hand side using labeled skyline diagrams called partisan skylines. A partisan skyline records cells retained from overline{γ(S)^*}, cells removed in a left-leaning set, cells added through a right-biased Pieri expansion for multiplying a Demazure character by an elementary symmetric function, and cells that are both removed and re-added. The Demazure dual Pieri rule of Haglund-Luoto-Mason-van Willigenburg expands e_d(x)κ_γ(x) as a sum of Demazure characters over right-biased cell additions. Thus the right-hand side is a signed sum over partisan skylines. The authors construct a sign-reversing, weight-preserving involution on all partisan skylines except one completely frozen skyline. The key local move changes the highest non-removed cell in the leftmost minimal unfrozen column from one label to another; two claims verify that the move preserves the partisan skyline conditions and is an involution. All terms cancel except the completely frozen skyline, whose weight is γ(S)^* and sign is +1, giving the identity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.6: detecting equality by all e_j^⊥
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let F(x) and G(x) be symmetric functions with equal constant terms. Then F(x) = G(x) if and only if e_j(x)^⊥ F(x) = e_j(x)^⊥ G(x) for every j ≥ 1, where e_j^⊥ is adjoint to multiplication by e_j under the Hall inner product.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The forward implication is immediate. Conversely, for every partition λ and every j ≥ 1, adjointness gives <F,e_j e_λ> = <e_j^⊥F,e_λ> = <e_j^⊥G,e_λ> = <G,e_j e_λ>. Together with equality of constant terms, this proves that F and G have the same inner products with every product e_μ. Since the elementary symmetric functions form a basis, F=G.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.7: e_j^⊥ recurrence for D_{n,k}
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For positive integers k ≤ n and j ≥ 1, the symmetric functions D_{n,k}(x;q) defined by D_{n,k} = rev_q(ω C_{n,k}) satisfy e_j(x)^⊥ D_{n,k}(x;q) = q^{binom(j,2)} [k choose j]_q ∑_{m=max(1,k-j)}^{min(k,n-j)} q^{(k-m)(n-j-m)} [j choose k-m]_q D_{n-j,m}(x;q). Here [a choose b]_q is the q-binomial coefficient and terms outside the displayed bounds are absent.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof tests both sides against products e_{β2}(x)...e_{βp}(x), with β = (j,β2,...,βp) a composition of n. Via the Gessel fundamental expansion and superization identities, the scalar product with e_β counts ordered set partitions σ whose reverse reading word is a shuffle of specified decreasing blocks. The action of e_j^⊥ corresponds to separating off the first decreasing block, i.e. choosing how to add the j largest letters n-j+1,...,n to an ordered set partition of [n-j]. If r = k-m of those large letters become singleton blocks, the remaining j-r large letters are inserted into existing blocks, subject to the reverse reading order condition. Counting the additional coinversions gives q^{r(n-j-k+r)+binom(j,2)} [k choose r]_q [k-r choose j-r]_q, which is rewritten as q^{binom(j,2)}[k choose j]_q q^{(k-m)(n-j-m)}[j choose k-m]_q. Summing over m gives the formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.9: bijection between ordered set partitions and nonskip monomials
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For k ≤ n, call a monomial m in Q[x1,...,xn] (n,k)-nonskip if x_i^k does not divide m for every i and x(S) does not divide m for every subset S of [n] with |S| = n-k+1, where x(S) is the skip monomial defined by x(S)=x_{s1}^{s1}x_{s2}^{s2-1}... for S={s1<...}. Let M_{n,k} be the set of all (n,k)-nonskip monomials. There is a bijection Ψ: OP_{n,k} → M_{n,k} such that deg Ψ(σ) = coinv(σ) for every σ in OP_{n,k}. Consequently |M_{n,k}| = |OP_{n,k}| and ∑_{m in M_{n,k}} q^{deg m} = ∑_{σ in OP_{n,k}} q^{coinv(σ)}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The map Ψ is recursive. For n=1, send the unique ordered set partition to 1. Given σ in OP_{n,k}, remove n, and if n was not a singleton block then σ was obtained by star insertion into block i+1 of an element σ_bar in OP_{n-1,k}; set Ψ(σ)=Ψ(σ_bar)x_n^i. If n was a singleton block, then σ was obtained by bar insertion in position i+1 from σ_bar in OP_{n-1,k-1}. A technical lemma gives a unique subset S of [n-1] of size n-k such that x(S) divides m(S)Ψ(σ_bar), but no skip monomial of size n-k+1 divides it; set Ψ(σ)=Ψ(σ_bar)m(S)x_n^i. The authors prove by induction that the output is nonskip. The inverse Φ removes the x_n exponent. If the remaining monomial lies in M_{n-1,k}, perform the corresponding star insertion; otherwise a unique skip divisor x(S) of size n-k exists, divide by m(S), and perform bar insertion. The recursive maps undo each other. The degree increments i for star insertion and n-k+i for bar insertion match exactly the coinv increments under the corresponding insertion operations.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.10: Hilbert series of R_{n,k}
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R_{n,k} = Q[x1,...,xn]/I_{n,k}, where I_{n,k} = <x1^k,...,xn^k,e_n(x),e_{n-1}(x),...,e_{n-k+1}(x)>. Then Hilb(R_{n,k};q) = ∑_{σ in OP_{n,k}} q^{coinv(σ)} = rev_q([k]!_q Stir_q(n,k)). In particular dim R_{n,k} = |OP_{n,k}| = k! Stir(n,k).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose distinct rationals α1,...,αk and let Y_{n,k} be the finite point set of all n-tuples whose coordinates use exactly the values α1,...,αk. There is an S_n-equivariant bijection Y_{n,k} ↔ OP_{n,k}. Let I(Y_{n,k}) be the vanishing ideal and T(Y_{n,k}) the ideal generated by top-degree components of nonzero polynomials in I(Y_{n,k}). The Garsia-Procesi point-set method gives dim Q[x]/T(Y_{n,k}) = |Y_{n,k}| and the same ungraded S_n-module as Q[Y_{n,k}]. Lemma 3.1 gives e_r(x) in T(Y_{n,k}) for r ≥ n-k+1, while ∏_{a=1}^k(x_i-α_a) gives x_i^k in T(Y_{n,k}); hence I_{n,k} ⊆ T(Y_{n,k}). On the other hand, Lemma 3.4 puts certain reverse Demazure characters in I_{n,k}, and Lemma 3.5 identifies their lexicographic leading terms as the skip monomials x(S); together with x_i^k, this shows the standard monomials for I_{n,k} are contained in M_{n,k}. The inclusions of standard monomial sets BT ⊆ BI ⊆ M_{n,k} and the cardinality equality |BT|=|OP_{n,k}|=|M_{n,k}| force equality throughout, so I_{n,k}=T(Y_{n,k}). The Hilbert series is then the degree generating function of M_{n,k}, which equals the coinv generating function by Theorem 4.9.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.11: ungraded S_n-module structure of R_{n,k}
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: As ungraded S_n-modules, R_{n,k} is isomorphic to Q[OP_{n,k}], the permutation representation on ordered set partitions of [n] into k blocks. Equivalently, Frob(R_{n,k}) = ∑_{λ partition of n, ℓ(λ)=k} multinomial(k; m1(λ),...,mn(λ)) h_λ(x), where m_i(λ) is the multiplicity of i as a part of λ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof uses the point-set model from Theorem 4.10. The quotient Q[x]/T(Y_{n,k}) is isomorphic, as an ungraded S_n-module, to Q[Y_{n,k}], and Y_{n,k} is S_n-equivariantly identified with OP_{n,k}. The dimension argument in Theorem 4.10 proves I_{n,k}=T(Y_{n,k}), so R_{n,k}=Q[x]/I_{n,k} has the same ungraded module. The Frobenius formula follows by decomposing OP_{n,k} according to the unordered block-size partition λ; for each λ of length k, the corresponding permutation representation has Frobenius h_λ and occurs with the number of orderings of equal-sized blocks, namely k!/(m1(λ)!...mn(λ)!).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.13: generalized Artin basis
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: An (n,k)-staircase is a shuffle of the sequence (0,1,...,k-1) with n-k additional copies of k-1. Let A_{n,k} be the set of monomials x1^{a1}...xn^{an} whose exponent vector (a1,...,an) is componentwise at most some (n,k)-staircase. Then A_{n,k}=M_{n,k}, the set of nonskip monomials, and A_{n,k} descends to a monomial basis of R_{n,k}. For k=n this recovers the classical Artin basis of the ordinary coinvariant algebra.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof of Theorem 4.10 already shows M_{n,k} is the standard monomial basis of R_{n,k}, so it remains to identify it with A_{n,k}. Any monomial bounded by an (n,k)-staircase is directly checked to avoid every forbidden divisor x_i^k and x(S), hence A_{n,k} ⊆ M_{n,k}. For the reverse containment, use the recursive bijection Ψ from Theorem 4.9. If σ is obtained by star-inserting n into an element of OP_{n-1,k}, then Ψ(σ)=Ψ(σ_bar)x_n^i with i≤k-1; appending k-1 to a bounding (n-1,k)-staircase gives a bounding (n,k)-staircase. If σ is obtained by bar insertion, then Ψ(σ)=Ψ(σ_bar)m(S)x_n^i for a set S of size n-k; define an (n,k)-staircase by putting k-1 in the positions S and n, and the remaining values 0,1,...,k-2 elsewhere. The nonskip condition prevents any exponent outside S∪{n} from exceeding the corresponding staircase value.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.14: lexicographic Gröbner basis of I_{n,k}
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let < be lexicographic order on Q[x1,...,xn]. A Gröbner basis for I_{n,k} consists of the variable powers x1^k,...,xn^k together with the reverse Demazure characters κ_{γ(S)^*}(xn,...,x1) for all subsets S of [n-1] of size n-k+1, where γ(S) is the skip composition and γ(S)^* is its reversal. If k < n, this Gröbner basis is reduced. When k=n, the corresponding reduced Gröbner basis for the classical invariant ideal is {h_i(x_i,x_{i+1},...,x_n): 1≤i≤n}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Lemma 3.4 shows that each listed reverse Demazure character belongs to I_{n,k}, because it is expressed as a combination of elementary generators e_r(x) with r≥n-k+1. Lemma 3.5 proves that its lexicographic leading monomial is the skip monomial x(S); the variable powers give leading terms x_i^k. Theorem 4.13 and the dimension computation show that the monomials not divisible by these leading terms are exactly the standard monomial basis of R_{n,k}. Therefore the listed polynomials generate the initial ideal and form a Gröbner basis. Reducedness for k<n follows from the additional part of Lemma 3.5: no leading monomial from one listed polynomial divides any monomial appearing in another listed polynomial, and all leading coefficients are 1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.1 and Theorem 5.3: generalized Garsia-Stanton basis
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a permutation π=π1...πn in S_n, let Des(π) be its descent set, maj(π)=∑_{i in Des(π)} i, asc(π)=n-1-|Des(π)|, and define the classical Garsia-Stanton monomial gs_π = ∏_{i in Des(π)} x_{π1}x_{π2}...x_{πi}. Lemma 5.1 states that ∑_{σ in OP_{n,k}} q^{comaj(σ)} = ∑_{π in S_n} q^{maj(π)} [asc(π) choose n-k]_q. Define G S_{n,k} to be the set of monomials gs_π x_{π1}^{i1}x_{π2}^{i2}...x_{π,n-k}^{i_{n-k}}, where π in S_n satisfies des(π)<k and the exponents satisfy k-des(π) > i1 ≥ i2 ≥ ... ≥ i_{n-k} ≥ 0. Theorem 5.3 states that G S_{n,k} descends to a basis of R_{n,k}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Lemma 5.1, view an ordered set partition as an ascent-starred permutation (π,S), with |S|=n-k. For fixed π, choosing the star set S among ascents is equivalent to choosing a partition inside an (n-k) by (asc(π)-n+k) rectangle, producing the q-binomial [asc(π) choose n-k]_q; the definition of comaj contributes the factor q^{maj(π)}. For Theorem 5.3, the generating function of the proposed monomials matches the right side of Lemma 5.1 and therefore has at most dim R_{n,k} terms. To prove spanning, use the Adin-Brenti-Roichman straightening relation for any monomial m: m = gs_{π(m)} e_{μ(m)}(x) - lower terms, where π(m) orders variables by decreasing exponent, μ(m) is determined from the exponent partition after subtracting the descent vector of π(m), and lower terms are smaller in a dominance/inversion partial order. Inducting on this order modulo I_{n,k}, if des(π(m)) ≥ k then gs_{π(m)} is killed by a variable power x_i^k, and if μ(m)_1 > n-k then e_{μ(m)_1}(x) is one of the elementary generators. In the remaining case, the monomial is either one of the specified generalized Garsia-Stanton monomials or is killed by a variable power. Thus G S_{n,k} spans; the Hilbert-count then gives a basis.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.7 and Equation (6.2): auxiliary quotients R_{n,k,s}
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For positive integers s ≤ k ≤ n, define I_{n,k,s} = <x1^k,...,xn^k,e_n(x),e_{n-1}(x),...,e_{n-s+1}(x)> and R_{n,k,s}=Q[x1,...,xn]/I_{n,k,s}. Let OP_{n,k,s} be the set of ordered set partitions of [n+k-s] into k blocks in which the distinguished large element n+i lies in block s+i for every 1≤i≤k-s; S_n acts only on the small letters 1,...,n. Let M_{n,k,s} be the set of monomials in Q[x1,...,xn] not divisible by any x_i^k and not divisible by any skip monomial x(S) with S⊆[n] and |S|=n-s+1. Then M_{n,k,s} is the standard monomial basis of R_{n,k,s}, dim R_{n,k,s}=|OP_{n,k,s}|, and as ungraded S_n-modules R_{n,k,s} is isomorphic to Q[OP_{n,k,s}].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof repeats the point-set and Gröbner strategy used for R_{n,k}. One introduces a point set Y_{n,k,s} in Q^{n+k-s} using k distinct scalars α1,...,αk, fixes the last k-s coordinates to α_{s+1},...,α_k, and requires all α_i to appear. This point set is S_n-equivariantly identified with OP_{n,k,s}. Passing to the top-degree ideal and then setting the last k-s variables to 0 gives an ideal T_{n,k,s}. Lemma 3.1 places e_r(x) for r≥n-s+1 in T_{n,k,s}, and the one-variable vanishing polynomials place x_i^k in it, so I_{n,k,s}⊆T_{n,k,s}. Lemma 6.4, which is Lemma 3.4 with s replacing k in the elementary-generator cutoff, supplies reverse Demazure characters in I_{n,k,s} with leading skip monomials x(S) for |S|=n-s+1. A comparison with the set M_{n,k,s} gives a Gröbner basis and the standard monomial basis. The cardinality |M_{n,k,s}|=|OP_{n,k,s}| is obtained by relating M_{n,k,s} to M_{n+k-s,k} and using the bijection Ψ from Theorem 4.9.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.8: antisymmetrization factorization
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let 1≤j≤k, with the s=0 convention for R_{r,k,0} when j=k. Let ε_j be the antisymmetrization idempotent for the subgroup permuting the last j variables of Q[x1,...,xn]. Let A_{n,k,j} be the alternating subspace of Q[z1,...,zj]/<z1^k,...,zj^k>, whose Hilbert series is q^{binom(j,2)}[k choose j]_q and whose basis is represented by ε_j(z1^{a1}...zj^{aj}) with 0≤a1<...<aj≤k-1. Then, as graded S_{n-j}-modules, ε_j R_{n,k} is isomorphic to R_{n-j,k,k-j} ⊗ A_{n,k,j}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Split variables into y=(x1,...,x_{n-j}) and z=(x_{n-j+1},...,x_n). Filter both ε_j R_{n,k} and R_{n-j,k,k-j}⊗A_{n,k,j} by total z-degree. Multiplication of representatives defines a map from the tensor product to ε_j R_{n,k}. On the associated graded, the elementary generators e_r(y) with r>n-k kill because e_r(y)g(z) differs from e_r(x)g(z) only by terms of higher z-degree, and e_r(x) is zero in R_{n,k}; the variable-power generators also kill. Thus the map factors through R_{n-j,k,k-j}. The authors then compare explicit bases: on the source, use nonskip monomials in y times alternating monomials with strictly increasing z-exponents; on the target, use ε_j of nonskip monomials in all variables whose z-exponents are strictly increasing. Lemma 6.5 identifies these bases, and multiplication sends one basis bijectively to the other on the associated graded, proving the isomorphism.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.9 and Lemma 6.10: exact sequence and Frobenius expansion for R_{n,k,s}
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For s<k≤n there is a short exact sequence of graded S_n-modules 0 → R_{n,k-1,s} → R_{n,k,s} → R_{n,k,s+1} → 0, where the first map is multiplication by e_{n-s}(x) and has degree n-s, and the second map is the quotient projection. Equivalently, grFrob(R_{n,k,s};q) = grFrob(R_{n,k,s+1};q) + q^{n-s} grFrob(R_{n,k-1,s};q). Iterating this recursion gives, for 1≤s≤k≤n, grFrob(R_{n,k,s};q) = ∑_{m=0}^{k-s} q^{m(n-k+m)} [k-s choose m]_q grFrob(R_{n,k-m};q), with the convention that terms with invalid indices vanish. In the indexing used for antisymmetrization, this is equivalent to grFrob(R_{n-j,k,k-j};q) = ∑_{m=max(1,k-j)}^{min(k,n-j)} q^{(k-m)(n-j-m)} [j choose k-m]_q grFrob(R_{n-j,m};q).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The projection R_{n,k,s} → R_{n,k,s+1} is induced by the ideal containment I_{n,k,s}⊆I_{n,k,s+1}. For the left map, multiply by e_{n-s}(x). The authors check directly that x_i^{k-1}e_{n-s}(x) is zero modulo I_{n,k,s}: repeatedly expand e_r(x1,...,xn) with respect to x_i and use x_i^k=0 and e_n,e_{n-1},...,e_{n-s+1}=0 to reduce to zero. Thus multiplication factors through R_{n,k-1,s}. Its image is the kernel of the projection, and exactness follows from the dimension identity |OP_{n,k,s}|=|OP_{n,k,s+1}|+|OP_{n,k-1,s}|, obtained by deciding whether the last forced large element forms a singleton final block. The closed formula is proved by induction using this exact sequence and the q-Pascal recursion for q-binomial coefficients.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 6.11: graded Frobenius character equals the t=0 Delta specialization
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For all k≤n, the graded Frobenius character of R_{n,k} is grFrob(R_{n,k};q) = D_{n,k}(x;q), where D_{n,k}(x;q)=rev_q(ω C_{n,k}(x;q)) and C_{n,k}(x;q) is the common t=0 specialization of the combinatorial Delta Conjecture expressions Risen_{n,k} and Val_{n,k}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof uses Lemma 3.6: it suffices to show that e_j^⊥ has the same effect on grFrob(R_{n,k};q) and on D_{n,k}(x;q) for all j≥1. For the algebraic side, e_j^⊥ grFrob(R_{n,k};q) is the Frobenius character of ε_j R_{n,k}, where ε_j antisymmetrizes the last j letters. Lemma 6.8 identifies ε_j R_{n,k} with R_{n-j,k,k-j}⊗A_{n,k,j}; the alternating factor contributes q^{binom(j,2)}[k choose j]_q. Lemma 6.10 expands grFrob(R_{n-j,k,k-j};q) as a q-binomial sum of grFrob(R_{n-j,m};q). By induction on n, those smaller Frobenius characters are D_{n-j,m}(x;q). The resulting expression is exactly the recurrence for e_j^⊥D_{n,k} in Lemma 3.7. Equality of constant terms is clear, so Lemma 3.6 proves the theorem.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 6.12: ordered multiset partition interpretations of grFrob(R_{n,k})
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let OP_{γ,k} be the set of ordered multiset partitions of content γ and k blocks, where γ ranges over weak compositions of n. Then grFrob(R_{n,k};q), after applying ω and q-reversal in the inverse direction, is equal to each of the four ordered multiset partition generating functions ∑_{|γ|=n}∑_{μ in OP_{γ,k}} q^{inv(μ)}x^γ, ∑ q^{maj(μ)}x^γ, ∑ q^{dinv(μ)}x^γ, and ∑ q^{minimaj(μ)}x^γ. Equivalently, grFrob(R_{n,k};q)=rev_q(ω of any of these four expressions).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The four ordered multiset partition expressions are known to be equal to C_{n,k}(x;q), the common t=0 specialization of the two combinatorial Delta Conjecture sides. Theorem 6.11 identifies grFrob(R_{n,k};q) with D_{n,k}=rev_q(ω C_{n,k}). Applying this definition to any of the four expressions yields the corollary.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 6.13: Schur expansion by standard Young tableaux
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every k≤n, grFrob(R_{n,k};q) = ∑_{T in SYT(n)} q^{maj(T)} [n-des(T)-1 choose n-k]_q s_{shape(T)}(x), where SYT(n) is the set of standard Young tableaux with n boxes, des(T) is the number of descents of T, maj(T) is the sum of descents of T, and s_{shape(T)} is the Schur function of the shape of T. Equivalently, the coefficient of s_μ(x) is ∑_{T in SYT(μ)} q^{maj(T)} [n-des(T)-1 choose n-k]_q.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof starts from a theorem of A. T. Wilson giving the Schur expansion of the ordered multiset partition function C_{n,k}(x;q). Taking the coefficient of the relevant parameter in Wilson's formula and then applying ω and rev_q gives an intermediate expression involving q^{binom(n,2)-maj(T)}, [des(T) choose n-k]_q, and s_{shape(T)'}(x). Transpose tableaux convert this to the displayed formula using maj(T')=binom(n,2)-maj(T) and des(T')=n-des(T)-1. This corollary is the paper's most explicit Schur-positive formula for grFrob(R_{n,k}).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 6.14: dual Hall-Littlewood expansion
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Q'_λ(x;q) be the dual Hall-Littlewood symmetric function and let m_i(λ) be the multiplicity of i as a part of λ. Then grFrob(R_{n,k};q) = rev_q[ ∑_{λ partition of n, ℓ(λ)=k} q^{∑_i (i-1)(λ_i-1)} [k; m1(λ),...,mn(λ)]_q Q'_λ(x;q) ], where [k; m1,...,mn]_q is the q-multinomial coefficient.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Using Theorem 6.11, it is enough to expand ω C_{n,k}(x;q). At t=0 in the rise version of the Delta expression, only balanced Dyck paths contribute; such paths are indexed by strong compositions α of n. For a fixed balanced Dyck path p(α), the sum over labelings is an LLT/Hall-Littlewood expression: ∑_λ q^{mindinv(p(α))} K_{λ',μ(α)}(q)s_λ(x), where μ(α) is the decreasing rearrangement of α and K is the Kostka-Foulkes coefficient in Q'_{μ(α)}. Grouping all α with the same decreasing rearrangement λ produces the q-multinomial coefficient. The minimum dinv of the reverse composition of λ is ∑_i(i-1)(λ_i-1). Substituting this into the Hall-Littlewood expansion and applying q-reversal gives the stated formula.

proof_techniques.name: Garsia-Procesi point-set degeneration
proof_techniques.description: The paper realizes the desired quotient as a top-degree degeneration of a finite S_n-stable point set. For R_{n,k}, the point set Y_{n,k} consists of n-tuples whose coordinates use exactly k fixed distinct scalars; it is S_n-equivariantly equivalent to ordered set partitions OP_{n,k}. The ideal T(Y) generated by top-degree components of the vanishing ideal has the same dimension and ungraded S_n-module structure as the coordinate ring of the point set. Showing I_{n,k}⊆T(Y_{n,k}) and then proving a matching dimension bound forces equality.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If hook Schur coefficients of a multigraded coinvariant object can be isolated by a degeneration or specialization, a finite point-set model may turn the coefficient problem into a count of labeled combinatorial objects. The technique bypasses regular-sequence arguments, which often fail for generalized coinvariant-type ideals.

proof_techniques.name: Skip monomials and Demazure-character Gröbner bases
proof_techniques.description: The authors introduce skip monomials x(S) and prove that they occur as lexicographic leading terms of carefully chosen reverse Demazure characters lying in the ideal I_{n,k}. The resulting initial ideal is generated by x_i^k together with skip monomials, and its standard monomials are exactly nonskip monomials. A signed Demazure-character identity, proved by a sign-reversing involution on partisan skylines, is the mechanism that places the needed Demazure characters in the ideal.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a multigraded or hook-refined coinvariant algebra, an analogous initial ideal could give a monomial model for coefficients. Skip-type divisibility conditions are especially useful because they naturally encode ordered set partitions and q-binomial factors.

proof_techniques.name: Recursive star/bar insertion preserving a complementary statistic
proof_techniques.description: Ordered set partitions are built by either inserting the largest letter into an existing block, called star insertion, or inserting it as a new singleton block, called bar insertion. The coinv statistic changes by explicitly known increments under these operations. The paper mirrors this recursion on nonskip monomials by either multiplying by a power of the last variable or by multiplying by a squarefree monomial m(S) attached to a uniquely determined skip set S.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Hook Schur coefficients often involve adding a cell to a row or column, or adding an exterior generator. A comparable insertion recursion could produce a direct combinatorial interpretation with weights matching multidegrees or Schur hook parameters.

proof_techniques.name: Frobenius determination by e_j^⊥ and antisymmetrization
proof_techniques.description: A central proof strategy is to show two symmetric functions are equal by proving all their e_j^⊥ images agree. Representation-theoretically, e_j^⊥ corresponds to applying the antisymmetrization idempotent ε_j on j letters and then taking the Frobenius character for the remaining symmetric group. The paper identifies ε_j R_{n,k} with a tensor product R_{n-j,k,k-j}⊗A_{n,k,j}, where A_{n,k,j} is a finite alternating quotient with Hilbert series q^{binom(j,2)}[k choose j]_q.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is highly relevant to hook coefficients, since hook Schur functions are controlled by exterior powers and Pieri operations involving elementary symmetric functions. A solver could try to express hook coefficients c_λ(n) through repeated e_j^⊥ operations or antisymmetric components of a related module.

proof_techniques.name: Auxiliary quotient family and exact sequences
proof_techniques.description: To make the e_j^⊥ recursion close, the paper enlarges the family R_{n,k} to R_{n,k,s}, where only the top s elementary symmetric functions are imposed. Multiplication by e_{n-s} gives a degree n-s map R_{n,k-1,s} → R_{n,k,s}, and quotienting gives R_{n,k,s} → R_{n,k,s+1}; these form a short exact sequence. Iterating the resulting Frobenius recursion expresses each R_{n,k,s} as a q-binomial combination of the original R_{n,m}.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the target hook coefficients are not directly recursive, introducing an extra parameter that records how many elementary or exterior conditions have been imposed may produce exact sequences whose graded Euler characteristics yield the desired coefficients.

proof_techniques.name: Gessel fundamental expansion via standardization of ordered multiset partitions
proof_techniques.description: The paper converts ordered multiset partition generating functions into sums of Gessel fundamental quasisymmetric functions by standardizing reading words. This bridges raw combinatorial statistics and symmetric-function Schur expansions, because Schur functions decompose positively into fundamentals over standard Young tableaux with fixed descent sets. The involution ω is handled combinatorially by reversing reading words.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Hook Schur coefficients can often be extracted from fundamental expansions by restricting to standard tableaux of hook shape or to descent sets with hook-compatible structure. This provides a plausible route from a multigraded Hilbert series to a concrete descent-set enumeration.

proof_techniques.name: Straightening against generalized Garsia-Stanton monomials
proof_techniques.description: The generalized Garsia-Stanton basis is proved using a straightening relation that rewrites any monomial as a Garsia-Stanton monomial times elementary symmetric functions, modulo lower terms in a dominance/inversion order. The generators of I_{n,k} kill the terms with too many descents or too large an elementary factor, leaving precisely the generalized basis monomials. Their degree generating function matches a major-index/q-binomial formula.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the hook coefficient problem can be reduced to a quotient where elementary symmetric functions vanish, a similar straightening algorithm may produce a basis indexed by permutations or tableaux with descent restrictions, which are well suited to hook-shaped Schur enumerations.

proof_techniques.name: Schur expansion through tableau statistics
proof_techniques.description: The final Schur formula expresses grFrob(R_{n,k}) as a sum over all standard Young tableaux T weighted by q^{maj(T)} and a q-binomial depending only on des(T). This packages the representation-theoretic multiplicity of each irreducible Specht module into a purely tableau-theoretic generating function.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For hook partitions λ, this formula specializes immediately to a weighted enumeration of standard Young tableaux of hook shape λ. Even if the original algebra differs from R_{n,k}, this suggests that hook coefficients should be sought as major-index/descent enumerators over hook tableaux or equivalent descent words.

other_useful_info: The paper itself does not state a hook-only corollary, but Corollary 6.13 gives the coefficient of every Schur function s_μ in grFrob(R_{n,k};q), so specializing μ to a hook shape gives an immediate hook-shaped tableau interpretation in the R_{n,k} setting. The ordered multiset partition formulas used in Corollary 6.12 are imported from Haglund-Remmel-Wilson, Wilson, and Rhoades; those cited works may contain additional hook-relevant refinements of inv, maj, dinv, and minimaj. The dual Hall-Littlewood expansion in Theorem 6.14 connects R_{n,k} to Springer/Tanisaki-style modules; the paper's Problem 7.1 asks for a direct algebraic relationship between I_{n,k} and Tanisaki ideals. The authors emphasize that R_{n,k} gives a singly graded module for the t=0 Delta specialization, while finding a full bigraded module for the Delta Conjecture remains open in the paper. Notational caution: the x alphabet in Frobenius and Schur formulas is a symmetric-function alphabet for S_n-characters, whereas q is the grading variable; this differs from the original problem's use of q_1,...,q_m as multigrading variables.
why_relevant: Constructs generalized coinvariant algebras whose combinatorics are controlled by ordered set partitions and identifies their Frobenius series with a t=0 Delta specialization. Ordered set partitions are a natural combinatorial model for the superspace Hilbert and Frobenius series relevant to hook coefficients.

title: A Proof of the Delta Conjecture when q = 0
overall_summary: The paper proves the q=0 specialization of the Delta Conjecture, namely that the symmetric-function expression Delta'_{e_{k-1}} e_n agrees with the rise-version combinatorial side Risen,k after setting the first Macdonald parameter to 0; by known symmetries and previously established equivalences, this also yields the t=0 case. The proof converts the Delta-operator expression into Hall-Littlewood/Kostka-Foulkes data, then proves the resulting coefficient identities using Hall-Littlewood Cauchy kernels, plethystic specializations, and finite q-binomial/basic-hypergeometric sums. The most relevant ingredients for hook-coefficient questions are the specialization s_λ[1-u], which vanishes except for hook shapes, the expansion h_n[X(1-u)] = (1-u) Σ_r (-u)^r s_{(n-r,1^r)}[X], and the construction of explicit q-coefficients c_i^(k)(q) that project Hall-Littlewood P-basis sums onto partitions of fixed length. These tools give a clean mechanism for isolating hook Schur coefficients and for translating between Schur/Kostka expressions and length-restricted Hall-Littlewood sums, which is plausibly useful for hook coefficients in multigraded coinvariant Hilbert series.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 2 background identities: Macdonald Cauchy formula and Koornwinder-Macdonald reciprocity
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let μ be a partition, let H̃_μ[X;q,t] denote the modified Macdonald polynomial, and for a cell c of μ let a(c), l(c), a'(c), l'(c) denote arm, leg, coarm, and coleg. Define M=(1-q)(1-t), B_μ(q,t)=Σ_{c in μ} q^{a'(c)}t^{l'(c)}, Π'_μ(q,t)=∏_{c in μ, c not the bottom-left cell}(1-q^{a'(c)}t^{l'(c)}), and w_μ(q,t)=∏_{c in μ}(q^{a(c)}-t^{l(c)+1})(t^{l(c)}-q^{a(c)+1}). In plethystic notation, with Ω(Z)=∏_i(1-z_i)^{-1} and ε the sign alphabet satisfying p_k[εX]=(-1)^k p_k[X], the paper uses the Cauchy formula Ω[-εXY/M]=Σ_μ H̃_μ[X;q,t]H̃_μ[Y;q,t]/w_μ(q,t). It also uses the Koornwinder-Macdonald reciprocity formula: for partitions λ and μ and a parameter u, H̃_μ[1+u(MB_λ(q,t)-1);q,t]/∏_{c in μ}(1-uq^{a'(c)}t^{l'(c)}) = H̃_λ[1+u(MB_μ(q,t)-1);q,t]/∏_{c in λ}(1-uq^{a'(c)}t^{l'(c)}).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: These formulas are quoted as standard Macdonald-polynomial identities rather than proved in the paper. The Cauchy formula is used by extracting homogeneous degree n in X and Y, giving e_n[XY/M]=Σ_{μ⊢n} H̃_μ[X;q,t]H̃_μ[Y;q,t]/w_μ(q,t). The reciprocity formula is then specialized by cancelling the common factor 1-u and setting u=1; with λ=(1), this evaluates H̃_μ[M;q,t] as M B_μ(q,t)Π'_μ(q,t). The same reciprocity formula, with λ the empty partition, is also used later to evaluate Hall-Littlewood specializations at the alphabet 1-u.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every positive integer n, the elementary symmetric function e_n(X) has the modified-Macdonald expansion e_n(X)=Σ_{μ⊢n} (1-q)(1-t) H̃_μ[X;q,t] Π'_μ(q,t) B_μ(q,t)/w_μ(q,t), with B_μ, Π'_μ, and w_μ as above.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Take the homogeneous component of degree n in both X and Y in the Macdonald Cauchy formula to obtain e_n[XY/M]=Σ_{μ⊢n} H̃_μ[X;q,t]H̃_μ[Y;q,t]/w_μ(q,t). It remains to set Y=M, since e_n[XM/M]=e_n[X]. The required evaluation H̃_μ[M;q,t]=M B_μ(q,t)Π'_μ(q,t) follows from Koornwinder-Macdonald reciprocity: after cancelling the common factor 1-u in the denominator and setting u=1, specialize λ=(1). Substituting this evaluation into the degree-n Cauchy identity gives the displayed formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let μ be a partition, let ℓ(μ) be its number of parts, and let n(μ)=Σ_{i≥1}(i-1)μ_i. Let H_μ[X;q] denote the Hall-Littlewood specialization used in the paper, equivalently H_μ[X;q]=Q_μ[X/(1-q);q] in Section 4 notation. For any monomial u, H_μ[1-u;q]=q^{n(μ)}∏_{j=0}^{ℓ(μ)-1}(1-u/q^j)=q^{n(μ)-binom(ℓ(μ),2)}∏_{j=0}^{ℓ(μ)-1}(q^j-u).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply Koornwinder-Macdonald reciprocity with λ equal to the empty partition, for which H̃_λ=1 and MB_λ-1=-1. This gives H̃_μ[1-u;q,t]=∏_{c in μ}(1-u t^{l'(c)}q^{a'(c)}) in the modified normalization. Using the relation H̃_μ(X;q,t)=t^{n(μ)}H_μ(X;q,t^{-1}), rewrite the expression as t^{n(μ)}∏_{c in μ}(1-u t^{-l'(c)}q^{a'(c)}). Set the Macdonald parameter q to 0: all cells with a'(c)>0 contribute factor 1, while the ℓ(μ) cells in the first column contribute t^{n(μ)}∏_{j=1}^{ℓ(μ)}(1-u t^{-(j-1)}). Renaming t as q yields the stated product formulas.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let C_{n,k}(X;t)=Risen,k(X;0,t), where Risen,k is the rise-version combinatorial side of the Delta Conjecture. Let ω be the usual symmetric-function involution sending s_λ to s_{λ'}. If m_i(μ) is the multiplicity of part i in μ, and if [k; m_1(μ),m_2(μ),...,m_n(μ)]_t denotes the t-multinomial (t;t)_k/∏_{i=1}^n(t;t)_{m_i(μ)}, then ω(C_{n,k}(X;t))=Σ_{μ⊢n, ℓ(μ)=k} t^{n(μ)-binom(k,2)} [k; m_1(μ),m_2(μ),...,m_n(μ)]_t Q_μ(X;t), where in this lemma Q_μ(X;t) is defined by Q_μ(X;t)=Σ_λ s_λ(X)K_{λ,μ}(t), with K_{λ,μ}(t) the Kostka-Foulkes polynomial. Equivalently, for every partition λ⊢n, ⟨C_{n,k}(X;t),s_λ⟩=Σ_{μ⊢n, ℓ(μ)=k} t^{n(μ)-binom(k,2)}K_{λ',μ}(t)[k; m_1(μ),...,m_n(μ)]_t.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper states this as a direct consequence of Theorems 6.11 and 6.14 of Haglund-Rhoades-Shimozono [8]. The equivalence of the two displayed forms follows by expanding Q_μ(X;t) in Schur functions, applying the involution ω, and using the Hall inner product in which Schur functions are orthonormal.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix n and 1≤k≤n, and after setting the first Macdonald parameter to 0 rename the remaining parameter q. The q=0 Delta Conjecture is equivalent to the following identity for every partition λ⊢n: Σ_{μ⊢n} (-1)^{n-ℓ(μ)} q^{-n-n(μ)+Σ_{i=1}^n binom(m_i(μ)+1,2)} [ℓ(μ)-1 choose k-1]_q (q;q)_{ℓ(μ)}/∏_{i=1}^n(q;q)_{m_i(μ)} K_{λ,μ}(q^{-1}) = q^{-k(k-1)} Σ_{μ⊢n, ℓ(μ)=k} q^{n(μ)} (q;q)_{ℓ(μ)}/∏_{i=1}^n(q;q)_{m_i(μ)} K_{λ',μ}(q). Here (q;q)_r=∏_{j=1}^r(1-q^j), [a choose b]_q is the Gaussian binomial coefficient, m_i(μ) is the multiplicity of i as a part of μ, and K_{λ,μ}(q) is the Kostka-Foulkes polynomial.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The right-hand side is obtained from Lemma 3.1: ⟨C_{n,k}(X;q),s_λ⟩ equals the length-k sum with q^{n(μ)-binom(k,2)} and the q-multinomial, and multiplying by q^{-binom(k,2)} gives the displayed factor q^{-k(k-1)}. For the Delta-operator side, start from Lemma 2.1 and apply Delta'_{e_{k-1}}, which acts diagonally on H̃_μ and contributes e_{k-1}[B_μ-1]. At the specialization q=0 one has B_μ(0,t)=1+t+...+t^{ℓ(μ)-1}, Π'_μ(0,t)=(1-t)(1-t^2)...(1-t^{ℓ(μ)-1}), w_μ(0,t)=(-1)^{n-ℓ(μ)}t^{2n(μ)+n-Σ_i binom(m_i(μ)+1,2)}∏_i(t;t)_{m_i(μ)}, and e_{k-1}[B_μ(0,t)-1]=t^{k-1+binom(k-1,2)}[ℓ(μ)-1 choose k-1]_t. Substitution gives an explicit sum for Delta'_{e_{k-1}}e_n at q=0. Taking the Schur coefficient uses the standard relation for modified Hall-Littlewood Kostka coefficients, namely the coefficient of s_λ in H̃_μ[X;0,q] is q^{n(μ)}K_{λ,μ}(q^{-1}). After renaming t as q and multiplying both sides of the desired equality by q^{-binom(k,2)}, the displayed identity is exactly the equality of the Schur coefficient of Delta'_{e_{k-1}}e_n at q=0 and the Schur coefficient of C_{n,k}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 4 Hall-Littlewood Cauchy-kernel identities
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For Hall-Littlewood P_μ[X;q] and Q_μ[X;q] in Macdonald's notation, the paper uses P_μ[X;q]=Q_μ[X;q]/∏_{i=1}^n(q;q)_{m_i(μ)} and the Hall-Littlewood Cauchy identity Σ_{μ⊢n}P_μ[X;q]Q_μ[Y;q]=Σ_{ρ⊢n} z_ρ^{-1}p_ρ[XY(1-q)]=h_n[XY(1-q)]. It also uses Q_μ[X;q]=Σ_{λ≥μ}s_λ[X(1-q)]K_{λ,μ}(q). Defining H_μ[X;q]=Q_μ[X/(1-q);q]=Σ_λ s_λ[X]K_{λ,μ}(q), these identities become the Cauchy kernel Σ_{μ⊢n}P_μ[X;q]H_μ[Y;q]=h_n[XY]. Comparing Schur coefficients in Y gives, for each λ⊢n, s_λ[X]=Σ_{μ≤λ}K_{λ,μ}(q)P_μ[X;q].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The identities P=Q divided by multiplicity Pochhammers, the Hall-Littlewood Cauchy identity, and the Schur expansion of Q_μ are quoted from Macdonald's book. Substituting Y/(1-q) into the Cauchy identity gives the kernel ΣP_μ[X;q]H_μ[Y;q]=h_n[XY]. Expanding H_μ[Y;q] in Schur functions gives Σ_λ s_λ[Y]Σ_μK_{λ,μ}(q)P_μ[X;q]. On the other hand, h_n[XY] is the degree-n Schur Cauchy kernel Σ_{λ⊢n}s_λ[X]s_λ[Y]. Equality of Schur coefficients in Y yields s_λ[X]=Σ_μK_{λ,μ}(q)P_μ[X;q].

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every monomial u, h_n[X(1-u)]=Σ_{μ⊢n}P_μ[X;q]H_μ[1-u;q]=(1-u)Σ_{s=0}^{n-1}(-u)^s s_{(n-s,1^s)}[X]. Equivalently, the plethystic specialization s_λ[1-u] is zero unless λ is a hook λ=(n-r,1^r), in which case s_{(n-r,1^r)}[1-u]=(-u)^r(1-u).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The first equality is the Hall-Littlewood Cauchy kernel ΣP_μ[X;q]H_μ[Y;q]=h_n[XY] with Y=1-u. For the hook expansion, write h_n[X(1-u)]=h_n[X-uX]=Σ_{s=0}^n h_{n-s}[X](-u)^s e_s[X]. For 1≤s≤n-1, the Pieri rule gives h_{n-s}e_s=s_{(n-s,1^s)}+s_{(n-s+1,1^{s-1})}; the boundary terms are h_n=s_n and e_n=s_{(1^n)}. Substitution makes adjacent terms telescope, leaving (1-u)Σ_{s=0}^{n-1}(-u)^s s_{(n-s,1^s)}[X]. The stated specialization of s_λ[1-u] is the corresponding standard hook-evaluation fact invoked in the paper.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Equations (28)-(32) and Lemma 4.3: fixed-length Hall-Littlewood projection
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For 1≤k≤n there are explicit coefficients c_i^(k)(q), 1≤i≤k, such that Σ_{μ⊢n, ℓ(μ)=k} q^{n(μ)}P_μ[X;q]=Σ_{i=1}^k c_i^(k)(q) h_n[X(1-q^i)]/(1-q^i). They are c_i^(k)(q)=(-1)^{k-i}q^{binom(k-i,2)}[k-1 choose i-1]_q/(q;q)_{k-1}=(-1)^{k-i}q^{binom(k-i,2)}/((q;q)_{k-i}(q;q)_{i-1}). Equivalently, these coefficients are the unique solution of the triangular system Σ_{i=h}^k c_i^(k)(q)(q^{i+1-h};q)_{h-1}=χ(h=k) for 1≤h≤k.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Set u=q^i in Lemma 4.2 and use Lemma 2.2 in the form H_μ[1-q^i;q]=q^{n(μ)}∏_{j=0}^{ℓ(μ)-1}(1-q^{i-j}). Dividing by 1-q^i gives h_n[X(1-q^i)]/(1-q^i)=Σ_{μ⊢n}q^{n(μ)}P_μ[X;q]∏_{j=1}^{ℓ(μ)-1}(1-q^{i-j}). A linear combination over i=1,...,k will isolate the terms with ℓ(μ)=k precisely when, for h=ℓ(μ), the coefficients satisfy Σ_{i=h}^k c_i^(k)(q)(q^{i+1-h};q)_{h-1}=χ(h=k); factors with i<h vanish because one factor is 1-q^0. This is a triangular system, hence has a unique solution. Lemma 4.3 proves the displayed formula for c_i^(k)(q): substituting it into the system and changing variables a=k-i reduces the h<k equations to Σ_{a=0}^{k-h}(-1)^a q^{binom(a,2)}/((q;q)_a(q;q)_{k-h-a})=0, which after multiplying by (q;q)_{k-h} is the q-binomial theorem at z=1; the h=k equation is immediate.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Equation (35): q-inverted Cauchy-kernel evaluation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every positive integer i, h_n[X(1-q^i)]/(1-q^i)=Σ_{μ⊢n}q^{-n(μ)}P_μ[X;1/q]∏_{j=1}^{ℓ(μ)-1}(1-q^{i+j}).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the Hall-Littlewood Cauchy kernel with q replaced by 1/q: h_n[X(1-q^i)]=Σ_{μ⊢n}P_μ[X;1/q]H_μ[1-q^i;1/q]. Lemma 2.2 with Hall-Littlewood parameter 1/q and u=q^i gives H_μ[1-q^i;1/q]=q^{-n(μ)}∏_{j=0}^{ℓ(μ)-1}(1-q^{i+j}). Dividing both sides by the j=0 factor 1-q^i gives the formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For positive integers h and k, with the convention [h-1 choose k-1]_q=0 if h<k, the scalar q-identity Σ_{a=0}^{k-1} (-1)^a q^{binom(a,2)}(q;q)_k(q;q)_{k-a+h-1}/((q;q)_{k-a-1}(q;q)_a(q;q)_{k-a}(q;q)_h) = q^{k(k-1)}[h-1 choose k-1]_q holds.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Rewrite the left-hand side by factoring out (q;q)_{k+h-1}/((q;q)_h(q;q)_{k-1}). The remaining finite sum becomes Σ_{a=0}^{k-1} q^{a(k-h)}(q^{1-k};q)_a(q^{-k};q)_a/((q;q)_a(q^{1-k-h};q)_a). Apply the terminating basic hypergeometric summation 2φ1, in the form (b;q)_m/(c;q)_m=Σ_{n=0}^m (q^{-m},c/b;q)_n/(q,c;q)_n (bq^m)^n, with m=k-1, b=q^{1-h}, and c=q^{1-k-h}. The sum evaluates to (q^{1-h};q)_{k-1}/(q^{1-k-h};q)_{k-1}. Multiplying by the prefactor and simplifying yields q^{k(k-1)}(q;q)_{h-1}/((q;q)_{h-k}(q;q)_{k-1}), which is exactly q^{k(k-1)}[h-1 choose k-1]_q, with zero arising when h<k.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The Delta Conjecture is true when q=0: for all positive integers n and 1≤k≤n, the symmetric-function side Delta'_{e_{k-1}}e_n(X) specialized at the first Macdonald parameter q=0 equals the rise-version combinatorial side Risen,k(X;0,t). In the paper's discussion, known symmetries and q=0/t=0 equivalences for the combinatorial sides then also give the t=0 case.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Proposition 3.2, it suffices to prove the Kostka-Foulkes identity labelled (14) in the paper for every λ⊢n. The proof first removes the multiplicity denominators by multiplying the proposed coefficient identity by s_{λ'}[X(1-q)] and summing over λ. Using the Section 4 Hall-Littlewood identities, the transformed left-hand side becomes Σ_{μ⊢n}q^{-n(μ)}P_μ[X;1/q][ℓ(μ)-1 choose k-1]_q(q;q)_{ℓ(μ)}, while the transformed right-hand side becomes q^{-k(k-1)}(q;q)_kΣ_{μ⊢n,ℓ(μ)=k}q^{n(μ)}P_μ[X;q]. The fixed-length projection identity expresses the length-k P-basis sum as a linear combination of h_n[X(1-q^i)]/(1-q^i) with coefficients c_i^(k)(q). The q-inverted Cauchy-kernel evaluation rewrites the same h_n-specializations in the P_μ[X;1/q] basis. Since the P_μ[X;1/q] form a basis, the remaining equality is a scalar identity in h=ℓ(μ), namely equation (37) of the paper; after a change of variables this is exactly Proposition 4.4. Thus the transformed identities agree, so the coefficient identity of Proposition 3.2 holds for all λ and k, proving the q=0 Delta Conjecture.

proof_techniques.name: Specialize Delta operators through the modified Macdonald eigenbasis
proof_techniques.description: The paper starts from an explicit modified-Macdonald expansion of e_n and uses that Delta'_{e_{k-1}} acts diagonally on H̃_μ by multiplying by e_{k-1}[B_μ-1]. At q=0 the quantities B_μ, Π'_μ, w_μ, and e_{k-1}[B_μ-1] simplify drastically: they depend only on ℓ(μ), n(μ), and the multiplicities m_i(μ), producing a finite sum involving Gaussian binomials and Kostka-Foulkes polynomials. This turns an operator identity into a concrete family of Schur coefficient identities.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If hook coefficients in the multigraded coinvariant Hilbert series can be related to Delta-operator or Macdonald-eigenbasis expressions, this specialization strategy converts them into explicit partition sums with Kostka-Foulkes weights and length/multiplicity statistics.

proof_techniques.name: Cauchy-kernel change of basis to eliminate multiplicity denominators
proof_techniques.description: The proof confronts sums containing factors ∏_i(q;q)_{m_i(μ)}^{-1}, which are difficult to interpret termwise. It multiplies the desired Schur coefficient identity by s_{λ'}[X(1-q)] and sums over λ; then the Schur expansion of Hall-Littlewood Q-functions and the relation P_μ=Q_μ/∏_i(q;q)_{m_i(μ)} convert the Kostka-weighted sums into clean Hall-Littlewood P-basis expansions. This is the central algebraic simplification that makes the proof feasible.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For universal Schur coefficients c_λ(n), especially if they arise from Kostka or Hall-Littlewood expansions, summing against transformed Schur functions s_{λ'}[X(1-q)] may replace coefficient extraction by a P-basis identity where fixed-length or hook-relevant structures are more visible.

proof_techniques.name: Hook isolation by the plethystic alphabet 1-u
proof_techniques.description: A key observation is that s_λ[1-u] vanishes unless λ is a hook, and for λ=(n-r,1^r) it equals (-u)^r(1-u). Equivalently, the Cauchy-kernel specialization h_n[X(1-u)] expands as (1-u)Σ_{r=0}^{n-1}(-u)^r s_{(n-r,1^r)}[X]. The proof obtains this expansion by writing h_n[X-uX]=Σ_s h_{n-s}[X](-u)^s e_s[X] and using Pieri to telescope.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is directly suited to hook-shape coefficient extraction: evaluating an appropriate symmetric function or Cauchy kernel at 1-u, then taking the coefficient of u^r, isolates the Schur hook s_{(n-r,1^r)}.

proof_techniques.name: Finite q-interpolation projector onto fixed partition length
proof_techniques.description: After specializing the Cauchy kernel at u=q^i, the coefficient of P_μ[X;q] is q^{n(μ)}∏_{j=1}^{ℓ(μ)-1}(1-q^{i-j}), which is automatically zero when i<ℓ(μ). The authors exploit this triangular vanishing to find coefficients c_i^(k)(q), independent of n, such that a linear combination over i=1,...,k is exactly the indicator of ℓ(μ)=k. The coefficients are explicit q-binomial products: c_i^(k)(q)=(-1)^{k-i}q^{binom(k-i,2)}/((q;q)_{k-i}(q;q)_{i-1}).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If hook coefficients decompose over partitions or combinatorial objects with a length/number-of-blocks statistic, the same interpolation projector can isolate the fixed-length contribution before applying hook extraction.

proof_techniques.name: Reduce symmetric-function identities to scalar q-series via basis independence
proof_techniques.description: Once both sides of the transformed Delta identity are expressed in the Hall-Littlewood P-basis, equality of symmetric functions is equivalent to equality of the scalar coefficient attached to each partition μ, depending only on h=ℓ(μ). The remaining scalar identity is then proved using the q-binomial theorem and a terminating 2φ1 summation. This separation of symmetric-function structure from scalar q-hypergeometric verification is a recurring pattern in the proof.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A hook-coefficient formula for coinvariant Hilbert series might similarly be attacked by first reducing to a P- or Schur-basis identity and then proving only finite scalar q-identities indexed by a simple statistic such as length.

proof_techniques.name: Use of ω to convert between λ and λ' in Schur/Kostka coefficients
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly uses the involution ω(s_λ)=s_{λ'} to move between Schur coefficients of C_{n,k} and Hall-Littlewood/Kostka expansions involving K_{λ',μ}(q). This is essential in Lemma 3.1 and in the transformation step where identities are multiplied by s_{λ'}[X(1-q)].
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Hook shapes are stable under conjugation up to swapping arm and leg lengths, so ω can be a useful way to translate a coefficient formula for one hook orientation into the other or to match conventions in coinvariant-algebra Hilbert series.

proof_techniques.name: Cauchy-kernel special evaluations as a proof architecture
proof_techniques.description: The introduction emphasizes a novel method: expand both sides of the target identity as linear combinations of special evaluations of a natural Cauchy kernel. In this paper the special evaluations are h_n[X(1-q^i)]/(1-q^i), which simultaneously have hook Schur expansions and Hall-Littlewood P-basis expansions with controllable length dependence.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For hook-shape universal coefficients c_λ(n), one can try to express the relevant Hilbert series or its coefficient functional as a combination of kernel evaluations whose Schur expansions are supported only on hooks.

other_useful_info: Combining Lemma 4.2 with the fixed-length projection identity gives the immediate hook-only expansion Σ_{μ⊢n,ℓ(μ)=k}q^{n(μ)}P_μ[X;q]=Σ_{r=0}^{n-1}(Σ_{i=1}^k c_i^(k)(q)(-q^i)^r)s_{(n-r,1^r)}[X], with c_i^(k)(q) as in Lemma 4.3; the paper does not label this as a separate theorem, but it follows directly from displayed equations (29) and Lemma 4.2. The introduction notes that Haglund-Rhoades-Shimozono [8] prove the q=0 combinatorial side Risen,k(X;0,t) is the graded Frobenius characteristic of a symmetric-group module and supply the Hall-Littlewood formula used in Lemma 3.1; this is a potentially relevant bridge to generalized coinvariant algebras. The paper also points out related hook-Schur work on the Delta Conjecture: Zabrocki [15] proved one hook coefficient conjecture from Haglund-Remmel-Wilson [7], and D'Adderio-Wyngaerd [2] proved the other. Known results cited from [11], [14], [7], and [12] imply equality of the rise and valley combinatorial sides under q=0/t=0 specializations, while the symmetric-function side is q,t-symmetric from its Macdonald expansion.
why_relevant: Proves a key specialization of the Delta conjecture and gives combinatorial formulas for the relevant Delta-operator expressions. These t=0/q=0 Delta specializations appear in formulas for superspace coinvariants and hence in hook-coefficient extraction.

title: A Module for the Delta Conjecture
overall_summary: This short note defines a tri-graded S_n-module M_n called the super-diagonal coinvariant module, formed from two commuting sets of variables x_i,y_i and one Grassmann set θ_i by quotienting by bosonic polarized power sums and fermionic analogues. Its central content is a conjecture, not a proved theorem, that the q,t,z-graded Frobenius characteristic of M_n is Δ'_{e_{n-1}+z e_{n-2}+...+z^{n-1}}(e_n), the symmetric-function expression appearing in the Delta conjecture. The construction is presented as a representation-theoretic model extending diagonal coinvariants: the specialization z=0 recovers Haiman's diagonal-coinvariant Frobenius theorem, while the t=0 case is motivated by known Hall-Littlewood expansions and related modules. For the original hook-coefficient problem, the paper is useful mainly as indirect evidence and methodology: it shows how adding anticommuting variables/exterior degree can refine diagonal coinvariant-type modules, a theme that may be relevant when isolating hook Schur coefficients. The paper does not itself give a combinatorial interpretation of the universal coefficients c_λ(n), nor does it discuss hook shapes directly.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Main Conjecture of the note, stated but not proved
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix an integer n ≥ 1. Let R_n be the super-polynomial algebra over Q generated by commuting variables x_1,...,x_n,y_1,...,y_n and Grassmann variables θ_1,...,θ_n, where the x_i and y_i commute with all variables, θ_i^2 = 0, and θ_i θ_j = -θ_j θ_i for i ≠ j. Let I_n be the ideal generated by p_{r,s} = sum_{i=1}^n x_i^r y_i^s for integers r,s ≥ 0 with 0 < r+s ≤ n, and by tilde-p_{r,s} = sum_{i=1}^n x_i^r y_i^s θ_i for integers r,s ≥ 0 with 0 ≤ r+s < n. Let M_n = R_n/I_n. The symmetric group S_n acts by simultaneously permuting the indices of the x-, y-, and θ-variables, and M_n is tri-graded by x-degree, y-degree, and θ-degree. For a,b,c ≥ 0, let M_n^(a,b,c) be the homogeneous component of degrees a,b,c, and for a partition μ of n let χ_{M_n^(a,b,c)}(μ) be the character value of a permutation of cycle type μ. Define z_μ = product over i ≥ 1 of m_i! i^{m_i}, where m_i is the number of parts of μ equal to i. The q,t,z-graded Frobenius characteristic is F_{qtz}(M_n) = sum_{a,b,c ≥ 0} sum_{μ partition of n} q^a t^b z^c χ_{M_n^(a,b,c)}(μ) p_μ/z_μ. The conjecture states that F_{qtz}(M_n) = Δ'_{e_{n-1}+z e_{n-2}+...+z^{n-1}}(e_n), where Δ'_f is the Macdonald eigenoperator defined by Δ'_f(Htilde_ν[X;q,t]) = f[B_ν(q,t)-1] Htilde_ν[X;q,t] on the modified Macdonald basis, and e_j denotes the elementary symmetric function, with e_0 = 1 implicit in the final term.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: No proof is given in the paper; the equality is explicitly presented as a conjecture. The note gives several sources of evidence and motivation. First, setting z=0 leaves the θ-degree-zero part and the right side becomes the known diagonal-coinvariant Frobenius expression, so the conjecture specializes to Haiman's theorem for diagonal coinvariants. Second, the author says the Hall-Littlewood expansion of the t=0 case of the Delta conjecture from Garsia-Haglund-Remmel-Yoo and Haglund-Rhoades-Shimozono was used to arrive at the conjecture. Third, the note points to related t=0 module structures proved by Haglund-Rhoades-Shimozono and Rhoades-Wilson, whose Frobenius images are equal, up to transformation, to Δ'_{e_k}(e_n) at t=0. Finally, a Macaulay2/Sage computation is reported to verify the conjecture up to n = 6.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Invariant-generation statement for super diagonal invariants, cited as a consequence of Orellana-Zabrocki Theorem 4.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R_n be the same super-polynomial algebra in commuting variables x_i,y_i and Grassmann variables θ_i, with S_n acting by simultaneous permutation of indices. Define p_{r,s} = sum_{i=1}^n x_i^r y_i^s for all integers r,s ≥ 0 satisfying 0 < r+s ≤ n, and tilde-p_{r,s} = sum_{i=1}^n x_i^r y_i^s θ_i for all integers r,s ≥ 0 satisfying 0 ≤ r+s < n. The paper states that these elements, which generate the ideal I_n defining M_n, also algebraically generate the full ring of S_n-invariants of R_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: No proof is included in this note; the statement is attributed to Theorem 4.5 of Orellana-Zabrocki. The role of the statement in the paper is structural: since the displayed polarized power sums and their fermionic analogues generate the invariant ring, quotienting R_n by the ideal they generate is the natural super analogue of forming a diagonal coinvariant algebra by killing positive-degree invariants. The generating ranges are finite and bounded by n: the bosonic generators have positive total x,y-degree at most n, while the fermionic generators have total x,y-degree strictly less than n and include tilde-p_{0,0} = sum_i θ_i.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Haiman's diagonal coinvariant Frobenius theorem, cited background and z=0 specialization
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the classical diagonal coinvariant module in two commuting sets of n variables, with S_n acting by simultaneous permutation of indices, Haiman proved that its q,t-graded Frobenius characteristic is ∇(e_n), where ∇ is the standard Macdonald eigenoperator. In this note, the author observes that the z=0 specialization of the super-diagonal coinvariant conjecture reduces to this theorem.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof of Haiman's theorem is not reproduced in this paper; it is cited as Haiman's 2002 result. The reduction mentioned in the note is formal: setting z=0 extracts the θ-degree-zero part of M_n and removes the fermionic degree from the Frobenius series. On the symmetric-function side, the conjectural expression becomes Δ'_{e_{n-1}}(e_n), which is the diagonal-coinvariant expression identified with ∇(e_n) in the standard Delta-operator notation.

proof_techniques.name: Superization by adding Grassmann variables
proof_techniques.description: The paper's central construction starts with the usual two commuting sets of diagonal variables x_i and y_i and adds a third set θ_i of anticommuting Grassmann variables. The symmetric group still acts diagonally by permuting the common index i. The ideal defining the quotient contains the usual bosonic polarized power sums p_{r,s} = sum_i x_i^r y_i^s and also fermionic polarized sums tilde-p_{r,s} = sum_i x_i^r y_i^s θ_i. The θ-degree is recorded by an additional grading variable z, producing a family of Frobenius characteristics interpolating between diagonal coinvariants and Delta-conjecture symmetric functions.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Hook Schur functions are often tied to exterior-power phenomena, so a fermionic or exterior-variable refinement may be a way to isolate hook-shaped Schur coefficients in a universal Hilbert-series expansion. A plausible transfer is to build a super version of the m-set coinvariant algebra and interpret hook coefficients through θ-degree or exterior-degree components.

proof_techniques.name: Coinvariant quotient via bounded polarized power-sum generators
proof_techniques.description: The ideal I_n is generated by finitely many polarized power sums with total x,y-degree bounded by n, together with fermionic analogues whose total x,y-degree is less than n. The note cites an invariant-generation theorem saying these same elements algebraically generate the S_n-invariant ring in the super setting. This mirrors the standard coinvariant-algebra principle: kill a generating set of positive-degree invariants to form a quotient carrying a graded S_n-representation.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For m sets of commuting variables, the analogous objects are multisymmetric polarized power sums indexed by multi-degrees. The finite bounded-generator viewpoint can help replace an infinite invariant ideal by explicit generators, which may make it easier to construct bases or standard monomials whose multidegrees contribute to specific Schur shapes such as hooks.

proof_techniques.name: Frobenius-characteristic packaging of multigraded S_n-modules
proof_techniques.description: Rather than recording only the Hilbert series, the paper records the full graded S_n-character by summing character values over all tri-degrees and converting them to a symmetric function through the Frobenius characteristic map: sum over partitions μ of n of χ(μ) p_μ/z_μ. The variables q,t,z keep track respectively of x-degree, y-degree, and θ-degree. This lets the conjectural module be compared directly with Macdonald-operator expressions such as Δ'_f(e_n).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem asks for Schur coefficients in a multigraded Hilbert series, which can often be viewed as multiplicities for a commuting GL_m action or via Frobenius/Schur-character transforms. Packaging graded pieces representation-theoretically may reveal that hook coefficients are dimensions or multiplicities of explicit isotypic or exterior components.

proof_techniques.name: Use of specializations as consistency checks and sources of formulas
proof_techniques.description: The conjecture is anchored by special cases: z=0 recovers Haiman's theorem on diagonal coinvariants, and the t=0 case was motivated by Hall-Littlewood expansions known from work on the Delta conjecture. The paper also points out that other module structures are already proved, up to transformations, for Δ'_{e_k}(e_n) at t=0. These specializations serve as evidence for the proposed full tri-graded module.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For hook coefficients c_λ(n), it may be useful to first understand specializations of the multigrading, such as one-variable, two-variable, or t=0-type degenerations, and then lift the interpretation. If hook coefficients behave like exterior refinements, their interpretation may be easier in a specialized or Hall-Littlewood-like regime.

proof_techniques.name: Computer verification of graded Frobenius characteristics
proof_techniques.description: The note reports a Macaulay2 and Sage program for computing F_{qtz}(M_n), used to verify the conjectural equality through n = 6. Although no algorithmic details are developed in the text, the method involves explicit quotient computations in the super-polynomial algebra followed by extraction of graded S_n-character data.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the hook-coefficient problem, small-n computations of the m-multigraded coinvariant Hilbert series and its Schur expansion could be used to guess the desired combinatorial objects or statistics before proving them. In particular, hook shapes are a restricted enough family that computational data may reveal stable patterns.

other_useful_info: The paper explicitly says that a proof of the main conjecture would imply Schur positivity of the symmetric-function expression sum_{k=1}^n z^{k-1} Δ'_{e_{n-k}}(e_n). The construction was inspired by a t=0 project called super-coinvariants and by the Hall-Littlewood expansion of the t=0 Delta conjecture from Garsia-Haglund-Remmel-Yoo and Haglund-Rhoades-Shimozono. The note cites Haglund-Rhoades-Shimozono and Rhoades-Wilson as proving other module structures whose Frobenius images are equal, up to transformation, to Δ'_{e_k}(e_n) at t=0. It also cites Orellana-Zabrocki for the invariant-generation theorem in the super-polynomial setting. A computational notebook for F_{qtz}(M_n) is listed at http://garsia.math.yorku.ca/~zabrocki/delta_conj.ipynb. No statement in this paper directly addresses universal coefficients c_λ(n) for m-set coinvariant Hilbert series or gives a hook-shape combinatorial interpretation.
why_relevant: Defines a super-diagonal coinvariant module conjecturally realizing Delta-conjecture symmetric functions. It is an important precursor to later bosonic-fermionic coinvariant modules and provides representation-theoretic intuition for fermionic refinements.

title: Schedules and the Delta Conjecture
overall_summary: The paper develops schedule formulas for weighted enumerations of parking functions and valley-marked parking functions, with the marked version giving an explicit product formula for the combinatorial side of the Delta Conjecture after taking Hilbert-series specializations. Its core technique is an insertion tree: build parking functions one car at a time so that area changes deterministically and dinv changes through a full q-integer, producing factors indexed by schedule numbers. It also proposes a monomial basis for the super-diagonal coinvariant ring SDR_n inspired by the Carlsson-Oblomkov basis for diagonal coinvariants. For the original problem, the most directly relevant ingredient is the recalled Schröder-path formula for hook Schur coefficients of ∇e_n, together with the paper's method of isolating hook coefficients via tensoring with an exterior, Grassmann algebra and taking the sign-isotypic component. The schedule and ordered-set-partition machinery is useful as a template for converting symmetric-function/operator coefficients into explicit combinatorial counts.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.1, Shuffle Theorem
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For n ≥ 1, let PF_n be the set of parking functions of size n with labels [n]. For a parking function PF, area(PF) is the number of cells between its Dyck path and the main diagonal, dinv(PF) is the number of diagonal inversion pairs, and ides(PF) is the inverse descent set of its diagonal-reading word. Let F_S denote Gessel's fundamental quasisymmetric function indexed by a subset S of {1,...,n−1}. Let ∇ be the Macdonald eigenoperator defined on modified Macdonald polynomials by ∇ H̃_μ(X;q,t) = t^{n(μ)} q^{n(μ')} H̃_μ(X;q,t), where n(μ)=∑_i (i−1) μ_i and μ' is the conjugate partition. Then ∇ e_n = ∑_{PF in PF_n} t^{area(PF)} q^{dinv(PF)} F_{ides(PF)}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this theorem as the Carlsson-Mellit proof of the Shuffle Conjecture and does not reprove it. The definitions of the statistics are included because the later schedule formulas refine the right-hand side. The theorem supplies the bridge from the operator ∇e_n to parking-function enumeration; taking an appropriate Hilbert-series specialization later removes the fundamental quasisymmetric functions and leaves the bivariate area-dinv generating function.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.2, Haiman's diagonal coinvariant theorem
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For n ≥ 1, let DR_n be the diagonal coinvariant ring C[x_1,...,x_n,y_1,...,y_n] / I_n(X,Y), where I_n(X,Y) is generated by all nonconstant S_n-invariant polynomials under the diagonal action permuting x_i and y_i simultaneously. With q tracking x-degree and t tracking y-degree, the Frobenius characteristic of DR_n is Frob(DR_n;q,t) = ∇ e_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites Haiman's theorem and does not reprove it. This result identifies the representation-theoretic object DR_n with the symmetric-function side of the Shuffle Theorem. Combining it with Theorem 2.1 gives parking-function formulas for the Frobenius characteristic and, after taking the scalar product with h_1^n, for the Hilbert series.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.1, Hilbert series of diagonal coinvariants by parking functions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For n ≥ 1, Hilb(DR_n;q,t) = ⟨∇e_n, h_1^n⟩ = ∑_{PF in PF_n} t^{area(PF)} q^{dinv(PF)}, where h_1^n is the n-th power of the first complete homogeneous symmetric function and PF_n is the set of parking functions of size n labeled by [n].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For any S_n-module M, the scalar product ⟨Frob(M), h_1^n⟩ gives the Hilbert series, because the coefficient of the monomial symmetric function m_{1^n} in a Schur function s_λ equals the dimension of the irreducible S_n-module indexed by λ. Applying this to Haiman's identity Frob(DR_n)=∇e_n gives the first equality. Applying the Shuffle Theorem then sums over all parking functions; in this Hilbert specialization each fundamental quasisymmetric function contributes one.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.4, uniqueness of unmarked insertion
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let PF be a nonempty parking function. In the paper's unmarked insertion algorithm, a new car c is inserted into diagonal k of a smaller parking function PF' under the hypotheses that the (k+1)-diagonal of PF' is empty and the k-diagonal contains no car smaller than c; possible insertion sites are determined by cars s<c in diagonal k−1, cars b>c in diagonal k, and additionally the lower-left corner when k=0. Then for every nonempty PF there is exactly one triple (PF', c, k) such that PF is obtained from PF' by this insertion algorithm. Namely, k is the highest nonempty diagonal of PF, c is the smallest car in that diagonal, and PF' is obtained by deleting c and shifting all cars in higher rows down and left once.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The highest nonempty diagonal of a parking function must contain the last inserted car, because the insertion algorithm only inserts into a diagonal above which there are no cars. Within that diagonal, the algorithm requires that no smaller car already lie in the target diagonal, so the inserted car is forced to be the smallest car in the highest diagonal. Deleting that car and reversing the row shift recovers the unique predecessor. The construction also checks that the predecessor satisfies the hypotheses needed for insertion.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.5, q-integer weight change under unmarked insertion
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let PF be a parking function, let c be a car not in PF, and let k be a diagonal index for which the unmarked insertion algorithm applies.
