For integers j,m,ℓ,k, the paper cites the identity h_j^⊥ Θ_mΘ_ℓ H̃_k(x;q,t) = sum_{r=0}^j [k choose r]_q sum_{a=0}^k sum_{b=1}^{j-r+a} Θ_{m-j+r}Θ_{ℓ+k-j-a}∇E_{j-r+a,b} · ( q^binom(k-r-a,2)[b-1 choose a]_q[b+r-a-1 choose k-a-1]_q + q^binom(k-r-a+1,2)[b-1 choose a-1]_q[b+k-r-a choose k-a]_q ).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This result is quoted from D'Adderio and Romero, Theorem 8.2, and is not reproved in the paper. The present paper uses it as the Theta-operator counterpart to Lemma 3.4: after substituting a by k-a and then taking q,t to 0, it becomes the finite recursion stated in Lemma 3.7.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For any integers m, ℓ, and k, (Θ_mΘ_ℓ∇e_k) evaluated at t=0 equals (Θ_mΘ_ℓ H̃_k(x;q,t)) evaluated at t=0, where H̃_k is the modified Macdonald polynomial for the one-row partition k in the paper's notation.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First, the paper uses the standard fact H̃_k(x;q,t)=∇e_k evaluated at t=0: the left side is the graded Frobenius characteristic of the ordinary coinvariant algebra of S_k with one bosonic variable set, while the right side is the bigraded Frobenius characteristic of the two-variable diagonal coinvariant algebra with one variable set specialized away. The remaining point is that applying Theta operators commutes with taking t=0 in the needed sense. The paper proves the more general claim that for any symmetric function G and any elementary-indexed Theta operator Θ_{e_λ}, (Θ_{e_λ}G)|_{t=0} = (Θ_{e_λ}(G|_{t=0}))|_{t=0}. It checks this on the Macdonald basis. If H̃_μ(x;q,0)=sum_ν D_{μ,ν}(q,t)H̃_ν(x;q,t), then D_{μ,ν}(q,0)=δ_{μ,ν}. Applying Θ_{e_λ} and setting t=0 gives the desired equality provided no poles occur at t=0. The absence of poles follows from the definition Θ_{e_λ}=Π e_λ[X/M] Π^{-1}: at t=0 the product e_λ[X/(1-q)]H̃_μ(x;q,0) expands in the modified Hall-Littlewood basis with coefficients regular in q, and the eigenvalue ratio Π_ρ/Π_μ has no pole at t=0. Applying this commutation twice gives the lemma.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For any integers j,m,ℓ,k, with out-of-range indices interpreted as zero, h_j^⊥(Θ_mΘ_ℓ∇e_k) evaluated at q=t=0 equals the sum of three families: sum_{r=0}^j (Θ_{m-r}Θ_{ℓ-r}∇e_{k-j+2r}) evaluated at q=t=0, plus sum_{r=0}^{j-1} ((Θ_{m-r-1}Θ_{ℓ-r}+Θ_{m-r}Θ_{ℓ-r-1})∇e_{k-j+2r+1}) evaluated at q=t=0, plus sum_{r=0}^{j-2} (Θ_{m-r-1}Θ_{ℓ-r-1}∇e_{k-j+2r+2}) evaluated at q=t=0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use Lemma 3.6 to replace ∇e_k by H̃_k at t=0, then apply Theorem 3.5. The paper first changes variables a to k-a in the D'Adderio-Romero formula. To evaluate at q=0, inspect the q-powers and q-binomial factors in the parenthesized coefficient. Nonzero contributions can occur only when a-r is -1, 0, or 1; the q-binomial factors also force r≤k and a≤j. Once a-r is fixed in these three cases, the surviving coefficient is independent of b, and the identity sum_{b=1}^N E_{N,b}=e_N collapses the b-sum to ∇e_N. If a=r-1, only one summand in the parenthesis survives and, after reindexing, gives sum_{r=0}^{j-1} Θ_{m-r}Θ_{ℓ-r-1}∇e_{k-j+2r+1}. If a=r+1, the symmetric contribution gives sum_{r=0}^{j-1} Θ_{m-r-1}Θ_{ℓ-r}∇e_{k-j+2r+1}. If a=r, both parenthesized summands survive except at the two endpoints r=0 and r=j; splitting the endpoint correction yields sum_{r=0}^j Θ_{m-r}Θ_{ℓ-r}∇e_{k-j+2r} plus sum_{r=0}^{j-2} Θ_{m-r-1}Θ_{ℓ-r-1}∇e_{k-j+2r+2}. Combining the three cases gives the displayed recursion.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 1.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let DR_n be the usual two-bosonic diagonal coinvariant ring and let TDR_n be the quotient of C[x_1,...,x_n,y_1,...,y_n] tensor the exterior algebra on θ_1,...,θ_n, ξ_1,...,ξ_n by the ideal generated by positive-degree S_n-invariants. There is a canonical surjection DR_n⊗FDR_n onto TDR_n. Therefore the bidegree support vanishing (FDR_n)_{i,j}=0 for i+j≥n implies the corresponding fermionic bidegree support vanishing for TDR_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The surjection is induced by multiplication of the bosonic and fermionic variables followed by quotienting by the larger invariant ideal defining TDR_n. In fermionic bidegree (i,j), the source is DR_n⊗(FDR_n)_{i,j}; if Theorem 1.2 gives (FDR_n)_{i,j}=0, then the source component and hence its quotient TDR_n component are also zero.

proof_techniques.name: Detect equality by complete skewing operators
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly proves equality of positive-degree symmetric functions F and G by showing h_j^⊥F=h_j^⊥G for all j≥1. Lemma 3.1 justifies this because the complete homogeneous functions h_λ form a basis, and the Hall-inner-product adjunction reduces every coefficient ⟨F,h_λ⟩ to a coefficient of h_{λ_1}^⊥F.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For hook Schur coefficient identities, one can try to prove a recursion under h_j^⊥ rather than compute coefficients directly. This is especially useful when both a Hilbert-series expression and a proposed combinatorial model have natural restrictions or deletion operations.

proof_techniques.name: Translate h_j^⊥ into restriction and S_j-invariants
proof_techniques.description: The identity h_j^⊥Frob(V)=Frob(Hom_{S_j}(1,Res^{S_n}_{S_j×S_{n-j}}V)) converts a symmetric-function operation into a representation-theoretic operation. In the paper, applying this to V=S^λ⊗S^μ gives a clean recursion for h_j^⊥ of a Kronecker product in terms of Littlewood-Richardson coefficients.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the hook coefficients c_λ(n) can be realized as Frobenius or Hilbert multiplicities of S_n-modules, this technique gives a way to derive recursive formulas by restricting from S_n to S_j×S_{n-j}.

proof_techniques.name: Kronecker-product recursion via Littlewood-Richardson branching
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 3.3 expresses h_j^⊥(s_λ*s_μ) as a sum over common S_j-shapes α of c^λ_{α,ν}c^μ_{α,ω}(s_ν*s_ω). The proof uses Littlewood-Richardson branching for each factor and character orthogonality to force the two S_j-shapes to agree.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Hook Schur coefficients often lead to hook Specht modules or exterior powers. This recursion reduces Kronecker products involving larger hooks to smaller Kronecker products, which can support inductive combinatorial interpretations.

proof_techniques.name: Hook telescoping through h_b e_a
proof_techniques.description: The identity h_b e_a=s_(b,1^a)+s_(b+1,1^(a-1)) lets adjacent hook Schur functions be packaged into a single product h_b e_a. Lemma 3.4 uses this packaging to make long sums of hook-Kronecker terms telescope, leaving only two boundary terms.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For coefficients indexed by hook partitions, grouping adjacent hooks into h_b e_a may simplify recurrences or reveal cancellation. This is a natural bridge between Schur-hook coefficients and exterior-variable constructions.

proof_techniques.name: Use fermionic variables to isolate hook-shaped representation data
proof_techniques.description: The fermionic quotient FDR_n is bigraded by two exterior degrees, and Kim-Rhoades' formula identifies each bidegree Frobenius characteristic as a difference of Kronecker products of hook Schur functions. The exterior variables encode hook shapes representation-theoretically, and the quotient by invariants produces the adjacent-hook subtraction.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem asks specifically for hook-shape Schur coefficients in a multigraded Hilbert series. A promising transfer is to introduce anticommuting variables whose exterior degrees pick out hooks, then interpret the desired hook coefficient as the Hilbert series or dimension of an appropriate fermionic or bosonic-fermionic quotient.

proof_techniques.name: Specialize Theta-operator recursions at q=t=0
proof_techniques.description: The paper imports a general D'Adderio-Romero formula for h_j^⊥Θ_mΘ_ℓH̃_k and obtains a much simpler recursion after taking q,t to 0. The simplification comes from a q-adic survival analysis: only terms with a-r in {-1,0,1} remain, and the E_{N,b} sum collapses to e_N.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the universal coefficients c_λ(n) are connected to Theta or Delta operator formulas, specializing or extracting low-parameter limits may turn complicated symmetric-function expressions into tractable hook recurrences.

proof_techniques.name: Prove equality by matching two independent recursive structures
proof_techniques.description: The proof of Theorem 1.2 does not compute either side directly. Instead, it obtains a recursion for the representation-theoretic hook-Kronecker side using Lemma 3.4, obtains a recursion for the Theta side using Lemma 3.7, and then shows the recursions agree with the same base case.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a proposed combinatorial interpretation of hook coefficients, it may be enough to show that the proposed objects satisfy the same h_j^⊥ or deletion recursion as the known Hilbert-series coefficients, avoiding a direct closed-form Schur expansion.

other_useful_info: Conjecture 1.1 of D'Adderio-Iraci-Vanden Wyngaerd, stated in the paper but not proved there, predicts for the full supersymmetric quotient TDR_n with two bosonic and two fermionic variable sets that the θ-degree i and ξ-degree j piece vanishes when i+j≥n and has bigraded Frobenius Θ_iΘ_j∇e_{n-i-j} when i+j<n; Theorem 1.2 proves the purely fermionic q=t=0 specialization. The paper points to Kim-Rhoades [12] for the Lefschetz proof and hook-Kronecker formula for FDR_n, to Rosas [15] for explicit Schur expansions of Kronecker products indexed by hook shapes, and to Jesse Kim-Rhoades [11] for a set-partition/noncrossing-set-partition model related to FDR_n. The introduction also notes that setting subsets of variables to zero in Conjecture 1.1 recovers the one-bosonic one-fermionic superspace coinvariant conjecture and a two-bosonic one-fermionic conjecture of Zabrocki, indicating that fermionic-variable methods are intended as a route from ordinary Schur coefficients to hook-controlled refinements.
why_relevant: Proves a fermionic case of the Theta-operator coinvariant conjectures, giving techniques for handling anticommuting variables in diagonal coinvariant quotients. These methods are relevant to proofs that pass from ordinary Schur coefficients to hook coefficients through fermionic variables.

title: The Hilbert Series of the Superspace Coinvariant Ring
overall_summary: The paper proves the doubly graded Hilbert series of the type A superspace coinvariant ring SR_n = Ω_n/SI_n, where Ω_n = C[x_1,...,x_n] ⊗ ∧{θ_1,...,θ_n} and SI_n is generated by S_n-invariants of positive degree. The main formula is Hilb(SR_n;q,z) = ∑_{k=1}^n z^{n-k} [k]!_q Stir_q(n,k), where z tracks fermionic degree and Stir_q is the q-Stirling number of the second kind. The core method is indirect: eliminate fermionic variables via a Gale-order triangular straightening, reduce to regular sequences and colon ideals in C[x_1,...,x_n], then prove a matching lower bound using specially constructed differential operators D_J and harmonic theory. For the original hook-coefficient problem, the crucial extract is that the q,z-coefficients of this one-boson/one-fermion superspace Hilbert series have an explicit subset-staircase interpretation; via the standard hook-detection relation between superspace and universal Schur coefficients, this is exactly the kind of formula needed for hook shapes.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.1, Sagan-Swanson staircase identity
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a subset J ⊆ [n], define its J-staircase st(J) = (st(J)_1,...,st(J)_n) by st(J)_1 = 0 if 1 ∈ J and st(J)_1 = 1 if 1 ∉ J, and recursively st(J)_{i+1} = st(J)_i if i+1 ∈ J while st(J)_{i+1} = st(J)_i + 1 if i+1 ∉ J. Equivalently st(J)_i = i - |J ∩ [i]|. With [r]_q = 1+q+...+q^{r-1}, [k]!_q = [k]_q [k-1]_q ... [1]_q, and q-Stirling numbers Stir_q(n,k) defined by Stir_q(n,k) = [k]_q Stir_q(n-1,k) + Stir_q(n-1,k-1) with Stir_q(0,0)=1 and Stir_q(0,k)=0 for k ≠ 0, one has the polynomial identity ∑_{J⊆[n]} z^{|J|} ∏_{i=1}^n [st(J)_i]_q = ∑_{k=1}^n z^{n-k} [k]!_q Stir_q(n,k).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this identity from Sagan-Swanson. The identity is compatible with the recurrence for q-Stirling numbers by conditioning on whether n lies in J. For fixed b=|J| and k=n-b, st(J)_n=k. Splitting subsets J⊆[n] according to n∈J gives the same recurrence as A(n,k)=[k]!_q Stir_q(n,k): the final factor [k]_q is contributed by st(J)_n, and the remaining product is the corresponding staircase product for n-1 with either k or k-1. Initial cases match. This identity is later used to convert a sum over subsets J of products ∏[st(J)_i]_q into the closed q-Stirling Hilbert series.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.2, regular sequence criterion
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f_1,...,f_n be homogeneous polynomials of positive degree in C[x_1,...,x_n]. Then f_1,...,f_n is a regular sequence in C[x_1,...,x_n] if and only if the affine variety V(f_1,...,f_n) ⊆ C^n cut out by these polynomials is exactly the origin {0}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is the standard complete-intersection criterion in a polynomial ring over a field: n homogeneous equations in n variables form a regular sequence exactly when their common zero locus has dimension zero. Since the equations are homogeneous, any zero-dimensional cone is supported at the origin. The paper uses this criterion repeatedly to prove that explicitly constructed homogeneous polynomial sequences are regular by proving their only common zero is 0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.3, colon ideals from Poincare duality
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let a,a′ ⊆ C[x_1,...,x_n] be homogeneous ideals and let f ∈ C[x_1,...,x_n] be homogeneous of degree k with f ∉ a. Suppose a′ ⊆ (a:f), where (a:f) = {g : gf ∈ a}. If C[x]/a′ is a Poincare duality algebra of socle degree r and C[x]/a is a Poincare duality algebra of socle degree r+k, then a′ = (a:f).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this result from Abe-Horiguchi-Masuda-Murai-Sato and uses it as a black box. The conceptual mechanism is that in a Poincare duality algebra, annihilators are controlled by perfect multiplication pairings. The inclusion a′⊆(a:f) gives a quotient map C[x]/a′ → C[x]/(a:f). The socle-degree shift by deg f and the nonvanishing of f modulo a force the annihilator of multiplication by f to be exactly a′, leaving no room for a larger colon ideal.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.1, differential stability of the supercoinvariant ideal
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Ω_n = C[x_1,...,x_n] ⊗ ∧{θ_1,...,θ_n}, with S_n acting diagonally on x_i and θ_i. Let SI_n be the ideal generated by S_n-invariant superspace elements with zero constant term, and let I_n ⊆ C[x_1,...,x_n] be the classical coinvariant ideal generated by positive-degree symmetric polynomials. Let d:Ω_n→Ω_n be the Euler differential d(f)=∑_{i=1}^n ∂f/∂x_i · θ_i. Then SI_n contains I_n and is closed under d.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Containment I_n⊆SI_n is immediate because symmetric polynomials in the x-variables are S_n-invariant elements of Ω_n. The operator d commutes with the diagonal S_n-action, satisfies d∘d=0, and obeys the super-Leibniz rule d(fg)=df·g ± f·dg for bihomogeneous f,g. Hence applying d to a generator from the invariant subalgebra gives another invariant-derived element, and applying d to any ideal multiple remains in the same ideal.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 3.2 and equation (3.8), construction of triangular supercoinvariant elements q_{J,i}
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For J⊆[n], write θ_J for the increasing product of θ_j with j∈J, and order subsets of the same size by Gale order: {a_1<...<a_r} ≤_Gale {b_1<...<b_r} if a_i≤b_i for all i. Let h_r(S) be the complete homogeneous symmetric polynomial of degree r in variables {x_s : s∈S}. For each J⊆[n] and 1≤i≤n, define q_{J,i}∈Ω_n as follows, with the convention min(∅)=∞. If i<min(J), set q_{J,i}=h_i({i,i+1,...,n}) θ_J. If i≥min(J), set s=max(J∩[i]), set r=n-|J∪{i+1,...,n}|+1, and set q_{J,i}=d h_r(J∪{i+1,...,n}) · θ_{J\{s}}. Define p_{J,i}∈C[x_1,...,x_n] by p_{J,i}=h_i({i,...,n}) in the first case, and p_{J,i}=∂_s h_r(J∪{i+1,...,n}) in the second case. Then q_{J,i}∈SI_n, q_{J,i} is bihomogeneous of bosonic degree st(J)_i and fermionic degree |J|, and q_{J,i} lies in ⊕_{J≤_Gale K} C[x]θ_K. More precisely, q_{J,i} ≡ p_{J,i} θ_J modulo ⊕_{J<_Gale K} C[x]θ_K.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The membership q_{J,i}∈SI_n uses two facts: h_r(S)∈I_n whenever r>n-|S|, and SI_n is closed under d by Lemma 3.1. The degree statement follows directly from the pointer construction: moving from i to i+1 increases the relevant complete-homogeneous degree exactly when the next position is not in J, reproducing the staircase st(J). For the Gale triangularity, expand d h_r(S)=∑_{k∈S} ∂_k h_r(S) θ_k and then multiply by θ_{J\{s}}. The surviving fermionic monomials replace the removed index s by an index k weakly to the right in the sense forced by the construction, so their indexing sets K satisfy J≤_Gale K; the coefficient of the unchanged set J is exactly p_{J,i}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.3, complete-homogeneous derivative identity
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S⊆[n], let a,b∈S, let c∉S, and let r>1. Then the following identity holds in C[x_1,...,x_n]: ∂_a h_r(S) = ∂_b h_r(S) + (x_c-x_b) ∂_b h_{r-1}(S∪{c}) - (x_c-x_a) ∂_a h_{r-1}(S∪{c}). Here h_d(T) is the complete homogeneous symmetric polynomial of degree d in the variables indexed by T.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof expands the right-hand side and groups terms using h_r(S∪{c}) = h_r(S) + x_c h_{r-1}(S∪{c}). After rewriting, the expression becomes ∂_a h_r(S) plus two correction terms of the form ∂_b(h_r(S∪{c})-x_b h_{r-1}(S∪{c})) and ∂_a(h_r(S∪{c})-x_a h_{r-1}(S∪{c})) with opposite signs. The first correction is zero because h_r(S∪{c})-x_b h_{r-1}(S∪{c}) = h_r((S∪{c})\{b}) is independent of x_b; the second vanishes similarly. This identity is the technical engine that moves partial derivatives from one variable to another inside the ideals generated by the p_{J,i}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.4, strategic partial derivatives lie in the p_{J,i}-ideal
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For J⊆[n], let I_J=(p_{J,1},...,p_{J,n})⊆C[x_1,...,x_n], where p_{J,i} are the polynomials defined from J as in Lemmas 3.2 and equation (3.8). Then for every j∈J, one has ∂_j h_{n-|J|+1}(J) ∈ I_J, where h_{n-|J|+1}(J) is the complete homogeneous symmetric polynomial in variables {x_j : j∈J}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof establishes a stronger claim using only the tail ideal I′_J=(p_{J,j_0},p_{J,j_0+1},...,p_{J,n}) in C[x_{j_0},...,x_n], where j_0=min(J). It inducts on the number of variables in this tail. If J is a terminal interval, the desired derivatives are already among the generators. Otherwise choose c_0 to be the smallest integer greater than j_0 that is not in J, and let S={j_0,j_0+1,...,n}\{c_0}. Repeated applications of Lemma 3.3 transfer membership of derivatives step-by-step leftward across the gap c_0. This shows that a modified generating set not involving x_{c_0} lies in I′_J. After deleting x_{c_0}, the situation identifies with the same problem for the cyclically shifted subset J′=(J\{j_0})∪{c_0} in a smaller variable set, so induction completes the proof.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.5, regular sequence p_{J,1},...,p_{J,n}
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let J⊆[n] and let st(J)=(st(J)_1,...,st(J)_n). If 1∉J, then the polynomials p_{J,1},...,p_{J,n} form a regular sequence in C[x_1,...,x_n], with deg p_{J,i}=st(J)_i for every i. If 1∈J, then p_{J,1}=1, so the ideal they generate is the unit ideal and the quotient is zero. Consequently, in all cases the quotient C[x]/(p_{J,1},...,p_{J,n}) has Hilbert series ∏_{i=1}^n [st(J)_i]_q, interpreting [0]_q=0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The degree assertion is inherited from Lemma 3.2. For 1∉J, Lemma 2.2 reduces regularity to proving that the common zero locus of the p_{J,i} is {0}. Lemma 3.4 implies that any common zero also zeros all polynomials ∂_j h_{n-|J|+1}(J) for j∈J. A result of Swanson-Wallach says these partial derivatives have only the origin as a common zero in the coordinate subspace spanned by variables indexed by J, so all coordinates x_j with j∈J vanish. After setting these variables to zero, the remaining p_{J,i} for i∉J become triangular homogeneous polynomials in the remaining variables, forcing those coordinates to vanish as well. Since the sequence is homogeneous and regular, the Hilbert series of the complete intersection is the product of q-integers corresponding to the degrees.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.6 and Proposition 3.7, abstract straightening and Hilbert-series upper bound
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every J⊆[n], choose a homogeneous set B_n(J)⊆C[x_1,...,x_n] whose image is a vector-space basis of C[x]/(p_{J,1},...,p_{J,n}); by Lemma 3.5 its degree generating function is ∑_{m∈B_n(J)} q^{deg m} = ∏_{i=1}^n [st(J)_i]_q. Then for every f∈C[x] there is an expression f θ_J = ∑_{m∈B_n(J)} c_{f,m} m θ_J + g + Σ, where c_{f,m}∈C, g∈SI_n, and Σ∈⊕_{J<_Gale K} C[x]θ_K. Consequently the images in SR_n=Ω_n/SI_n of the union ⨆_{J⊆[n]} B_n(J)θ_J span SR_n, and Hilb(SR_n;q,z) ≤ ∑_{J⊆[n]} z^{|J|} ∏_{i=1}^n [st(J)_i]_q = ∑_{k=1}^n z^{n-k} [k]!_q Stir_q(n,k).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Because B_n(J) is a basis modulo the ideal generated by p_{J,i}, any f∈C[x] can be written as f=∑ c_{f,m}m + ∑_i A_i p_{J,i}. Multiplying by θ_J and using q_{J,i}≡p_{J,i}θ_J modulo higher Gale terms replaces each A_i p_{J,i}θ_J by A_i q_{J,i} plus a higher-Gale error. Since every q_{J,i} lies in SI_n, this gives the stated straightening relation. A descending induction on Gale order shows that the union of all B_n(J)θ_J spans the quotient SR_n. Taking degree generating functions gives the upper bound, and Lemma 2.1 converts the subset sum into the q-Stirling expression.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.7, membership criterion for f_J in the classical coinvariant ideal
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For J⊆[n], define f_J = ∏_{j∈J} x_j ∏_{i=j+1}^n (x_j-x_i) ∈ C[x_1,...,x_n]. Let I_n be the classical coinvariant ideal. Then f_J∈I_n if and only if 1∈J.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If 1∈J, it suffices to show f_{ {1} }=x_1(x_1-x_2)...(x_1-x_n) lies in I_n. Modulo I_n, all positive-degree symmetric polynomials vanish, so the generating-function identity for elementary symmetric functions gives (−1)^d e_d(x_2,...,x_n) ≡ x_1^d modulo I_n. Expanding f_{ {1} } in terms of e_d(x_2,...,x_n) yields f_{ {1} }≡n x_1^n≡0, since x_1^n∈I_n. Conversely, if 1∉J, it is enough to consider the largest such subset J_0={2,3,...,n}, because f_J divides f_{J_0}. The polynomial f_{J_0}=x_2...x_n∏_{2≤r<s≤n}(x_r-x_s) is, up to sign, the sum of the terms of the Vandermonde δ_n in which x_1 does not appear. Thus f_{J_0}⊙δ_n is a nonzero scalar multiple of f_{J_0}⊙f_{J_0}, hence nonzero under the harmonic pairing. Since ann(δ_n)=I_n, f_{J_0}∉I_n, and therefore no divisor f_J with 1∉J lies in I_n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 4.2, 4.5, and 4.8, triangular differential operators D_J
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let d_r:Ω_n→Ω_n be the higher Euler operator d_r(g)=∑_{i=1}^n ∂_i^r(g) θ_i, and for I={i_1<...<i_s} write d_I=d_{i_1}...d_{i_s}. Let H be the n by n lower-triangular matrix H_{a,b}=h_{a-b}(x_a,x_{a+1},...,x_n), with h_0=1 and h_t=0 for t<0. For K⊆[n], set K^*={n-k+1:k∈K}. For J⊆[n], define D_J = ∑_{|I|=|J|} (−1)^{∑_{i∈I} i} Δ_{[n]\J,([n]\I)^*}(H) ⊙ d_I, where Δ denotes the indicated minor and ⊙ means applying a polynomial as a differential operator. Then for every f∈C[x_1,...,x_n], D_J(f)=∑_{|K|=|J|} (F_{J,K}⊙f) θ_K for certain polynomials F_{J,K}. These polynomials satisfy F_{J,K}=0 unless K≤_Gale J, and F_{J,J}=± f_J, where f_J=∏_{j∈J} x_j∏_{i>j}(x_j-x_i). Thus D_J(δ_n) has θ_J-coefficient ±(f_J⊙δ_n) and only Gale-lower fermionic terms besides it.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Lemma 4.2 computes the θ_K coefficient of d_I(f) as the determinant det(∂_{k_p}^{i_q} f) for K={k_1<...<k_s}. This converts the coefficient of θ_K in D_J(f) into F_{J,K}⊙f, where F_{J,K} is a signed sum of products of minors of H and monomial determinants. Lemma 4.5 packages this signed sum as a single n by n determinant A_{J,K}, whose top block is built from powers x_k^n,...,x_k and whose bottom block consists of the relevant h-polynomial rows from H. Lemma 4.8 evaluates this determinant by factoring ∏_{k∈K}x_k from the top rows and performing column operations from right to left to eliminate complete homogeneous polynomials in the lower block. The result is F_{J,K}=±(∏_{k∈K}x_k) det(∏_{i>j_q}(x_{k_p}-x_i))_{p,q}. If K is not ≤_Gale J this determinant has a southwest zero block crossing the diagonal and vanishes; for K=J it is triangular and its diagonal product is exactly f_J, up to sign.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.10, inclusion p_{J,i}∈(I_n:f_J)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every J⊆[n] and every 1≤i≤n, the polynomial p_{J,i} belongs to the colon ideal (I_n:f_J) = {g∈C[x] : g f_J∈I_n}, where I_n is the classical coinvariant ideal and f_J=∏_{j∈J}x_j∏_{i>j}(x_j-x_i).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the triangular supercoinvariant element q_{J,i}=p_{J,i}θ_J plus Gale-higher terms from Lemma 3.2, and apply it to D_J(δ_n), whose θ_J coefficient is ±f_J⊙δ_n and whose other terms are Gale-lower by Lemma 4.8. Swanson-Wallach had shown that the space generated from δ_n by partial derivatives and higher Euler operators lies inside the superharmonic space SH_n, so D_J(δ_n)∈SH_n. Since q_{J,i}∈SI_n and SI_n annihilates SH_n under the ⊙ pairing, q_{J,i}⊙D_J(δ_n)=0. Gale triangularity leaves the θ_J pairing as the only possible contribution, giving (p_{J,i} f_J)⊙δ_n=0. Because ann_{C[x]}(δ_n)=I_n, this is equivalent to p_{J,i} f_J∈I_n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.11, enumeration of J-Nietzschean permutations
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For 1≤j≤n, a permutation w∈S_n is called j-resentful if either w(j)=n or the value w(j)+1 appears among w(j+1),...,w(n). It is j-Nietzschean if it is not j-resentful. For J⊆[n], let N_J be the set of permutations that are j-Nietzschean for every j∈J. Then |N_J| = ∏_{i=1}^n st(J)_i.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Build a permutation of size n by starting from the unique permutation of size 1 and repeatedly appending a new last letter, standardizing at each step. Suppose a permutation of length k-1 has been built and one wants to append a letter from {1,...,k}. To ensure the final permutation is J-Nietzschean, one forbids appending k if k∈J, and for each previous Nietzschean position j∈J with j<k one forbids appending the value currently equal to v(j)+1, since that would eventually put w(j)+1 to the right of position j. Thus the number of allowed choices at stage k is k-|J∩[k]|=st(J)_k. Multiplying over k gives the formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.12, three realizations of the same ideal
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For J⊆[n], let f_J=∏_{j∈J} x_j∏_{i>j}(x_j-x_i). Let p_{J,i} be the complete-homogeneous derivative polynomials defined by p_{J,i}=h_i({i,...,n}) if i<min(J), and otherwise p_{J,i}=∂_s h_r(J∪{i+1,...,n}) with s=max(J∩[i]) and r=n-|J∪{i+1,...,n}|+1. Let N_J⊆S_n be the set of J-Nietzschean permutations, embedded in C^n as the coordinate permutations of (1,2,...,n). For a finite set Z⊆C^n, let I(Z) be its vanishing ideal and gr I(Z) the ideal generated by top homogeneous parts of nonzero elements of I(Z). Then the following homogeneous ideals in C[x_1,...,x_n] are equal: (1) the colon ideal (I_n:f_J), (2) the ideal (p_{J,1},...,p_{J,n}), and (3) gr I(N_J). If I_J denotes this common ideal, then Hilb(C[x]/I_J;q)=∏_{i=1}^n [st(J)_i]_q.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If 1∈J, then f_J∈I_n by Lemma 4.7, p_{J,1}=1, and N_J is empty, so all three ideals are the unit ideal. Assume 1∉J. Lemma 4.10 gives (p_{J,1},...,p_{J,n})⊆(I_n:f_J). Lemma 3.5 makes the p-ideal a complete intersection, hence a Poincare duality algebra with socle degree ∑ st(J)_i − n. The classical coinvariant ring C[x]/I_n is also Poincare duality with socle degree binomial(n,2). The degree of f_J is ∑_{i=1}^n (i−st(J)_i), and these degrees add correctly to binomial(n,2); moreover f_J∉I_n by Lemma 4.7. Lemma 2.3 therefore forces (p_{J,1},...,p_{J,n})=(I_n:f_J). For the orbit-harmonic realization, introduce the deformed coinvariant ideal ~I_n=(e_d(x_1,...,x_n)−e_d(1,...,n):1≤d≤n), whose variety is the embedded copy of S_n, and the inhomogeneous polynomial ~f_J=∏_{j∈J}(x_j−n)∏_{i>j}(x_j−x_i+1), whose zero set inside S_n is the J-resentful complement. Thus N_J=V(~I_n)−V(~f_J), and the Nullstellensatz gives I(N_J)=(~I_n:~f_J). Passing to associated graded gives gr I(N_J)⊆(gr ~I_n : f_J)=(I_n:f_J). The quotient by gr I(N_J) has dimension |N_J|, while the quotient by (I_n:f_J) has dimension ∏ st(J)_i by the complete-intersection Hilbert series, and these are equal by Proposition 4.11. Hence the inclusion is equality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.1, operator theorem for superharmonics
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let SH_n=SI_n^⊥⊆Ω_n be the superharmonic space under the standard superspace differential pairing. Let δ_n=∏_{1≤i<j≤n}(x_j-x_i) be the Vandermonde determinant. For r≥1 let d_r=∑_{i=1}^n ∂_i^r θ_i be the higher Euler operator, and for I={i_1<...<i_s}⊆[n−1] write d_I=d_{i_1}...d_{i_s}. Then SH_n = ∑_{I⊆[n−1]} C[x_1,...,x_n] ⊙ d_I(δ_n). Equivalently, SH_n is the smallest linear subspace of Ω_n containing δ_n and closed under all x-variable partial derivatives ∂_1,...,∂_n and all higher Euler operators d_r.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let SH′_n be the right-hand side. Swanson-Wallach had proved SH′_n⊆SH_n. To prove equality, use the operators D_J. For each J, D_J(δ_n)∈SH′_n and has θ_J coefficient ±f_J⊙δ_n with only Gale-lower fermionic terms. By Theorem 4.12, choose a homogeneous basis B_n(J) of C[x]/(I_n:f_J), so the set {g⊙(f_J⊙δ_n):g∈B_n(J)} is linearly independent. Suppose a linear combination of elements g θ_J annihilates SH′_n; by fermionic degree homogeneity and choosing a Gale-minimal J_0 with nonzero coefficient, applying the combination to D_{J_0}(δ_n) isolates exactly the θ_{J_0} leading coefficient and contradicts that linear independence. Therefore dim SH′_n is at least ∑_J |B_n(J)|. Proposition 3.7 gives dim SH_n=dim SR_n at most the same sum, and SH′_n⊆SH_n, so equality holds.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.2, linear independence criterion in SR_n
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Suppose that for every J⊆[n] one has a homogeneous set C_n(J)⊆C[x_1,...,x_n] whose image in C[x]/(I_n:f_J) is linearly independent. Then the set C_n=⨆_{J⊆[n]} C_n(J) θ_J has linearly independent image in SR_n=Ω_n/SI_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume a nontrivial linear relation among the images of C_n(J)θ_J in SR_n. Equivalently, the corresponding superspace element annihilates SH_n under the harmonic pairing. Choose J_0 Gale-minimal among subsets appearing with nonzero coefficient. Apply the relation as a differential operator to D_{J_0}(δ_n)∈SH_n. The Gale triangularity of D_{J_0}(δ_n) ensures that all terms except the θ_{J_0} component either vanish by fermionic mismatch or involve subsets excluded by the minimality choice. The remaining equality is ∑_{g∈C_n(J_0)} c_g g⊙(f_{J_0}⊙δ_n)=0. Since ann(f_{J_0}⊙δ_n)=(I_n:f_{J_0}), this contradicts linear independence modulo the colon ideal.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.3, Hilbert series of the superspace coinvariant ring
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For SR_n=Ω_n/SI_n, where q records polynomial x-degree and z records fermionic θ-degree, the bigraded Hilbert series is Hilb(SR_n;q,z) = ∑_{k=1}^n z^{n-k} [k]!_q Stir_q(n,k), where Stir_q(n,k) is defined by Stir_q(n,k)=[k]_q Stir_q(n−1,k)+Stir_q(n−1,k−1), Stir_q(0,0)=1, and Stir_q(0,k)=0 for k≠0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For each J⊆[n], choose a homogeneous basis B_n(J) of C[x]/(I_n:f_J). By Theorem 4.12 its degree generating function is ∏_i [st(J)_i]_q. Lemma 5.2 shows that ⨆_J B_n(J)θ_J is linearly independent in SR_n, so Hilb(SR_n;q,z) is at least ∑_J z^{|J|}∏_i[st(J)_i]_q. Proposition 3.7 gives the reverse inequality from the straightening spanning set. Thus equality holds, and Lemma 2.1 rewrites the subset-staircase sum as the q-Stirling expression.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.4, basis recipe from colon-ideal bases
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For each J⊆[n], let B_n(J)⊆C[x_1,...,x_n] be a set of polynomials and let B_n=⨆_{J⊆[n]} B_n(J)θ_J⊆Ω_n. The following are equivalent: (1) for every J, B_n(J) descends to a basis of C[x]/(I_n:f_J); (2) the set ⨆_{J⊆[n]} { (b_J θ_J ⊙ D_J(δ_n)) ⊙ D_J(δ_n) : b_J∈B_n(J) } is a basis of the superharmonic space SH_n. Either condition implies that B_n descends to a basis of SR_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The implication from (1) to a basis of SR_n is the proof of Theorem 5.3. For equivalence with the harmonic basis, define a linear map Ψ:⊕_J C[x]/(I_n:f_J)→SH_n by Ψ((h_J)_J)=∑_J (h_J θ_J⊙D_J(δ_n))⊙D_J(δ_n). This is well-defined because the θ_J coefficient of D_J(δ_n) is ±f_J⊙δ_n, and (I_n:f_J) annihilates f_J⊙δ_n. The domain and codomain have equal dimension by Theorems 4.12 and 5.3. Surjectivity follows by Gale induction: modulo lower-Gale fermionic terms, (C[x]θ_J⊙D_J(δ_n))⊙D_J(δ_n) recovers C[x]⊙D_J(δ_n), and Theorem 5.1 says these pieces generate SH_n. Therefore Ψ is an isomorphism, proving the equivalence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.6 and Proposition 5.7, terminal J-Artin bases
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For r≥1 and 1≤p≤n+1, let J_{r,p,n} be the ideal in C[x_1,...,x_n] generated by ∂_1 h_r,...,∂_{p−1}h_r,∂_p h_{r+1},...,∂_n h_{r+1}, where h_d means h_d(x_1,...,x_n). Then the monomials M_{r,p,n}={x_1^{b_1}...x_n^{b_n}: b_i
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Lemma 5.6, first show h_r∈J_{r,p,n} using the Euler-type identity x_1∂_1h_r+...+x_{p−1}∂_{p−1}h_r+∂_p h_{r+1}+...+∂_n h_{r+1}=C h_r with C>0. This also implies x_p J_{r,p+1,n}⊆J_{r,p,n}. One obtains an exact sequence C[x]/J_{r,p+1,n} --×x_p→ C[x]/J_{r,p,n} → C[¯ x]/¯ J_{r,p,n−1} → 0, where ¯ x deletes x_p. Regularity and Hilbert-series dimensions make the first map injective, giving a short exact sequence. Induction on n and p, together with the decomposition M_{r,p,n}=x_p M_{r,p+1,n} ⊔ ¯ M_{r,p,n−1}, proves the monomial basis. For Proposition 5.7, Theorem 4.12 gives explicit generators of (I_n:f_J): h_1(x_1,...,x_n),...,h_r(x_r,...,x_n) and the derivatives ∂_{r+1}h_{r+1},...,∂_n h_{r+1} in the terminal variables. The first generators triangularly reduce exponents in variables x_i for i≤r to a_i<i, and Lemma 5.6 handles the remaining derivative quotient, giving exactly the inequalities a_i<st(J)_i. The count agrees with Theorem 4.12, so spanning implies basis.

proof_techniques.name: One-boson/one-fermion superspace as a hook detector
proof_techniques.description: The paper studies Ω_n=C[x_1,...,x_n]⊗∧{θ_1,...,θ_n}, with one commuting and one anticommuting copy of the defining permutation representation. Its Hilbert series has two grading variables: q for bosonic degree and z for fermionic degree. The main theorem gives a completely explicit formula for this q,z series, and Lemma 2.1 refines it into a sum over subsets J with staircase products ∏[st(J)_i]_q.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the original universal Schur coefficient c_{(a,1^b)}(n) is related to the q^a z^b component of this superspace quotient by the usual diagonal supersymmetry/hook specialization, then this paper directly supplies the desired combinatorial interpretation: count subset-staircase exponent vectors, equivalently take the coefficient of q^a in [n-b]!_q Stir_q(n,n-b).

proof_techniques.name: Gale-order triangular straightening
proof_techniques.description: The paper orders fermionic monomials θ_J by Gale order and constructs, for each J and each i, elements q_{J,i}∈SI_n whose leading fermionic term is p_{J,i}θ_J and whose remaining terms are strictly higher in Gale order. Because the p_{J,i} form regular sequences, one can straighten any fθ_J modulo SI_n into a chosen basis modulo the p-ideal plus higher-Gale errors. Induction over Gale order then gives a global spanning set and a Hilbert-series upper bound.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a way to prove hook coefficient formulas without first finding an explicit monomial basis of the full multigraded diagonal coinvariant algebra. A solver can try to isolate hook components by a similar triangular filtration indexed by fermionic subsets.

proof_techniques.name: Regular sequences from complete-homogeneous derivative identities
proof_techniques.description: The regularity of p_{J,1},...,p_{J,n} is not obtained by Gröbner bases. Instead, the paper proves a special identity among derivatives of complete homogeneous symmetric polynomials, uses it to force certain partial derivatives ∂_j h_{n-|J|+1}(J) into the p-ideal, then invokes a no-common-zero result for those derivatives and triangularity after setting J-variables to zero. Lemma 2.2 converts the resulting zero-locus statement into regularity.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For hook shapes, the relevant enumerator is a product of q-integers from these regular sequences. If an attempted proof needs a finite-dimensional quotient with Hilbert series ∏[st(J)_i]_q, this provides the exact construction and regularity proof.

proof_techniques.name: Triangular differential operators D_J for lower bounds
proof_techniques.description: The paper builds differential operators D_J using minors of a lower-triangular matrix H of complete homogeneous polynomials. Applied to the Vandermonde δ_n, D_J(δ_n) has θ_J coefficient ±f_J⊙δ_n and only Gale-lower fermionic terms. This gives a triangular dual family to the q_{J,i} straightening elements and is the key to proving linear independence.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: When upper bounds come from straightening, these D_J operators give matching lower bounds. In a hook-coefficient proof, they can certify that all subset-staircase contributions are genuinely present rather than merely spanning candidates.

proof_techniques.name: Colon ideals (I_n:f_J) as the commutative shadow of fermionic degree J
proof_techniques.description: The leading coefficient f_J=∏_{j∈J}x_j∏_{i>j}(x_j-x_i) controls the θ_J piece of D_J(δ_n). The annihilator of f_J⊙δ_n is the colon ideal (I_n:f_J). Theorem 4.12 proves that this colon ideal equals the regular-sequence ideal generated by p_{J,i}, so its Hilbert series is exactly ∏[st(J)_i]_q.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This identifies the algebraic object whose graded dimension supplies each subset-J contribution to a hook coefficient. It also gives a concrete route to constructing bases or statistics if the advisor wants more than a generating-function interpretation.

proof_techniques.name: Orbit harmonics and J-Nietzschean permutations
proof_techniques.description: The paper realizes (I_n:f_J) as gr I(N_J), where N_J is a subset of permutations defined by avoiding j-resentment conditions for all j∈J. This uses a deformation of the classical coinvariant ideal to the finite orbit of (1,2,...,n), removes the zero locus of an inhomogeneous polynomial ~f_J, and passes to top homogeneous parts. The resulting quotient has dimension |N_J|=∏ st(J)_i.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This gives a permutation-set model behind each staircase product. If the original problem asks for a more explicitly combinatorial interpretation of hook coefficients, one can combine J-Nietzschean permutations with a graded statistic coming from orbit harmonics or use the simpler exponent-vector model from the product formula.

proof_techniques.name: Poincare-duality comparison to prove colon equality
proof_techniques.description: After proving only the inclusion (p_{J,1},...,p_{J,n})⊆(I_n:f_J), the paper upgrades it to equality using a Poincare-duality lemma. The ingredients are: C[x]/I_n has socle degree binomial(n,2), C[x]/(p_{J,i}) is a complete intersection with socle degree ∑st(J)_i−n, f_J has degree ∑(i−st(J)_i), and f_J∉I_n when 1∉J. The socle degrees add exactly.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a compact way to prove that an apparently larger annihilator/colon ideal has no hidden generators. It may be useful whenever hook components are described as annihilators of differentiated Vandermondes.

proof_techniques.name: Sandwich proof of the Hilbert series
proof_techniques.description: The final Hilbert-series proof is a dimension sandwich. The straightening argument gives Hilb(SR_n)≤∑_J z^{|J|}∏[st(J)_i]_q. The D_J/colon-ideal argument gives a linearly independent set of exactly the same degree generating function, hence Hilb(SR_n)≥the same sum. Lemma 2.1 then rewrites the common expression as the q-Stirling formula.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This proof structure is robust: one can establish a hook formula by independently proving a spanning upper bound and a harmonic lower bound, even if no canonical full basis of the multigraded algebra is known.

proof_techniques.name: Basis assembly from smaller commutative quotients
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 5.4 says that any choice of bases B_n(J) for the quotients C[x]/(I_n:f_J) assembles into a basis ⨆_J B_n(J)θ_J of SR_n, and equivalently into a basis of the superharmonic space through the D_J operators. Thus the superspace quotient decomposes, at the level of bases and Hilbert series, into a family of ordinary polynomial quotients indexed by subsets J.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For hook coefficients, this means one can focus on interpreting or choosing bases for the colon quotients indexed by subsets of size b, rather than attacking the full m-variable diagonal coinvariant algebra at once.

other_useful_info: Combining Theorem 5.3 with Lemma 2.1 gives the explicit coefficient form inside the paper's setting: the coefficient of q^a z^b in Hilb(SR_n;q,z) is the number of pairs (J,α) such that J⊆[n], |J|=b, 0≤α_i<st(J)_i=i−|J∩[i]| for every i, and α_1+...+α_n=a; equivalently it is [q^a]([n-b]!_q Stir_q(n,n-b)). Subsets J containing 1 contribute zero because st(J)_1=0. At q=z=1 the Hilbert series gives ∑_{k=1}^n k! Stir(n,k), the ordered Bell number. The paper proves only the bigraded Hilbert series, not the full bigraded S_n-Frobenius characteristic; the Fields conjecture grFrob(SR_n;q,z)=∑_{k=1}^n z^{n-k} Δ′_{e_{k-1}} e_n |_{t=0} remains open in the paper. Conjecture 5.5 proposes a concrete monomial basis for each colon quotient: A_n(J)={x^a:a_i<st(J)_i} should be a basis of C[x]/(I_n:f_J); if true, ⨆_J A_n(J)θ_J is the Sagan-Swanson Artin-like basis of SR_n. Proposition 5.7 proves this monomial-basis conjecture for terminal Gale-maximal subsets J={r+1,...,n}. The paper points to Sagan-Swanson for the q-Stirling staircase identity and to Swanson-Wallach for prior harmonic bounds and the no-common-zero lemma used in the regular-sequence proof.
why_relevant: Computes the doubly graded Hilbert series of the one-boson/one-fermion superspace coinvariant ring and proves an operator theorem for its harmonics. Via diagonal supersymmetry, this is one of the most direct sources for extracting and interpreting hook coefficients c_(a,1^b)(n).

title: The Super Nabla Operator
overall_summary: The paper defines the super nabla operator ∇_* by declaring the modified Macdonald polynomials Htilde_μ to be joint eigenvectors with tensor-valued eigenvalue Htilde_μ itself, i.e. ∇_* Htilde_μ = Htilde_μ ⊗ Htilde_μ. It shows that many standard Macdonald eigenoperators, including nabla, Delta operators, and Π, are obtained from ∇_* by scalar products or plethystic specializations. The main combinatorial work specializes t=1 and converts expansions of iterated super nabla operators into positive area enumerators of multi-labeled Dyck paths and marked-return variants, using forgotten symmetric functions, column-composition tableaux, and sign-reversing involutions. The most relevant output for the original problem is the Schur expansion of ∇_*^k e_n at t=1 in terms of lattice multi-labeled Dyck paths, together with operator identities expressing Ξ e_λ and Ξ s_λ through scalar products against ∇_*^k e_n. These tools are plausibly useful whenever universal Schur coefficients of multigraded Hilbert series can be recast as coefficients or scalar products of Macdonald-eigenoperator expressions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 1.1 and Proposition 1.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Λ be the ring of symmetric functions over Q(q,t), and let Htilde_μ(x;q,t) denote the modified Macdonald polynomial indexed by a partition μ. Define the super nabla operator ∇_*: Λ → Λ ⊗ Λ by ∇_* Htilde_μ = Htilde_μ ⊗ Htilde_μ; equivalently, for a second variable set y, ∇_y Htilde_μ(x) = Htilde_μ(y) Htilde_μ(x). For a symmetric function F, define the scalar-product specialization ⟨F,∇_*⟩ by ⟨F,∇_*⟩ Htilde_μ = ⟨F,Htilde_μ⟩ Htilde_μ, where ⟨ , ⟩ is the Hall inner product. For a cell c=(i,j) of μ in French coordinates, set B_μ(q,t)=Σ_{(i,j) in μ} q^i t^j and Π_μ(q,t)=Π_{(i,j) in μ, (i,j)≠(0,0)}(1-q^i t^j). Let Δ_{e_k} be the Macdonald eigenoperator Δ_{e_k} Htilde_μ = e_k[B_μ] Htilde_μ, and let Π be the eigenoperator Π Htilde_μ = Π_μ Htilde_μ. Then for every homogeneous symmetric function G of degree n, ⟨p_n,∇_*⟩G = ΠG and ⟨e_k h_{n-k},∇_*⟩G = Δ_{e_k}G.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: It suffices to compare eigenvalues on the Macdonald basis. The key evaluation is Htilde_μ[1-u] = Π_{(i,j) in μ}(1-u q^i t^j). On the other hand, for any homogeneous F of degree n, plethystic Cauchy reproduction gives F[1-u] = ⟨F[X], h_n[X(1-u)]⟩, and h_n[X(1-u)] = Σ_{k=0}^n (-u)^k e_k[X]h_{n-k}[X]. Comparing coefficients of (-u)^k gives ⟨F,e_k h_{n-k}⟩, so for F=Htilde_μ this coefficient is e_k[B_μ]. Thus ⟨e_k h_{n-k},Htilde_μ⟩=e_k[B_μ]. For p_n, divide Htilde_μ[1-u] by 1-u and set u=1; because the cell (0,0) contributes the factor 1-u, the result is Π_μ. In the power-sum expansion of F[1-u], after division by 1-u and specialization u=1 only the partition (n) survives, yielding exactly ⟨F,p_n⟩. Hence ⟨p_n,Htilde_μ⟩=Π_μ. These eigenvalue identities prove the operator statements.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Specialization identities in Section 3.3, especially Equations (3.15)--(3.20)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the plethystic sign alphabet ε satisfying p_r[ε]=(-1)^r, the modified Macdonald polynomials satisfy Htilde_μ[1-u] = Π_{(i,j) in μ}(1-u q^i t^j) and equivalently Htilde_μ[1-εu] = Π_{(i,j) in μ}(1+u q^i t^j). Consequently, on homogeneous degree n symmetric functions, ∇_1 is the identity, ∇_{-1}=(-1)^n ∇, and ∇_{-ε}=∇, where ∇ is the usual nabla operator with eigenvalue T_μ=Π_{(i,j) in μ}q^i t^j. Also ∇_{1-εu}=Σ_{k≥0}u^k Δ_{e_k}; more generally, applying two independent such specializations to ∇_* gives ∇_{1-εu}∇_{1-εv}=Σ_{k,j≥0}u^k v^j Δ_{e_k e_j}. The paper notes that all Δ_{e_λ}, hence all Δ_F by linearity, can be obtained from suitable coefficients of iterated super nabla specializations, and Π is obtained on nonconstant symmetric functions as lim_{u→1}(1-u)^{-1}∇_{1-u}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: All statements follow by evaluating eigenvalues on Htilde_μ. The identity Htilde_μ[1-εu]=Π_c(1+u q^{i(c)}t^{j(c)}) expands as Σ_k u^k e_k[B_μ], so ∇_{1-εu} has eigenvalue Σ_k u^k e_k[B_μ], exactly the generating series of Δ_{e_k}. Two independent specializations multiply eigenvalues, producing e_k[B_μ]e_j[B_μ] and hence Δ_{e_k e_j}. The specializations at 1, -1, and -ε are obtained by plugging those alphabets into Htilde_μ and using the known evaluations Htilde_μ[1]=1, Htilde_μ[-1]=(-1)^{|μ|}T_μ, and Htilde_μ[-ε]=T_μ. For Π, the factor corresponding to the cell (0,0) produces a simple zero in ∇_{1-u}; dividing by 1-u and taking u→1 leaves the product over all nonzero cells.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.4 and Corollary 3.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a partition μ of n, let R(μ) be the set of rearrangements α=(α_1,...,α_ℓ) of the parts of μ. A column-composition tableau of type μ is a pair C=(α,c), where α∈R(μ), c=(c_1,...,c_n) is a weakly increasing sequence of nonnegative integers, and c_i<c_{i+1} implies i is a partial sum of α. Let CC_all_μ be the set of all such C and CC_zero_μ the subset with c_1=0. Let |C|=c_1+...+c_n, and define generating functions CC_all_μ(q)=Σ_{C in CC_all_μ}q^{|C|} and CC_zero_μ(q)=Σ_{C in CC_zero_μ}q^{|C|}. If f_μ is the forgotten symmetric function and Fhat denotes plethystic substitution F[X/(1-q)], then fhat_μ(1)=(-1)^{|μ|-ℓ(μ)} CC_all_μ(q), and (1-q^{|μ|})fhat_μ(1)=(-1)^{|μ|-ℓ(μ)} CC_zero_μ(q). In particular, for μ=(1^n), f_{1^n}=h_n and hhat_n(1)=1/((1-q)(1-q^2)...(1-q^n)).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The starting point is the Egecioglu-Remmel combinatorial formula f_μ(x)=(-1)^{n-ℓ(μ)}Σ_{α∈R(μ)}Σ_{i_1≤...≤i_ℓ}x_{i_1}^{α_1}...x_{i_ℓ}^{α_ℓ}. Plethystically substituting X=1/(1-q), i.e. variables 1,q,q^2,..., yields a sum over weakly increasing indices with q-weight i_1α_1+...+i_ℓα_ℓ. Replacing each part α_j by a block of α_j columns all having height i_j gives exactly a column-composition tableau, and the exponent is the total column height |C|. This proves the formula for CC_all. Multiplying by 1-q^n shifts all column heights simultaneously and cancels all configurations with positive first height, leaving precisely the c_1=0 subset. For μ=(1^n), the forgotten function is h_n, giving the classical principal specialization of h_n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Macdonald Cauchy identities and Equation (3.14)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M=(1-q)(1-t), let F^*(X)=F[X/M], and let w_μ(q,t)=Π_{c in μ}(q^{a(c)}-t^{ℓ(c)+1})(t^{ℓ(c)}-q^{a(c)+1}), where a(c) and ℓ(c) are the arm and leg of c. The modified Macdonald polynomials satisfy the q,t-Cauchy identity e_n^*[XY]=Σ_{μ⊢n} Htilde_μ(X;q,t)Htilde_μ(Y;q,t)/w_μ(q,t). Using the evaluation Htilde_μ[M]=M B_μ(q,t)Π_μ(q,t), the elementary symmetric function has the Macdonald expansion e_n(X)=Σ_{μ⊢n} M B_μ Π_μ Htilde_μ(X;q,t)/w_μ(q,t). Equivalently, e_n=M Δ_{e_1} Π e_n^*.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The q,t-Cauchy identity is the standard reproducing identity for the modified Macdonald basis with respect to the q,t scalar product for which ⟨Htilde_λ,Htilde_μ⟩_* = δ_{λμ}w_μ. Substituting the alphabet M into one side of the kernel gives e_n[M X/M]=e_n[X]. On the right side, the substituted Macdonald polynomial evaluates to Htilde_μ[M]=M B_μΠ_μ. This immediately gives the stated expansion of e_n in the Macdonald basis. Since e_n^*=Σ_μ Htilde_μ/w_μ and the operators Δ_{e_1} and Π multiply Htilde_μ by B_μ and Π_μ, respectively, the operator form e_n=MΔ_{e_1}Πe_n^* follows.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 3.8 and Proposition 3.9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Define Ξ:Λ→Λ by ΞF(X)=M Δ_{e_1} Π F[X/M]. Then for every partition λ of n and every k≥0, ∇_*^k Ξ e_λ = ⟨h_λ,∇_*⟩ ∇_*^k e_n, and ∇_*^k Ξ s_λ = ⟨s_λ,∇_*⟩ ∇_*^k e_n. Here e_λ, h_λ, and s_λ are elementary, complete, and Schur symmetric functions, and the scalar product in ⟨h_λ,∇_*⟩ or ⟨s_λ,∇_*⟩ is the ordinary Hall scalar product applied to the Macdonald eigenvalue Htilde_μ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the Macdonald expansion of e_n from Equation (3.14). Orthogonality for the q,t scalar product gives Ξe_λ = Σ_{μ⊢n} (M B_μΠ_μ/w_μ) Htilde_μ ⟨e_λ^*,Htilde_μ⟩_*. The relation between the q,t scalar product and the Hall scalar product converts ⟨e_λ^*,Htilde_μ⟩_* into ⟨h_λ,Htilde_μ⟩. Therefore Ξe_λ is obtained from the Macdonald expansion of e_n by multiplying each Htilde_μ coefficient by the eigenvalue ⟨h_λ,Htilde_μ⟩, i.e. Ξe_λ=⟨h_λ,∇_*⟩e_n. The same argument with the self-duality of Schur functions gives Ξs_λ=⟨s_λ,∇_*⟩e_n. Since all these operators are diagonal in the Htilde_μ basis, they commute with ∇_* and its iterates, proving the displayed identities.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 3.10, Equation (3.26), and the t=1 specialization framework
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: At t=1, the modified Macdonald polynomial specializes to Htilde_μ(X;q,1)=hhat_μ(X)/hhat_μ(1), where hhat_μ(X)=h_μ[X/(1-q)]. Define b∇_*:Λ_{Q(q)}→Λ_{Q(q)}⊗Λ_{Q(q)} by b∇_* hhat_μ = hhat_μ⊗hhat_μ / hhat_μ(1). If F has no pole at t=1, then (∇_*F)|_{t=1}=b∇_*(F|_{t=1}). For n≥1 and k≥0, b∇_*^k e_n = Σ_{μ⊢n} f_μ[1-q] hhat_μ(1) (hhat_μ/hhat_μ(1))^{⊗(k+1)}. The paper also uses f_μ[1-q]=(-1)^{n-ℓ(μ)}Σ_{β∈R(μ)}(1-q^{β_1}). Finally, the monomial coefficients of hhat_μ/hhat_μ(1) are described by the t=1 specialization of the Haglund-Haiman-Loehr formula: for a composition β rearranging μ and a word α with prescribed multiplicities, the weight is q^{revmaj_β(α)}, where revmaj_β is the sum, over the blocks of β, of the reverse major index of the corresponding subword.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The specialization Htilde_μ(X;q,1)=hhat_μ(X)/hhat_μ(1) is standard and makes the t=1 version of ∇_* a diagonal operator on the hhat_μ basis with the displayed tensor eigenvalue. Expanding e_n=e_n[(1-q)X/(1-q)] in the dual forgotten-complete bases gives e_n=Σ_{μ⊢n} f_μ[1-q]hhat_μ. Applying b∇_* repeatedly to each hhat_μ produces one tensor copy per iteration and the normalization hhat_μ(1), giving Equation (3.26). The specialization formula for f_μ[1-q] follows either by taking the t=1 limit of M B_μΠ_μ/w_μ or by summation formulas; it is also consistent with the column-composition tableau formula. The HHL formula at t=1 gives the revmaj expansion of each normalized hhat_μ factor, which is the input for the later signed tableau and Dyck-path models.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.11
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For n≥1 and k≥0, b∇_*^k e_n admits a preliminary expansion with one elementary tensor factor. Let PR(η,μ) be the set of sequences of partitions ν_vec=(ν_1,...,ν_r) whose part sizes are grouped according to a rearrangement of μ and whose collective multiset of parts rearranges to η. For a sequence ν_vec set fhat_{ν_vec}(1)=Π_i fhat_{ν_i}(1). Then b∇_*^k e_n = Σ_{η⊢n} e_η ⊗ (Σ_{μ⊢n} (-1)^{n-ℓ(μ)}Σ_{β∈R(μ)}(1-q^{β_1})Σ_w q^{revmaj_β(w)} X^w Σ_{ν_vec∈PR(η,μ)} fhat_{ν_vec}(1)), where w runs over words of k-tuples of label indices, revmaj_β(w) is the sum of the reverse major indices in each coordinate and β-block, and X^w is the corresponding monomial in the k remaining alphabets.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Start from the t=1 spectral expansion in Equation (3.26). Expand one tensor factor hhat_μ in the elementary basis by Cauchy duality: hhat_μ = Σ_{η⊢n} e_η Σ_{ν_vec∈PR(η,μ)} fhat_{ν_vec}(1). Expand the remaining normalized hhat_μ/hhat_μ(1) tensor factors in monomials using the t=1 HHL formula, producing q^{revmaj_β(w)}. Substitute the explicit expression f_μ[1-q]=(-1)^{n-ℓ(μ)}Σ_{β∈R(μ)}(1-q^{β_1}). Collecting terms gives the displayed formula. This expansion is not manifestly positive; Section 4 provides the sign-reversing involution that turns its signed rational pieces into area enumerators.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 4 fixed-point theorem: Propositions 4.6, 4.10, 4.15, and Corollary 4.16
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix an alphabet X, a set W of words in X, a ring A, and a weight function ρ:W→A[q] that looks right, meaning ρ(w)=Π_{i=1}^{ℓ(w)} q^{lwt(w,i)(ℓ(w)-i)} wt(w_i), where wt:X→A and lwt(w,i) is a nonnegative integer depending only on the adjacent letters w_i and w_{i+1}. For a partition η⊢n, define a signed set LC_{W,η} of sequences of W-labeled column-composition tableaux of type η, with signs given by the parity of the total number of vertical bars and weights q^{column area}ρ(labels). Let D_{W,η}(q)=Σ_{T∈LC_{W,η}} sign(T)weight(T). Then D_{W,η}(q) equals the positive weight generating function over ρ-compatible W-labeled parallelogram polyominoes of type η: D_{W,η}(q)=Σ_{P∈P_{W,η}}q^{area(P)}wt(P). Here a parallelogram polyomino is a pair of nonintersecting north-east lattice paths P,Q with common endpoints, its area is the number of cells between them minus the minimal m+n-1, type η means the maximal vertical run lengths of the top path rearrange to η, and ρ-compatibility means the bottom path has exactly lwt(w,i)+δ_{i,1} east steps on the line y=i-1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof has two stages. First, a sign-reversing involution is built on labeled column-composition tableau sequences. If a tableau S has a vertical bar, split(S) cuts at the first bar and adds d=Σ lwt(w,i) over the left part to every column of the right part. Proposition 4.6 verifies exact weight preservation: the column-area factor changes by q^{d times the length of the right part}, while the factor ρ decomposes into the two subwords with precisely the inverse q-power, because ρ looks right. Lemma 4.7 gives the inverse join criterion, namely the second piece can be joined to the first exactly when its first column height is at least the last height of the first piece plus the corresponding local-weight sum; Lemma 4.8 ensures that recursive choices are compatible. The involution ψ scans from the left, splitting the first splittable component, joining the first joinable adjacent pair, or recursing. Its fixed points are precisely sequences with no vertical bars and no allowed adjacent joins, equivalently sequences T=(T_i) satisfying c(T_{i+1}) < c(T_i)+Σ_{j in block i}lwt(w,j). Second, Proposition 4.15 constructs a bijection φ from these fixed points to compatible parallelogram polyominoes. For fixed block lengths β_i, heights c(T_i), and labels w, set s_i=c(T_i)+Σ_{j in block i}lwt(w,j)-c(T_{i+1}); then define the top path P=(Π_i N^{β_i}E^{s_i})E and bottom path Q=E(Π_{j=1}^n E^{lwt(w,j)}N). The fixed-point inequalities make all s_i positive and guarantee P stays above Q. A direct row-by-row calculation shows that the polyomino area equals the original tableau area plus the local-weight exponents in ρ. This proves the positive formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let k,n be positive integers. A multi-labeled kn-Dyck path in LD_{kn} is a pair L=(π,w), where π is a lattice path from (0,0) to (kn,n) with north and east steps staying weakly above the diagonal x=ky, and w=(w_1,...,w_n) labels the north steps by (k+1)-tuples w_i=(w_{i0},...,w_{ik}) of positive integers. If s_i=#{j:0≤j≤k and w_{ij}≥w_{i+1,j}} is the number of weak descents between consecutive label tuples, then the i-th north step of π must be followed by at least s_i east steps, equivalently s_i≤col_π(i+1)-col_π(i). Let area(π) be the number of whole cells between π and x=ky, and let X^w=Π_{i=1}^nΠ_{j=0}^k x_{j,w_{ij}}, interpreted tensorially in k+1 alphabets. Then b∇_*^k e_n = Σ_{(π,w)∈LD_{kn}} q^{area(π)} X^w.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose W to be the set of all words in the alphabet of positive (k+1)-tuples, set lwt(w,i)=#{j: w_{ij}<w_{i+1,j}}, the number of strict ascents, and set wt(w_i)=Π_j x_{j,w_{ij}}. With these choices, the revmaj statistics from the t=1 HHL expansion are exactly of the looks-right form required in Section 4. The signed expansion from Proposition 3.11 is therefore transformed by Corollary 4.16 into a positive sum over compatible labeled parallelogram polyominoes. The map ι of Proposition 5.7 converts those polyominoes into the stated multi-labeled rectangular Dyck paths by inserting east steps according to the non-ascents, i.e. weak descents, of the label tuples. The area is preserved by this insertion-deletion process, and the monomial label weight is unchanged.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix a partition λ⊢n and a partition η⊢n. Let W_λ be the set of words w=(w_1,...,w_n) of (k+1)-tuples such that the first coordinate word w_{10}...w_{n0} has multiplicity type 1^{λ_1}2^{λ_2}... and the remaining coordinates are positive. Let LD_{kn}(λ,η) be the subset of multi-labeled kn-Dyck paths with this first-coordinate content and with e-composition η, where i is a partial sum of η exactly when #{j: w_{ij}≥w_{i+1,j}} < col_π(i+1)-col_π(i). Let P_{W_λ,η} be the set of ρ-compatible W_λ-labeled parallelogram polyominoes of type η for the local weight lwt=#{coordinatewise strict ascents}. The map ι sends L=(π,w) to a parallelogram polyomino by taking P=πE and Q=E(E^kN)^n, then for each 0≤i<n removing #{j:w_{ij}≥w_{i+1,j}} east steps of P on the line y=i and the same number of east steps of Q on the line y=i-1. This defines a weight-preserving bijection LD_{kn}(λ,η) ↔ P_{W_λ,η}; in particular area(L)=area(ι(L)).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The Dyck-path label condition ensures that there are enough east steps after the i-th north step of π to remove the required number of non-ascents. Removing the same number of east steps from the bottom path on the row below changes neither the relative area contribution of that row nor the label word. After removal, the bottom path has exactly k-#{weak descents}+δ_{i,1}=#{strict ascents}+δ_{i,1}=lwt(w,i)+δ_{i,1} east steps on row y=i-1, so the polyomino is ρ-compatible. The definition of e-composition records precisely the rows where at least one east step remains in the top path after the deletion, hence the vertical run lengths of the top path have type η. The construction is reversible by reinserting the deleted east steps in both paths, so it is bijective, and the equal deletion in adjacent rows proves area preservation.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollaries 5.4 and 5.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For L=(π,w) with k coordinate labels rather than k+1, define η(L) to be the composition of n whose partial sums are the indices i such that #{j: w_{ij}≥w_{i+1,j}} < col_π(i+1)-col_π(i). Let w(L) be the k-coordinate label word and X^{w(L)} its tensor monomial. Then b∇_*^k e_n = Σ_{L∈LD^k_{kn}} q^{area(L)} e_{η(L)} ⊗ X^{w(L)}. Moreover, if λ⊢n and LD_{kn}(λ) denotes the multi-labeled paths in LD_{kn} whose first coordinate word has multiplicity type 1^{λ_1}2^{λ_2}..., then ∇_*^k Ξ e_λ evaluated at t=1 equals Σ_{L∈LD_{kn}(λ)} q^{area(L)} e_{η(L)} ⊗ X^{w(L)}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the first formula, start from the monomial expansion with k+1 label coordinates and group the first coordinate labels. Given π and the last k coordinates, the allowed first-coordinate words are exactly encoded by the elementary symmetric factor e_{η(L)}: a break in η(L) occurs precisely when the path has surplus east steps beyond those forced by the weak descents in the last k coordinates, allowing the first-coordinate label to reset without violating the multi-label condition. For the second formula, apply Proposition 3.9, which identifies Ξe_λ at t=1 with the scalar-product specialization ⟨h_λ,∇_*⟩e_n. Taking this scalar product in the first tensor coordinate of the monomial formula extracts exactly the first-coordinate words of content λ, producing the displayed restricted sum.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A word a_1...a_n of positive integers is a lattice word of type λ if every prefix contains at least as many r's as (r+1)'s for every r, and the multiplicities form the partition λ. Let LLD_{kn} be the subset of the paths in the elementary expansion of Corollary 5.4 for which, in each of the k label coordinates, the coordinate word is a lattice word; write λ_j(w(L)) for the type of the j-th coordinate word. Then b∇_*^k e_n = Σ_{L∈LLD_{kn}} q^{area(L)} e_{η(L)} ⊗ s_{λ_1(w(L))} ⊗ ... ⊗ s_{λ_k(w(L))}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The Schur expansion of hhat_β/hhat_β(1) at t=1 is obtained from the same HHL specialization by restricting monomial label words to lattice words: hhat_β/hhat_β(1)=Σ_{λ⊢n}s_λ Σ_{α∈LW_λ}q^{revmaj_β(α)}, where LW_λ is the set of lattice words of type λ. Thus one repeats the Section 4 sign-reversing involution with W equal to the set of k-tuples of lattice words of specified types and with the same local ascent weight. The fixed-point and polyomino-to-Dyck bijections are unchanged, and the lattice-word restriction converts each monomial tensor factor into the corresponding Schur function s_{λ_j}. This yields the displayed Schur-positive q-area formula at t=1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The super nabla image of e_n satisfies ∇_* e_n = Σ_λ m_λ ⊗ Ξ e_λ, where the sum is over partitions λ of n and m_λ is the monomial symmetric function.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: View ∇_*e_n as ∇_Y e_n(X), a symmetric function in two independent alphabets. Expanding in the monomial basis in the Y variables gives coefficients obtained by Hall scalar products with the dual complete basis h_λ. Proposition 3.9 gives ⟨h_λ,∇_*⟩e_n=Ξe_λ. Hence the coefficient of m_λ(Y) is Ξe_λ(X), proving the expansion.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 6.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a partition μ, let SR(μ) be the set of selected rearrangements obtained by choosing a rearrangement α=(α_1,...,α_{ℓ(μ)}) of the parts of μ and then marking one cell in the first row α_1. Thus |SR(μ)|=Σ_{α∈R(μ)}α_1. Newton's identity is expressed as p_n = Σ_{μ⊢n} (-1)^{ℓ(μ)-1}|SR(μ)| h_μ. Equivalently, after plethystic substitution X/(1-q), p_n[X/(1-q)] = p_n(X)/(1-q^n) = -Σ_{μ⊢n}(-1)^{ℓ(μ)}|SR(μ)| hhat_μ, and applying ω gives ω(p_n)=(1-q^n)Σ_{μ⊢n}(-1)^{n-ℓ(μ)}|SR(μ)|hhat_μ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is Newton's identity rewritten in the complete basis and with a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficient. The coefficient |SR(μ)| records both the choice of an ordered rearrangement of the parts and the choice of a distinguished cell in the first selected part, so it equals Σ_{α∈R(μ)}α_1. The plethystic version follows from p_n[X/(1-q)]=p_n(X)/(1-q^n) and h_μ[X/(1-q)]=hhat_μ. Applying the involution ω changes p_n by the standard sign (-1)^{n-1}, giving the final displayed formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Propositions 6.3, 6.5, 6.10, and 6.11
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let γ be a nonempty partition, and let bΔ_{m_γ} denote the t=1 specialization of the Delta operator, acting diagonally on hhat_μ by the eigenvalue m_γ[Σ_i[μ_i]_q]. The paper expands b∇_*^k bΔ_{m_γ}ω(p_n) as a signed sum over selected labeled column-composition tableaux. An element S of SC_γ(η,μ) is built by choosing a selected rearrangement α∈SR(μ), a partition vector ν_vec∈PR(η,α), column-composition tableaux C_i of types ν_i with at least one first column of height 0, a circle inherited from the selected cell of α_1, and labels by (k+1)-tuples whose zeroth coordinate word rearranges γ together with zeros and whose other coordinates are positive. The weight is q^{column area} times q^{rw of zeroth labels} times the coordinatewise q^{revmaj} monomial weight, and the sign is (-1)^{ℓ(η)-ℓ(μ)}. Proposition 6.5 states b∇_*^k bΔ_{m_γ}ω(p_n)=Σ_{η⊢n}Σ_{μ⊢n}Σ_{S∈SC_γ(η,μ)} sign(S)ρ(S)⊗e_η. Proposition 6.10 defines a weight-preserving sign-reversing involution ψ_circle on these objects, and Proposition 6.11 concludes that only the fixed points SM^γ_η remain: b∇_*^k bΔ_{m_γ}ω(p_n)=Σ_{η⊢n}Σ_{S∈SM^γ_η}ρ(S)⊗e_η.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Proposition 6.3 starts from the ω(p_n) expansion in Proposition 6.2, applies bΔ_{m_γ} to hhat_μ using the t=1 eigenvalue m_γ[Σ_i[μ_i]_q], and applies b∇_*^k using the normalized hhat_μ tensor expansion. The factor (1-q^n) together with the forgotten-function principal specializations is interpreted as sequences of column-composition tableaux with at least one component whose first column has height 0. The selected cell in SR(μ) becomes the circle, and the Delta eigenvalue m_γ[Σ_i[μ_i]_q] becomes the zeroth-coordinate label word with content γ plus zeros, weighted by rw. This gives the signed tableau formula of Proposition 6.5. The involution ψ_circle modifies the Section 4 split-join involution to handle the circle cyclically. If the circle is not leading, split at the rightmost vertical bar to its left and rotate the left piece to the end. If the circle is leading and the last component can join the first, perform that cyclic join. Otherwise apply the ordinary ψ from Section 4. Splitting and joining preserve weight by the same local-weight calculation as Proposition 4.6 and reverse sign by changing the number of bars by one. The only delicate case is when there is a single component; the assumption |γ|>0 ensures that after splitting, a nonzero zeroth label forces enough added column height to prevent the image from falling into the cyclic-join case. Thus ψ_circle is an involution, and its fixed points are exactly the no-bar configurations with no ordinary or cyclic joins and with the circle leading.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Propositions 6.14, 6.16, and 6.18
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let γ be a nonempty partition. A ρ-compatible polyomino with a marked return is obtained from the fixed points SM^γ_η by a bijection φ_circle: rotate the fixed-point sequence so that a component with first-column height 0 comes first, apply the Section 4 polyomino map φ, and keep the circle as a mark on a return before the first return of the paths. Proposition 6.14 states that φ_circle is weight-preserving and bijective onto a set RP^γ_{k,η} of compatible labeled parallelogram polyominoes with marked return and with the top path ending in two east steps. Applying the deletion-insertion map ι gives multi-labeled (γ,kn)-Dyck paths with a marked return. In these paths the bottom path has the form Q=E E^{a_1}N ... E^{a_n}N with (a_1-k,...,a_n-k) a rearrangement of γ together with n-ℓ(γ) zeros, the top path satisfies the label descent condition, and a return row is marked before the first return. With tensor order as in Proposition 6.16, b∇_*^k bΔ_{m_γ}ω(p_n)=Σ_{L∈RLD^γ_{kn}}q^{area(L)}X^L⊗e_{η(L)}. The Schur version, Proposition 6.18, restricts labels to coordinatewise lattice words and gives b∇_*^k bΔ_{m_γ}ω(p_n)=Σ_{L∈RLLD^γ_{kn}}q^{area(L)}e_{η(L)}⊗s_{λ_1(w(L))}⊗...⊗s_{λ_k(w(L))}, with the paper also presenting equivalent tensor-order variants.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a fixed point S=(S_1,...,S_r), if c(S_1)=0 apply φ directly; if not, choose the largest j with c(S_j)=0, cyclically rotate to (S_j,...,S_r,S_1,...,S_{j-1}), and apply φ. The no-join condition around the chosen zero-height component implies that either the preceding height or the relevant local weight is positive, which forces the resulting top path to end with two east steps. The circle lies in the initial block after rotation and becomes a marked row before the first return. Weight preservation is inherited from φ and from the fact that cyclic rotation is compensated by the cyclic join inequalities in the fixed-point set. Then the map ι removes or inserts east steps according to coordinatewise label non-ascents exactly as in Proposition 5.7; because the zeroth labels now encode the parts of γ, the bottom path becomes the specified (γ,kn)-staircase. The Schur formula is obtained by replacing arbitrary positive label words in the k nonzero coordinates by lattice words, using the same lattice-word Schur expansion as in Theorem 5.8.

proof_techniques.name: Macdonald eigenoperator extraction by scalar product
proof_techniques.description: The paper treats ∇_* as a universal Macdonald eigenoperator whose eigenvalue on Htilde_μ is the entire symmetric function Htilde_μ in a second alphabet. Pairing this eigenvalue with a chosen symmetric function F produces the ordinary scalar eigenoperator with eigenvalue ⟨F,Htilde_μ⟩. Evaluations such as Htilde_μ[1-u]=Π_c(1-uq^i t^j) then identify these scalar products with standard eigenvalues such as e_k[B_μ] and Π_μ.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the Hilbert series coefficients c_λ(n) can be expressed as scalar products against a Macdonald-eigenoperator formula, hook shapes may be attacked by choosing F to be a hook Schur function or a related e_a h_b expression and then translating the scalar product into a restriction on labels.

proof_techniques.name: Reduction through the Ξ basis
proof_techniques.description: The operator ΞF=MΔ_{e_1}ΠF[X/M] is related to ∇_* by Ξe_λ=⟨h_λ,∇_*⟩e_n and Ξs_λ=⟨s_λ,∇_*⟩e_n. Thus all expressions ∇_*^kΞe_λ and ∇_*^kΞs_λ are obtained from ∇_*^{k+1}e_n by taking an ordinary Hall scalar product in one tensor factor.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is useful if the coinvariant Hilbert series is known or suspected to involve Θ or Ξ operators, since hook Schur coefficients can then be reduced to controlled coefficient extraction from the master object ∇_*^{k+1}e_n.

proof_techniques.name: t=1 degeneration to complete symmetric functions
proof_techniques.description: At t=1, Htilde_μ becomes h_μ[X/(1-q)] divided by its principal specialization. This turns Macdonald eigenoperator calculations into complete-basis calculations and makes the HHL formula collapse to reverse-major-index weights. The expansion of e_n in the hhat_μ basis uses forgotten symmetric functions evaluated at 1-q.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a hook coefficient problem has a specialization or filtration whose Hilbert series is governed by t=1 Macdonald theory, this degeneration replaces difficult two-parameter Macdonald combinatorics by q-enumeration of words, descents, and tableaux.

proof_techniques.name: Forgotten functions as column-composition tableaux
proof_techniques.description: Principal specializations of forgotten symmetric functions are interpreted as signed generating functions for column-composition tableaux. The factor 1-q^n imposes a zero first column condition. These tableaux encode the negative signs appearing in complete-basis expansions before cancellation.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This provides a way to convert alternating symmetric-function sums, which often arise from hook or power-sum expansions, into signed combinatorial objects amenable to involution.

proof_techniques.name: Looks-right local weights and split-join involution
proof_techniques.description: A weight ρ on words looks right when it factors as local adjacent contributions q^{lwt(w,i)(n-i)} times letter weights. This exact form allows a split operation at a vertical bar to preserve weight: added column area on the right is canceled by the change in local q-exponents. A recursively defined split-join involution cancels all non-fixed signed column-composition tableau sequences.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For hook coefficients, one can try to encode hook restrictions as local label conditions while preserving a looks-right weight. If successful, the same involution can turn an alternating algebraic coefficient formula into a positive path model.

proof_techniques.name: Fixed points to parallelogram polyominoes
proof_techniques.description: After cancellation, fixed points are sequences with no vertical bars and no joinable adjacent pair. Their heights and local weights determine a top path P and bottom path Q of a parallelogram polyomino by explicit formulas, and a direct area computation proves that the original q-weight is q^{area(P,Q)} times label weight.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a general positive-combinatorics mechanism: once an algebraic coefficient is expressed in the paper's D_{W,η} form, the coefficient becomes an area generating function over compatible polyominoes.

proof_techniques.name: Deleting east steps to pass from polyominoes to multi-labeled Dyck paths
proof_techniques.description: The bijection ι starts from a rectangular Dyck path with tuple labels and deletes, in both the top and bottom paths, east steps corresponding to coordinatewise weak descents. Compatibility of the polyomino bottom path becomes the Dyck-path label condition that enough east steps follow each north step. Since equal deletions are made in adjacent rows, area is preserved.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This converts polyomino fixed-point models into more familiar Dyck-path models. Hook coefficients might be expressible as counts of paths with special label content or reading-word restrictions.

proof_techniques.name: Schur extraction via lattice words
proof_techniques.description: The Schur expansion is obtained by restricting monomial label words to lattice words. A lattice word records the row word of a standard tableau, and summing over lattice words of type λ converts monomial expansions into Schur functions s_λ.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Since the original problem asks for Schur coefficients c_λ(n), especially for hook λ, this technique suggests interpreting such coefficients by imposing hook-type lattice-word conditions on one or more label coordinates.

proof_techniques.name: Marked-return cyclic involution for power sums
proof_techniques.description: For power-sum inputs, Newton's identity introduces a selected cell in the first part of a rearrangement. The ordinary split-join involution is modified cyclically around this selected cell: nonleading circles are handled by splitting and rotating, while leading circles allow a cyclic join of the last and first components. Fixed points become polyominoes or Dyck paths with a marked return.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Hook Schur functions have close relationships with e-h combinations and power-sum expansions; if a hook coefficient is transformed into a power-sum or cyclically selected expression, the marked-return model may provide the needed combinatorial interpretation.

other_useful_info: The paper notes that the family Ξe_λ is a basis of Λ and that Ξe_{λ,1}=Δ_{e_1}Θ_{e_λ}e_1 in terms of Theta operators, so understanding ∇_*^{k+1}e_n is enough to understand ∇_*^k applied to any Ξe_λ. Section 5.3 explains that when k=1, scalar products of the two-label Dyck-path formula recover familiar t=1 forms of the shuffle theorem and the valley Delta conjecture: pairing with e_n forces one label coordinate to be strictly increasing in reading order, while pairing with e_{n-k}h_k corresponds to decorated valleys. Proposition 5.9 combined with D'Adderio-Iraci-Le Borgne-Romero-Vanden Wyngaerd is said to give a rooted tiered-tree and tree-inversion interpretation for an m⊗m expansion of ∇_*e_n at t=1; the same cited work relates coefficients of Ξe_{1^n} to Kac polynomials of certain dandelion quivers. Section 6.4 observes that the γ=1^n, k=0 case of the marked-return formula coincides at t=1 with the Square Paths theorem for ∇ω(p_n) after circularly rearranging square paths and marking a return; the same methods give the t=1 Delta Square conjecture. In Section 7 the authors conjecture that ∇_*e_n is s⊗s-positive, checked by computer for n≤9, but s^{⊗k}-positivity fails in general for k>2 before specializing t=1; Theorem 5.8 restores q-positivity at t=1. They also conjecture m^{⊗k}-positivity of ∇_*^{k-1}e_n for general q,t and ask for a t-statistic on multi-labeled Dyck paths refining the t=1 area formula, pointing to Mellit and Carlsson-Mellit methods as a possible route.
why_relevant: Introduces a unified Macdonald-eigenoperator framework containing nabla, Delta, and Theta operators as specializations. Since superspace and bosonic-fermionic coinvariant formulas are expressed through these operators, this is useful for translating hook-coefficient identities into symmetric-function identities.

title: Superspace Coinvariants and Hyperplane Arrangements
overall_summary: The paper proves the Sagan-Swanson conjecture giving an explicit monomial basis for the type A superspace coinvariant ring SR = Ω/I, where Ω = K[x1,...,xn] ⊗ exterior(θ1,...,θn) and I is generated by positive-degree S_n-invariants in superspace.
