The central result is that, for each fermionic support J⊆[n], the bosonic monomials with exponents below the J-staircase form a basis of a commutative colon quotient S/((S^{S_n}_+):f_J), and therefore their products with θ_J form a basis of SR. The proof converts the colon quotients into Solomon-Terao algebras of hyperplane arrangements, proves freeness and exact deletion-restriction sequences for a class of southwest arrangements, and inducts to obtain monomial bases. For the original hook-coefficient problem, the most useful output is that every bigraded component of SR is counted explicitly by pairs consisting of a subset J and an exponent vector a with 0≤a_i<st(J)_i; once hook Schur coefficients are related to superspace bidegrees, this turns them into a direct combinatorial counting problem.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.2, Rhoades-Wilson transfer principle
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a characteristic-zero field, S=K[x1,...,xn], Ω=S⊗exterior(θ1,...,θn), and SR=Ω/I be the superspace coinvariant ring, where I is the ideal generated by S_n-invariants in Ω with vanishing constant term. For J⊆[n], define θ_J=θ_{j1}...θ_{jr} for J={j1<...<jr} and define f_J=∏_{j∈J} x_j ∏_{i=j+1}^n (x_j−x_i) in S. If, for every J⊆[n], B(J)⊆S is a set of homogeneous polynomials whose images form a K-basis of S/((S^{S_n}_+):f_J), then the disjoint union B=⊔_{J⊆[n]} B(J) θ_J has images forming a K-basis of SR.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this theorem from Rhoades-Wilson and uses it as a black box. Its role is to decompose the superspace quotient by fermionic support J: the bosonic relations seen in the θ_J-sector are encoded by the colon ideal (S^{S_n}_+):f_J. Therefore, once one supplies a basis for each commutative quotient S/((S^{S_n}_+):f_J), multiplying those basis elements by θ_J and taking the disjoint union gives a basis of the whole superspace coinvariant ring.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Rhoades-Wilson colon-ideal dichotomy quoted in the introduction
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For J⊆[n], let st(J)=(st(J)_1,...,st(J)_n) be defined recursively by st(J)_1=1 if 1∉J and st(J)_1=0 if 1∈J, and for i>1 by st(J)_i=st(J)_{i−1}+1 if i∉J and st(J)_i=st(J)_{i−1} if i∈J. With f_J=∏_{j∈J} x_j ∏_{i=j+1}^n (x_j−x_i), Rhoades-Wilson proved that (S^{S_n}_+):f_J is the unit ideal S if 1∈J, while if 1∉J it is generated by a homogeneous regular sequence p_{J,1},...,p_{J,n} with deg(p_{J,i})=st(J)_i.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is stated in the introduction as a prior result from Rhoades-Wilson and is not reproved in this paper. The paper uses the dichotomy mainly for dimension and Hilbert-series control: in the nonunit case the quotient S/((S^{S_n}_+):f_J) is a complete intersection with Hilbert series ∏_{i=1}^n (1−q^{st(J)_i})/(1−q), while in the unit case the quotient is zero, matching the fact that the staircase monomial set M(J) is empty when 1∈J.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.1, Poincare-duality colon criterion
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let a and a′ be homogeneous ideals in S=K[x1,...,xn], and let f∈S be homogeneous of degree k with f∉a. Suppose a′⊆(a:f). If S/a′ is a Poincare duality algebra with socle degree m and S/a is a Poincare duality algebra with socle degree m+k, then a′=(a:f).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this lemma from Abe-Horiguchi-Masuda-Murai-Sato. The mechanism is that the inclusion a′⊆(a:f) gives a quotient map S/a′→S/(a:f), while multiplication by f embeds S/(a:f), shifted by degree k, into S/a. The assumed socle degrees differ by exactly k, and Poincare duality forces the annihilator of f in S/a to have the same size as S/a′; hence the quotient map cannot have a nonzero kernel and the two ideals coincide.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.3, trichotomy for Solomon-Terao algebras of free arrangements
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a free hyperplane arrangement in K^n with exponent multiset {e1,...,en}. Let Der(A) be its module of logarithmic derivations, let c:Der(S)→S be an S-module map homogeneous of degree d≥0, meaning each c(∂_i) is homogeneous of degree d, and define cA=c(Der(A)) and ST(A,c)=S/cA. Exactly one of the following holds: ST(A,c)=0; ST(A,c) is infinite-dimensional over K; or cA is a complete intersection and ST(A,c) is a Poincare duality algebra with Hilbert series ∏_{i=1}^n (1−q^{e_i+d})/(1−q)^n and socle degree e1+...+en+n(d−1)=|A|+n(d−1).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose a homogeneous free S-basis ρ1,...,ρn of Der(A) with deg ρ_i=e_i. Then cA is generated by the n homogeneous elements c(ρ_i), whose degrees are e_i+d. If S/cA is neither zero nor infinite-dimensional, then cA is a proper Artinian ideal generated by n homogeneous elements in a polynomial ring of dimension n, hence these generators form a regular sequence. Standard complete-intersection theory gives the Hilbert series and Poincare duality. The equality e1+...+en=|A| is the usual consequence of Terao factorization for free arrangements, obtained by comparing the coefficient of t^{n−1} in the characteristic polynomial.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.4, Solomon-Terao ideals as colon ideals
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let B⊆A be free hyperplane arrangements in K^n, let Q(A) and Q(B) be defining products of linear forms for A and B, and let c:Der(S)→S be homogeneous. Assume ST(A,c)=S/cA is finite-dimensional over K and Q(A)/Q(B)∉cA. Then cB=cA:(Q(A)/Q(B)) as ideals of S.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Since B⊆A, every derivation tangent to A is tangent to B, and multiplication by Q(A)/Q(B) sends Der(B) into Der(A). Applying c gives cB⊆cA:(Q(A)/Q(B)). The assumptions imply that ST(A,c) is a nonzero finite-dimensional Poincare duality algebra by Lemma 3.3. Because cA⊆cB, the quotient ST(B,c) is also finite-dimensional; the preceding inclusion and the hypothesis Q(A)/Q(B)∉cA ensure it is nonzero, so Lemma 3.3 again gives Poincare duality. The socle degrees of ST(A,c) and ST(B,c) differ by deg(Q(A)/Q(B))=|A|−|B|, exactly the hypothesis needed in Lemma 2.1, which yields equality of the colon ideal with cB.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.2, deletion and restriction for southwest arrangements
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let AeΦ+ be the augmented braid arrangement in K^n with hyperplanes H_{i,j} for 0≤i<j≤n, where H_{0,j} is the coordinate hyperplane x_j=0 and H_{i,j} for i>0 is x_i=x_j. A subarrangement A⊆AeΦ+ is southwest if H_{i,j}∈A and j>i+1 imply H_{i,j−1}∈A. Define h(A)=(h1,...,hn) by h_i=|A∩{H_{0,i},H_{1,i},...,H_{i−1,i}}|. If A is southwest, contains at least one coordinate hyperplane, and p=max{k:H_{0,k}∈A}, then A\H_{0,p} is southwest with h-sequence (h1,...,h_{p−1},h_p−1,h_{p+1},...,hn), and the restriction A(p)=A^{H_{0,p}} is southwest inside H_{0,p} with h-sequence (h1,...,h_{p−1},h_{p+1},...,hn).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Deleting the coordinate hyperplane H_{0,p} simply removes one hyperplane from the p-th northwest-to-southeast diagonal, so the southwest condition and all other h_i are unchanged. For restriction to H_{0,p}, one sets x_p=0. Hyperplanes not involving x_p remain of the same form, while hyperplanes x_i−x_p=0 become coordinate hyperplanes x_i=0 in the restricted space; pictorially, the hyperplanes involving x_p form a V-shape in the augmented root poset, and restriction removes one arm and moves the other arm to the top of the corresponding diagonals. The maximality of p among coordinate hyperplanes in A ensures this process preserves southwest-closedness and has precisely the stated effect on h-sequences.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.3, southwest arrangements are free
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Every southwest subarrangement A⊆AeΦ+ is supersolvable, and hence free.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Replace each coordinate hyperplane x_k=0 by x_0−x_k=0 to obtain a graphical arrangement A′ in K^{n+1} associated with a graph Γ on vertices {0,1,...,n}. The intersection posets of A and A′ are isomorphic, so supersolvability is the same for the two arrangements. The southwest condition implies that Γ is chordal. Stanley's criterion says that a graphical arrangement is supersolvable if and only if its graph is chordal. Since supersolvable arrangements are free, A is free.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.6, explicit free basis for derivations of a southwest arrangement
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A⊆AeΦ+ be a southwest arrangement in K^n. For 0≤i<k≤n set α_{0,k}=x_k and α_{i,k}=x_i−x_k for i>0. For j=1,...,n define ρ_j^A=Σ_{k=j}^n (∏_{0≤i<j, H_{i,j}∈A} α_{i,k}) ∂_k, with the empty product equal to 1. Then ρ_1^A,...,ρ_n^A form a homogeneous free S-basis of Der(A). In particular, the exponent multiset of A is h(A)=(h1,...,hn), where h_j=|A∩{H_{0,j},H_{1,j},...,H_{j−1,j}}|.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof applies Saito's criterion. The degree of ρ_j^A is h_j, so the sum of the degrees is |A|. The derivations are S-linearly independent because ρ_j^A only involves ∂_j,∂_{j+1},...,∂_n and the coefficient of ∂_j is nonzero, giving a triangular form. It remains to show ρ_j^A is tangent to every hyperplane H_{s,t} in A. If t<j, then ρ_j^A(α_{s,t})=0. If s<j≤t, southwest-closedness implies H_{s,j}∈A, so the factor α_{s,t} appears in the relevant product and divides ρ_j^A(α_{s,t}). If j≤s, then ρ_j^A(α_{s,t}) is a difference of two products that becomes zero after setting x_s=x_t, hence is divisible by x_s−x_t. Thus every ρ_j^A lies in Der(A), and Saito's criterion gives the basis.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.9 and Lemma 4.10, exact derivation sequence for southwest deletion-restriction triples
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a hyperplane arrangement in K^n and let H=ker(α)∈A. Suppose there are nonnegative integers e1≤...≤en and an index k such that A is free with exponent multiset {e1,...,e_{k−1},e_k,e_{k+1},...,en}, A\H is free with exponent multiset {e1,...,e_{k−1},e_k−1,e_{k+1},...,en}, and A^H is free with exponent multiset {e1,...,e_{k−1},e_{k+1},...,en}. Then there is a short exact sequence 0→Der(A\H) --multiply by α--> Der(A) --restriction to H--> Der(A^H)→0. Moreover, if A⊆AeΦ+ is an essential southwest arrangement and p=max{k:H_{0,k}∈A}, then the triple (A, A\H_{0,p}, A^{H_{0,p}}) satisfies these freeness and exponent hypotheses.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the exact sequence, a derivation tangent to A restricts to a derivation tangent to the restricted arrangement A^H on H; the kernel consists of derivations divisible by α, which identify with Der(A\H). Surjectivity is the content of the addition-deletion theorem under the stated exponent hypotheses. For an essential southwest arrangement, Proposition 4.3 gives freeness, Proposition 4.6 identifies the exponents with h(A), and Lemma 4.2 shows that deletion of H_{0,p} subtracts 1 from h_p while restriction deletes h_p. Thus the exponents match Terao's addition-deletion pattern.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.1, cospanning criterion for products of augmented positive roots
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let V=K^n with dual basis x1,...,xn, and let eΦ+={x_i−x_j:1≤i<j≤n}∪{x1,...,xn}. For any subset T⊆eΦ+, the product ∏_{α∈T} α lies in the classical coinvariant ideal (S^{S_n}_+) if and only if the complement eΦ+\T does not span V* over K.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is in the appendix. First suppose T^c=eΦ+\T does not span V*. Let B={b:x_b∈span_K(T^c)} and A=[n]\B, so A is nonempty. Then x_a∈T for a∈A and, up to sign, x_a−x_b∈T for a∈A,b∈B; hence it is enough to show P=∏_{a∈A} x_a ∏_{a∈A,b∈B}(x_a−x_b) lies in (S^{S_n}_+). Expanding in elementary symmetric polynomials in the B-variables and using the congruence h_d(A)≡(−1)^d e_d(B) modulo (S^{S_n}_+) rewrites P modulo the coinvariant ideal in terms of complete homogeneous functions in the A-variables. Repeatedly, Pieri-rule identities show the terms to be Schur polynomials s_λ(A) with λ_1>|B|=n−|A|, hence in (S^{S_n}_+); eventually P is congruent to the rectangular Schur polynomial s_{(|B|+1)^{|A|}}(A), also in the ideal. Conversely, suppose T^c spans V*. Build a forest F on [n] whose edges correspond to differences in T^c and such that each component has a distinguished root i_p with x_{i_p}∈T^c. Then ∏_{α∈T}α divides a polynomial P_F formed from x_i over nonroot vertices and x_i−x_j over nonedges of F. If ∏_{α∈T}α were in the coinvariant ideal then P_F would be also. By Steinberg's inverse-system criterion, membership in (S^{S_n}_+) is equivalent to annihilating the Vandermonde δ_n under differential action. Comparing P_F with the full Vandermonde product, monomials are interpreted as orientations of the complete graph; orient the forest away from roots and then repeatedly choose leaves, orienting all remaining incident edges toward the chosen leaf. This produces a unique Vandermonde-type monomial x_{w(1)}^{n−1}x_{w(2)}^{n−2}...x_{w(n)}^0 appearing without cancellation in P_F, so P_F⊙δ_n≠0. Hence P_F and therefore ∏_{α∈T}α are not forced into the coinvariant ideal.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.2, Solomon-Terao algebras of subarrangements of the augmented braid arrangement
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A⊆AeΦ+ be any subarrangement, where AeΦ+ consists of H_{i,j}=ker(α_{i,j}) for 0≤i<j≤n with α_{0,j}=x_j and α_{i,j}=x_i−x_j for i>0. Let i:Der(S)→S be the S-module map i(∂_r)=1 for all r, let iA=i(Der(A)), define ST(A,i)=S/iA, and set β_A=∏_{0≤i<j≤n, H_{i,j}∉A} α_{i,j}. Then: 1. ST(A,i) is a quotient of the classical coinvariant ring S/(S^{S_n}_+), equivalently iA contains (S^{S_n}_+). 2. If A is not essential, meaning ∩_{H∈A}H≠{0}, then iA=S and ST(A,i)=0. 3. If A is free, then iA=(S^{S_n}_+):β_A.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For part 1, the explicit free bases for the braid arrangement AΦ+ and augmented braid arrangement AeΦ+ imply i(Der(AeΦ+))=a(Der(AΦ+))=(S^{S_n}_+), where a:Der(S)→S sends ∂_r to x_r. Since A⊆AeΦ+ gives Der(AeΦ+)⊆Der(A), iA contains the coinvariant ideal. For part 2, after harmless closure reductions, let T={r:H_{0,r}∈A}. Nonessentiality implies T≠[n]. On [n]\T, put i∼j when H_{i,j}∈A. If C1,...,Cr are the equivalence classes, then the constant derivation ∑_{u∈C1∪...∪Cr} ∂_u is tangent to every hyperplane of A, and applying i gives a nonzero scalar, hence a unit. For part 3, if A is nonessential this follows from part 2 and Lemma 5.1, since β_A∈(S^{S_n}_+). If A is essential and free, then Der(A) has no nonzero degree-zero elements, so iA has no nonzero constants; Lemma 5.1 gives β_A∉(S^{S_n}_+). Applying Lemma 3.4 to the inclusion A⊆AeΦ+ and using i(Der(AeΦ+))=(S^{S_n}_+) gives iA=(S^{S_n}_+):β_A.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.3, characteristic polynomial and freeness of A_J
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For J⊆[n], define the arrangement A_J⊆AeΦ+ by A_J={H_{x_j−x_i}: j∉J and i>j} ∪ {H_{x_j}: j∉J}. With st(J)_i as in the J-staircase recursion, the characteristic polynomial of A_J is χ_{A_J}(t)=∏_{i=1}^n (t−st(J)_i). In particular, A_J is supersolvable and free with exponents st(J)_1,...,st(J)_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The characteristic polynomial is computed by the finite-fields method. For a sufficiently large prime p, χ_{A_J}(p) equals the number of points (a1,...,an)∈(Z/pZ)^n avoiding all hyperplanes of A_J. Choose the coordinates sequentially. At step i, the forbidden values are 0 if i∉J, together with the previously chosen a_j for j<i and j∉J. The number of forbidden values is exactly st(J)_i, so the number of choices is p−st(J)_i and the total count is ∏_i(p−st(J)_i). Supersolvability can be checked from the associated chordal graph, and freeness follows from supersolvability; the roots of the characteristic polynomial match the exponents.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.4, colon quotients as Solomon-Terao algebras for A_J
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every J⊆[n], with f_J=∏_{j∈J} x_j ∏_{i=j+1}^n (x_j−x_i) and A_J={H_{x_j−x_i}: j∉J and i>j} ∪ {H_{x_j}: j∉J}, one has ST(A_J,i)=S/((S^{S_n}_+):f_J), where i:Der(S)→S sends every ∂_r to 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The missing hyperplanes of A_J inside AeΦ+ are exactly the coordinate hyperplanes H_{x_j} with j∈J and the difference hyperplanes H_{x_j−x_i} with j∈J and i>j. Therefore β_{A_J}=∏_{H∉A_J} α_H equals f_J up to the fixed sign convention. Lemma 5.3 gives that A_J is free, so Proposition 5.2(3) applies and gives iA_J=(S^{S_n}_+):f_J. Taking quotients yields the stated equality of algebras.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.5 and Corollary 5.6, explicit regular sequence generating (S^{S_n}_+):f_J
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For J⊆[n], a homogeneous free basis ρ_1^J,...,ρ_n^J of Der(A_J) is given as follows. If i∉J, then ρ_i^J=Σ_{k=i}^n (∏_{j∉J, j<i} (x_j−x_k)) x_k ∂_k. If i∈J, then ρ_i^J=(∏_{j∉J, j<i} (x_j−x_i)) ∂_i. Define g_{J,i}=i(ρ_i^J), where i(∂_k)=1; explicitly, if i∉J then g_{J,i}=Σ_{k=i}^n (∏_{j∉J, j<i}(x_j−x_k))x_k, while if i∈J then g_{J,i}=∏_{j∉J, j<i}(x_j−x_i). If 1∈J then (S^{S_n}_+):f_J=S. If 1∉J then (S^{S_n}_+):f_J is generated by the regular sequence g_{J,1},...,g_{J,n}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the derivation basis, the proof again uses Saito's criterion. The degree of ρ_i^J is st(J)_i, so the degrees sum to |A_J| by Lemma 5.3. The derivations are triangular in the partial derivatives because ρ_i^J only involves ∂_i,...,∂_n and has nonzero ∂_i coefficient. Divisibility by the defining forms of A_J is checked case by case: for coordinate forms x_j with j∉J, ρ_i^J(x_j) is either zero or visibly divisible by x_j; for difference forms x_j−x_l with j∉J and j<l, the image is either a difference of two products that vanishes when x_j=x_l, or contains x_j−x_l as a displayed factor, or is zero. Thus ρ_i^J∈Der(A_J). Applying the map i to this free basis gives generators of iA_J. Theorem 5.4 identifies iA_J with the colon ideal. If 1∉J the degrees are positive and the quotient is finite-dimensional, so the generators form a regular sequence; if 1∈J the colon ideal is the unit ideal by the Rhoades-Wilson dichotomy, equivalently by Lemma 5.1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.1, short exact sequence of Solomon-Terao algebras
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A⊆AeΦ+ and H=ker(α)∈A. Assume A, A\H, and A^H satisfy the addition-deletion freeness and exponent hypotheses: their exponent multisets differ by replacing one exponent e_k of A by e_k−1 for A\H and deleting e_k for A^H. Then, for i:Der(S)→S with i(∂_r)=1, the restricted Solomon-Terao algebra satisfies ST(A^H,i)=S/(iA+(α)), under the natural identification of the coordinate ring of H with S/(α). Moreover there is a short exact sequence 0→ST(A\H,i) --multiply by α--> ST(A,i) → ST(A^H,i)→0, where the last map is induced by the quotient S→S/(α).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The restriction map η_H:Der(A)→Der(A^H) from the derivation exact sequence is compatible with the quotient map π:S→S/(α) and the maps i, giving a commutative diagram Der(A)→Der(A^H) and S→S/(α). Hence i(Der(A^H))=(iA+(α))/(α), which proves ST(A^H,i)=S/(iA+(α)). The standard colon exact sequence gives 0→S/(iA:α) --multiply by α--> S/iA → S/(iA+(α))→0. Lemma 3.4 identifies iA:α with i(A\H), so the left term is ST(A\H,i), yielding the stated short exact sequence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 6.2, monomial basis for Solomon-Terao algebras of essential southwest arrangements
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A⊆AeΦ+ be an essential southwest arrangement in K^n, and let h(A)=(h1,...,hn) with h_i=|A∩{H_{0,i},H_{1,i},...,H_{i−1,i}}|. Then the monomials {x1^{a1}...xn^{an}: 0≤a_i<h_i for all i} descend to a K-basis of ST(A,i)=S/i(Der(A)), where i(∂_r)=1 for every r.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is a double induction on n and |A|. The cases n=1 and |A|=n are immediate: for |A|=n essentiality forces a linear complete intersection, so the quotient is K and h_i=1 for all i. In the inductive step, choose p=max{k:H_{0,k}∈A}. By Lemma 4.10 and Lemma 6.1 there is an exact sequence 0→ST(A\H_{0,p},i) --multiply by x_p--> ST(A,i) → ST(A^{H_{0,p}},i)→0. Lemma 4.2 identifies the h-sequences of the deletion and restriction. If h_p>1, the deletion has h_p decreased by 1 and the restriction has the p-th entry removed; by induction, monomials in ST(A,i) are exactly the union of monomials not involving x_p coming from the restriction and x_p times monomials from the deletion, giving exponents 0≤a_p<h_p. If h_p=1, the deletion is nonessential, so Proposition 5.2 gives ST(A\H_{0,p},i)=0; hence ST(A,i)≅ST(A^{H_{0,p}},i) via setting x_p=0, and the allowed monomials are precisely those with a_p=0. This proves both spanning and linear independence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 7.1, monomial basis for the colon quotients S/((S^{S_n}_+):f_J)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For J⊆[n], define the J-staircase st(J) by st(J)_1=1 if 1∉J and 0 if 1∈J, and st(J)_i=st(J)_{i−1}+1 for i>1 with i∉J and st(J)_i=st(J)_{i−1} for i>1 with i∈J. Let M(J)={x1^{a1}...xn^{an}: 0≤a_i<st(J)_i for all i}. Then M(J) descends to a K-basis of S/((S^{S_n}_+):f_J), where f_J=∏_{j∈J} x_j ∏_{i=j+1}^n (x_j−x_i).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The quotient dimension agrees with |M(J)| by the Rhoades-Wilson Hilbert-series result, equivalently by the complete-intersection description in Corollary 5.6, so it suffices to prove linear independence. Define the modified product f̃_J=∏_{j∈J}∏_{i=j+1}^n(x_j−x_i), omitting the coordinate factors x_j. Let A= {H_{j,i}: j∉J, i=j+1,...,n} ∪ {H_{x1},...,H_{xn}} = A_J ∪ {H_{x_k}:k∈J}. This A is an essential southwest arrangement, and β_A=f̃_J, so Proposition 5.2 gives S/((S^{S_n}_+):f̃_J)=ST(A,i). Multiplication by ∏_{j∈J}x_j gives an injection S/((S^{S_n}_+):f_J) → S/((S^{S_n}_+):f̃_J). The h-sequence of A satisfies h_k=st(J)_k for k∉J and h_k=st(J)_k+1 for k∈J. By Theorem 6.2, monomials with exponents b_k<st(J)_k for k∉J and b_k<st(J)_k+1 for k∈J form a basis of the target. Multiplying an element x^a∈M(J) by ∏_{j∈J}x_j shifts exactly the exponents indexed by J up by 1, producing distinct target basis monomials; hence the original M(J) is linearly independent in the source.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 7.2, Sagan-Swanson superspace Artin basis
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Ω=K[x1,...,xn]⊗exterior(θ1,...,θn), let SR=Ω/I be the superspace coinvariant ring, and for J⊆[n] let θ_J be the product of θ_j over j∈J in increasing order. With M(J) as in Theorem 7.1, the set M=⊔_{J⊆[n]} M(J) θ_J descends to a K-basis of SR.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply the Rhoades-Wilson transfer principle, Theorem 1.2, using the bases B(J)=M(J) supplied by Theorem 7.1 for all commutative colon quotients S/((S^{S_n}_+):f_J). The resulting disjoint union over fermionic supports J is exactly the Sagan-Swanson set of superspace Artin monomials.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 9.1, auxiliary facts about the classical coinvariant ideal
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let δ_n=∏_{1≤i<j≤n}(x_i−x_j) be the Vandermonde determinant and let the differential action of S on itself be f⊙g=f(∂_1,...,∂_n)g. Then: 1. f∈(S^{S_n}_+) if and only if f⊙δ_n=0. 2. If A⊆[n] and λ is a partition with λ_1>n−|A|, then the Schur polynomial s_λ(A), obtained by setting variables outside A to zero, lies in (S^{S_n}_+). 3. If [n]=A⊔B, then h_d(A)≡(−1)^d e_d(B) modulo (S^{S_n}_+) for every d≥0, where h_d is complete homogeneous and e_d is elementary symmetric.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Part 1 is Steinberg's inverse-system description of the coinvariant ideal. For part 2, first handle one-row partitions using h_d(A)=h_d(A∪{a})−x_a h_{d−1}(A∪{a}) and h_d([n])∈(S^{S_n}_+) for d>0, inducting downward on n−|A|; then use the Jacobi-Trudi determinant for general λ and expand along the first row. For part 3, work in the coinvariant ring R=S/(S^{S_n}_+) and adjoin a variable t. Since ∏_{i=1}^n(1−x_i t)=1 in R[t] and each 1−x_i t is invertible, multiplying by ∏_{a∈A}(1−x_a t)^{−1} gives ∏_{b∈B}(1−x_b t)=∏_{a∈A}(1−x_a t)^{−1}; comparing coefficients of t^d gives the congruence.

proof_techniques.name: Transfer from superspace to commutative colon quotients
proof_techniques.description: The paper avoids direct Grobner or straightening analysis in the supercommutative ring Ω by using the Rhoades-Wilson transfer principle. The θ-support J of a superspace monomial determines a polynomial f_J, and the bosonic part of the quotient in that θ-sector is controlled by the colon ideal (S^{S_n}_+):f_J. Thus the problem of producing a basis of SR is reduced to producing compatible bases of ordinary commutative quotients of S.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For hook coefficients, one can first pass through the superspace model and then count basis elements by fermionic degree and bosonic degree. This bypasses the need to manipulate the full multigraded m-set coinvariant algebra directly.

proof_techniques.name: Model colon ideals as Solomon-Terao ideals
proof_techniques.description: For a subarrangement A of the augmented braid arrangement AeΦ+, the paper studies the Solomon-Terao ideal iA=i(Der(A)), where i sends every partial derivative ∂_r to 1. Proposition 5.2 proves that, when A is free, iA is the colon ideal (S^{S_n}_+):β_A, where β_A is the product of the linear forms corresponding to hyperplanes missing from A. This converts algebraic colon-ideal questions into freeness and derivation-basis questions for arrangements.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The hook-relevant quotients S/((S^{S_n}_+):f_J) are exactly ST(A_J,i). This gives both their Hilbert series and explicit generators, and provides a geometric route to monomial bases.

proof_techniques.name: Southwest arrangements and deletion-restriction induction
proof_techniques.description: The paper introduces southwest subarrangements of AeΦ+, whose defining hyperplanes are closed under moving southwest in the augmented type A root poset. These arrangements are supersolvable and free, have explicit h-sequences, and are closed under deleting the maximal coordinate hyperplane and restricting to it. The deletion and restriction h-sequences differ in exactly the way needed for an induction on monomial exponent bounds.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The final proof of the M(J) basis embeds the quotient of interest into a quotient for a southwest arrangement. The recursive structure explains why the J-staircase bounds are the correct combinatorial constraints.

proof_techniques.name: Exact sequences of Solomon-Terao algebras
proof_techniques.description: From Terao addition-deletion, the paper obtains a short exact sequence of derivation modules and then pushes it through the map i:Der(S)→S. This yields 0→ST(A\H,i) --×α--> ST(A,i) → ST(A^H,i)→0. The sequence separates monomials in ST(A,i) into those divisible by α and those surviving after setting α=0.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For α=x_p, this exact sequence gives a clean induction proving that exponent bounds are 0≤a_p<h_p. Similar exact sequences could be used to prove other monomial-counting interpretations without constructing a Grobner basis.

proof_techniques.name: Triangular derivation bases via Saito's criterion
proof_techniques.description: For southwest arrangements and for A_J, the paper writes down derivations ρ_i whose coefficient matrix is triangular with nonzero diagonal entries. Saito's criterion reduces freeness to checking that each ρ_i sends every defining linear form into its own principal ideal and that the sum of degrees is the number of hyperplanes. The divisibility checks are elementary case analyses using the southwest condition or the defining pattern of A_J.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The explicit derivations yield the concrete regular sequence g_{J,1},...,g_{J,n} generating the hook-sector colon ideals. This can be used if a solver wants an algebra presentation in addition to a counting basis.

proof_techniques.name: Poincare-duality colon comparison
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 3.4 identifies one Solomon-Terao ideal as a colon of another by combining two facts: multiplication by a missing product of hyperplane forms sends Der(B) into Der(A), and the corresponding quotients are Poincare duality complete intersections with socle degrees differing by the degree of that missing product. The Poincare-duality colon criterion then forces the inclusion of ideals to be equality.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is the technical bridge proving that arrangement-generated ideals are exactly the desired coinvariant colon ideals, not merely subideals. It is useful whenever the hook-sector quotient is approached through a larger complete intersection.

proof_techniques.name: Cospanning criterion for products of roots
proof_techniques.description: The appendix proves that a product of augmented positive roots lies in the classical coinvariant ideal exactly when the omitted roots fail to span the dual space. The proof combines Schur-function congruences in partial variable sets for one direction and Steinberg's Vandermonde inverse-system criterion plus a forest-orientation construction for the other direction.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This criterion verifies nonvanishing of colon multipliers such as β_A and f_J modulo the coinvariant ideal. It helps justify injections and exclude degenerate cases in hook-sector computations.

proof_techniques.name: Finite-fields method for degrees and exponents
proof_techniques.description: For A_J, the characteristic polynomial is computed by counting points over a large finite field outside the arrangement. The coordinates are chosen in order; the number of forbidden values at step i is exactly st(J)_i. This gives χ_{A_J}(t)=∏_i(t−st(J)_i), matching the degrees of the regular sequence and the staircase Hilbert product.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This provides an independent combinatorial explanation of the factors ∏[st(J)_i]_q appearing in the hook-sector Hilbert series. It is a useful sanity check for any proposed hook coefficient formula.

proof_techniques.name: Remove coordinate factors, prove independence in a larger southwest quotient, then inject back
proof_techniques.description: The key move in Theorem 7.1 is to replace f_J by f̃_J, omitting the coordinate factors x_j for j∈J. The corresponding arrangement is southwest, so Theorem 6.2 gives a monomial basis for S/((S^{S_n}_+):f̃_J) with one extra allowed exponent in positions j∈J. Multiplication by ∏_{j∈J}x_j injects S/((S^{S_n}_+):f_J) into this larger quotient and sends the proposed M(J) monomials to distinct basis monomials.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This directly explains the combinatorics of the J-staircase basis: positions in J are shifted once by the injection, while all positions retain their original staircase bounds in the source quotient.

other_useful_info: Immediate consequence of Corollary 7.2: the bigraded Hilbert series of SR with q marking total x-degree and z marking θ-degree is counted by Hilb(SR;q,z)=Σ_{J⊆[n]} z^{|J|} ∏_{i=1}^n [st(J)_i]_q, where [r]_q=1+q+...+q^{r−1} for r>0 and [0]_q=0. Equivalently, the coefficient of q^d z^r is the number of pairs (J,(a1,...,an)) with J⊆[n], |J|=r, 0≤a_i<st(J)_i, and Σ_i a_i=d. The paper emphasizes that its transfer principle is non-equivariant; it proves Hilbert-series and basis statements, but not the conjectural Sn-isotypic basis of Kelvin Chan. Section 8 points to possible extensions to other reflection types and notes that analogous superspace coinvariant bases in type B remain conjectural in related work of Sagan-Swanson.
why_relevant: Gives the first explicit monomial basis for the superspace coinvariant ring, proving the Sagan-Swanson basis conjecture. This basis turns the Hilbert-series data needed for hook coefficients into an explicit combinatorial counting problem.

title: Diagonal Supersymmetry for Coinvariant Rings
overall_summary: The paper proves a diagonal supersymmetry theorem for bosonic-fermionic coinvariant rings R_G^(k,j), where k sets of commuting variables and j sets of anticommuting variables are acted on diagonally by a finite group G ⊂ GL(n). Its central result is that the multigraded character series decomposes in the super-Schur basis s_λ(q/u) with nonnegative integer coefficients independent of the numbers k and j of bosonic and fermionic variable sets. The proof uses the natural U(gl(k|j)) action by superderivations, Howe duality for Sym(C^{k|j} ⊗ C^n), semisimplicity after restricting GL(n)-modules to G, and specialization identities for super Schur functions. For the symmetric group, applying the Frobenius characteristic gives universal coefficients c_{λµ}, and pairing with h_(1^n) gives universal Hilbert-series coefficients c_λ. The most relevant bridge to hook-shaped ordinary Schur coefficients is the case R_n^(1,1): in one bosonic and one fermionic variable, all non-hook super Schur functions vanish and s_(a,1^b)(q/u) = q^a u^b + q^(a-1) u^(b+1), so known formulas for Hilb(R_n^(1,1); q; u), especially the Rhoades-Wilson q-Stirling formula cited in the paper, can be used to extract the universal hook coefficients.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Super Schur definition and identities, Equations (12), (14)-(16)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For finite alphabets q = (q_1,...,q_k) and u = (u_1,...,u_j), the paper uses the super Schur function s_λ(q/u) defined by s_λ(q/u) = ∑_{ν⊆λ} s_ν(q) s_{λ'/ν'}(u), where λ' and ν' are conjugate partitions and s_{λ'/ν'} is a skew Schur function. These functions satisfy cancellation: s_λ(q_1,...,q_{k-1},q_k/u_1,...,u_{j-1},u_j) evaluated at u_j = -q_k equals s_λ(q_1,...,q_{k-1}/u_1,...,u_{j-1}). They also satisfy restriction: setting q_k = 0 gives s_λ(q_1,...,q_{k-1}/u_1,...,u_j), and setting u_j = 0 gives s_λ(q_1,...,q_k/u_1,...,u_{j-1}).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites these as standard properties of super Schur functions, referring to Macdonald and related super Schur literature. The cancellation identity is the key supersymmetric property: an even variable and an odd variable cancel when the odd variable is specialized to the negative of the even variable. The restriction identities are ordinary alphabet restrictions and are used to compare character expansions after deleting one bosonic or fermionic variable set.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.1, (gl(k|j), GL(n))-Howe duality
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let V = C^n and let C^{k|j} be the complex superspace with even dimension k and odd dimension j. For every d ≥ 0, there is an isomorphism of gl(k|j) × GL(n)-modules Sym^d(C^{k|j} ⊗ V) ≅ ⊕_{λ∈P(k,j,n), λ⊢d} U^λ_{k|j} ⊗ U^λ_n, where P(k,j,n) is the set of partitions λ with length(λ) ≤ n and λ_{k+1} ≤ j. Here U^λ_{k|j} is a simple gl(k|j)-module whose character is the super Schur function s_λ(q/u), and U^λ_n is the simple GL(n)-module whose character is the ordinary Schur function s_λ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The theorem is quoted from Howe duality. In the paper it is used as the initial semisimple decomposition of the full bosonic-fermionic polynomial algebra before quotienting by invariant generators. The action of gl(k|j) is on the C^{k|j} factor and the action of GL(n) is on V; these actions commute. Taking characters of the decomposition gives the super Cauchy identity and identifies the U(gl(k|j))-characters appearing in the polynomial algebra with super Schur functions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.2, composition factors in quotient modules
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be an associative algebra over C. If M is a finite-dimensional A-module and N is an A-submodule of M, then for any simple A-module S, the multiplicity of S in a composition series of the quotient M/N is less than or equal to the multiplicity of S in a composition series of M.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is an immediate consequence of the Jordan-Hölder theorem. A composition series for N can be refined with a composition series for M/N to obtain one for M, so every simple constituent of M/N occurs among the simple constituents of M with no larger multiplicity. The paper uses this to show that the coinvariant quotient cannot contain simple U(gl(k|j)) ⊗ C[G]-modules other than those already present in Sym^d(C^{k|j} ⊗ V).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.1, U(gl(k|j)) ⊗ C[G]-module decomposition of bosonic-fermionic coinvariant rings
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix a positive integer n and a finite group G ⊂ GL(V) with V = C^n. Define R_G^(k,j) = Sym(C^{k|j} ⊗ V) / ⟨Sym(C^{k|j} ⊗ V)^G_+⟩, where Sym(C^{k|j} ⊗ V)^G_+ is the subspace of positive-total-degree G-invariant elements and the angle brackets denote the ideal it generates. Let N_µ range over the simple C[G]-modules. For every fixed k and j, there is an isomorphism of U(gl(k|j)) ⊗ C[G]-modules R_G^(k,j) ≅ ⊕_{λ∈P(k,j,n)} ⊕_µ (U^λ_{k|j} ⊗ N_µ)^{⊕ c_{λµ}}, for some nonnegative integer multiplicities c_{λµ}. Here P(k,j,n) is the set of partitions λ with length(λ) ≤ n and λ_{k+1} ≤ j, and U^λ_{k|j} has character s_λ(q/u).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper first identifies the bosonic-fermionic polynomial algebra with Sym(C^{k|j} ⊗ V). The Lie superalgebra gl(k|j) acts by superderivations, explicitly by operators such as E_{a,b} = ∑_p x_p^(a) ∂/∂x_p^(b), E_{a,d'} = ∑_p x_p^(a) ∂/∂θ_p^(d), E_{c',b} = ∑_p θ_p^(c) ∂/∂x_p^(b), and E_{c',d'} = ∑_p θ_p^(c) ∂/∂θ_p^(d). The group G acts on the V factor and trivially on C^{k|j}; the G-action commutes with the gl(k|j)-action. On the total-degree d part, Howe duality decomposes Sym^d(C^{k|j} ⊗ V) as a sum of U^λ_{k|j} ⊗ U^λ_n. Restrict each U^λ_n from GL(V) to G; since G is finite over C, the restriction is semisimple, say Res_G U^λ_n = ⊕_µ N_µ^{⊕ d_{λµ}}. Therefore Sym^d(C^{k|j} ⊗ V) decomposes as a finite direct sum of simple modules U^λ_{k|j} ⊗ N_µ. The positive-degree invariant subspace is stable under gl(k|j) because the gl-action commutes with G and preserves degree. Its generated ideal is the image of the multiplication map from Sym(C^{k|j} ⊗ V) ⊗ Sym(C^{k|j} ⊗ V)^G_+ to Sym(C^{k|j} ⊗ V), hence is also a U(gl(k|j)) ⊗ C[G]-submodule. Thus R_G^(k,j) is a quotient module. Applying Lemma 2.2 degree by degree shows that each homogeneous piece of the quotient has only the simple constituents already appearing in Sym^d(C^{k|j} ⊗ V), with finite nonnegative multiplicities. Summing over all total degrees gives the stated decomposition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.2, universal super-Schur character coefficients
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix a positive integer n and a finite group G ⊂ GL(n). For every partition λ with length(λ) ≤ n and for every irreducible character χ_µ of G, there exist nonnegative integers c_{λµ}, independent of k and j, such that for every pair of nonnegative integers k,j the multigraded character series of R_G^(k,j) is Char(R_G^(k,j); q; u) = ∑_{λ∈P(k,j,n)} ∑_{χ_µ∈IrrChar(G)} c_{λµ} s_λ(q/u) χ_µ. Here P(k,j,n) = {λ : length(λ) ≤ n and λ_{k+1} ≤ j}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Taking U(gl(k|j)) ⊗ C[G]-characters in Theorem 1.1 gives such an expansion for each fixed k,j, since the character of U^λ_{k|j} is s_λ(q/u). The key point is independence of k,j. The paper proves this by deleting variable sets. Setting the last bosonic variable set x^(k) to zero identifies the quotient R_G^(k,j) at q_k = 0 with R_G^(k-1,j). This uses two facts: the specialization of positive-degree invariant generators is exactly the positive-degree invariant ideal for the smaller polynomial algebra, and restricting the multiplication map to zero q_k-degree gives the smaller generated ideal. Therefore Char(R_G^(k-1,j); q_1,...,q_{k-1}; u) equals Char(R_G^(k,j); q_1,...,q_k; u) evaluated at q_k = 0. Comparing the super-Schur expansion after this specialization with the independently obtained expansion for R_G^(k-1,j), and using the restriction identity s_λ(...,q_k/u)|_{q_k=0} = s_λ(.../u), shows that any coefficient c_{λµ} appearing in both expansions is the same. The same argument with u_j = 0 deletes a fermionic variable set and proves equality across j. Iterating these adjacent restrictions proves that the coefficients are universal whenever the corresponding super Schur function is nonzero.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 1.3, Diagonal Supersymmetry for the symmetric group
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix a positive integer n. For the symmetric group S_n acting diagonally by permuting indices in each variable set, there exist nonnegative integers c_{λµ}, indexed by partitions λ with length(λ) ≤ n and partitions µ⊢n, such that for every k,j the multigraded Frobenius series of R_n^(k,j) is Frob(R_n^(k,j); q; u) = ∑_{λ∈P(k,j,n)} ∑_{µ⊢n} c_{λµ} s_λ(q/u) s_µ(z). The coefficients c_{λµ} do not depend on k or j.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is obtained from Theorem 1.2 by applying the Frobenius characteristic map to the S_n-characters in each multigraded component. Under the Frobenius characteristic, the irreducible character indexed by µ⊢n maps to the Schur function s_µ(z), where z is an auxiliary alphabet. The universality and nonnegativity of the coefficients are inherited from Theorem 1.2.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.5, universal Frobenius series for S_n
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For fixed n, there is a well-defined universal series Frob(R_n^(∞,∞); q; u) = ∑_{λ∈P(∞,∞,n)} ∑_{µ⊢n} c_{λµ} s_λ(q/u) s_µ(z), where P(∞,∞,n) consists of all partitions λ with length(λ) ≤ n. This series determines every coefficient c_{λµ} that can appear in the expansion of Frob(R_n^(k,j); q; u) for any finite or infinite k,j.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is essentially the restriction part of Theorem 1.2 applied uniformly. If k' ≤ k and j' ≤ j, then setting the extra q-variables and u-variables to zero specializes the expansion for R_n^(k,j) to the expansion for R_n^(k',j'). Hence a single coefficient system c_{λµ} controls all finite pairs k,j. The formal infinite-alphabet expression is therefore a convenient universal object encoding all possible finite specializations.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.1, recovering mixed Frobenius series from sufficiently many pure variables
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the symmetric group, fix n and suppose n ≤ k+j. Then the multigraded Frobenius series of R_n^(k,j) can be calculated from the multigraded Frobenius series of either the purely bosonic ring R_n^(k+j,0) or the purely fermionic ring R_n^(0,k+j). More explicitly, the same universal coefficients c_{λµ} occurring in Frob(R_n^(k,j); q; u) are determined by the Schur expansion of Frob(R_n^(k+j,0); q_1,...,q_{k+j}) = ∑_{λ∈P(k+j,0,n)} ∑_{µ⊢n} c_{λµ} s_λ(q_1,...,q_{k+j}) s_µ(z), or by the analogous purely fermionic expansion Frob(R_n^(0,k+j); u_1,...,u_{k+j}) = ∑_{λ∈P(0,k+j,n)} ∑_{µ⊢n} c_{λµ} s_{λ'}(u_1,...,u_{k+j}) s_µ(z).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Corollary 1.3, all mixed Frobenius series use the same universal coefficients, restricted to those λ for which s_λ(q/u) is nonzero. The cited result of Bergeron says that the multigraded Frobenius series for R_n^(k+j,0) is coefficient-stable once k+j ≥ n, so the purely bosonic expansion with k+j variables determines all c_{λµ} that can occur for length(λ) ≤ n. The fermionic case is analogous by conjugation of partitions in the super Schur functions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.1, Hilbert-series version of diagonal supersymmetry
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix a positive integer n. There exist nonnegative integers c_λ, indexed by partitions λ with length(λ) ≤ n and independent of k,j, such that for every k,j the multigraded Hilbert series of the symmetric-group bosonic-fermionic coinvariant ring satisfies Hilb(R_n^(k,j); q; u) = ∑_{λ∈P(k,j,n)} c_λ s_λ(q/u). Moreover, if c_{λµ} are the universal Frobenius coefficients from Corollary 1.3, then c_λ = ∑_{µ⊢n} c_{λµ} ⟨s_µ(z), h_(1^n)(z)⟩, where ⟨ , ⟩ is the Hall inner product.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The Hilbert series is obtained from the Frobenius series by pairing with h_(1^n)(z): Hilb(R_n^(k,j); q; u) = ⟨Frob(R_n^(k,j); q; u), h_(1^n)(z)⟩. Substitute the universal Frobenius expansion from Corollary 1.3, move the inner product through the finite or locally finite sum, and collect the coefficient of each s_λ(q/u). The resulting coefficient is c_λ = ∑_µ c_{λµ} ⟨s_µ,h_(1^n)⟩. The summands are nonnegative integers and do not depend on k,j, giving the claimed universal Hilbert coefficients.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.4, super Schur functions in one bosonic and one fermionic variable
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let q be one bosonic variable and u one fermionic variable. If λ is a hook partition of the form λ = (a,1^b) with a ≥ 1 and b ≥ 0, then s_λ(q/u) = q^a u^b + q^(a-1) u^(b+1). If λ = ∅, then s_λ(q/u) = 1. If λ is not contained in a hook shape, then s_λ(q/u) = 0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper says this follows by direct calculation from the definition s_λ(q/u) = ∑_{ν⊆λ} s_ν(q) s_{λ'/ν'}(u). With only one q-variable, s_ν(q) is nonzero only for one-row partitions ν. With only one u-variable in the skew Schur factor, s_{λ'/ν'}(u) is nonzero only when the skew shape λ'/ν' is compatible with a one-letter semistandard filling. For a hook λ = (a,1^b), exactly two terms contribute, giving q^a u^b and q^(a-1) u^(b+1). If λ is not a hook in the sense λ_2 > 1 after the first row, no such contributing terms exist for k=j=1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Rhoades-Wilson formula for Hilb(R_n^(1,1)), Equation (50)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The paper cites the theorem of Rhoades and Wilson, conjectured earlier by Sagan and Swanson, that the Hilbert series of the superspace coinvariant ring R_n^(1,1) is Hilb(R_n^(1,1); q; u) = ∑_{d=0}^n [d]_q! Stir_q(n,d) u^(n-d). Here [d]_q = 1+q+...+q^(d-1), [d]_q! = [d]_q [d-1]_q ... [1]_q, and the q-Stirling numbers Stir_q(n,d) are defined by Stir_q(n,d) = [d]_q Stir_q(n-1,d) + Stir_q(n-1,d-1) with initial conditions Stir_q(0,d) = δ_{d,0}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper does not prove this formula; it invokes it as a known result from Rhoades-Wilson. In the paper it is used together with the super Schur cancellation identity to rederive a Sagan-Swanson specialization identity. For extracting hook-shaped universal Hilbert coefficients, the important point is that this formula gives every coefficient of u^b in Hilb(R_n^(1,1); q; u) explicitly as a q-Stirling polynomial.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.5, sign-isotypic hook expansion in R_n^(1,1)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For n ≥ 1, define g_{i,d,n} as the coefficient of q^i in the Gaussian binomial [n-2 choose d]_q; equivalently, g_{i,d,n} is the number of partitions of i contained in the d by n-2-d rectangle. Then the sign-character component of the Frobenius series of R_n^(1,1) has the super-Schur expansion ⟨Frob(R_n^(1,1); q; u), s_(1^n)(z)⟩ = ∑_{d=0}^{n-1} ∑_{i≥0} g_{i,d,n} s_(binom(n-d,2)+i,1^d)(q/u).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The starting point is the Swanson-Wallach formula, written in the paper as ⟨Frob(R_n^(1,1); q; u), s_(1^n)(z)⟩ = ∑_{d=0}^{n-1} q^binom(n-d,2) [n-1 choose d]_q u^d. The proposed right-hand side is expanded using Lemma 4.4: s_(A,1^d)(q/u) = q^A u^d + q^(A-1) u^(d+1). Substituting A = binom(n-d,2)+i and summing over i with weights g_{i,d,n} turns the coefficient of u^d into q^binom(n-d,2)([n-2 choose d]_q + q^(n-d-1)[n-2 choose d-1]_q). The q-Pascal identity identifies this expression with q^binom(n-d,2)[n-1 choose d]_q, matching the Swanson-Wallach formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 4.6, universal Frobenius coefficients for hooks in the sign character
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let c_{λµ} be the universal Frobenius coefficients in Frob(R_n^(∞,∞); q; u) = ∑_{λ∈P(∞,∞,n)} ∑_{µ⊢n} c_{λµ} s_λ(q/u) s_µ(z). If λ is contained in a hook shape, then c_{λ,(1^n)} = g_{i,d,n} if there exist i,d ≥ 0 such that λ = (binom(n-d,2)+i,1^d), and c_{λ,(1^n)} = 0 otherwise. Here g_{i,d,n} is the coefficient of q^i in [n-2 choose d]_q.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply Corollary 1.3 to the particular case k=j=1 and take the coefficient of the sign Schur function s_(1^n)(z). Since Lemma 4.4 says only hook-shaped λ contribute to s_λ(q/u) in one bosonic and one fermionic variable, the super-Schur expansion in Proposition 4.5 uniquely identifies the universal coefficients c_{λ,(1^n)} for hook λ. All hook λ not appearing in that expansion have coefficient zero in the sign component.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.2, cancellation for Frobenius and Hilbert series
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For any n ≥ 1 and any integer m with 0 ≤ m ≤ min(k,j), the Frobenius and Hilbert series of R_n^(k,j) satisfy simultaneous cancellation of m bosonic-fermionic variable pairs. Specifically, Frob(R_n^(k,j); q; u) evaluated at q_k = -u_j, q_{k-1} = -u_{j-1}, ..., q_{k-m+1} = -u_{j-m+1} equals Frob(R_n^(k-m,j-m); q_1,...,q_{k-m}; u_1,...,u_{j-m}). The same specialization identity holds for Hilb(R_n^(k,j); q; u).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Insert the universal Frobenius expansion from Corollary 1.3. Applying the super Schur cancellation identity once replaces s_λ(q_1,...,q_k/u_1,...,u_j) at u_j = -q_k by s_λ(q_1,...,q_{k-1}/u_1,...,u_{j-1}). Repeating m times gives the Frobenius identity. The Hilbert identity follows by applying the Hall inner product with h_(1^n)(z), equivalently by using Proposition 5.1 and the same super Schur cancellation.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 5.3, q-Stirling cancellation identity
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For n ≥ 0, the q-Stirling polynomials appearing in Hilb(R_n^(1,1)) satisfy ∑_{d=0}^n [d]_q! Stir_q(n,d) (-q)^(n-d) = 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Proposition 5.2 with k=j=1, Hilb(R_n^(1,1); q; u) evaluated at u = -q equals Hilb(R_n^(0,0)) = 1. Substituting the Rhoades-Wilson formula Hilb(R_n^(1,1); q; u) = ∑_{d=0}^n [d]_q! Stir_q(n,d) u^(n-d) and then setting u = -q gives the identity. The paper notes that Sagan-Swanson originally proved this by a sign-reversing involution.

proof_techniques.name: Decompose before quotienting via super Howe duality
proof_techniques.description: The paper first treats the full bosonic-fermionic polynomial algebra Sym(C^{k|j} ⊗ V), where a clean representation-theoretic decomposition is available from (gl(k|j), GL(n))-Howe duality. After restricting the GL(n)-factor to a finite group G, semisimplicity of finite group representations gives a decomposition into simple U(gl(k|j)) ⊗ C[G]-modules. The invariant ideal defining the coinvariant ring is shown to be a submodule, so the quotient inherits only those simple constituents, with smaller multiplicities controlled by Jordan-Hölder.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This technique explains why Schur or super-Schur coefficients should be nonnegative representation multiplicities rather than just formal expansion coefficients. For the ordinary m-set Hilbert series, the j=0 specialization of this framework is the source of the universal Schur coefficients.

proof_techniques.name: Universality by deleting variable sets
proof_techniques.description: To prove independence of the coefficients from k and j, the paper specializes an entire bosonic or fermionic variable set to zero. On the algebra side, setting x^(k)=0 identifies R_G^(k,j) at q_k=0 with R_G^(k-1,j), and similarly setting θ^(j)=0 identifies R_G^(k,j) at u_j=0 with R_G^(k,j-1). On the symmetric-function side, super Schur functions satisfy matching restriction identities, so comparing the two expansions forces the same coefficient c_{λµ} to occur in all allowable k,j.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is the formal justification for computing a coefficient in a convenient superspace specialization and then transferring it back to the ordinary bosonic Schur expansion. In particular, hook coefficients can be read from the k=j=1 case even though the original ring has only commuting variables.

proof_techniques.name: Hook isolation in one even and one odd variable
proof_techniques.description: The case k=j=1 is special because s_λ(q/u) vanishes unless λ is a hook, while for λ = (a,1^b) one has the two-term formula s_(a,1^b)(q/u) = q^a u^b + q^(a-1) u^(b+1). The paper uses this explicitly in Lemma 4.4 and in Proposition 4.5 to convert a bigraded polynomial into a super-Schur expansion indexed by hooks.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is the main bridge to hook-shaped coefficients: the universal Hilbert coefficients c_(a,1^b) are exactly the coefficients in the super-Schur expansion of Hilb(R_n^(1,1); q; u), because no non-hook λ contributes in one even and one odd variable.

proof_techniques.name: Triangular extraction from the R_n^(1,1) Hilbert series
proof_techniques.description: Although the paper does not state the full triangular extraction as a separate proposition, it is the same mechanism used in the sign-character calculation. If Hilb(R_n^(1,1); q; u) = ∑_b H_b(q) u^b and the universal Hilbert expansion is c_∅ + ∑_{a≥1,b≥0} c_(a,1^b)(q^a u^b + q^(a-1) u^(b+1)), then the hook coefficient polynomials C_b(q) = ∑_{a≥1} c_(a,1^b) q^a satisfy H_b(q) = δ_{b,0} c_∅ + C_b(q) + q^(-1) C_{b-1}(q), with C_{-1}(q)=0. Since c_∅=1 and the Rhoades-Wilson formula gives H_b(q) explicitly in terms of q-Stirling numbers, the hook coefficients are recoverable recursively.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This gives a concrete route to the desired combinatorial interpretation: interpret the coefficient of q^a in the recursively defined polynomial C_b(q), where H_b(q) = [n-b]_q! Stir_q(n,n-b). Any known combinatorial model for the q-Stirling Hilbert formula may potentially be refined by this triangular subtraction to model c_(a,1^b).

proof_techniques.name: Pairing Frobenius series with h_(1^n) to get Hilbert coefficients
proof_techniques.description: For S_n, the paper repeatedly uses Hilb(R_n^(k,j); q; u) = ⟨Frob(R_n^(k,j); q; u), h_(1^n)(z)⟩. This turns universal Frobenius coefficients c_{λµ} into universal Hilbert coefficients c_λ by the formula c_λ = ∑_µ c_{λµ} ⟨s_µ,h_(1^n)⟩.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a proof first obtains information about Frobenius coefficients for hooks, this pairing tells how to aggregate over S_n-isotypic components to obtain the Hilbert-series coefficient asked for in the original problem.

proof_techniques.name: Boson-fermion cancellation specialization
proof_techniques.description: The super Schur cancellation identity implies that specializing an odd grading variable to the negative of an even grading variable removes both variables from the Frobenius or Hilbert series. The paper applies this to show Hilb(R_n^(1,1); q; -q)=1 and to relate conjectural formulas for larger superspace coinvariant rings to known formulas for smaller ones.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Cancellation identities can be used as consistency checks on any proposed hook-coefficient formula. In the hook extraction recurrence, the endpoint identity C_{n-1}=0 and the q-Stirling specialization ∑_d [d]_q! Stir_q(n,d)(-q)^(n-d)=1 reflect the same cancellation phenomenon.

other_useful_info: Combining Proposition 5.1, Lemma 4.4, and the Rhoades-Wilson formula gives an explicit algebraic extraction scheme for universal Hilbert hook coefficients. Let H_b(q) be the coefficient of u^b in Hilb(R_n^(1,1); q; u), so H_b(q) = [n-b]_q! Stir_q(n,n-b) for 0 ≤ b ≤ n. Let C_b(q) = ∑_{a≥1} c_(a,1^b) q^a, with C_{-1}(q)=0 and c_∅=1. Then H_b(q) = δ_{b,0} + C_b(q) + q^(-1) C_{b-1}(q), so C_0(q) = [n]_q! - 1 and, for b ≥ 1, C_b(q) = [n-b]_q! Stir_q(n,n-b) - q^(-1) C_{b-1}(q). Thus c_(a,1^b) is the coefficient of q^a in C_b(q). Equivalently, C_b(q) = ∑_{r=0}^b (-q^(-1))^(b-r) [n-r]_q! Stir_q(n,n-r) - (-q^(-1))^b. The paper itself cites Rhoades-Wilson for the q-Stirling Hilbert formula and Sagan-Swanson for the related cancellation identity; it does not provide a standalone combinatorial model for the resulting C_b(q) coefficients. The paper also records that Swanson-Wallach give the sign-character formula used in Proposition 4.5, while Kim-Rhoades and Lentfer give formulas for the two-fermion case R_n^(0,2), which determine universal Frobenius coefficients for partitions with all parts at most 2.
why_relevant: Proves the diagonal supersymmetry theorem: bosonic-fermionic coinvariant rings have universal super-Schur coefficients independent of the numbers of bosonic and fermionic variable sets. Taking one bosonic and one fermionic set isolates exactly hook-shaped Schur coefficients, making this a direct bridge from the original coefficients c_lambda(n) to superspace coinvariants.

title: A Proof of the Fields Conjectures
overall_summary: The paper proves the Fields Conjectures for the type A superspace coinvariant ring SR_n = Omega_n / SI_n, where Omega_n is the algebra of polynomial differential forms with commuting variables x_i and anticommuting variables theta_i. The main results identify SR_n, as an ungraded S_n-module, with the sign-twisted permutation representation on ordered set partitions, and identify its bigraded Frobenius characteristic as sum_{k=1}^n z^{n-k} C_{n,k}(x;q), where C_{n,k} is the Delta-Conjecture symmetric function with ordered multiset partition interpretations. The core technique is to test representations by parabolic antisymmetrizers epsilon_mu, obtain upper bounds from an explicit monomial basis of SR_n, and obtain matching lower bounds inside the inverse system SH_n using newly constructed parabolic D-operators with controlled fermionic leading terms. For the original hook-coefficient problem, the most relevant content is that the one-bosonic/one-fermionic superspace coinvariant ring gives explicit q,z Hilbert data and ordered-set-partition models for fermionic degrees; under the standard hook/superization bridge, these are the kinds of objects that encode hook Schur coefficients.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Equations (2.5)-(2.10): equivalent descriptions of C_{n,k}(x;q)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For integers 1 <= k <= n, the paper recalls the symmetric function C_{n,k}(x;q) in several equivalent forms. In the Schur basis, C_{n,k}(x;q) = sum_T q^{maj(T)+binom(n-k,2)-(n-k)des(T)} [des(T) choose n-k]_q s_{shape(T)}, where T ranges over standard Young tableaux with n boxes, des(T) is the number of descents, maj(T) is the major index, and [a choose b]_q is the q-binomial coefficient. It also has a Hall-Littlewood expansion C_{n,k}(x;q) = sum_{lambda partition n, lambda'_1 = k} q^{sum_i (i-1)(lambda_i-1)} [k; m_1(lambda),...,m_k(lambda)]_q omega Htilde_lambda(x;q), where m_i(lambda) is the multiplicity of i as a part of lambda, omega is the standard involution sending s_lambda to s_{lambda'}, and Htilde_lambda(x;q) is a Hall-Littlewood symmetric function. Combinatorially, if OMP_{n,k} is the set of ordered multiset partitions with total size n and k blocks, no repeated entry within a block, and x^mu records entry multiplicities, then C_{n,k}(x;q) = sum_{mu in OMP_{n,k}} q^{inv(mu)} x^mu = sum q^{maj(mu)} x^mu = sum q^{dinv(mu)} x^mu = sum q^{minimaj(mu)} x^mu. Finally, C_{n,k}(x;q) = Delta'_{e_{k-1}} e_n evaluated at t=0 in the modified Macdonald eigenoperator notation.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: These identities are not proved in this paper; they are recalled from prior work. The Schur and Hall-Littlewood formulas are cited from the Delta-Conjecture literature, the ordered multiset partition formulas combine results of Haglund-Remmel-Wilson, Rhoades, and Wilson, and the Macdonald-operator formula is cited from results proving the t=0 Delta Conjecture. Their role in the paper is to give explicit symmetric-function targets for the Frobenius characteristic of SR_n and to provide combinatorial models once Theorem 5.2 identifies these functions with fermionic pieces of SR_n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem A / Fields Conjecture 1 component: bigraded Hilbert series of SR_n
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let SR_n be the superspace coinvariant ring SR_n = Omega_n / SI_n, where Omega_n = F[x_1,...,x_n] tensor exterior(theta_1,...,theta_n), S_n acts diagonally by permuting subscripts, and SI_n is the ideal generated by S_n-invariants with vanishing constant term, equivalently by e_1,...,e_n,de_1,...,de_n. If q tracks x-degree and z tracks theta-degree, then Hilb(SR_n;q,z) = sum_{k=1}^n z^{n-k} [k]!_q Stirq(n,k). Here [k]!_q is the q-factorial and Stirq(n,k) is determined by Stirq(0,0)=1, Stirq(0,k)=0 for k>0, and Stirq(n,k)=Stirq(n-1,k-1)+[k]_q Stirq(n-1,k). In particular dim_F SR_n = #OP_n, the number of ordered set partitions of [n].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper states this as Fields Conjecture 1 and notes that it was already proved by Rhoades-Wilson. The present paper proves the full Fields Conjectures, and Remark 4.20 explains that the new D-operator lower-bound method, specialized to mu=(1^n), gives a simpler route to the lower bound in the Hilbert-series proof when combined with the known upper bound. The formula is also compatible with Theorem 5.2 because the Frobenius characteristic of fermionic degree n-k is C_{n,k}(x;q), whose Hilbert specialization gives [k]!_q Stirq(n,k).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.2: parabolic antisymmetrizers compute e_mu^perp of Frobenius characteristics
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let V be a finite-dimensional S_n-module over a characteristic-zero field. For a partition mu=(mu_1,...,mu_p) of n, let S_mu = S_{mu_1} x ... x S_{mu_p} be the corresponding parabolic subgroup of S_n, let epsilon_mu = sum_{w in S_mu} sign(w) w in the group algebra, and let e_mu = e_{mu_1}...e_{mu_p} be the elementary symmetric function. Then e_mu^perp(Frob(V)) = dim_F(epsilon_mu V), where e_mu^perp is adjoint to multiplication by e_mu under the Hall inner product.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By linearity it is enough to treat V=V^lambda irreducible. Restrict V^lambda from S_n to S_mu and decompose it by the Littlewood-Richardson rule: Res V^lambda is a direct sum over tensor products V^{nu(1)} tensor ... tensor V^{nu(p)} with multiplicity c^lambda_{nu(1),...,nu(p)}. Applying epsilon_mu extracts the sign-isotypic part for S_mu, so only nu(j)=(1^{mu_j}) contributes. Thus dim epsilon_mu V^lambda = c^lambda_{(1^{mu_1}),...,(1^{mu_p})}. By the dual Pieri rule this coefficient is the Kostka number K_{lambda',mu}, and this is also <s_lambda,e_mu> = e_mu^perp s_lambda. Summing over irreducible multiplicities proves the lemma.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.1: superspace Artin-type monomial basis of SR_n
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For J subset [n], define the J-staircase st(J)=(st(J)_1,...,st(J)_n) recursively by st(J)_1=0 if 1 in J and st(J)_1=1 otherwise, and for i>1 by st(J)_i=st(J)_{i-1} if i in J and st(J)_i=st(J)_{i-1}+1 otherwise. Let A_n(J) be the set of monomials x_1^{a_1}...x_n^{a_n} with a_i < st(J)_i for every i. Let theta_J denote the product of theta_j for j in J in increasing order, and define A_n = disjoint union over J subset [n] of A_n(J) theta_J. Then A_n descends to an F-vector-space basis of SR_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The result is cited from Angarone-Commins-Karn-Murai-Rhoades. The cited proof uses Solomon-Terao algebras of hyperplane arrangements and a transfer principle from commutative colon-ideal bases to the supercommutative quotient. In this paper the theorem supplies the upper-bound side of the argument: applying a parabolic antisymmetrizer epsilon_mu to every monomial basis element gives a spanning set of epsilon_mu SR_n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.2: colon-ideal bases associated to theta supports
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let I_n=(e_1,...,e_n) be the ordinary type A coinvariant ideal in F[x_1,...,x_n]. For J subset [n], define f_J = product over j in J of x_j times product over j<i<=n of (x_j-x_i). Then the set A_n(J) from Theorem 3.1 descends to a vector-space basis of F[x_1,...,x_n] / (I_n : f_J), where (I_n : f_J) = {g in F[x_1,...,x_n] : g f_J in I_n}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is cited from Angarone-Commins-Karn-Murai-Rhoades. For J empty it reduces to Artin's basis of the usual coinvariant ring. For general J, the proof is not by direct Grobner theory; the cited work uses Solomon-Terao algebras to prove the basis theorem for the colon ideals. The present paper repeatedly uses this theorem to turn nonvanishing and independence questions for coefficients of harmonic forms into explicit staircase monomial conditions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.3: Operator Theorem for the superharmonic space SH_n
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let SH_n = SI_n^perp be the inverse system of the superspace coinvariant ideal SI_n under the differential-contraction action. Let delta_n = product_{1<=i<j<=n}(x_i-x_j) be the Vandermonde determinant. For j>=1 define the higher Euler operator d_j on Omega_n by d_j(f)=sum_{i=1}^n (partial^j f / partial x_i^j) theta_i. Then SH_n is the smallest linear subspace of Omega_n that contains delta_n, is closed under d_1,d_2,...,d_{n-1}, and is closed under the partial derivatives partial/partial x_1,...,partial/partial x_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The theorem is cited from Rhoades-Wilson and is the superspace analogue of Steinberg's theorem for ordinary coinvariant harmonics. This paper only needs the easier containment direction: SH_n contains delta_n and is closed under the higher Euler operators and polynomial differential operators. That direction ensures that the paper's constructed D-operator images of delta_n lie in SH_n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.3: antisymmetrized monomial spanning set for epsilon_mu SR_n
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix a partition mu=(mu_1,...,mu_p) of n. A signed partition with underlying mu is a sequence gamma=(gamma_1,...,gamma_p) with 0 <= gamma_j <= mu_j. Let J(mu,gamma) be the subset of [n] obtained by taking, in each consecutive block {mu_1+...+mu_{j-1}+1,...,mu_1+...+mu_j}, the last gamma_j entries. Define A_n(mu,gamma) to be the set of monomials x_1^{a_1}...x_n^{a_n} such that a_i < st(J(mu,gamma))_i for all i, and within each mu-block the exponents before the last gamma_j positions are strictly increasing while the exponents on the last gamma_j positions are weakly increasing. Then the union over all 0 <= gamma <= mu of epsilon_mu applied to A_n(mu,gamma) theta_{J(mu,gamma)} descends to a spanning set of epsilon_mu SR_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Start with the basis A_n of SR_n from Theorem 3.1 and apply epsilon_mu to each basis element. Any term that antisymmetrizes to zero can be discarded. If two basis monomials lie in the same S_mu-orbit, their antisymmetrizations differ at most by sign, so one canonical representative is kept. The chosen representative has theta support equal to J(mu,gamma) for a unique gamma and has the stated strict and weak inequalities on exponents; these inequalities precisely prevent zero antisymmetrization and avoid duplicate orbit representatives.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.4: counting antisymmetrized monomials by ordered set partitions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every partition mu of n, sum_{0<=gamma<=mu} #A_n(mu,gamma) = dim_F epsilon_mu(F[OP_n] tensor sign), where OP_n is the set of ordered set partitions of [n], F[OP_n] is its permutation representation, and sign is the one-dimensional sign representation of S_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Write mu=(mu_1,...,mu_p) and partition [n] into consecutive batches of sizes mu_j. The right side is the number of ordered set partitions in which the entries from each batch appear in increasing order from left to right. Such an ordered set partition is built batch by batch. At stage j, choose gamma_j entries of the j-th batch that are inserted into existing/new blocks as non-singleton additions and create mu_j-gamma_j new singleton positions. This gives the product formula product_j binom((mu_1-gamma_1)+...+(mu_j-gamma_j), mu_j-gamma_j) binom((mu_1-gamma_1)+...+(mu_{j-1}-gamma_{j-1})+mu_j-1, gamma_j), summed over gamma. A direct count of the exponent sequences defining A_n(mu,gamma) gives the same product for each gamma, proving the identity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Observation 4.8 and Lemma 4.9: factor matrices and Gale-vanishing determinants
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let mu=(mu_1,...,mu_p) be a partition of n with consecutive block endpoints b_j=mu_1+...+mu_j. For 0<=r<=n, let P_r(y,mu) be the r by n power matrix whose i-th row is (y_i^n,y_i^{n-1},...,y_i). Let F_r(y,mu) be the r by n factor matrix whose column c in the block ending at b_j has entries y_i^{b_j-c+1} product_{m=b_j+1}^n (y_i-x_m). Observation 4.8 states that there is a lower unitriangular n by n matrix C(mu), with entries in the S_mu-invariant polynomial ring F[x_1,...,x_n]^{S_mu}, such that F_r(y,mu)=P_r(y,mu) C(mu). Now let T=(T_1,...,T_p) be a mu-translation sequence, meaning T_j is a subset of the j-th mu-block, put gamma_j=#T_j and T=union T_j. Let eF(y,mu,T) be the n by n matrix formed by putting F_{#T}(y,mu) on top and below it the echelon 0-1 matrix with pivots in the columns not belonging to T. For J subset [n] with #J=#T, let P_{T,J}=det eF(x_J,mu,T), up to sign, where y variables are replaced by {x_j : j in J}. Then P_{T,J}=0 unless J <=_Gale J(mu,gamma). For J=J(mu,gamma), P_{T,J}=± f_{J(mu,gamma)} s(T), where f_J is the product x_j product_{j<i<=n}(x_j-x_i) over j in J, and s(T) is the product over blocks of the Schur polynomials determined by the displacement of T_j from the last gamma_j positions of its block.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The matrix factorization is obtained by column operations on P_r: process columns left to right and use elementary symmetric polynomials in terminal variable sets {x_{b_j+1},...,x_n} to replace powers by the factored expressions. These elementary symmetric polynomials are invariant under S_mu, and only later columns are added to earlier columns, giving a lower unitriangular C(mu). For the determinant statement, expanding along the echelon lower block reduces det eF to the determinant of the columns indexed by T in the upper factor matrix. If J is not Gale-below J(mu,gamma), after a row rearrangement the relevant submatrix has a southwest zero block crossing the diagonal, so the determinant is zero. For J=J(mu,gamma), the matrix is block upper triangular. Factoring the terms x_j and (x_j-x_i) gives exactly f_J; the remaining diagonal-block determinants are alternants, and the bialternant formula identifies their quotient by Vandermonde factors with the Schur factors comprising s(T).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.11: parabolic D-operators produce superharmonics with controlled leading fermionic monomial
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let mu be a partition of n and T=(T_1,...,T_p) a mu-translation sequence with gamma_j=#T_j and r=#T. Using the matrix C(mu) from Observation 4.8 and the echelon matrix E with pivots outside T, set H=E C(mu)^{-1}. The paper defines an operator D^T_mu on Omega_n by D^T_mu(f)=sum_{I subset [n], #I=n-r} (-1)^{sum_{i in I} i} Delta_I(H) ⊙ d_{([n]-I)^*}(f), where Delta_I(H) is the maximal minor of H using columns I, ([n]-I)^*={n-a+1 : a in [n]-I}, d_K is the composition of higher Euler operators indexed by K, and ⊙ is the differential action. If 1 is not in T_1, then D^T_mu(delta_n) is a nonzero element of epsilon_mu SH_n. Its unique Gale-maximal fermionic monomial is theta_{J(mu,gamma)}, and the coefficient of this monomial is ±(s(T) f_{J(mu,gamma)}) ⊙ delta_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The minors Delta_I(H) are S_mu-invariant polynomials, and higher Euler operators preserve SH_n and commute with epsilon_mu, so Lemma 4.5 places D^T_mu(delta_n) in epsilon_mu SH_n. The coefficient of theta_J in D^T_mu(delta_n) is computed by the Cauchy-Binet expansion of det(P_r(x_J,mu); H) acting on delta_n. Because F_r=P_r C(mu) and C(mu) is lower unitriangular, this determinant equals det(F_r(x_J,mu); E), i.e. P_{T,J}. Lemma 4.9 then gives Gale vanishing for all J not <=_Gale J(mu,gamma) and gives the claimed top coefficient for J(mu,gamma). Nonvanishing uses Steinberg's theorem: (s(T) f_J) ⊙ delta_n is nonzero iff s(T) is not in (I_n:f_J). The hypothesis 1 not in T_1 ensures every monomial in s(T) lies in the colon-quotient basis A_n(J), so Theorem 3.2 implies s(T) is nonzero modulo (I_n:f_J).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.12: lower bound via S_mu-invariant modules in colon quotients
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a partition mu=(mu_1,...,mu_p) of n and 0<=gamma<=mu, let R_{mu,gamma} be the F[x_1,...,x_n]^{S_mu}-submodule of F[x_1,...,x_n]/(I_n:f_{J(mu,gamma)}) generated by the set Z_{mu,gamma} of products product_{j=1}^p s_{nu_j}(x_{b_j-gamma_j+1},...,x_{b_j}), where b_j=mu_1+...+mu_j, nu_1 is contained in the rectangle ((mu_1-gamma_1-1)^{gamma_1}), and for j>=2, nu_j is contained in ((mu_j-gamma_j)^{gamma_j}). Then dim_F epsilon_mu SH_n >= sum_{0<=gamma<=mu} dim_F R_{mu,gamma}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Lemma 4.11 gives, for each generator s(T) in Z_{mu,gamma}, an element of epsilon_mu SH_n whose unique Gale-maximal theta term is theta_{J(mu,gamma)} with coefficient (s(T) f_J) ⊙ delta_n. Since epsilon_mu SH_n is closed under differential action by S_mu-invariant polynomials, the same holds for all elements of the F[x]^{S_mu}-module generated by Z_{mu,gamma}. Steinberg's theorem identifies linear independence of coefficients (g f_J) ⊙ delta_n with linear independence of the classes g in F[x]/(I_n:f_J). Different gamma give different Gale-maximal theta supports, so the independent families for different gamma do not interfere.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 4.15, 4.17, and 4.18: elevated staircase polynomial sets L(m,k,t) and their counts
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For integers m,k,t with 0<=k<=m, define L(m,k,t) to be the set of polynomials e_lambda(x_1,...,x_m) s_nu(x_{m-k+1},...,x_m), where nu is contained in the rectangle ((m-k)^k), lambda is contained in (m^chi), and chi=t if nu_1<m-k while chi=t-1 if nu_1=m-k. Lemma 4.15 states: (1) any monomial x_1^{b_1}...x_m^{b_m} appearing in an element of L(m,k,t) has exponent vector componentwise <= (t,t+1,...,t+m-k-1,t+m-k-1,...,t+m-k-1), with k copies of the final entry; and (2) L(m,k,t) is linearly independent in F[x_1,...,x_m]. Lemma 4.17 defines I(m,k,t) to be the set of integer sequences c_1,...,c_m with c_1<...<c_{m-k}, c_{m-k+1}<=...<=c_m, and the same componentwise upper bound, and proves #L(m,k,t)=#I(m,k,t). Lemma 4.18 applies this blockwise: if st(J(mu,gamma)) has block-end heights t_j, then #A_n(mu,gamma)=product_j #I(mu_j,gamma_j,t_{j-1})=product_j #L(mu_j,gamma_j,t_{j-1}), with t_0=0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the monomial bound, e_lambda contributes exponents bounded by lambda'_1 and the Schur factor in the last k variables contributes exponents bounded by nu_1; the definition of chi gives exactly the elevated staircase bound. For linear independence, first observe that the Schur polynomials s_nu(x_{m-k+1},...,x_m) with nu contained in ((m-k)^k) are linearly independent modulo the ordinary coinvariant ideal I_m because all monomials in them lie in Artin's basis. Lemma 4.14 then says that multiplying a coinvariant basis by arbitrary monomials in the elementary symmetric functions e_1,...,e_m gives a basis of the full polynomial ring, so the specified e_lambda multiples are independent. The count #L=#I is proved by splitting the choices according to whether lambda lies in an m by (t-1) rectangle or contains the first column of height t, yielding a binomial formula matching the direct count of the constrained sequences. Lemma 4.18 is then a direct product decomposition over the consecutive mu-blocks.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.19: explicit basis and dimension formula for epsilon_mu SR_n
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every partition mu of n, dim_F epsilon_mu SH_n = sum_{0<=gamma<=mu} #A_n(mu,gamma). Equivalently, since SR_n and SH_n are isomorphic as bigraded S_n-modules, the set union over 0<=gamma<=mu of epsilon_mu(A_n(mu,gamma) theta_{J(mu,gamma)}) descends to a basis of epsilon_mu SR_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The spanning statement of Lemma 4.3 gives the upper bound dim epsilon_mu SR_n <= sum_gamma #A_n(mu,gamma). For the lower bound, Lemma 4.12 reduces the problem to proving dim R_{mu,gamma} >= #A_n(mu,gamma) for each gamma. The proof constructs an explicit set E(mu,gamma) inside F[x]^{S_mu} Z_{mu,gamma}: in each mu-block it uses the corresponding L(mu_j,gamma_j,t_{j-1}) set, with the heights t_j determined by the staircase st(J(mu,gamma)), and then takes all products over blocks. Lemma 4.18 gives #E(mu,gamma)=#A_n(mu,gamma). Lemma 4.15 shows every monomial appearing in E(mu,gamma) lies under the staircase st(J(mu,gamma)), hence represents a basis monomial in the colon quotient by Theorem 3.2, and also shows E(mu,gamma) is linearly independent in the polynomial ring. Therefore E(mu,gamma) is independent in R_{mu,gamma}, giving the lower bound. Upper and lower bounds coincide, so the spanning set is a basis.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.1: Fields Conjecture 2, ungraded S_n-structure
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: As ungraded S_n-modules, SR_n is isomorphic to F[OP_n] tensor sign, where OP_n is the set of ordered set partitions of [n] and sign is the sign representation of S_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: It is enough to show e_mu^perp Frob(SR_n)=e_mu^perp Frob(F[OP_n] tensor sign) for every partition mu of n, because the elementary symmetric functions e_mu form a basis and the Hall inner product is nondegenerate. By Lemma 2.2 these scalars are dim epsilon_mu SR_n and dim epsilon_mu(F[OP_n] tensor sign). Lemma 4.19 computes dim epsilon_mu SR_n as sum_gamma #A_n(mu,gamma), and Lemma 4.4 identifies the same sum with dim epsilon_mu(F[OP_n] tensor sign). Thus all e_mu^perp probes agree, proving the module isomorphism.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.2: Fields Conjecture 3, bigraded Frobenius characteristic
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The bigraded Frobenius characteristic of SR_n is grFrob(SR_n;q,z) = sum_{k=1}^n C_{n,k}(x;q) z^{n-k}, where q records polynomial x-degree, z records exterior theta-degree, and C_{n,k}(x;q) is the symmetric function described in Equations (2.5)-(2.10).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix fermionic degree r and put k=n-r. The proof compares SR_n with the superspace Vandermonde module W_{n,k}=Omega_n / ann_Omega(delta_{n,k}), where delta_{n,k}=epsilon_n(x_1^0 x_2^1 ... x_k^{k-1} x_{n-k+1}^{k-1}...x_n^{k-1} theta_{n-k+1}...theta_n). Prior work gives that the top fermionic degree r of W_{n,k} has basis union_{|J|=r} A_n(J) theta_J and Frobenius characteristic C_{n,k}(x;q). After applying epsilon_mu and using the same canonical representative analysis as in Lemma 4.3, the relevant basis elements are exactly epsilon_mu(x^a theta_{J(mu,gamma)}) with sum_j gamma_j=r and x^a in A_n(mu,gamma). Lemma 4.19 gives the same q-graded Hilbert series for epsilon_mu applied to the fermionic degree r piece of SR_n. Hence for every mu, e_mu^perp of the Frobenius characteristic of that SR_n piece equals e_mu^perp C_{n,k}(x;q). Since this holds for all mu, the Frobenius characteristics agree in each fermionic degree.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 5.3: fermionic pieces are ordered-set-partition representations
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For each k with 1<=k<=n, the fermionic degree n-k piece (SR_n)_{*,n-k}, forgetting the q-grading, is isomorphic as an S_n-module to F[OP_{n,k}] tensor sign, where OP_{n,k} is the set of ordered set partitions of [n] with k blocks.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Theorem 5.2 gives Frob((SR_n)_{*,n-k};q=1)=C_{n,k}(x;1). The paper recalls that C_{n,k}(x;1)=Frob(F[OP_{n,k}] tensor sign). Therefore the ungraded Frobenius characteristics coincide, which is equivalent to the asserted S_n-module isomorphism.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 5.4: Reiner's restriction recursion
