key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For 1<=k<=n, the restriction of the fermionic degree n-k piece from S_n to S_{n-1} satisfies grFrob(Res^{S_n}_{S_{n-1}} (SR_n)_{*,n-k};q) = [k]_q (grFrob((SR_{n-1})_{*,n-k};q) + grFrob((SR_{n-1})_{*,n-k-1};q)).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For any S_n-module V, Frob(Res^{S_n}_{S_{n-1}} V)=e_1^perp Frob(V). By Theorem 5.2 the left side is e_1^perp C_{n,k}(x;q). The needed identity e_1^perp C_{n,k}(x;q) = [k]_q(C_{n-1,k-1}(x;q)+C_{n-1,k}(x;q)) is cited from earlier work on the C_{n,k}. Substituting the Frobenius characteristics of the corresponding SR_{n-1} fermionic pieces proves the recursion.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 6.2 and Corollary 6.3: geometric model for R_W^(1|1) and type A SR_n
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a compact simple Lie group, T a maximal torus, W=N_G(T)/T its Weyl group acting on V=Lie(T), and define the generalized superspace coinvariant ring R_W^(1|1)=(F[V] tensor exterior V)/I_W, where I_W is generated by W-invariants of positive degree. Let tilde G=G/T x T with diagonal W-action and p:tilde G -> G given by p(gT,t)=g t g^{-1}. If J is the ideal in H^*(tilde G) generated by p^*(H^{>0}(G)), then Proposition 6.2 gives an isomorphism of bigraded W-algebras R_W^(1|1) ≅ H^*(tilde G)/J. In type A, with G=SU(n), Corollary 6.3 states that H^*(tilde G)/J ≅ SR_n ≅ F[OP_n] tensor sign ≅ direct sum over all compositions alpha of n of H_top(p^{-1}(t_alpha)), where t_alpha in T has stabilizer S_alpha.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Reeder's theorem says p^*:H^*(G)->H^*(tilde G) is injective with image H^*(tilde G)^W. Borel's theorem and the Kunneth formula identify H^*(tilde G)=H^*(G/T) tensor H^*(T)=R_W^(1|0) tensor R_W^(0|1). The proof then uses averaging over W in characteristic zero to show that quotienting F[V] tensor exterior V by all positive-degree W-invariants is the same as first passing to R_W^(1|0) tensor R_W^(0|1) and then quotienting by its positive-degree W-invariants, which Reeder identifies with p^*(H^{>0}(G)). For type A, the fiber p^{-1}(t_alpha) decomposes into components indexed by cosets S_n/S_alpha, each homotopy equivalent to a Levi flag variety L_alpha/T. Its top cohomology carries Ind_{S_alpha}^{S_n}(sign_{S_alpha}), which is F[OP_alpha] tensor sign. Summing over all compositions alpha gives F[OP_n] tensor sign.

proof_techniques.name: Parabolic antisymmetrizer probes for Frobenius characteristics
proof_techniques.description: The paper systematically replaces direct character computations by the scalar tests dim epsilon_mu V for all partitions mu of n. Lemma 2.2 identifies these dimensions with e_mu^perp Frob(V). Since the e_mu form a basis of symmetric functions, equality of all these scalars for two S_n-modules forces equality of Frobenius characteristics. This converts a representation-theoretic problem into explicit linear-algebra and counting problems inside epsilon_mu-isotypic antisymmetric pieces.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If hook Schur coefficients can be realized through a superspace or exterior specialization, these e_mu^perp tests give a way to prove equality of Schur expansions without constructing all irreducible multiplicities directly.

proof_techniques.name: Apply antisymmetrizers to an explicit superspace monomial basis
proof_techniques.description: The basis A_n of SR_n is indexed by a theta support J and a monomial lying below the J-staircase. Applying epsilon_mu to this basis gives an immediate spanning set for epsilon_mu SR_n. The paper then removes zero terms and duplicate orbit representatives, producing canonical monomials A_n(mu,gamma) theta_{J(mu,gamma)} with strict exponent inequalities before the theta-selected positions and weak inequalities on the selected positions.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a concrete route from algebraic Hilbert data to combinatorial objects: when a hook coefficient corresponds to a fixed fermionic degree, one can try to count the relevant staircase monomials or their antisymmetrized representatives.

proof_techniques.name: Batch insertion model for ordered set partitions
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 4.4 proves that the canonical antisymmetrized monomial representatives are counted by ordered set partitions with entries in each parabolic batch appearing left-to-right in increasing order. The insertion procedure introduces gamma_j as the number of entries from batch j inserted into already available block positions, while mu_j-gamma_j entries create new singleton positions. The resulting product formula matches the exponent-sequence count of A_n(mu,gamma).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This supplies a direct combinatorial dictionary between staircase basis elements and ordered set partitions, which is the main interpretation likely needed for hook coefficients.

proof_techniques.name: Inverse-system lower bounds from controlled leading fermionic terms
proof_techniques.description: To show that the antisymmetrized spanning set is independent, the paper passes from SR_n to the inverse system SH_n. It constructs elements of epsilon_mu SH_n whose unique Gale-maximal theta monomial is prescribed and whose coefficient is an explicitly controlled polynomial differential applied to the Vandermonde. Since different leading theta monomials cannot cancel, and Steinberg's theorem converts nonzero coefficients to nonzero classes modulo colon ideals, this yields lower bounds matching the spanning-set upper bounds.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If one needs a proof rather than just a guessed formula for hook coefficients, this method shows how to build enough independent harmonic representatives in each fermionic degree.

proof_techniques.name: Power-to-factor matrix factorization for D-operators
proof_techniques.description: The D-operators are built from a matrix identity F_r(y,mu)=P_r(y,mu) C(mu), where P_r is a power matrix and F_r is a factored matrix with terminal products (y_i-x_m). The lower unitriangular matrix C(mu) has S_mu-invariant entries, so its minors can be used as invariant differential coefficients. Determinants of augmented factor matrices then vanish outside a Gale order ideal, and the top determinant factors as f_J times a product of Schur polynomials via the bialternant formula.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The appearance of Schur polynomials from determinant quotients is a potentially portable mechanism for isolating hook-shaped contributions after a bosonic-fermionic specialization.

proof_techniques.name: Colon-ideal reduction using Steinberg's theorem
proof_techniques.description: The coefficient of a leading theta term has the form (g f_J) ⊙ delta_n. Steinberg's theorem says h ⊙ delta_n is zero exactly when h lies in the ordinary coinvariant ideal I_n. Therefore (g f_J) ⊙ delta_n is nonzero exactly when g is nonzero in F[x]/(I_n:f_J). The colon-ideal basis A_n(J) then gives a concrete monomial criterion for nonvanishing and independence.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This can bypass difficult Grobner theory for superspace quotients: reduce hook-piece independence questions to known bases of ordinary colon quotients.

proof_techniques.name: Elevated staircase independence via L(m,k,t)
proof_techniques.description: The lower-bound proof needs polynomial families that fit under shifted staircase bounds. The paper defines L(m,k,t), products of elementary symmetric functions in all variables and Schur functions in the last k variables, and proves they are linearly independent while all their monomials satisfy an elevated staircase inequality. The proof combines Artin's coinvariant basis with the fact that multiplying a coinvariant basis by arbitrary powers of elementary symmetric functions gives a basis of the full polynomial ring.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is useful for q-degree enumeration of hook pieces, because it gives independent families whose monomials are constrained by exactly the kind of staircase bounds that lead to q-Stirling-type counts.

proof_techniques.name: Comparison with superspace Vandermonde modules W_{n,k}
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 5.2 upgrades an ungraded ordered-set-partition module result to a q-graded Frobenius formula by comparing each fermionic degree of SR_n with the top fermionic degree of W_{n,k}, a quotient defined by annihilating a superspace Vandermonde delta_{n,k}. Prior work identifies the top piece of W_{n,k} with C_{n,k}(x;q), and its basis has the same A_n(J) theta_J form. The same antisymmetrizer analysis shows that all epsilon_mu Hilbert series match.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For hook coefficients with an internal q-degree, this gives the bridge from ordered set partitions at q=1 to the refined C_{n,k}(x;q) and ordered multiset partition statistics.

proof_techniques.name: Fermionic degree records number of blocks
proof_techniques.description: Corollary 5.3 identifies the fermionic degree n-k piece of SR_n, after forgetting q-degree, with the sign-twisted permutation representation on ordered set partitions with k blocks. The full q-graded Frobenius version is Theorem 5.2: the same piece has Frobenius C_{n,k}(x;q), whose combinatorial expansions are in terms of ordered multiset partitions and standard Young tableaux.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Under a hook-specialization principle, the hook leg length should correspond to fermionic degree, so this result suggests interpreting the hook coefficient with leg n-k or related indexing by ordered set partitions with k blocks, refined by q-statistics when polynomial degree is tracked.

proof_techniques.name: Geometric realization through Springer-type fibers
proof_techniques.description: Section 6 uses Reeder's theorem to realize R_W^(1|1) as H^*(tilde G)/J for a compact Lie group G and Weyl group W. In type A this recovers SR_n and identifies its ungraded module with a direct sum of top cohomologies of fibers p^{-1}(t_alpha), indexed by compositions alpha of n. Each fiber contribution is induced from a sign representation of a Young subgroup and corresponds to ordered set partitions of a fixed block-size composition.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Although the original problem asks for a combinatorial interpretation, this geometric model may offer an alternate proof of the sign-twisted ordered-set-partition representation that underlies the hook interpretation.

other_useful_info: The paper explicitly points to three prior inputs that may be needed for a complete hook-coefficient proof: Rhoades-Wilson [33] for the Hilbert series and Operator Theorem, Angarone-Commins-Karn-Murai-Rhoades [3] for the superspace monomial basis and colon-ideal bases, and Rhoades-Wilson [34,35] for the superspace Vandermonde modules W_{n,k} whose top fermionic Frobenius characteristic is C_{n,k}(x;q). The ordered multiset partition formulas for C_{n,k}(x;q) provide several interchangeable q-statistics, inv, maj, dinv, and minimaj, that can serve as refined combinatorial interpretations. Reiner's recursion in Corollary 5.4 mirrors the operation of erasing n from an ordered set partition and may be useful for inductive proofs. Section 7 conjectures analogous determinant-twisted Coxeter-complex quotients for other Weyl types, but the type A result needed here is proved.
why_relevant: Determines the bigraded symmetric-group structure of the superspace coinvariant ring and identifies its ungraded structure with a sign-twisted action on ordered set partitions. This supplies refined combinatorial models that can be specialized to the Hilbert-series hook coefficients.

title: (GL_k × Sym_n)-modules and Nabla of hook-indexed Schur functions
overall_summary: The paper constructs, for every hook partition rho=(a|b) of n in Frobenius notation, explicit multigraded GL_infinity × S_n modules M_rho and quotient modules S_rho generated from a punctured Vandermonde-type determinant by derivations and higher polarization operators. Their graded Frobenius characteristics have tensor Schur expansions S_rho^⊗ = sum c^rho_{lambda,mu} s_lambda ⊗ s_mu, so the paper works directly in the stable GL-character framework in which universal multigraded Hilbert coefficients naturally live. The main hook-specific claim is conjectural: after restricting the GL side to Schur functions of length at most two, S_rho^⊗ should equal ∇ applied to a normalized hook-indexed Schur function; the paper proves supporting one-set/t=0 specializations and skew-restriction identities. The paper also records a shuffle/LLT formula for ∇ of normalized hook Schur functions, one-set Hall-Littlewood/Kostka descriptions, conjectural hook-component formulas via skewing by e_a^⊥, and plethystic e-positivity phenomena. For the original hook-coefficient problem, the most relevant content is the stable Schur-multiplicity setup, the Dyck path/LLT formula for hook-indexed nabla expressions, and the paper's methods for isolating hook components through quotient filtrations and skewing operators.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 1 construction: determinant-generated hook modules and their Frobenius expansion
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix n≥1 and a hook rho=(a|b), meaning the partition (a+1,1^b) with a,b≥0 and a+b=n−1. Let X be an infinite matrix of variables with n columns; its rows are sets u=(u_1,...,u_n), with first row x=(x_1,...,x_n). Let theta_1,...,theta_n be inert variables of degree 0. Define D_(a|b)(x) as the n by n determinant whose i-th row is (theta_i, 1, x_i, ..., x_i^{a-1}, x_i^{a+1}, ..., x_i^{n-1}), i.e. the Vandermonde columns 1,x,...,x^{n-1} with the x^a column removed and an inert theta column inserted. For rows u,v of X and integer r≥1 define the higher polarization operator E_uv^(r)=sum_{i=1}^n v_i ∂^r/∂u_i^r. The module M_(a|b) is the smallest subspace of Q[X,theta] containing D_(a|b)(x) and closed under the allowed derivations and all higher polarizations; equivalently it is written in the paper as Q({∂_{x_i}}; {E_uv^(r)}) D_(a|b)(x). The total derivative satisfies sum_i ∂_{x_i} D_(a|b)(x) = (a+1) D_(a+1|b−1)(x) for 0≤a<n−1, and is 0 otherwise; hence the M_(a|b) form a filtration M_(n−1|0) ⊂ M_(n−2|1) ⊂ ... ⊂ M_(0|n−1). With the convention M_(n|−1)=0, define the quotient S_(a|b)=M_(a|b)/M_(a+1|b−1). The N^infinity-graded Frobenius characteristic has a Schur-tensor expansion S_rho^⊗ = sum_{mu⊢n} sum_lambda c^rho_{lambda,mu} s_lambda ⊗ s_mu, where s_lambda is the GL_infinity character in the grading variables and s_mu is the Frobenius characteristic of the S_n-isotypic component. The coefficient of s_mu on the S_n side is denoted c_{rho,mu}=sum_lambda c^rho_{lambda,mu} s_lambda, and the alternant coefficient is A_rho=c_{rho,1^n}. The length restriction operator L_{≤k} keeps only those terms with l(lambda)≤k.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is the basic construction rather than a theorem. The determinant D_(a|b) is homogeneous in the row grading except for the inert theta column, and the actions F(X)↦F(gX sigma) of GL_infinity and S_n commute. The displayed total-derivative identity is obtained by differentiating the determinant with respect to all x_i and using the fact that differentiating the sequence of powers shifts the missing column; all terms cancel or combine to the determinant with the next omitted power, up to the scalar a+1. Since M_(a|b) is closed under these derivations, D_(a+1|b−1) lies in M_(a|b), giving the filtration. Semisimplicity of polynomial GL_infinity representations and of S_n representations gives the Schur-tensor expansion with nonnegative multiplicities for the quotient module. Restricting to finitely many rows, i.e. k sets of variables, corresponds to retaining only GL Schur functions of length at most k.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.1, shuffle formula for ∇ of normalized hook Schur functions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let rho=(a|b) be a hook of size n=a+b+1. The paper uses the normalization s_hat_rho = (-1/(q t))^{iota_rho} s_rho, where iota_(a|b)=a. Let delta(n)=(n−1,n−2,...,1,0), and identify a Dyck path gamma below the diagonal with a partition gamma contained in delta(n). Define Gamma_a = delta(n) − (0,...,0,1,...,1,0), where there are a consecutive copies of 1 as in the paper. For each Dyck path gamma⊆Gamma_a, let area(gamma) be its usual area and let L_gamma(q;z) be the associated vertical-strip LLT polynomial, defined as a dinv-weighted generating function over semistandard Young tableaux of skew shape (gamma+1^n)/gamma. Then ∇(s_hat_(a|b))(q,t;z) = sum_{gamma⊆Gamma_a} t^{area(gamma)−a} L_gamma(q;z).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper does not reprove the shuffle formula from first principles; it states that the identity follows from results of Haglund-Morse-Zabrocki together with the compositional shuffle theorems of Carlsson-Mellit and Mellit. The compositional shuffle framework expresses ∇ applied to the relevant hook-indexed Schur input as a sum over Dyck paths with prescribed constraints; for rho=(a|b), those constraints are encoded by containment gamma⊆Gamma_a. For each fixed path gamma, summing over the semistandard fillings of the vertical-strip skew shape (gamma+1^n)/gamma with dinv weight gives precisely the LLT polynomial L_gamma(q;z). The factor t^{area(gamma)−a} records the shifted area contribution induced by the hook normalization.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Conjecture 1.2, module conjecture for hook-indexed nabla
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a hook rho=(a|b), expand ∇(s_hat_rho) in tensor notation as ∇(s_hat_rho)^⊗ = sum_{mu⊢n} sum_lambda b^rho_{lambda,mu} s_lambda ⊗ s_mu, meaning that ∇(s_hat_rho)(q,t;z)=sum b^rho_{lambda,mu} s_lambda(q,t) s_mu(z). The conjecture states that, for every hook rho, L_{≤2}(S_rho^⊗)=∇(s_hat_rho)^⊗. Equivalently, for every rho, every mu⊢n, and every partition lambda of length at most 2, the multiplicity c^rho_{lambda,mu} in S_rho equals the coefficient b^rho_{lambda,mu} in ∇(s_hat_rho).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is a conjecture, not proved in the paper. The paper provides several forms of evidence: explicit computations for all hooks of sizes n≤4, with the first discrepancy between S_rho^⊗ and ∇(s_hat_rho)^⊗ occurring only in GL length 3; a proof of the t=0 or one-set specialization in Proposition 1.6; and skew-operator consistency results in Theorem 1.4. The conjecture is deliberately formulated with L_{≤2} because ∇(s_hat_rho)(q,t;z) is naturally a two-grading object, while S_rho exists with arbitrarily many sets of variables.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Conjecture 1.3 and Theorem 1.4, skew identities and their length-two verification
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let e_1^⊥ denote the operator adjoint to multiplication by e_1 under the Hall inner product. Conjecture 1.3 asserts two skew identities for the quotient modules: (Id ⊗ e_1^⊥) S_(n)^⊗ = sum_{a=0}^{n−2} S_(a|n−a−2)^⊗, where S_(n) means S_(n−1|0); and (e_1^⊥ ⊗ Id) S_{1^n}^⊗ = sum_{a=1}^{n−1} S_(a|n−a−1)^⊗, where S_{1^n} means S_(0|n−1). The paper states that these identities were checked computationally for n≤6. Theorem 1.4 proves the length-two restriction of the first identity. It also states, under the paper's standing use of the cited conjectural input from Bergeron's earlier work and assuming Conjecture 1.2, that the length-two restriction of the second identity holds.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the first identity, L_{≤2} commutes with Id ⊗ e_1^⊥ because length restriction concerns the GL side, while e_1^⊥ acts on the S_n Frobenius side. Representation-theoretically, Id ⊗ e_1^⊥ is restriction of the S_n action to the subgroup S_{n−1} fixing one letter. The paper invokes the lattice-diagram discussion of Bergeron-Bergeron-Garsia-Haiman-Tesler to identify (Id ⊗ e_1^⊥) L_{≤2}(S_(n)^⊗) with the derivation-polarization span of a determinant D^− obtained by omitting the variable x_n; in the notation of the paper this span is isomorphic to M_{(1|n−2)}^{<2>}. Unfolding the definition S_(a|b)=M_(a|b)/M_(a+1|b−1) gives the sum of the length-two restricted quotients. For the second identity, after applying Conjecture 1.2, the desired statement becomes an identity involving e_1^⊥∇ applied to normalized hook Schur functions. The proof uses an operator identity from Bergeron-Garsia-Haiman-Tesler, e_1^⊥ ∇ = ∇(e_1^⊥ Δ_{e_1} − Δ_{e_1} e_1^⊥), and the identity Δ_{e_1} s_hat_n = sum_{a=0}^{n−1} s_hat_(a|n−a−1), cited from Bergeron-Garsia-Sergel Leven-Xin. The final comparison uses the cited conjectural identification from Bergeron's earlier paper relating Δ'_{e_{n−2}} e_n to the relevant length-two part of (e_1^⊥ ⊗ Id) S_{1^n}^⊗.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.5, Haiman n!-Theorem as used in the paper
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a Ferrers diagram mu with n cells, let D_mu(x,y)=det(x_i^k y_i^l) be the lattice diagram determinant, with cells (k,l) ordered as in the paper, and let G_mu be the module obtained by closing D_mu under all partial derivatives in the x and y variables. Haiman's n!-Theorem states that the bigraded Frobenius characteristic of G_mu is the combinatorial Macdonald polynomial H_tilde_mu(q,t;z).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This theorem is quoted from Haiman and not proved in the paper. The paper uses it as an input to identify certain one-set pieces of the hook modules M_(a|b) with top y-degree components of Garsia-Haiman modules. In particular, when the relevant lattice diagram is the Ferrers hook (n−2|1), the coefficient of t in H_tilde_(n−2|1)(q,t;z) gives the one-set Frobenius characteristic needed for M_(n−1|0).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Equation (25), one-set Hall-Littlewood formula for M_(a|b) and S_(a|b)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let H_mu(q;z) be the symmetric function H_mu=omega Q'_{mu'} associated with the dual Hall-Littlewood function Q'_{mu'}, so that H_mu(q;z)=sum_lambda K_{lambda',mu'}(q) s_lambda(z), with K_{lambda,mu}(q) the Kostka-Foulkes polynomials. For every hook rho=(a|b) of size n, so a+b=n−1, the one-set graded Frobenius characteristic of M_(a|b) is M_(a|b)(q;z) = (1+q+...+q^b) H_(n−2|1)(q;z). Consequently, since S_(a|b)=M_(a|b)/M_(a+1|b−1), one obtains S_(a|b)(q;z)=q^b H_(n−2|1)(q;z) in one set of variables.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The determinant D_(a|b)(x) is obtained from the lattice diagram d(a,b)={(i,0): 0≤i≤a+b and i≠a} ∪ {(0,1)} by replacing the y variables by inert theta variables. Thus M_(a|b) with one row of variables is the top y-degree piece of the corresponding Garsia-Haiman-type module. For a=n−1,b=0 the diagram is the Ferrers hook (n−2|1), so Haiman's theorem identifies M_(n−1|0)(q;z) with the coefficient of t in H_tilde_(n−2|1)(q,t;z), denoted H_(n−2|1)(q;z). The total derivative pi=sum_i ∂_{x_i} maps D_(a|b) to a nonzero scalar multiple of D_(a+1|b−1), inducing a projection M_(a|b) onto M_(a+1|b−1) and a degree-one reducing isomorphism S_(a|b)≅S_(a+1|b−1). Therefore S_(a|b)(q;z)=q S_(a+1|b−1)(q;z), and telescoping down the filtration gives the displayed geometric sum for M_(a|b).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.6, t=0 specialization of the module conjecture
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every hook rho=(a|b), Conjecture 1.2 holds after setting the second nabla grading variable t equal to 0. Equivalently, the one-set specialization of S_rho matches ∇(s_hat_rho)(q,0;z).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper combines the one-set formula M_(a|b)(q;z)=(1+q+...+q^b) H_(n−2|1)(q;z), the induced quotient formula S_(a|b)(q;z)=q^b H_(n−2|1)(q;z), and the Hall-Littlewood/Delta identities cited from Haglund-Rhoades-Shimozono. On the nabla side, Proposition 1.1 gives ∇(s_hat_(a|b))(q,t;z)=sum_{gamma⊆Gamma_a} t^{area(gamma)−a} L_gamma(q;z). When t=0, only the unique path gamma=Gamma_a with area(gamma)=a contributes, so ∇(s_hat_(a|b))(q,0;z)=L_{Gamma_a}(q;z). The cited t=0 Hall-Littlewood identities identify this LLT polynomial with the one-set Frobenius obtained from the module filtration, yielding the stated specialization.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 1.3 conditional Garsia-Haiman intersection formulas
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assuming conjectures from Bergeron-Garsia on intersections of Garsia-Haiman modules, let I_n = G_{d(n−2,1)} ∩ G_{d(n−1,0)}, where d(a,b) is the lattice diagram {(i,0):0≤i≤a+b and i≠a} ∪ {(0,1)} and G_d is the derivative closure of the corresponding lattice diagram determinant. Then the paper records the conditional Frobenius formula I_n(q,t;z) = (q^{n−1} H_tilde_(n−1,1)(q,t;z) − t H_tilde_(n)(q,t;z))/(q^{n−1}−t). Under the same assumptions, G_{d(n−2,1)} decomposes as I_n ⊕ I_n^⊥ with dim I_n=dim I_n^⊥=n!/2, and I_n^⊥(q,t;z)=t H_(n−1,1)(q;z). Consequently H_tilde_(n−1,1)(q,t;z)=q^{binom(n−1,2)} omega H_(n−1,1)(1/q;z)+t H_(n−1,1)(q;z).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The intersection formula is not proved in this paper; it is presented as a specialization of conjectures from Bergeron-Garsia. The displayed expression for I_n^⊥ follows by subtracting the conjectural expression for I_n from H_tilde_(n−1,1), the Frobenius of G_{d(n−2,1)}, and simplifying. The final two-term formula for H_tilde_(n−1,1) separates the q-reversed omega image of the one-set Hall-Littlewood piece from the top t-degree Hall-Littlewood piece. This decomposition is then used to motivate explicit bases for one-set hook quotient modules.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 1.7, basis for one-set hook quotients from an intersection basis
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let B_n be a basis of the intersection module I_n described in Section 1.3. For each a≥1 with b=n−a−1 and d=d(a,b), the set { phi(∂_x) D_d(x) : phi(x) in B_n } forms a basis of the one-set quotient S_(a|b)^{<1>}. Here D_d(x) is the determinant corresponding to the diagram d(a,b), with y variables replaced by inert theta variables, and phi(∂_x) means the differential operator obtained from phi by substituting ∂_{x_i} for x_i.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper says it only needs to check linear independence. If phi is a nonzero element in the span of B_n, then by hypothesis phi(x)=psi(∂_x) D_d(x) for some polynomial psi. Suppose one applies psi(∂_x) to phi(∂_x)D_d(x). Since the differential operators commute, psi(∂_x) phi(∂_x)D_d(x)=phi(∂_x) psi(∂_x)D_d(x)=phi(∂_x)phi(x). The constant term of phi(∂_x)phi(x) is the sum of the squares of the coefficients of phi, hence is nonzero. Therefore phi(∂_x)D_d(x) is nonzero for every nonzero phi in the span of B_n, giving injectivity of the map phi↦phi(∂_x)D_d(x). Together with the known dimension of the quotient, this gives a basis.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Conjecture 1.8, hook components from the alternant component
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every hook rho of size n and every hook mu=(a|b) of size n, with 0≤a≤n−1 and b=n−a−1, the paper conjectures c_{rho,(a|b)} = e_a^⊥ A_rho. Here c_{rho,mu}=sum_lambda c^rho_{lambda,mu} s_lambda is the GL-character coefficient of the S_n-isotypic component indexed by mu in S_rho^⊗, A_rho=c_{rho,1^n} is the alternant coefficient, and e_a^⊥ is adjoint to multiplication by e_a. The paper notes the consequence A_(n+1)=A_{1^n} from the module definition, which would imply c_{(n|0),(a|b+1)} = c_{(0|n−1),(a|b)} for all a.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is a conjecture, not proved in the paper. Its content is that all hook-indexed S_n-isotypic coefficients of S_rho are obtained by skewing the single alternant coefficient A_rho. The stated consequence follows formally by applying the conjectural identity to the equality of alternant components A_(n+1)=A_{1^n}; the indices shift because the row hook (n|0) has size n+1 while 1^n has size n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Conjecture 1.9 and Section 1.6, coefficient-length bound and reconstruction of alternants
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a symmetric function f=sum_lambda a_lambda s_lambda, let ell(f)=max{ell(lambda): a_lambda≠0}. Conjecture 1.9 states that if rho=(a|b) is a hook of size n with a≥1, then ell(c_{rho,mu}) ≤ n−mu_1 for every partition mu⊢n. The paper combines this conjecture with the Hook Components Conjecture to describe a reconstruction of A_rho. In particular, e_{n−1}^⊥ A_rho=0, e_{n−2}^⊥ A_rho=c_{rho,(n−2|1)}=s_b, so A_rho has top-length term s_(b|n−3) plus lower-length terms. More generally, the terms of A_rho of length n−3 are determined from e_{n−3}^⊥ A_rho = c_{rho,(n−3|2)} = <S_rho^⊗, s_(n−3|2)>; by the length bound, this coefficient has length at most 2 and is therefore determined by the length-two nabla expression predicted by Conjecture 1.2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is conditional reasoning, not a proved theorem. The Hook Components Conjecture turns the desired hook coefficients into successive skewings e_a^⊥ A_rho. The length conjecture says that for hook mu=(n−r|r−1), c_{rho,mu} has GL-Schur length at most r, so low r coefficients are completely visible in the length-two data once r≤2 and partially determine higher-length layers of A_rho by inversion of e_a^⊥. The paper illustrates the method for hooks of size 6: it computes the required coefficients of ∇(s_hat_rho)^⊗ against s_411 and then adds exactly the missing length-3 and higher Schur terms to A_rho, with the length≤2 remainder supplied by <∇(s_hat_rho)^⊗, e_6>.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.1, e_n coefficient after plethystic specialization
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Define T_rho = S_rho^⊗[1+q;z] by plethystically substituting the GL alphabet q by 1+q in each coefficient c_{rho,mu}; write T_rho=sum_{nu⊢n} d_{rho,nu} ⊗ e_nu(z) in the elementary basis on the S_n side. If the Hook Components Conjecture 1.8 holds, then the coefficient d_{rho,(n)} of e_n in T_rho is Schur-positive; in fact d_{rho,(n)}=A_rho.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let f_n be the forgotten symmetric function dual to e_n. The coefficient of e_n in T_rho is <S_rho^⊗[1+q;z], f_n>. The paper uses the known pairing <s_mu,f_n>, which is 0 unless mu is a hook (k|j), and equals (−1)^k for mu=(k|j). Therefore d_{rho,(n)} = (sum_{k=0}^{n−1} (−1)^k c_{rho,(k|n−k−1)})[1+q]. Assuming Conjecture 1.8, c_{rho,(k|n−k−1)}=e_k^⊥ A_rho, so the sum becomes (sum_k (−1)^k e_k^⊥ A_rho)[1+q]. The standard plethystic identity sum_k (−1)^k e_k^⊥ F(q)=F[q−1] gives d_{rho,(n)}=(A_rho[q−1])[1+q]=A_rho. Since A_rho is an actual GL-character of a module component, it is Schur-positive.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Conjecture 2.2, trivariate shuffle formula for selected hooks
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For hooks rho=(a|b) with a equal to 0, 1, or n−1, the paper conjectures a trivariate formula S_rho(q,t,1;z) = sum_{Gamma_a ≤ alpha ≤ beta} q^{d(alpha,beta)} L_beta(t;z). The sum is over pairs of Dyck paths alpha,beta with alpha below beta in the Tamari poset and alpha at least Gamma_a in the indicated order. The statistic d(alpha,beta) is the length of the longest strict chain from alpha to beta in the Tamari poset, and L_beta(t;z) is the LLT polynomial associated with beta.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is conjectural in the paper. The author notes that the case a=0 is the trivariate shuffle conjecture from Bergeron-Préville-Ratelle, and that the case a=n−1 is more or less implicit in work of Bousquet-Mélou-Chapuy-Préville-Ratelle on m-Tamari intervals. The conjecture extends those Tamari-interval sums to the hook modules S_rho in three sets of variables with the third grading specialized to 1.

proof_techniques.name: Stable GL_infinity × S_n Frobenius bookkeeping
proof_techniques.description: The paper treats a multigraded module with arbitrarily many sets of variables as a polynomial GL_infinity representation commuting with S_n. A homogeneous basis of each S_n-isotypic component produces a GL-character, and polynomial GL_infinity characters expand in Schur functions s_lambda(q). This yields tensor notation sum c_{lambda,mu} s_lambda ⊗ s_mu, with the left factor recording multidegrees and the right factor recording the S_n representation. Passing to k sets of variables is implemented by the operator L_{≤k}, which discards all GL Schur functions of length greater than k.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The universal coefficients c_lambda(n) in a Schur expansion of a multigraded Hilbert series can often be interpreted as stable GL_m multiplicities; the paper's framework suggests proving hook cases by first constructing or identifying a stable GL_infinity module and then extracting the hook-shaped left Schur coefficient.

proof_techniques.name: Punctured Vandermonde determinant with inert variables
proof_techniques.description: For each hook (a|b), the determinant D_(a|b) is obtained from the Vandermonde determinant by removing the x^a column and inserting an inert theta column. Closing this determinant under derivations and higher polarization operators generates a GL_infinity × S_n module M_(a|b). The total derivative shifts the missing column from a to a+1, producing a nested filtration by hook parameters and allowing the quotient S_(a|b) to isolate the layer associated with that hook.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the coinvariant algebra or its harmonic model admits determinant generators or lattice-diagram pieces, a similar punctured-determinant filtration could isolate hook-shaped Schur contributions rather than requiring a global basis of the full algebra.

proof_techniques.name: Filtration quotient telescoping
proof_techniques.description: The identity sum_i ∂_{x_i}D_(a|b) proportional to D_(a+1|b−1) gives inclusions M_(a+1|b−1)⊂M_(a|b). The quotient definition S_(a|b)=M_(a|b)/M_(a+1|b−1) converts information about adjacent M-modules into information about the individual hook layer. In one set of variables, the induced quotient isomorphisms shift degree by one, giving S_(a|b)(q;z)=q S_(a+1|b−1)(q;z) and hence a geometric-sum formula for M_(a|b).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Hook coefficients in a Hilbert series might be accessible by setting up a filtration whose successive quotients correspond to hooks; telescoping can turn a hard coefficient extraction into a degree shift from a single base case.

proof_techniques.name: Dyck path and LLT polynomial expansion for hook-indexed nabla
proof_techniques.description: Using the compositional shuffle theorem, the paper expresses ∇(s_hat_(a|b)) as a sum over Dyck paths gamma contained in a hook-dependent boundary Gamma_a. Each path contributes a vertical-strip LLT polynomial L_gamma(q;z), itself a generating function over semistandard tableaux of a skew shape immediately to the right of the path, weighted by dinv, and the whole term receives an area weight t^{area(gamma)−a}. At t=0 only the extremal path Gamma_a contributes.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This gives a ready-made combinatorial model, Dyck paths plus semistandard tableaux, for hook-indexed Schur phenomena. If the desired hook c_lambda(n) can be related to a nabla hook expression or to the length-two part of S_rho, its coefficients may be interpreted by extracting Schur coefficients from these LLT sums.

proof_techniques.name: Top y-degree reduction to Hall-Littlewood and Kostka-Foulkes data
proof_techniques.description: The paper observes that replacing y variables by inert theta variables makes M_(a|b) with one set of variables the top y-degree piece of a lattice-diagram/Garsia-Haiman module. For the base hook, Haiman's n!-Theorem identifies this top piece with H_(n−2|1)=omega Q'_(n−2|1)' and hence with a Kostka-Foulkes expansion. The entire one-set family then follows from the derivative-induced degree shifts.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: One-variable specializations of multigraded Hilbert coefficients often have Hall-Littlewood or Kostka-Foulkes interpretations; for hook shapes, this suggests looking for charge/Kostka counts as the t=0 shadow of the desired combinatorics.

proof_techniques.name: Skewing operators as restriction and hook extraction
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses e_1^⊥ on the S_n side as the Frobenius-characteristic operation corresponding to restricting an S_n representation to S_{n−1}. It also conjectures that e_a^⊥ on the GL side extracts hook S_n-isotypic coefficients from the alternant coefficient A_rho. These skewing operators connect branching rules, quotient filtrations, and hook components in a compact symmetric-function language.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For hook-shaped Schur coefficients, try expressing the coefficient as a skewing of a better-understood component, especially an alternant or sign-isotypic component. This can reduce the hook case to proving an e_a^⊥ identity rather than constructing all multigraded pieces.

proof_techniques.name: Length bounds to reconstruct Schur-positive characters
proof_techniques.description: The coefficient-length conjecture says that the GL-Schur length of c_{rho,mu} is controlled by n−mu_1. Combined with hook-component skewing, the paper reconstructs the high-length part of A_rho layer by layer from low-length coefficients that are visible in the conjectural nabla formula. This is a triangular inversion strategy based on Schur length.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If hook c_lambda(n) are only partially visible in low numbers of variable sets, a length bound could justify reconstruction of the full stable coefficient from two-variable or one-variable data.

proof_techniques.name: Plethystic e-positivity via forgotten symmetric functions
proof_techniques.description: The paper studies T_rho=S_rho^⊗[1+q;z], expands it in the elementary basis e_nu on the S_n side, and relates the e_n coefficient to hook Schur components by pairing with the forgotten function f_n. Because <s_mu,f_n> vanishes except for hook mu, where it is an alternating sign, the Hook Components Conjecture converts this coefficient into a plethystic difference A_rho[q−1] evaluated at 1+q, i.e. A_rho itself.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Alternating sums over hook components can sometimes be packaged as an elementary-basis coefficient after a 1+q plethystic substitution. This may provide both positivity and a combinatorial interpretation if an e-positive expansion is available.

proof_techniques.name: Differential-operator basis from module intersections
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 1.7 uses a basis B_n of an intersection of Garsia-Haiman modules to build a basis of one-set hook quotients by applying each polynomial phi in B_n as a differential operator phi(∂_x) to the determinant D_d. Injectivity follows from the nonzero constant term of phi(∂)phi, which is the sum of squares of coefficients. This is a standard apolarity-type argument adapted to these determinant modules.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A direct combinatorial basis for hook pieces of a coinvariant algebra might be built by applying differential operators indexed by a known basis of an intersected or simpler harmonic module, with nonvanishing certified by the same apolar pairing.

other_useful_info: Normalization and bounds: for a hook rho=(a|b), the paper uses s_hat_rho=(-1/(q t))^a s_rho. In the Schur-tensor expansion of S_rho, the GL partition lambda has at most n−1 parts and size bounded by (n choose 2)+b. Explicit computational data: the paper lists complete S_rho^⊗ expansions for all hooks of size up to 4 in the main text, hooks of size 5 and 6 in the appendix, and e-expansions of T_rho for hooks of size at most 4. The smallest case where S_rho^⊗ is larger than ∇(s_hat_rho)^⊗ is rho=1111, where S_1111^⊗ = ∇(s_hat_1111)^⊗ + s_111 ⊗ s_1111; thus the extra part has GL length 3 and is invisible to L_{≤2}. For hooks of size 5 the appendix records, for example, S_5^⊗ = ∇(s_hat_5)^⊗ + s_111 ⊗ s_11111, S_41^⊗ = ∇(s_hat_41)^⊗ + s_111 ⊗ s_2111 + s_211 ⊗ s_11111, and analogous triangular extra terms for S_311, S_2111, and S_11111. Section 2 notes that experiments suggest all coefficients d_{rho,nu} in T_rho=sum d_{rho,nu}⊗e_nu are Schur-positive; moreover, if mu is largest in dominance order among partitions with c_{rho,mu} nonzero, then d_{rho,mu'}=c_{rho,mu}[1+q]. The paper also reports the experimental hook formula d_{rho,mu}=sum_{i=0}^{min(j,k)} c_{(a|b−i),(k−i|j)} for rho=(a|b) and mu=(k|j), except in the special case rho=mu=1^n where d_{rho,mu}=1. Another observed lift of a known nabla identity is <S_(n+1)^⊗, s_(a|b)> = <S_{1^n}^⊗, s_(a|b−1)> for all a+b=n. Important cited sources for extending these ideas are: Carlsson-Mellit and Mellit for the shuffle theorem; Haglund-Rhoades-Shimozono for Hall-Littlewood/Delta identities at t=0; Bergeron's earlier GL_k × S_n diagonal-harmonics paper for the length-two conjectural input; Bergeron-Préville-Ratelle and Bousquet-Mélou-Chapuy-Préville-Ratelle for Tamari-interval/trivariate shuffle interpretations; and Bergeron-Bergeron-Garsia-Haiman-Tesler for lattice diagram modules and restriction arguments.
why_relevant: Focuses specifically on GL_k x S_n structures and nabla applied to hook-indexed Schur functions. Its hook-specific multiplicity formulas and conjectures are directly aligned with the hook-shape case of c_lambda(n).

title: Hilbert schemes, polygraphs, and the Macdonald positivity conjecture
overall_summary: Haiman proves that the isospectral Hilbert scheme X_n, the reduced fiber product of the Hilbert scheme H_n = Hilb^n(C^2) with (C^2)^n over the symmetric power S^n C^2, is normal, Cohen-Macaulay, and Gorenstein. The proof combines Hilbert-scheme geometry, a nested-Hilbert-scheme induction, duality, and a substantial commutative-algebra theorem asserting freeness of coordinate rings of certain subspace arrangements called polygraphs. The main algebraic consequence is the n! theorem and the graded character formula identifying transformed Kostka-Macdonald coefficients with bigraded S_n-character multiplicities in the derivative span D_µ of a lattice determinant. For the original problem, the paper is most useful as a source of rigorous representation-theoretic interpretations of multigraded Hilbert series coefficients via Frobenius characteristics, Macdonald positivity, character sheaves, and diagonal coinvariant-type modules; however, it does not itself state a hook-shape combinatorial formula for the universal Schur coefficients c_λ(n) of m-set coinvariant algebras.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.1.1, characterization of transformed Macdonald polynomials
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a partition µ of n, let µ′ be the conjugate partition, let s_λ be the Schur function, and let ≥ denote dominance order on partitions of n. The transformed integral-form Macdonald polynomial H̃_µ(x;q,t), defined in the paper by H̃_µ(x;q,t) = t^{n(µ)} J_µ[X/(1−t^{−1}); q,t^{−1}], is uniquely characterized by the three conditions: (1) H̃_µ(x;q,t) lies in the Q(q,t)-span of the functions s_λ[X/(1−q)] with λ ≥ µ; (2) H̃_µ(x;q,t) lies in the Q(q,t)-span of the functions s_λ[X/(1−t)] with λ ≥ µ′; and (3) H̃_µ[1;q,t] = 1, where square brackets denote plethystic substitution.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this characterization from Haiman's earlier work and uses it as the final recognition criterion in the proof of the graded character theorem. The strategy later is to construct a symmetric function F_{R_µ}(x;q,t), the Frobenius series of a doubly graded S_n-module R_µ, and verify precisely these three properties. The q = 0 or y-degree-zero quotient supplies condition (1) through the known Garsia-Procesi/Springer-fiber model and Hall-Littlewood triangularity; symmetry in x and y supplies condition (2); and the fact that only constants are S_n-invariant supplies condition (3).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2, n! theorem, Macdonald positivity, and graded character formula
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let µ be a partition of n. Let D(µ) = {(i,j) in N × N : j < µ_{i+1}} be its diagram with rows and columns indexed from 0, and define the lattice determinant Δ_µ(x,y) = det(x_i^{p_j} y_i^{q_j})_{1≤i,j≤n}, where (p_j,q_j) are the cells of D(µ) in some fixed order. Let D_µ = C[∂x,∂y]Δ_µ be the vector space spanned by all partial derivatives of Δ_µ in the polynomial ring C[x_1,y_1,...,x_n,y_n]. The symmetric group S_n acts by simultaneously permuting the pairs (x_i,y_i), and D_µ is doubly graded by x-degree and y-degree. If χ_λ is the irreducible S_n-character indexed by λ, then for all partitions λ,µ of n, Haiman proves the formula H̃K_{λµ}(q,t) = ∑_{r,s} t^r q^s ⟨χ_λ, ch((D_µ)_{r,s})⟩. Consequently H̃K_{λµ}(q,t) is a polynomial with nonnegative integer coefficients, and dim_C D_µ = n! for every partition µ of n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is obtained after the geometric Theorem 1. Define J_µ = {p in C[x,y] : p(∂x,∂y)Δ_µ = 0} and R_µ = C[x,y]/J_µ. A duality argument from earlier work gives ch((R_µ)_{r,s}) = ch((D_µ)_{r,s}), so it suffices to compute the Frobenius series F_{R_µ}(x;q,t). Theorem 1 implies that X_n is Cohen-Macaulay and Gorenstein; Proposition 3.7.3 then identifies R_µ with the coordinate ring of the scheme-theoretic fiber of X_n over the monomial ideal I_µ in H_n. Let S_µ = O_{X_n,Q_µ} be the local ring at the unique point Q_µ over I_µ. Flatness relates the Frobenius series of S_µ and R_µ by a scalar Hilbert-series factor coming from O_{H_n,I_µ}. The global functions y_1,...,y_n form a regular sequence in S_µ, giving the plethystic relation F_{S_µ/(y)}(x;q,t) = F_{S_µ}[X(1−q);q,t]. The quotient R_µ/(y) is the Garsia-Procesi ring for the Springer fiber of type µ, whose Frobenius series is the transformed Hall-Littlewood polynomial t^{n(µ)}Q_µ[X/(1−t^{−1});t^{−1}], so only χ_λ with λ ≥ µ occur. This verifies the first triangularity condition in Proposition 2.1.1 for F_{R_µ}; the x/y symmetry verifies the second condition; and the only invariant elements of R_µ are constants, verifying normalization. Hence F_{R_µ} = H̃_µ, yielding the stated graded character formula and positivity. Setting q=t=1 recovers the regular representation dimension n!.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.4.1 and Proposition 3.4.2, blowup descriptions of H_n and X_n
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S = C[x_1,y_1,...,x_n,y_n] and let A = S^ε be the subspace of S_n-alternating polynomials. Equivalently, A has basis the determinants Δ_D = det(x_i^{p_j}y_i^{q_j}) indexed by n-element subsets D of N × N. Let A_d be the span of products of d elements of A, with A_0 = S^{S_n}, and set T = ⊕_{d≥0} A_d as a graded S^{S_n}-algebra. Then H_n is isomorphic, as a scheme projective over S^n C^2 = Spec S^{S_n}, to Proj T. If J = S A is the ideal generated by all alternating polynomials, then the isospectral Hilbert scheme X_n is isomorphic, as a scheme over (C^2)^n = Spec S, to the blowup Proj S[tJ] of (C^2)^n along J.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The Hilbert-scheme Proj description is quoted from Haiman's earlier work. For X_n, the Rees algebra S[tJ] is generated by S and the homogeneous subalgebra T, because A_d generates J^d as an S-module. Therefore Proj S[tJ] is a closed subscheme of the scheme-theoretic fiber product H_n ×_{S^n C^2} (C^2)^n. Since Proj S[tJ] is reduced and has the same generic locus as the reduced fiber product defining X_n, and since X_n is irreducible of dimension 2n, the closed subscheme is all of X_n. This blowup model is then used to study normality and the canonical sheaf.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.7.1, annihilator criterion using alternation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a partition µ of n, let J_µ = {p in C[x,y] : p(∂x,∂y)Δ_µ = 0}. Let Θ_ε be the alternation operator Θ_ε(g) = ∑_{w in S_n} ε(w) w g, where ε is the sign character. Then J_µ is exactly the set of polynomials p in C[x,y] such that for every g in C[x,y], the coefficient of Δ_µ in the alternating polynomial Θ_ε(gp), expanded in the determinant basis {Δ_D}, is zero.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The result is cited from Haiman's earlier paper. The underlying idea is that the determinant basis {Δ_D} identifies alternating polynomials with the exterior power ∧^n C[x,y], and the differential pairing between polynomials and constant-coefficient differential operators tests coefficients in this basis. A polynomial p annihilates Δ_µ under the differential action exactly when every product gp has zero projection, after alternation, onto the Δ_µ line. This criterion is the bridge between the local fiber of the Hilbert-scheme construction and the explicit differential annihilator ideal J_µ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.7.2, ideal sheaf of X_n inside the n-fold universal family
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let F be the universal family over H_n, let B = π_*O_F be the tautological rank n bundle, and identify the relative n-fold product F^n over H_n with Spec B^{⊗n}. Let X_n be considered as a closed reduced subscheme of F^n, with ideal sheaf J in B^{⊗n}. Multiplication in B^{⊗n}, followed by alternation, gives an O_{H_n}-bilinear map B^{⊗n} ⊗ B^{⊗n} → B^{⊗n} → ∧^n B. Let φ: B^{⊗n} → (B^{⊗n})^* ⊗ ∧^n B be the induced homomorphism. Then the ideal sheaf J of X_n in F^n is precisely ker φ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: On the generic open subset U of H_n corresponding to n distinct reduced points, F^n is reduced and decomposes into n! points over each ideal, and X_n is the component corresponding to ordered spectra. A section s of B^{⊗n} belongs to the ideal of X_n iff its restriction vanishes on the generic part W = ρ^{−1}(U) of X_n. If s vanishes on X_n, then for every section g, the alternating section Θ_ε(gs) vanishes on X_n and also vanishes on the other components of F^n because it is alternating and hence zero when two points coincide; thus Θ_ε(gs)=0. Conversely, if s does not vanish at some generic ordered point Q, one chooses g to vanish on all other points of the S_n-orbit of Q but not at Q; then Θ_ε(gs) is nonzero. Therefore the vanishing ideal is exactly the kernel of the alternation pairing.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.7.3, local geometry at monomial ideals and the n! conjecture
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let I_µ be the monomial ideal in C[x,y] whose standard monomials are indexed by the diagram D(µ), and let Q_µ be the unique point of X_n lying over I_µ in H_n. The following are equivalent: (1) X_n is locally Cohen-Macaulay and Gorenstein at Q_µ; (2) dim_C D_µ = n!, where D_µ = C[∂x,∂y]Δ_µ. When these equivalent conditions hold, the ideal of the scheme-theoretic fiber ρ^{−1}(I_µ) ⊂ X_n, viewed as a closed subscheme of (C^2)^n, is exactly J_µ = {p : p(∂x,∂y)Δ_µ=0}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the bundle map φ from Proposition 3.7.2. At a point I in H_n, φ specializes to φ(I): B^{⊗n}(I) → B^{⊗n}(I)^* ⊗ ∧^nB(I), and the rank of φ(I) is lower semicontinuous; generically it is n!. For I=I_µ, compose the quotient map C[x,y] → (C[x,y]/I_µ)^{⊗n} with φ(I_µ) to obtain η. The alternating functional λ: A → ∧^n(C[x,y]/I_µ) kills every determinant Δ_D except Δ_µ, so Proposition 3.7.1 gives ker η = J_µ. If dim C[x,y]/J_µ = n!, then rank φ(I_µ)=n!, hence the rank is locally constant near I_µ; im φ = ρ_*O_{X_n} is locally free, so ρ is flat and X_n is Cohen-Macaulay near Q_µ. The fiber algebra is C[x,y]/J_µ, known from Emsalem to be Gorenstein, so the local ring is Gorenstein. Conversely, if X_n is locally Gorenstein at Q_µ, quotient its local ring by the parameter ideal from O_{H_n,I_µ}; the result is a Gorenstein Artin quotient C[x,y]/J of length n!. Since ρ_*O_{X_n} is locally free and generically the regular S_n-representation, every fiber affords the regular representation. The socle is one-dimensional and S_n-stable, hence must be the alternating line rather than constants. The factorization through η gives J ⊂ J_µ. If strict containment held, the socle would map into J_µ/J, forcing the alternating part of C[x,y]/J_µ to vanish and hence Δ_µ in J_µ, impossible. Thus J=J_µ and dim C[x,y]/J_µ=n!.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1, main geometric theorem on the isospectral Hilbert scheme
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let H_n = Hilb^n(C^2) and let X_n be the reduced fiber product of H_n with (C^2)^n over S^n C^2, whose closed points are tuples (I,P_1,...,P_n) with σ(I)=⟦P_1,...,P_n⟧. Then X_n is normal, Cohen-Macaulay, and Gorenstein. Moreover its canonical sheaf is O_{X_n}(−1), where O(1) is the natural ample line bundle inherited from the blowup construction X_n = Proj S[tJ].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Normality is obtained from the blowup model X_n=Proj S[tJ]. Proposition 3.8.1 implies every power J^d of the alternant ideal is free over C[y_1,...,y_n], and Corollary 3.8.3 identifies J^d with the intersection ∩_{i<j}(x_i−x_j,y_i−y_j)^d. Since powers of complete-intersection diagonal ideals are integrally closed and intersections of integrally closed ideals are integrally closed, all J^d are integrally closed; hence the Rees algebra S[tJ] is normal. The Cohen-Macaulay and Gorenstein properties are proved by induction on n. The cases n≤3 are handled directly via the n! statement in small rank. Assuming the result for n−1, form the nested isospectral Hilbert scheme X_{n−1,n}=H_{n−1,n} ×_{H_{n−1}} X_{n−1}; flatness of X_{n−1} over H_{n−1} makes X_{n−1,n} Cohen-Macaulay, and the canonical-bundle computation for H_{n−1,n} gives ω_{X_{n−1,n}}=O(0,−1). Consider the proper projection g:X_{n−1,n}→X_n. Lemma 3.8.5 is applied with z_i=y_i−y_n for i=1,...,n−1. The z_i form a regular sequence on X_n because X_n is flat over Spec C[y] by Corollary 3.8.2, and on X_{n−1,n} because this scheme is Cohen-Macaulay and its y=0 locus has the expected dimension. Away from V(z_i), product decompositions reduce Rg_*O=O to the induction hypothesis. The fiber-dimension condition follows from the estimate r ≥ binom(d+2,2) for the nested Hilbert scheme. Therefore Rg_*O_{X_{n−1,n}}=O_{X_n}. Grothendieck duality then pushes the dualizing complex O(0,−1)[2n] forward to O(−1)[2n], showing that X_n is Gorenstein with canonical sheaf O(−1).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.8.5, proper pushforward criterion via regular sequences
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let g:Y→X be a proper morphism of schemes. Let z_1,...,z_m be global regular functions on X, also viewed on Y by pullback. Let Z=V(z_1,...,z_m)⊂X and U=X∖Z. Suppose: (1) z_1,...,z_m is a regular sequence in O_{X,P} for every P in Z; (2) z_1,...,z_m is a regular sequence in O_{Y,Q} for every Q in g^{−1}(Z); (3) every fiber of g has dimension less than m−1; and (4) the natural map O_X→Rg_*O_Y restricts to an isomorphism over U. Then O_X→Rg_*O_Y is an isomorphism globally.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The assertion is local on X, so take X affine. Conditions (1) and (2) imply depth_Z O_X ≥ m and depth_{g^{−1}Z}O_Y ≥ m, so local cohomology H^i_Z(O_X) and H^i_{g^{−1}Z}(O_Y) vanish for i≤m−1. The exact sequence of local cohomology then identifies H^i(Y,O_Y) with H^i(g^{−1}U,O_Y) and H^i(X,O_X) with H^i(U,O_X) for i<m−1. Condition (4) identifies the cohomology over g^{−1}U with that over U. Condition (3) gives H^i(Y,O_Y)=0 for i≥m−1. Since X is affine, H^i(X,O_X)=0 for i>0. Combining these gives H^i(Y,O_Y)=0 for all i>0 and H^0(Y,O_Y)=H^0(X,O_X), which is exactly Rg_*O_Y=O_X.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Nested Hilbert scheme geometric inputs, Proposition 3.5.2, Proposition 3.5.3, Proposition 3.6.3, and Proposition 3.6.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let H_{n−1,n} be the reduced closed subscheme of H_{n−1}×H_n consisting of pairs (I_{n−1},I_n) with I_n⊂I_{n−1}. The paper uses the theorem that H_{n−1,n} is nonsingular and irreducible of dimension 2n. Let α:H_{n−1,n}→F be the morphism to the universal family over H_n sending (I_{n−1},I_n) to (I_n,P), where P is the distinguished added point in σ(I_n). If d is the dimension of the fiber of α over (I,P), and r is the multiplicity of P in σ(I), then r ≥ binom(d+2,2). The canonical sheaf of H_n is trivial, ω_{H_n}≅O_{H_n}, while the canonical sheaf of H_{n−1,n} is O(1,−1), where O(k,l) denotes the tensor product of the pullbacks of O(k) from H_{n−1} and O(l) from H_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Smoothness and irreducibility of H_{n−1,n} are cited from Tikhomirov/Cheah. For the fiber estimate, the possible ideals I_{n−1} over fixed (I,P) correspond to one-dimensional subspaces in the socle of the Artin local ring (C[x,y]/I)_P. Thus d+1 is the socle dimension. Among length-r Artin local rings generated by two elements, the maximal possible socle dimension s satisfies r ≥ binom(s+1,2), seen by degenerating to monomial ideals and counting corners of Young diagrams. Setting s=d+1 gives the inequality. For canonical sheaves, Haiman covers the curvilinear locus by open sets U_x and U_y where C[x,y]/I is generated by x or y. On U_x, coordinates are the elementary symmetric functions e_i of x_1,...,x_n and interpolation coefficients a_i expressing y as a polynomial in x; the invariant form dx_1...dx_n dy_1...dy_n becomes da de, so ω_{H_n} is trivial, and the complement has codimension at least two. For H_{n−1,n}, the extra distinguished point produces local coordinates and a line bundle L=O(−1,1) with fiber I_{n−1}/I_n; comparing the local top forms on U_x and U_y shows ω_{H_{n−1,n}}≅L^{−1}=O(1,−1), again extending across codimension two.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3 and Theorem 4, polygraph freeness and common ideal bases
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let k be a field of characteristic zero, E=A^2(k), and consider E^n×E^l with coordinates x_j,y_j for the n factors of E^n and a_i,b_i for the l factors of E^l. For each function f:[l]→[n], let W_f be the graph defined by a_i=x_{f(i)} and b_i=y_{f(i)} for all i. The polygraph Z(n,l) is the reduced union ⋃_f W_f, and R(n,l)=O(Z(n,l)). Theorem 3 states that R(n,l) is a free module over k[y_1,...,y_n]. The stronger Theorem 4 defines, for integers m,r,k, subspace arrangements Y(m,r,k) inside Z(n,l), with ideals I(m,r,k), by taking the union of V(x_j:j in T)∩W_f over all f and all T⊂[n] satisfying |T∩[r]∖f([k])|≥m. It asserts that R(n,l) has a k[y]-basis B such that every ideal I(m,r,k) is spanned by a subset of B.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is a long induction on n and l. The base l=0 is the polynomial ring k[x,y], with monomial basis and monomial ideals. The central local device is to work over open sets in Spec k[y]: U_1, where all y_i are distinct, and U_2, where at most one equality y_p=y_q occurs. Over U_1, the components W_f are disjoint. Over U_2∖U_1, the local geometry reduces to E^{[n]∖N}×Z(N,L) with |N|=2, so all nontrivial local checks reduce to n=2. A Hartogs-type lemma for torsion-free k[y]-modules says that a candidate basis which is a basis after localizing at every point of U_2 is a global basis, since the complement has codimension two. The case n=2 is worked out explicitly: basis elements p[e,f] indexed by e∈N^2 and f:[l]→[2] are constructed, and the ideals I(m,r,k) are shown to be spanned by precisely those p[e,f] satisfying |[r]∖({j:e_j>0}∪f([k]))|<m. The induction uses partial elimination ideals for finite algebras R[b]/J, allowing a common ideal basis in a base ring to be lifted to one extra coordinate. Special arrangements Z′(r,k) are introduced so that their partial elimination ideals are locally the ideals I(m,r,k−1). Finally, three recursive steps construct bases: from R(n−1,l) to R(n,l)/I(1,1,l); descending from R(n,l)/I(1,1,t) to R(n,l)/I(1,1,t−1); and from R(n,l)/I(1,1,0) to R(n,l). The explicit n=2 analysis verifies the local radical and elimination identities needed in the lifting steps.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.8.1 and Corollaries 3.8.2-3.8.3, freeness of powers of the alternant ideal and flatness over one set of variables
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S=C[x_1,y_1,...,x_n,y_n], let A=S^ε be the space of S_n-alternating polynomials, and let J=SA be the ideal generated by A. Proposition 3.8.1 states that J^d is a free C[y_1,...,y_n]-module for every d≥0. Consequently, the projection X_n→C^n=Spec C[y_1,...,y_n] is flat. Another consequence is the ideal identity J^d = ∩_{i<j}(x_i−x_j,y_i−y_j)^d for all n and d.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For d fixed, set l=nd and consider the polygraph Z(n,l). Let G=S_n^d act on the l pairs of a,b variables in d consecutive blocks of length n. By Theorem 3, R(n,l) is free over C[y]. Its G-alternating part R(n,l)^ε is a direct summand and hence also free over C[y]. Restrict functions from Z(n,l) to the component W_{f_0} where f_0(kn+i)=i. This sends R(n,l)^ε onto C[x,y]A^d=J^d. A G-alternating function on Z(n,l) is determined by its restriction to this component, because it vanishes on components where some block has repeated f-values and other relevant components are G-translates of W_{f_0}. Thus R(n,l)^ε≅J^d, proving freeness. The flatness of X_n over Spec C[y] follows because X_n=Proj S[tJ] and the Rees algebra has homogeneous pieces J^d free over C[y]. The intersection formula for J^d is quoted as following from the freeness result together with Haiman's earlier Proposition 6.2; it identifies powers of the alternant ideal with the intersection of powers of all pairwise diagonal ideals.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 3.9, Frobenius-series identification mechanism
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For R_µ=C[x,y]/J_µ, define its bigraded Frobenius series by F_{R_µ}(x;q,t)=∑_{r,s} t^r q^s Ψ(ch((R_µ)_{r,s})), where Ψ sends an S_n-character to its Frobenius characteristic and Ψ(χ_λ)=s_λ. The paper proves F_{R_µ}(x;q,t)=H̃_µ(x;q,t), equivalently the Schur coefficients of H̃_µ are the bigraded irreducible multiplicities in R_µ, hence also in D_µ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof uses the local ring S_µ=O_{X_n,Q_µ}. Flatness and fiber identification give F_{S_µ}=H(q,t)F_{R_µ} for an explicit scalar H(q,t), the Hilbert series of O_{H_n,I_µ}; H(q,t) is the reciprocal of a product over cells x of D(µ) involving arm and leg lengths, ∏(1−q^{−a(x)}t^{1+l(x)})∏(1−q^{1+a(x)}t^{−l(x)}). Since y_1,...,y_n form a regular sequence in S_µ, quotienting by y corresponds to plethystic substitution X→X(1−q). The quotient R_µ/(y) is identified with the Garsia-Procesi/Springer-fiber ring, whose Frobenius series is the q=0 specialization of H̃_µ and whose irreducible constituents satisfy dominance λ≥µ. This gives the first triangular condition for H̃_µ; switching x and y gives the second triangular condition; and the invariant part condition gives normalization. Proposition 2.1.1 then forces equality with H̃_µ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 6, identification with the S_n-Hilbert scheme and character sheaf interpretation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let V=(C^2)^n and G=S_n acting by permuting the n factors. Let V∥G denote the G-Hilbert scheme, the component of Hilb^{n!}(V) parametrizing G-orbits. Then the natural morphism φ:(C^2)^n∥S_n → H_n is an isomorphism, and under this isomorphism the universal family over the G-Hilbert scheme is identified with the isospectral Hilbert scheme X_n. If P=ρ_*O_{X_n} is the tautological rank n! bundle on H_n≅V∥G and V^λ is the irreducible S_n-module with character χ_λ, then the character sheaf C_λ=Hom_{S_n}(V^λ,P) has rank dim V^λ. The transformed Kostka-Macdonald coefficient H̃K_{λµ}(q,t) is the T^2-character, equivalently the doubly graded Hilbert series, of the fiber of C_λ at the torus-fixed point I_µ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the first morphism, take the universal family Y over (C^2)^n∥S_n. The quotient Y/S_{n−1} is a flat family of degree n naturally embedded as a family of subschemes of C^2; the universal property of H_n gives φ. Conversely, Theorem 1 implies that X_n is flat of degree n! over H_n, so X_n defines a morphism H_n→Hilb^{n!}((C^2)^n), whose image lies in the S_n-Hilbert scheme. The two morphisms are inverse on the generic locus of distinct points, hence inverse everywhere by irreducibility. The character sheaf statement follows by decomposing P as ⊕_λ C_λ⊗V^λ and evaluating at the torus-fixed point I_µ; Proposition 3.7.3 identifies this fiber with R_µ, and Theorem 2 computes its graded irreducible multiplicities as H̃K_{λµ}(q,t).

proof_techniques.name: Realizing graded multiplicities as Hilbert-scheme fibers
proof_techniques.description: The paper converts the algebraic module R_µ=C[x,y]/J_µ, dual to the derivative span D_µ, into the scheme-theoretic fiber of the finite morphism ρ:X_n→H_n over the monomial ideal I_µ. This is achieved by describing X_n inside the n-fold universal family F^n using the alternation pairing B^{⊗n}⊗B^{⊗n}→∧^nB and then specializing the pairing at I_µ. Flatness and Cohen-Macaulayness of X_n imply that all fibers have the expected length n!, and Gorensteinness identifies the fiber ideal exactly with the differential annihilator J_µ.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For Schur coefficients in multigraded coinvariant algebras, one can try to realize the relevant coefficients as graded fibers or isotypic components of tautological or character sheaves on a Hilbert or G-Hilbert scheme. Hook coefficients might then be isolated by exterior-power or sign-twisted pieces of such bundles.

proof_techniques.name: Frobenius characteristic plus Macdonald triangularity recognition
proof_techniques.description: Rather than compute every irreducible multiplicity directly, Haiman packages the bigraded S_n-character into a Frobenius series F(x;q,t). He proves that F satisfies the same two plethystic triangularity conditions and normalization as H̃_µ. The triangularity is obtained from a simpler quotient, R_µ/(y), already known through the Garsia-Procesi/Springer-fiber model, while the second condition follows by x/y symmetry.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the universal coefficients c_λ(n) are Schur coefficients of a stable Hilbert series, a comparable tactic is to identify a Frobenius or symmetric-function series and prove it satisfies a characterization whose hook coefficients are known or easier to extract after a specialization.

proof_techniques.name: Alternation pairing and coefficient-of-determinant test
proof_techniques.description: The ideal J_µ is characterized by testing whether Θ_ε(gp) has zero Δ_µ coefficient for every g. Geometrically, the same test appears as the kernel of the alternation pairing on B^{⊗n}. This turns a differential-annihilator question into a statement about alternating components and determinant bases.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Hook Schur functions are closely tied to exterior powers and alternating constructions. This coefficient-testing method suggests looking for determinant or alternant bases in m-set coinvariant algebras and characterizing hook multiplicities through projections onto alternating or near-alternating components.

proof_techniques.name: Polygraph freeness via codimension-two local checking
proof_techniques.description: The polygraph theorem proves freeness over one set of variables by constructing a common ideal basis. A crucial local-to-global principle says that for torsion-free k[y]-modules it suffices to verify a proposed basis after localization over the open set where at most two y-coordinates coincide, because the complement has codimension two. Over this open set, the local geometry reduces to the n=2 case, which is explicitly computable.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For m-set diagonal or generalized coinvariant algebras, freeness or stability over one grading set may be approachable by reducing local collisions to two-particle calculations. This is especially relevant if c_λ(n) is independent of m because such independence often reflects a hidden free-module or stable-basis structure.

proof_techniques.name: Common ideal bases for arrangements
proof_techniques.description: Haiman strengthens plain freeness of R(n,l) to the existence of a basis simultaneously adapted to many subspace-arrangement ideals I(m,r,k). The induction requires carefully designed arrangements Y(m,r,k) whose ideals form a controlled lattice locally, and whose quotients remain torsion-free or free over k[y]. This allows successive extensions of bases while retaining compatibility with all needed ideals.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A hook coefficient formula may require tracking not just the full Hilbert series but a filtration or family of quotients corresponding to descent, support, or exterior-power conditions. A common-basis framework could organize such filtrations and make combinatorial indexing possible.

proof_techniques.name: Partial elimination ideals for lifting bases
proof_techniques.description: Given a finite algebra S=R[b]/J over a base ring R, Haiman uses the partial elimination ideals I_m consisting of leading coefficients of relations of degree m−1 in b. Geometrically, V(I_m) records where the fiber of Spec S→Spec R has length at least m. If R has a common ideal basis compatible with these I_m, one lifts it to a basis of S by adjoining 1,b,...,b^{m−1} to the appropriate basis elements.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If an m-set coinvariant algebra is built by adding one set of variables at a time, partial elimination ideals could give an inductive way to lift bases and track how Schur or hook coefficients change when m increases.

proof_techniques.name: Nested Hilbert scheme induction and duality
proof_techniques.description: The proof of Gorensteinness of X_n uses X_{n−1,n} as an intermediary between X_{n−1} and X_n. After proving the nested scheme is Cohen-Macaulay and computing its canonical bundle, Haiman proves Rg_*O=O for the projection to X_n using a regular-sequence/local-cohomology lemma. Grothendieck duality then transports the dualizing sheaf from the nested scheme to X_n.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For modules whose Hilbert series satisfy recursions in n, nested Hilbert schemes or analogous induction correspondences may supply structural recurrences. Hook-shape coefficients often admit branching rules, so an n−1 to n geometric correspondence could be relevant.

proof_techniques.name: Use of q=0 Springer-fiber/Garsia-Procesi model as a base case
proof_techniques.description: The two-parameter Macdonald character formula is proved by reducing one triangularity condition to the q=0 quotient R_µ/(y), which was already understood as a Springer-fiber cohomology ring by Garsia-Procesi. This imports known Hall-Littlewood/t-Kostka positivity and dominance restrictions into the two-variable setting.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For hook coefficients of multigraded coinvariant algebras, one may seek a one-variable specialization with a known combinatorial model, such as major index, descent sets, or Springer theory, and then lift the result to the multigraded or stable Schur expansion.

proof_techniques.name: Character sheaves on the G-Hilbert scheme
proof_techniques.description: After identifying H_n with (C^2)^n∥S_n, the tautological bundle P decomposes according to irreducible S_n-representations as P=⊕_λ C_λ⊗V^λ. The fiber of C_λ at the fixed point I_µ has T^2-character H̃K_{λµ}(q,t). This gives a geometric vector-bundle interpretation of Schur coefficients in transformed Macdonald polynomials.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The universal coefficients c_λ(n) in Schur expansions may similarly be interpretable as Hilbert series or dimensions of fibers of character sheaves, with hook λ corresponding to specific irreducible representations such as exterior powers of the reflection representation.

other_useful_info: The paper explicitly discusses the diagonal coinvariant ring R_n=C[x,y]/I_n, where I_n is generated by S_n-invariant polynomials without constant term; it states that R_n has the same graded character as diagonal harmonics and recalls conjectures, not proved in this paper, that dim R_n=(n+1)^{n−1} and that bases should be indexed by parking functions. Section 5.3 emphasizes that Theorem 2 proves positivity and the n! theorem but does not give an explicit combinatorial basis for R_µ or D_µ; Problem 5.3.1 asks for such a basis giving a purely combinatorial interpretation of H̃K_{λµ}(q,t). Problem 5.3.2 asks for a doubly homogeneous S_n-compatible basis of the diagonal coinvariant ring R_n with distinguished subsets giving bases of each R_µ. Problem 5.3.3 relates the polygraph basis of R(n,n) to a hoped-for parking-function basis of R_n. Section 5.2 proposes a higher-dimensional polygraph freeness conjecture for E=A^d, namely that the coordinate ring of the corresponding polygraph is free over one set of variables; this is potentially relevant to coinvariant algebras with more than two sets of variables. The paper itself contains no hook-shape combinatorial interpretation for the universal coefficients c_λ(n) in the m-set coinvariant Hilbert series.
why_relevant: Proves the n! conjecture and Macdonald positivity using Hilbert schemes and isospectral Hilbert schemes. These geometric methods underpin the positivity and representation-theoretic interpretation of diagonal coinvariant modules.

title: Vanishing Theorems and Character Formulas for the Hilbert Scheme of Points in the Plane
overall_summary: Haiman proves vanishing theorems for tensor products P ⊗ B^⊗l of the unusual tautological bundle P and the usual tautological bundle B on H_n = Hilb^n(C^2), and also for their restrictions to the zero fiber Z_n of the Chow morphism. These vanishings identify spaces of global sections with polygraph coordinate rings R(n,l), and identify the diagonal coinvariant ring C[x,y]/C[x,y]^{S_n}_+ C[x,y] with H^0(Z_n,P). Applying Atiyah-Bott localization at the torus-fixed monomial ideals I_μ, together with the fact that the fiber character of P at I_μ is the transformed Macdonald polynomial Htilde_μ, gives the character formula for diagonal harmonics: its Frobenius series is ∇ e_n. The paper also proves the operator conjecture in two sets of variables, showing that diagonal harmonics are generated from the Vandermonde determinant by partial derivatives and polarization operators. For the original problem, the most relevant extracted material is the precise two-variable diagonal coinvariant character formula, its parking-function and q,t-Catalan specializations, and the refined Schur-functor formulas for tensor powers of B, since these are the paper's concrete combinatorial and representation-theoretic outputs.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let H_n = Hilb^n(C^2). Let F_n be the universal family over H_n and B = π_* O_{F_n} the usual tautological rank n bundle. Identify H_n with the Hilbert scheme C^{2n}//S_n of regular S_n-orbits for the diagonal action on C^{2n}; let X_n be the isospectral Hilbert scheme with projection ρ: X_n → H_n, and let P = ρ_* O_{X_n}, the rank n! tautological bundle with S_n action. For l ≥ 0, let Z(n,l) be the reduced polygraph in C^{2n+2l} consisting of tuples (P_1,...,P_n,Q_1,...,Q_l), with P_i,Q_j in C^2, such that every Q_j is equal to one of the P_i; let R(n,l) be its coordinate ring. Then H^i(H_n, P ⊗ B^⊗l) = 0 for every i > 0, and the canonical map R(n,l) → H^0(H_n, P ⊗ B^⊗l) obtained by pulling functions on Z(n,l) back to X_n ×_{H_n} F_n^l is an isomorphism.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The map R(n,l) → H^0(H_n, P ⊗ B^⊗l) is constructed from W = X_n ×_{H_n} F_n^l: a point of W is (I,P_1,...,P_n,Q_1,...,Q_l) with σ(I) = [[P_1,...,P_n]] and Q_j in V(I), hence the image in C^{2n+2l} lies in Z(n,l). The proof is postponed to Section 5 and is recast as a statement in the derived category using the Bridgeland-King-Reid equivalence. For V = C^{2n}, G = S_n, the equivalence becomes Φ = RΓ(P ⊗ −): D(H_n) → D_{S_n}(C^{2n}); Theorem 2.1 is equivalent to Φ(B^⊗l) ≅ R(n,l). The inverse functor is Ψ = O(−1) ⊗ (ρ_* Lf^*(−))^ε, where ε denotes S_n-alternants. Using the freeness theorem that R(n,l) is a free C[y_1,...,y_n]-module and its consequences, one chooses an S_n-equivariant C[x,y]-free resolution of R(n,l) of length n−1; the mapping cone of ΨR(n,l) → B^⊗l is a bounded complex of locally free sheaves. Exactness is proved first on the open locus where the support of the corresponding length n scheme has at least two distinct points, by induction on n and the product decomposition of X_n and of the polygraph over that locus. Exactness is then checked on the curvilinear charts U_x and U_y using explicit coordinates and Lagrange interpolation; finally the new intersection theorem implies global exactness because the remaining complement has sufficiently large codimension. This proves ΨR(n,l) ≅ B^⊗l and hence Theorem 2.1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Propositions 2.3 and 2.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let tr: B → O_{H_n} be the trace map, so that tr(x^r y^s) = p_{r,s}(x,y) = ∑_{i=1}^n x_i^r y_i^s when x^r y^s is viewed as a global section of B. Let B' = ker(tr), so B = O_{H_n} ⊕ B'. Let O_t and O_q be copies of O_{H_n} with T^2 weights t and q. Define ν: B' ⊕ O_t ⊕ O_q → B by inclusion on B' and by sending the generators of O_t and O_q to x and y. Let ξ: B ⊗ (B' ⊕ O_t ⊕ O_q) → B be multiplication after 1 ⊗ ν. If J is the sheaf of ideals in B generated by x, y, and B', then B/J ≅ O_{Z_n}, where Z_n = σ^{-1}(0) is the zero fiber of the Chow morphism σ: H_n → S^n C^2. Moreover, the Koszul complex on ξ is a T^2-equivariant locally free resolution of O_{Z_n}: ... → B ⊗ ∧^k(B' ⊕ O_t ⊕ O_q) → ... → B ⊗ (B' ⊕ O_t ⊕ O_q) → B → O_{Z_n} → 0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The trace formula is verified on the generic locus where B(I) is the coordinate ring of n distinct points, so multiplication by x^r y^s has eigenvalues x_i^r y_i^s. The sections x^r y^s − (1/n)p_{r,s}(x,y) generate B'. The ideal J is generated by x, y, and these trace-zero sections; set-theoretically these equations force x = y = 0 and all polarized power sums p_{r,s} to vanish, hence they cut out the preimage of the zero fiber. Weyl's theorem that the polarized power sums generate the invariant ring C[x,y]^{S_n} is used to identify the zero fiber scheme-theoretically. Since F_n is flat finite over the smooth scheme H_n, it is Cohen-Macaulay; the locus cut out by J has the expected codimension n+1, equal to the rank of B' ⊕ O_t ⊕ O_q. Therefore J is locally generated by a regular sequence. The resulting Koszul complex is exact and resolves B/J ≅ O_{Z_n}; generic reducedness then gives the reduced zero fiber.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: With H_n, B, P, R(n,l), and Z_n as above, let m = C[x,y]^{S_n}_+ be the homogeneous maximal ideal in the invariant ring C[x_1,y_1,...,x_n,y_n]^{S_n}. For every l ≥ 0, H^i(Z_n, P ⊗ B^⊗l) = 0 for i > 0, and the canonical restriction map induces an isomorphism H^0(Z_n, P ⊗ B^⊗l) ≅ R(n,l)/m R(n,l). In particular, for l = 0, H^0(Z_n,P) is the diagonal coinvariant ring C[x,y]/m C[x,y] as a doubly graded S_n-module.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Tensor the locally free Koszul resolution of O_{Z_n} from Proposition 2.4 with P ⊗ B^⊗l. Each term is a direct sum of bundles P ⊗ B^⊗r with degree shifts, so Theorem 2.1 makes all higher cohomology vanish termwise. The complex of global sections therefore computes H^*(Z_n, P ⊗ B^⊗l), and since the resolution has no positive cohomological degrees, the higher cohomology vanishes. For H^0, the last nontrivial terms identify via Theorem 2.1 with R(n,l+1) and modules built from R(n,l+2); using coordinates x,y for the extra B factor, the image of the last differential is the ideal J = (x,y) + m R(n,l+1). Because x and y generate R(n,l+1) as an R(n,l)-module, R(n,l+1)/J is a quotient of R(n,l)/mR(n,l). To prove that no larger kernel occurs, Haiman uses the splitting B = O ⊕ B' and the projection θ: R(n,l+1) → R(n,l) satisfying θ(x^r y^s) = (1/n)p_{r,s}(x,y). This projection sends J into mR(n,l), inducing a left inverse to R(n,l)/mR(n,l) → R(n,l+1)/J. Thus H^0 is exactly R(n,l)/mR(n,l).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let T^2 = (C*)^2 act on C^2 by τ_{t,q} x = t x and τ_{t,q} y = q y, and hence on H_n.
