For a partition μ of n, let d(μ) = {(i,j) in N^2 : j < μ_{i+1}} be its Ferrers diagram, and let I_μ be the monomial ideal spanned by all x^r y^s with (r,s) not in d(μ). The T^2-fixed points of H_n are exactly the ideals I_μ for partitions μ of n. If x is a cell of d(μ), with arm a(x) and leg l(x), then the cotangent space of H_n at I_μ has a T^2-eigenbasis {u_x,d_x : x in d(μ)} with weights τ_{t,q} d_x = t^{1+l(x)} q^{−a(x)} d_x and τ_{t,q} u_x = t^{−l(x)} q^{1+a(x)} u_x.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: An ideal I in C[x,y] is fixed by the diagonal torus if and only if it is doubly homogeneous, equivalently monomial. Since C[x,y]/I has dimension n, the monomials outside I form a Ferrers diagram of a partition μ of n, giving I = I_μ. The cotangent weights are those computed by Ellingsrud and Strømme; Haiman recalls that in his earlier work explicit local coordinates produce eigenvectors u_x and d_x with the displayed weights. These weights are then inserted into the equivariant Euler class denominator in localization.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Propositions 3.2 and 3.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a T^2-equivariant locally free sheaf of finite rank on H_n. Write H_A^i(q,t) for the Hilbert series of H^i(H_n,A), and χ_A(q,t) = ∑_i (−1)^i H_A^i(q,t). Then χ_A(q,t) = ∑_{|μ|=n} H_{A(I_μ)}(q,t) / ∏_{x in d(μ)}(1 − t^{1+l(x)} q^{−a(x)})(1 − t^{−l(x)} q^{1+a(x)}). If A also has an S_n action commuting with T^2, the same formula holds for Frobenius series: χF_A(z;q,t) is the same fixed-point sum with H_{A(I_μ)} replaced by the Frobenius series F_{A(I_μ)}(z;q,t).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The formula is the Atiyah-Bott fixed point formula adapted to the nonprojective setting. Haiman justifies it via Thomason's algebraic equivariant K-theory Lefschetz theorem. Apply Thomason to the proper Chow morphism σ: H_n → S^n C^2. The fixed locus in S^n C^2 is the origin, while the fixed locus in H_n consists of the finitely many I_μ. In localized equivariant K-theory, pushing forward a class equals the sum over fixed points divided by the exterior class of the conormal bundle. The conormal weights at I_μ are exactly the cotangent weights from Proposition 3.1, producing the denominator. Applying the Hilbert-series character map from equivariant K-theory to Q(q,t) gives the displayed formula; decomposing into S_n-isotypic components gives the Frobenius version.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For each partition μ of n, the Frobenius series of the S_n-module fiber P(I_μ) is the transformed Macdonald polynomial Htilde_μ(z;q,t). Explicitly, Htilde_μ(z;q,t) = t^{n(μ)} J_μ[Z/(1−t^{−1}); q,t^{−1}], where J_μ is the integral form Macdonald polynomial, Z = z_1+z_2+..., and n(μ)=∑_i (i−1)μ_i. Equivalently, Htilde_μ = ∑_λ Ktilde_{λμ}(q,t) s_λ(z), and Ktilde_{λμ}(q,t) is the Hilbert series of the λ-isotypic character bundle fiber P_λ(I_μ).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is imported from Haiman's earlier paper on Hilbert schemes, polygraphs, and Macdonald positivity. The isospectral Hilbert scheme X_n realizes the Garsia-Haiman modules as fibers of P at the torus fixed points I_μ. The n! theorem and the identification of these fiber characters with transformed Macdonald polynomials give F_{P(I_μ)} = Htilde_μ. In this paper the proposition is used as a fixed-point input rather than reproved.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 3.6 and 3.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a finitely generated doubly graded C[x_1,y_1,...,x_n,y_n]-module with an equivariant S_n action. If x_1,...,x_n form an M-regular sequence, then F_M(z;q,t) = F_{M/(x)M}[Z/(1−t); q,t] in plethystic notation; similarly, if y_1,...,y_n form an M-regular sequence, then one has the analogous formula with q in place of t. As a consequence, the Frobenius series of the polynomial ring C[x,y] is F_{C[x,y]}(z;q,t) = h_n[Z/((1−q)(1−t))].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The regular sequence condition allows the Hilbert-Frobenius series to be computed from the Koszul resolution on the variables x_i. Passing from M/(x)M to M multiplies the character by the symmetric algebra on the natural permutation representation graded in x-degree, which is plethystically encoded by replacing Z with Z/(1−t). Repeating for the y variables gives the second substitution. For C[x,y], first mod out by x_1,...,x_n and then by y_1,...,y_n; the remaining quotient is the trivial S_n-representation C, whose Frobenius characteristic is h_n = s_{(n)}. This yields h_n[Z/((1−q)(1−t))].

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.5 and Corollaries 3.8-3.9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let B_μ(q,t) = ∑_{(r,s) in d(μ)} t^r q^s. Let F_{R(n,l)}(z;q,t) denote the Frobenius series of R(n,l) as a doubly graded S_n-module. Then F_{R(n,l)}(z;q,t) = ∑_{|μ|=n} B_μ(q,t)^l Htilde_μ(z;q,t) / ∏_{x in d(μ)}(1 − t^{1+l(x)} q^{−a(x)})(1 − t^{−l(x)} q^{1+a(x)}). If Δ is the linear operator on symmetric functions defined by Δ Htilde_μ = B_μ(q,t) Htilde_μ, then equivalently F_{R(n,l)}(z;q,t) = Δ^l h_n[Z/((1−q)(1−t))]. The ordinary Hilbert series is H_{R(n,l)}(q,t) = <s_1^n, Δ^l h_n[Z/((1−q)(1−t))]> = ((1−q)^n(1−t)^n)^{−1} <e_n, Δ^l s_1^n>, where < , > is the Hall inner product.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Theorem 2.1, R(n,l) is H^0(H_n,P ⊗ B^⊗l), with no higher cohomology. Apply the Atiyah-Bott formula to A = P ⊗ B^⊗l. The fiber B(I_μ) has monomial basis {x^r y^s : (r,s) in d(μ)}, so its Hilbert series is B_μ(q,t), and the tensor power contributes B_μ(q,t)^l. Proposition 3.4 supplies F_{P(I_μ)} = Htilde_μ. The fixed point denominator comes from the cotangent weights in Proposition 3.1. For the operator form, observe from the fixed point formula that increasing l applies Δ, and for l = 0 one has R(n,0)=C[x,y], whose Frobenius series is h_n[Z/((1−q)(1−t))] by Lemma 3.7. The second Hilbert-series expression uses the Macdonald star inner product, in which the Htilde_μ are orthogonal and Δ is self-adjoint.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.10 and Proposition 3.11
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R_n = C[x_1,y_1,...,x_n,y_n]/m C[x_1,y_1,...,x_n,y_n], where m = C[x,y]^{S_n}_+ is the homogeneous maximal ideal of diagonal invariants. Equivalently, R_n is dual to the space DH_n of diagonal harmonics. Define Π_μ(q,t) = ∏_{(r,s) in d(μ), (r,s) ≠ (0,0)} (1 − t^r q^s). Then the Frobenius series of R_n is F_{R_n}(z;q,t) = ∑_{|μ|=n} (1−q)(1−t) Π_μ(q,t) B_μ(q,t) Htilde_μ(z;q,t) / ∏_{x in d(μ)}(1 − t^{1+l(x)} q^{−a(x)})(1 − t^{−l(x)} q^{1+a(x)}). Equivalently, if ∇ is defined by ∇ Htilde_μ = t^{n(μ)} q^{n(μ')} Htilde_μ, then F_{R_n}(z;q,t) = ∇ e_n(z).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Theorem 2.2 with l = 0 identifies R_n with H^0(Z_n,P) and gives higher cohomology vanishing. To compute the Euler characteristic by localization on H_n, replace O_{Z_n} with the Koszul resolution of Proposition 2.4. Its k-th term is B ⊗ ∧^k(B' ⊕ O_t ⊕ O_q). At I_μ, the eigenvalues on B' ⊕ O_t ⊕ O_q are q, t, and the monomials t^r q^s for nonzero cells (r,s) in d(μ). Hence the alternating exterior sum contributes (1−q)(1−t)Π_μ(q,t). The extra B factor contributes B_μ(q,t), and P contributes Htilde_μ by Proposition 3.4. Dividing by the cotangent Euler class gives the displayed sum. Haiman then cites the reformulation developed with Garsia: this fixed-point sum is exactly ∇ e_n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.12
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the diagonal coinvariant ring R_n, the specialization t = q^{−1} of the Frobenius series satisfies q^{binom(n,2)} F_{R_n}(z;q,q^{−1}) = (1 + q + ... + q^n)^{−1} h_n[Z(1−q^{n+1})/(1−q)] = ∑_{|λ|=n} s_λ(1,q,...,q^n)/(1+q+...+q^n) · s_λ(z). Consequently q^{binom(n,2)} H_{R_n}(q,q^{−1}) = (1+q+...+q^n)^{n−1}, and in particular dim R_n = (n+1)^{n−1}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper states this as a consequence of known specializations of transformed Macdonald polynomials and of the formula F_{R_n}=∇e_n, as developed in Garsia-Haiman. The first equality is a plethystic symmetric-function specialization; expanding h_n in Schur functions gives the second equality through principal specialization s_λ(1,q,...,q^n). The ordinary Hilbert series is recovered by pairing the Frobenius series with s_1^n. Setting q = 1 after the specialization t = q^{−1} gives the dimension formula (n+1)^{n−1}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.13
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A parking function is a function f: {1,...,n} → {1,...,n} such that |f^{−1}({1,...,k})| ≥ k for every 1 ≤ k ≤ n. Its weight is w(f)=∑_{i=1}^n f(i) − i. Let PF_{n,d} be the set of parking functions of weight d, and let S_n act by permuting the domain. Then F_{R_n}(z;q,1) = ∑_d q^d φ char(ε ⊗ C PF_{n,d}), where ε is the sign representation and φ is the Frobenius characteristic map. Thus, after ignoring x-degree and retaining y-degree, R_n is isomorphic as a graded S_n-module to ε ⊗ C PF_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The result is stated as another consequence of the known t = 1 specialization of the Macdonald-side formula F_{R_n}=∇e_n. The symmetric-function specialization matches the graded Frobenius characteristic of the parking-function permutation representation, up to tensoring by the sign representation. Since the number of parking functions is (n+1)^{n−1}, this also recovers the dimension formula. The paper does not reprove the parking-function enumeration; it uses the earlier Garsia-Haiman analysis.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.14 and Proposition 3.15
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R_n^ε be the S_n-alternating subspace of the diagonal coinvariant ring R_n. Its Hilbert series is H_{R_n^ε}(q,t) = <e_n(z), F_{R_n}(z;q,t)> = C_n(q,t), where C_n(q,t) = ∑_{|μ|=n} t^{n(μ)} q^{n(μ')} (1−q)(1−t) Π_μ(q,t) B_μ(q,t) / ∏_{x in d(μ)}(1 − t^{1+l(x)} q^{−a(x)})(1 − t^{−l(x)} q^{1+a(x)}). This is the q,t-Catalan polynomial. The paper also cites Garsia-Haglund's combinatorial formula: if D_n is the set of sequences e=(e_1,...,e_n) of nonnegative integers with e_1=0 and e_{k+1} ≤ e_k+1, if |e|=∑ e_i, and if i(e) is the number of pairs i<j such that e_j=e_i or e_j=e_i−1, then C_n(q,t)=∑_{e in D_n} t^{|e|} q^{i(e)}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Taking the Hall inner product of Theorem 3.10 with e_n extracts the alternating isotypic component. The required scalar product is <e_n,Htilde_μ> = Ktilde_{(1^n),μ}(q,t) = t^{n(μ)}q^{n(μ')}; geometrically this is the T^2-character of the fiber ∧^n B(I_μ)=O(1)(I_μ). Substituting this into the diagonal coinvariant fixed-point formula gives C_n(q,t). The positivity and explicit Dyck-sequence formula are not proved in this paper; Haiman cites the then-recent proof of Garsia and Haglund.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.18 and Corollary 3.19
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S_l act on R(n,l) by permuting the l auxiliary pairs (a_i,b_i), equivalently by permuting tensor factors in B^⊗l. For a partition ν of l, define R(n,l)_ν = Hom_{S_l}(V^ν,R(n,l)). Also set S(n,l,ν) = (R(n,l)/mR(n,l))_ν. Then F_{R(n,l)_ν}(z;q,t) = ∑_{|μ|=n} s_ν[B_μ(q,t)] Htilde_μ(z;q,t) / ∏_{x in d(μ)}(1 − t^{1+l(x)} q^{−a(x)})(1 − t^{−l(x)} q^{1+a(x)}), and F_{S(n,l,ν)}(z;q,t) = ∑_{|μ|=n} (1−q)(1−t)Π_μ(q,t)B_μ(q,t)s_ν[B_μ(q,t)]Htilde_μ(z;q,t) / ∏_{x in d(μ)}(1 − t^{1+l(x)} q^{−a(x)})(1 − t^{−l(x)} q^{1+a(x)}). If Δ_f is defined by Δ_f Htilde_μ = f[B_μ(q,t)] Htilde_μ, then these become F_{R(n,l)_ν} = Δ_{s_ν} h_n[Z/((1−q)(1−t))] and F_{S(n,l,ν)} = Δ_{s_ν}∇e_n = Δ_{e_n s_ν} e_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The S_l-isotypic component R(n,l)_ν corresponds geometrically to H^0(H_n, P ⊗ S_ν(B)), where S_ν is the Schur functor. Schur's classical trace formula gives H_{S_ν(B(I_μ))}(q,t) = s_ν[B_μ(q,t)], because the eigenvalues of B(I_μ) are precisely the monomials t^r q^s for cells of d(μ). Applying localization with Theorem 2.1 gives the first formula. For S(n,l,ν), use the zero-fiber theorem and the same Koszul-resolution correction factor as in Theorem 3.10, namely (1−q)(1−t)Π_μ(q,t)B_μ(q,t). The operator expressions follow directly from the definition of Δ_f and the l=0 cases.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.21
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every partition ν, the Euler-characteristic Frobenius series of the sheaf O_{Z_n} ⊗ P^* ⊗ S_ν(B) on H_n is χF_{O_{Z_n}⊗P^*⊗S_ν(B)}(z;q,t) = Δ_{s_ν} e_n(z).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Equation F_{S(n,l,ν)} = Δ_{s_ν}∇e_n is obtained for P rather than P^*. The line bundle O(1)=∧^nB has fiber character t^{n(μ)}q^{n(μ')} at I_μ, and Haiman recalls from earlier work that P^* ≅ O(−1) ⊗ P. Replacing P by P^* therefore divides each fixed-point numerator by t^{n(μ)}q^{n(μ')}, cancelling the ∇ eigenvalue. The result is Δ_{s_ν}e_n. This is an Euler characteristic statement, not a higher-cohomology vanishing theorem.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.1 and Theorem 4.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let DH_n = {f in C[x_1,y_1,...,x_n,y_n] : p(∂_x,∂_y)f = 0 for every p in C[x,y]^{S_n}_+} be the space of diagonal harmonics. For k>0 define the polarization operators E_k = ∑_{i=1}^n y_i ∂_{x_i}^k. Then DH_n is closed under all E_k. Moreover, if Δ(x)=∏_{i<j}(x_i−x_j) is the Vandermonde determinant, then DH_n is generated by Δ(x) as a module over the commutative algebra C[∂_{x_1},...,∂_{x_n},E_1,...,E_{n−1}]; that is, DH_n = C[∂_{x_1},...,∂_{x_n},E_1,...,E_{n−1}] Δ(x).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The closure statement was already proved in Haiman's earlier work and is recalled here. For generation, introduce the symmetric differential inner product (f,g)=g(∂_x,∂_y)f evaluated at x=y=0. Under this pairing, DH_n is the orthogonal complement of I=mC[x,y]. Let OP_n be the space generated from Δ(x) by the displayed operators. Since OP_n ⊆ DH_n, it is enough to show OP_n^⊥ ⊆ I. The adjoints of E_k are E_k^* = ∑ x_i^k ∂_{y_i}. Using the exponential of ∑ λ_k E_k^*, Haiman proves that f in OP_n^⊥ is equivalent to f(x,φ_λ(x)) lying in the classical coinvariant ideal (e_1(x),...,e_n(x)) for φ_λ(z)=λ_{n−1}z^{n−1}+...+λ_1z. This uses Steinberg's theorem that ordinary harmonics are generated by Δ(x) under x-derivatives. Finally, Proposition 4.5 shows that this substitution condition implies f in mC[x,y], using Theorem 2.2 and explicit coordinates on the curvilinear chart U_x of H_n. Hence OP_n^⊥=I and OP_n=DH_n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Propositions 4.4 and 4.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let OP_n = C[∂_{x_1},...,∂_{x_n},E_1,...,E_{n−1}]Δ(x), and let φ_λ(z)=λ_{n−1}z^{n−1}+...+λ_1z. A polynomial f in C[x,y] lies in OP_n^⊥ if and only if f(x,φ_λ(x)) lies in the ordinary coinvariant ideal (e_1(x),...,e_n(x)) identically in the parameters λ. If f satisfies this substitution condition, then f lies in mC[x,y], where m=C[x,y]^{S_n}_+.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Proposition 4.4, take adjoints in the differential inner product: ∂_{x_i} is adjoint to multiplication by x_i, and E_k is adjoint to E_k^*=∑ x_i^k ∂_{y_i}. The exponential exp(∑λ_k E_k^*) acts by substituting y_i+φ_λ(x_i) for y_i; after setting y=0 this gives f(x,φ_λ(x)). Orthogonality to all x-multiples of Δ(x), together with Steinberg's ordinary harmonic theorem, is exactly membership in (e_1,...,e_n). For Proposition 4.5, use the chart U_x in H_n consisting of ideals I for which 1,x,...,x^{n−1} is a basis of B(I). Its coordinates e_1,...,e_n,γ_0,...,γ_{n−1} give universal-family equations x^n−e_1x^{n−1}+...+(−1)^n e_n=0 and y=γ_{n−1}x^{n−1}+...+γ_0. On the preimage in X_n, y_i=φ_γ(x_i). The zero fiber inside U_x is defined by γ_0=0 and e_1=...=e_n=0. If f(x,φ_γ(x)) is in (e_1,...,e_n), then the section of P represented by f vanishes on the dense open curvilinear part of Z_n, hence on Z_n. The isomorphism H^0(Z_n,P)=C[x,y]/mC[x,y] from Theorem 2.2 then forces f in mC[x,y].

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.1, Corollary 5.2, and Proposition 5.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Bridgeland-King-Reid's theorem says that if a finite subgroup G of SL(V) has Hilbert scheme of regular orbits V//G whose Chow morphism to V/G satisfies a smallness criterion, then V//G is a crepant resolution and the Fourier-Mukai functor Φ=Rf_*∘ρ^*:D(V//G)→D_G(V) is an equivalence. In the case V=C^{2n}, G=S_n, Haiman uses the isomorphism C^{2n}//S_n ≅ H_n and obtains an equivalence Φ=RΓ(P⊗−):D(H_n)→D_{S_n}(C^{2n}). The inverse is Ψ=O(−1)⊗(ρ_*∘Lf^*)^ε, where ε denotes the S_n-sign isotypic part and O(1)=∧^nB.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The diagonal action of S_n on C^{2n} lies in SL(C^{2n}). Haiman had previously proved C^{2n}//S_n ≅ H_n; the Hilbert scheme H_n is a crepant resolution of S^nC^2, and the required smallness condition follows either from Briançon's description of Chow fibers or from the symplectic converse noted by Bridgeland-King-Reid. Because ρ: X_n→H_n is finite and P=ρ_*O_{X_n}, the Fourier-Mukai functor becomes RΓ(P⊗−). Grothendieck duality gives the inverse. The canonical sheaf of X_n is O(−1), with the sign equivariant structure, so the inverse simplifies to O(−1)⊗(ρ_*Lf^*(−))^ε. This equivalence is the categorical framework used to prove Theorem 2.1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.6 and Corollaries 5.7-5.9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the polygraph coordinate ring R(n,l), viewed as a quotient of C[x_1,y_1,...,x_n,y_n,a_1,b_1,...,a_l,b_l], R(n,l) is a free module over C[y_1,...,y_n]. Consequently, by translation invariance in the x-direction, R(n,l) is also free over C[x_1,y_1,...,y_n] for any chosen coordinate x_1, and R(n,l) has a free C[x_1,y_1,...,x_n,y_n]-resolution of length n−1. More generally, if S is a C-algebra and y' is the image of y under an S-algebra automorphism of S[x,y], then S⊗_C R(n,l) is free over S[y'_1,...,y'_n].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The freeness theorem is quoted from Haiman's earlier polygraph paper, where an explicit basis construction for the union of subspaces defining Z(n,l) is given. The corollaries use the fact that automorphisms of C^2 induce automorphisms of the polygraph and preserve its defining ideal. Translations in the x-direction show that after modding out by all y_i, any coordinate x_i or a_j is a non-zero-divisor; this gives freeness over a polynomial ring enlarged by one x-coordinate. The existence of a free C[x,y]-resolution of length n−1 follows from this freeness and standard homological dimension considerations. The automorphism variant is used in the U_x chart, where y_i is replaced by y_i−φ_γ(x_i).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let U_k be the open subset of X_n consisting of points (I,P_1,...,P_n) for which the sets {P_1,...,P_k} and {P_{k+1},...,P_n} are disjoint. Then U_k is isomorphic to an open subset of X_k × X_{n−k}, compatibly with the maps to C^{2k}×C^{2(n−k)}=C^{2n}. Over U_k, the pullback of the universal family F_n decomposes as the disjoint union of the pullbacks from H_k and H_{n−k}; equivalently, ρ^*B decomposes as η_k^*ρ_k^*B_k ⊕ η_{n−k}^*ρ_{n−k}^*B_{n−k}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: When the support splits into two disjoint groups of points, a length n subscheme decomposes uniquely as the disjoint union of a length k and a length n−k subscheme. The same decomposition holds on the isospectral Hilbert scheme after ordering the two groups. Since the universal family over a disjoint union is the disjoint union of the universal families, its pushforward tautological sheaf splits as a direct sum. This local product structure is used in the induction step of Theorem 2.1, where the polygraph similarly decomposes according to whether auxiliary points lie in the first or second group.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.11
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let 0 → C_n → ... → C_1 → C_0 → 0 be a bounded complex of locally free coherent sheaves on a Noetherian scheme X. Let Supp(C.) be the union of the supports of its homology sheaves. Then every component of Supp(C.) has codimension at most n in X. In particular, if C. is exact on an open subset U whose complement has codimension greater than n, then C. is exact everywhere.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is the new intersection theorem of Peskine-Szpiro and Roberts, quoted by Haiman. In the proof of Theorem 2.1, the mapping cone of ΨR(n,l)→B^⊗l is a locally free complex of length n. Haiman verifies its exactness away from a closed subset of codimension n+1; the theorem then forbids nonzero homology supported entirely on that subset, forcing global exactness.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 5.12 and 5.13
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let U_x be the open subset of H_n where 1,x,...,x^{n−1} is a basis of B(I), and define U_y analogously. Then the complement Z_n \ (U_x ∪ U_y) in the zero fiber has dimension n−3. Also, the map ΨR(n,l) → B^⊗l used in the proof of Theorem 2.1 is an isomorphism over U_x; by symmetry it is an isomorphism over U_y.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the dimension statement, Haiman uses Briançon-Ellingsrud-Strømme's cell decomposition of Z_n. The open cell of dimension n−1 is U_x∩Z_n. The unique cell of dimension n−2 meets U_y, so its complement in U_y has dimension at most n−3. All remaining cells already have dimension at most n−3. For the local isomorphism over U_x, use coordinates e_1,...,e_n,γ_0,...,γ_{n−1} in which the universal family is given by x^n−e_1x^{n−1}+...+(−1)^n e_n=0 and y=φ_γ(x). On the preimage U'_x in X_n, coordinates are x_1,...,x_n,γ, with y_i=φ_γ(x_i). The derived pullback Lf^*R(n,l) is computed by the Koszul complex on y_i−φ_γ(x_i); the freeness corollary for polygraphs makes this Koszul complex exact. Alternating elements are multiples of the Vandermonde Δ(x), and after the O(−1) twist the local map sends Δ(x)p to p. Surjectivity is immediate from generators; injectivity follows by showing Δ(x) annihilates b_i−φ_γ(a_i) in the relevant quotient, using Lagrange interpolation identities expressing y_jΔ(x) and φ_γ(a_i)Δ(x) in terms of the same interpolation determinants.

proof_techniques.name: Realize diagonal coinvariants as global sections on the Hilbert-scheme zero fiber
proof_techniques.description: The paper identifies the diagonal coinvariant ring R_n = C[x,y]/C[x,y]^{S_n}_+C[x,y] with H^0(Z_n,P), where Z_n is the zero fiber of H_n → S^nC^2 and P is the unusual rank n! tautological bundle from the isospectral Hilbert scheme. More generally, H^0(Z_n,P⊗B^⊗l) is R(n,l)/mR(n,l). This turns algebraic quotients by diagonal invariants into geometric objects whose characters can be computed by localization after resolving O_{Z_n}.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For questions about multigraded coinvariant Hilbert series, this gives a model case in which coefficients are accessed geometrically rather than by direct quotient computation. A solver could try to interpret hook Schur coefficients via analogous tautological tensor or Schur-functor pieces whose global sections have combinatorial bases.

proof_techniques.name: Koszul resolution of the zero fiber from trace-zero tautological sections
proof_techniques.description: The zero fiber Z_n is resolved by a Koszul complex built from B'⊕O_t⊕O_q, where B'=ker(tr:B→O) and O_t,O_q correspond to the coordinate functions x,y. The alternating exterior sum of this resolution produces the factor (1−q)(1−t)Π_μ(q,t) in the fixed-point character formula, and the extra B factor in the resolution contributes B_μ(q,t). This is a compact way to convert the quotient by positive diagonal invariants into an explicit localization numerator.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If hook coefficients can be expressed as Euler characteristics or quotients by invariant ideals, this technique suggests looking for a small equivariant resolution whose exterior weights encode the desired hook statistic.

proof_techniques.name: Atiyah-Bott localization with monomial-ideal fixed points
proof_techniques.description: The T^2-fixed points of H_n are indexed by partitions μ of n, and the cotangent weights are expressed by arms and legs of cells in μ. For any tautological vector bundle expression with vanishing higher cohomology, the Hilbert or Frobenius series becomes a sum over partitions μ with a universal arm-leg denominator and a numerator given by the character of the fiber at I_μ. This is the mechanism producing all Macdonald-polynomial character formulas in Section 3.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Even if the final target is a combinatorial interpretation, localization supplies exact algebraic formulas that can be specialized or compared to candidate hook-counting statistics.

proof_techniques.name: Macdonald fiber characters of the isospectral Hilbert scheme
proof_techniques.description: The key geometric input from Haiman's earlier work is that the Frobenius character of P(I_μ) is the transformed Macdonald polynomial Htilde_μ. Thus tautological bundle character calculations become Macdonald eigenoperator formulas: tensoring with B corresponds to multiplying each fixed point contribution by B_μ, i.e. applying the operator Δ, and restricting to the zero fiber gives ∇e_n for diagonal coinvariants.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Universal Schur-positive coefficients in diagonal coinvariant problems often arise from Macdonald eigenoperators. Hook cases may be approachable by extracting the hook component from such eigenoperator expressions or by using known combinatorial models for specializations.

proof_techniques.name: Schur-functor refinement via the auxiliary S_l action
proof_techniques.description: The ring R(n,l) has an S_l action permuting the l auxiliary points, corresponding geometrically to permuting tensor factors in B^⊗l. Taking the V^ν-isotypic component replaces B^⊗l by the Schur functor S_ν(B). At a fixed point I_μ, Schur's trace formula gives the fiber character s_ν[B_μ(q,t)], yielding formulas involving the operators Δ_{s_ν}.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Since the original problem asks about Schur coefficients in grading variables, this refinement is particularly suggestive: hook shapes correspond to Schur functors S_{(a,1^b)}, so one may try to connect hook coefficients to isotypic pieces or Schur-functor constructions of tautological bundles.

proof_techniques.name: Parking-function specialization
proof_techniques.description: The specialization t=1 of the diagonal coinvariant Frobenius series is identified with the sign-twisted parking-function representation graded by the classical parking-function weight. This gives a direct combinatorial model for a one-variable specialization of the two-variable character formula and recovers the total dimension (n+1)^{n−1}.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Parking functions are a natural candidate model for coefficients associated with diagonal coinvariants. Hook coefficients may require a refinement of the parking-function statistics visible in this specialization.

proof_techniques.name: Alternating component and q,t-Catalan extraction
proof_techniques.description: Pairing the diagonal coinvariant Frobenius series with e_n extracts the alternating component, whose Hilbert series is the q,t-Catalan polynomial C_n(q,t). The fixed-point expression simplifies using <e_n,Htilde_μ>=t^{n(μ)}q^{n(μ')}, and Garsia-Haglund's Dyck-sequence formula gives an explicit positive combinatorial interpretation.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This shows that some distinguished isotypic or Schur components of diagonal coinvariants admit clean combinatorial models. For hook coefficients, an analogous extraction pairing or Schur-functor fixed-point factor may be the right object to combinatorialize.

proof_techniques.name: Operator-generation method for diagonal harmonics
proof_techniques.description: The paper proves that DH_n is generated from the Vandermonde determinant by partial derivatives and polarization operators E_k=∑ y_i∂_{x_i}^k. The proof uses orthogonal complements under the differential inner product, reduces to the one-variable Steinberg theorem after substituting y_i=φ(x_i), and then uses the Hilbert-scheme realization H^0(Z_n,P) to show the required ideal containment.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For multiple sets of variables, hook-shaped multidegrees often arise from applying a controlled number of polarization operators. This theorem gives the proven two-variable generation mechanism that any hook interpretation may need to refine or generalize.

proof_techniques.name: Derived McKay equivalence plus polygraph freeness to prove vanishing
proof_techniques.description: The main vanishing theorem is proved by translating it under the Bridgeland-King-Reid equivalence into an isomorphism ΨR(n,l)≅B^⊗l. Polygraph freeness supplies short free resolutions; local product decompositions reduce exactness by induction; explicit curvilinear charts handle the remaining open pieces; and the new intersection theorem upgrades codimension estimates to global exactness.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a proof requires new vanishing or positivity statements for tautological constructions, this is the template. The paper itself cautions that the method uses special properties of Hilb^n(C^2), so higher-variable analogues may require reductions to two variables or new geometry.

other_useful_info: The paper explicitly says that combinatorializing all the character formulas remains an important open problem; at the time, the only two combinatorial interpretations known for the q,t-Schur-positive expressions arising here were the implicit polygraph-basis interpretation of H_{R(n,l)}(q,t) and the Garsia-Haglund interpretation of the q,t-Catalan polynomial. Conjecture 3.20 states that Δ_{s_ν}e_n is q,t-Schur positive for all ν and n; Proposition 3.21 identifies it as an Euler characteristic, and Conjecture 3.22 proposes the stronger vanishing H^i(H_n,P^*⊗B^⊗l)=0 and hence H^i(Z_n,P^*⊗B^⊗l)=0 for i>0, which would imply the positivity. In the introduction Haiman notes expected but open extensions to three or more sets of variables: an analogue of the operator conjecture should hold for additional variable sets, and for exactly three sets empirical conjectures from earlier work predict dimensions 2n(n+1)^{n−2} for triagonal harmonics and (3n+3)(3n+4)...(4n+1)/(3·4·...·(n+1)) for the alternating subspace. He emphasizes that the methods of this paper do not readily apply to Hilb^n(C^d) for d≥3 because they rely on special properties of the Hilbert scheme of points in the plane. Related cited sources that may matter for the original problem include Haiman 1994 on conjectures for diagonal invariants, Garsia-Haiman 1996 on q,t-Catalan and q-Lagrange inversion, Haiman 2001 on polygraphs and Macdonald positivity, and Garsia-Haglund on the combinatorial q,t-Catalan formula.
why_relevant: Proves the character formula for diagonal harmonics and, in particular, the dimension formula for the two-variable diagonal coinvariant ring. This is the main geometric theorem connecting diagonal coinvariants to nabla/Macdonald theory.

title: A Combinatorial Formula for the Character of the Diagonal Coinvariants
overall_summary: The paper proposes a parking-function and Dyck-path formula D_n(z;q,t) for the bigraded Frobenius characteristic ∇e_n of the diagonal coinvariant ring in two variable sets, and proves strong structural evidence for it: D_n is symmetric in the Frobenius variables and Schur-positive. The main proof technique embeds the relevant inversion-generating functions into Lascoux-Leclerc-Thibon ribbon-tableau spin generating functions, then uses parabolic Kazhdan-Lusztig positivity. A second major tool is superization: replacing variables by Z+W and applying ω in the W variables turns scalar products with products e_η h_µ into explicit counts of super tableaux or, equivalently, parking functions whose reading permutation is a prescribed shuffle. The paper verifies many specializations of the conjectural formula and extends the same constructions to higher powers ∇^m e_n. For the original hook-coefficient problem, the most directly reusable content is the superization/shuffle machinery for e_η h_µ scalar products, since hook Schur coefficients can often be isolated from products h_a e_b via Pieri/Jacobi-Trudi-type identities, together with the parking-function statistics and ribbon-tableau symmetry method.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.1.1, Haiman character formula quoted from [13]
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R_n=C[x_1,y_1,...,x_n,y_n]/I be the diagonal coinvariant ring, where S_n permutes the pairs (x_i,y_i) simultaneously and I is the ideal generated by all diagonal S_n-invariant polynomials with zero constant term. Let F_{R_n}(z;q,t)=Σ_{r,s} q^r t^s F char((R_n)_{r,s}) be its bigraded Frobenius series, where the Frobenius map sends the irreducible S_n-character χ^λ to the Schur function s_λ(z). Let ∇ be the linear operator on modified Macdonald polynomials defined by ∇ H̃_µ = t^{n(µ)} q^{n(µ')} H̃_µ, with n(µ)=Σ_i (i-1)µ_i and µ' the conjugate partition. Then F_{R_n}(z;q,t)=∇e_n(z).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This theorem is quoted, not reproved, in the paper. Its role is to identify the algebraic object, the bigraded S_n-character of diagonal coinvariants, with the symmetric function ∇e_n. Consequently any combinatorial formula for ∇e_n, and any scalar product ⟨∇e_n,f⟩ with a symmetric function f, becomes a formula for a graded multiplicity in R_n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.4.1, Proposition 2.4.2, and Corollary 2.4.3, quasisymmetric and super-quasisymmetric expansion
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a subset D of {1,...,n-1}, define Gessel's quasisymmetric function Q_{n,D}(z)=Σ z_{a_1}...z_{a_n}, summed over positive integers a_1≤...≤a_n such that a_i=a_{i+1} implies i not in D. If T is a standard Young tableau, let d(T) be its descent set, where i is a descent when the entry i+1 is weakly left of i. Then for any skew shape λ, s_λ(z)=Σ_{T in SYT(λ)} Q_{|λ|,d(T)}(z). Now use the ordered alphabet A±={1<bar1<2<bar2<...}, set z_{bar a}=w_a, and define the super-quasisymmetric function Q̃_{n,D}(z,w)=Σ z_{a_1}...z_{a_n}, summed over a_1≤...≤a_n in A± such that equal positive adjacent letters force i not in D and equal negative adjacent letters force i in D. Then the super Schur function ω_W s_λ[Z+W], where Z=z_1+z_2+... and W=w_1+w_2+..., satisfies ω_W s_λ[Z+W]=Σ_{T in SYT(λ)} Q̃_{|λ|,d(T)}(z,w)=Σ_{T in SSYT±(λ)} z^T. More generally, if f(z) is homogeneous symmetric of degree n and f=Σ_D c_D Q_{n,D}(z), then ω_W f[Z+W]=Σ_D c_D Q̃_{n,D}(z,w). Also, for partitions or compositions µ,η of total degree n, the coefficient of z^µ w^η in ω_W f[Z+W] is ⟨f,e_η h_µ⟩.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For ordinary tableaux this is Gessel's standard expansion of skew Schur functions. For super tableaux, standardize each super tableau T uniquely: if a block of equal entries is a positive letter, its cells are labelled so that no descent appears inside the block; if the common letter is negative, its cells are labelled so that every adjacent position inside the block is a descent. Horizontal-strip and vertical-strip conditions are exactly what make this standardization possible and unique. Conversely, for a fixed standard tableau S, all weakly increasing words compatible with the descent restrictions contribute precisely Q̃_{n,d(S)}. The final corollary follows by linearity from the Schur expansion and the linear independence of the Q_{n,D}. The coefficient identity ⟨f,e_η h_µ⟩ is obtained from plethysm and the Hall inner product: in ω_W f[Z+W], positive multiplicities correspond to h-parts and negative multiplicities to e-parts.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Conjecture 3.1.2, main parking-function/Dyck-path formula, not proved in this paper
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let δ_n=(n-1,n-2,...,1,0). For a partition λ contained in δ_n, consider the vertical strip (λ+(1^n))/λ with cells (i,λ_{i+1}) for i=0,...,n-1. For a cell x=(i,j), set d(x)=i+j. If T is a semistandard tableau of this vertical strip and T(x)=a<T(y)=b, with x=(i,j) and y=(i',j'), the pair is a d-inversion if either d(y)=d(x) and j>j', or d(y)=d(x)+1 and j<j'. Let dinv(T) be the number of such pairs and z^T the monomial product of the entries. Define D_n(z;q,t)=Σ_{λ⊆δ_n} Σ_{T in SSYT((λ+(1^n))/λ)} t^{|δ_n/λ|} q^{dinv(T)} z^T. The conjecture is ∇e_n(z)=D_n(z;q,t), equivalently ⟨∇e_n,h_µ⟩ equals the same weighted sum restricted to tableaux of content µ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper does not prove this conjecture. It proves that the right side has necessary structural properties, such as symmetry and Schur positivity, and verifies many specializations and consequences already known or conjectured for ∇e_n. The formula is designed so that λ is the Dyck path or parking-function area data and dinv is the diagonal inversion statistic.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.1.3, symmetry and Schur positivity of D_n
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For D_n(z;q,t) as in Conjecture 3.1.2, D_n(z;q,t) is a symmetric function in the z variables and is Schur-positive, meaning ⟨D_n(z;q,t),s_µ(z)⟩ belongs to N[q,t] for every partition µ. More strongly, for each fixed λ⊆δ_n, the summand D_n^λ(z;q)=Σ_{T in SSYT((λ+(1^n))/λ)} q^{dinv(T)} z^T is individually symmetric and Schur-positive.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Write λ=(0^{α_0},1^{α_1},..., (n-1)^{α_{n-1}}), with α_0 chosen so that Σα_i=n. Replace the vertical strip (λ+(1^n))/λ by an n-tuple of one-column shapes µ^{(j)}=(1^{α_j}). A cell x in column j of the original strip is sent to a corresponding cell x' in µ^{(j)}. Choose adjusted-content offsets s_j=j-n(j+λ'_{j+1}); then the adjusted content on the tuple satisfies c̃(x')=j-n d(x). For two cells x,y in the strip, the geometric d-inversion conditions are equivalent to 0<c̃(x')-c̃(y')<n. Thus the dinv generating function is an adjusted-content inversion generating function on an n-quotient tuple. Corollary 5.2.4, derived from Lascoux-Leclerc-Thibon ribbon-tableau spin generating functions, gives symmetry. Schur positivity follows by realizing the tuple as the n-quotient of a skew shape η/ν with ν an n-core and applying Proposition 5.3.1, which identifies the Schur coefficients with parabolic Kazhdan-Lusztig polynomials with nonnegative coefficients.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.2.1, superization formula for D_n
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Extend dinv to super tableaux with entries in A±={1<bar1<2<bar2<...} as follows: for cells satisfying one of the same two geometric d-inversion conditions, the pair contributes when the entries are a<b or when they are equal negative letters a=b in A−. With z_{bar a}=w_a, the superization of D_n is ω_W D_n[Z+W;q,t]=Σ_{λ⊆δ_n} Σ_{T in SSYT±((λ+(1^n))/λ)} t^{|δ_n/λ|} q^{dinv(T)} z^T. Therefore, if Conjecture 3.1.2 holds, then for all µ,η, ⟨∇e_n,e_η h_µ⟩ equals the same weighted sum over super tableaux whose entries are 1^{µ_1}, bar1^{η_1}, 2^{µ_2}, bar2^{η_2}, and so on.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Order cells by reverse diagonal lexicographic order: x<d y if d(x)>d(y), or d(x)=d(y) and the column of x is smaller. On any subshape of the vertical strip there is a unique standard tableau decreasing in this order; there is an increasing one exactly for horizontal strips. Define a d-descent of a standard tableau S to be a position a such that entries a and a+1 occupy cells x>_d y. Standardize a super tableau T by requiring equal positive blocks to have no d-descents and equal negative blocks to have all possible d-descents. This gives a quasisymmetric expansion Σ_S q^{dinv(S)} Q̃_{n,dd(S)}. Since all geometric d-inversion pairs have the relevant order relation, standardization preserves dinv. Setting w=0 gives the ordinary quasisymmetric expansion of D_n; Theorem 3.1.3 supplies symmetry, and Corollary 2.4.3 then replaces every Q by Q̃. The coefficient extraction identity gives the scalar product with e_η h_µ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.3.1, shuffle formulation via parking functions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A parking function is a map f:{1,...,n}->{1,...,n} such that |f^{-1}({1,...,k})|≥k for all k. Each parking function is encoded by a unique λ⊆δ_n and a standard tableau T of shape (λ+(1^n))/λ, where entry a lies in column f(a)-1. Reading the entries of T in increasing reverse diagonal order gives a permutation w(f), and the d-descent set of T is D(w(f)^{-1}). A permutation w is called a µ,η-shuffle if w^{-1} is a concatenation of alternately increasing and decreasing sequences of lengths µ_1,η_1,µ_2,η_2,.... With area a(f)=|δ_n/λ| and dinv(f)=dinv(T), Conjecture 3.1.2 implies ⟨∇e_n,e_η h_µ⟩=Σ_f t^{a(f)} q^{dinv(f)}, summed over parking functions f for which w(f) is a µ,η-shuffle. Even without assuming the conjecture, this parking-function sum is independent of the order of the parts of µ and η, and also of the interleaving after allowing zero parts.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The encoding by λ and T is immediate from the fiber sizes of f; the parking-function inequalities are equivalent to λ⊆δ_n. The reading permutation in reverse diagonal order converts d-descents into descents of w(f)^{-1}. In the superization formula, a monomial with positive multiplicities µ and negative multiplicities η forces the inverse reading word to be increasing on positive blocks and decreasing on negative blocks, exactly the shuffle condition. Independence of the order of parts follows from the symmetry statement and the fact that the corresponding coefficient of ω_W D_n[Z+W] is ⟨D_n,e_η h_µ⟩, which depends only on the symmetric functions e_η and h_µ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Propositions 4.1.1, 4.2.2, 4.3.2 and Lemmas 4.2.1, 4.3.1, specializations at q=1, t=0, and q=0
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: At q=1, D_n(z;1,t)=∇e_n(z)|_{q=1}=Σ_{λ⊆δ_n} t^{|δ_n/λ|} e_α(z), where λ=(0^{α_0},1^{α_1},2^{α_2},...) and α_0 is chosen so that Σα_i=n. At t=0, ∇e_n(z)|_{t=0}=(q;q)_n h_n[Z/(1-q)] and D_n(z;q,0) equals the same expression. At q=0, D_n(z;0,t)=∇e_n(z)|_{q=0}. The q=0 proof uses the following criterion: for a standard tableau T of shape (λ+(1^n))/λ encoding a parking function f, dinv(T)=0 if and only if λ' has distinct parts and the descent set of w(f)w_0 is the set of parts of λ', where w_0(i)=n+1-i.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For q=1, dinv disappears, and the skew Schur function of (λ+(1^n))/λ is e_α because the strip has α_j cells in column j; this matches a formula of Garsia-Haiman for ∇e_n at q=1. For t=0, only λ=δ_n contributes. All cells lie on one diagonal, so semistandard tableaux are words and dinv is ordinary inversion number; summing over words of content µ gives the q-multinomial coefficient [n choose µ_1,...,µ_l]_q, which matches the Cauchy expansion of (q;q)_n h_n[Z/(1-q)]. For q=0, the criterion for dinv=0 first eliminates λ whose conjugate has repeated parts by finding three cells that force an inversion; when λ' has distinct parts, dinv=0 is equivalent to a descent condition on w(f)w_0. Summing over permutations gives Σ_w t^{maj(w_0 w w_0)} Q_{n,D(w^{-1})}. Applying Robinson-Schensted, the fact that Q_{w_0 w w_0} is Schützenberger evacuation of Q_w, and the standard major-index formula (t;t)_n s_λ(1/(1-t))=Σ_{T in SYT(λ)} t^{maj(T)}, the sum becomes Σ_λ (t;t)_n s_λ(1/(1-t)) s_λ(z), which is the Cauchy expression for ∇e_n at q=0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.4.1 and Catalan specialization
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For λ⊆δ_n, let b(λ) be the number of cells x in λ whose arm a(x) and leg l(x) satisfy l(x)≤a(x)≤l(x)+1. Let T be the super tableau of shape (λ+(1^n))/λ all of whose entries are the negative letter bar1. Then dinv(T)=b(λ); equivalently b(λ) is the number of pairs of cells of (λ+(1^n))/λ satisfying the two geometric d-inversion conditions. Consequently the Garsia-Haglund q,t-Catalan formula C_n(q,t)=⟨∇e_n,e_n⟩=Σ_{λ⊆δ_n} t^{|δ_n/λ|} q^{b(λ)} agrees with the e_n specialization of the superized D_n formula.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Given a cell x of λ, let u be the cell of the vertical strip just outside the end of the arm of x and let t be the cell or boundary position just outside the end of the leg. Moving from t along a diagonal to the next strip cell v gives a pair u,v. The equality a(x)=l(x) corresponds to one of the d-inversion configurations and a(x)=l(x)+1 to the other. Conversely every pair of strip cells in one of those configurations arises uniquely from such a cell x of λ. Since all entries are equal negative letters, every geometrically eligible pair contributes to dinv.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 4.5, Haglund-Loehr Hilbert-series specialization
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The Hilbert series of the diagonal coinvariant ring R_n is H_n(q,t)=⟨∇e_n,e_1^n⟩. Conjecture 3.1.2 implies H_n(q,t)=Σ_{λ⊆δ_n} Σ_{T in SYT((λ+(1^n))/λ)} t^{|δ_n/λ|} q^{dinv(T)}, equivalently Σ_f t^{a(f)}q^{dinv(f)} over all parking functions f on n cars. This is the Haglund-Loehr conjectural Hilbert-series formula cited in the paper.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The scalar product with e_1^n extracts standard tableaux. In the parking-function encoding, standard tableaux of all shapes (λ+(1^n))/λ with λ⊆δ_n are exactly parking functions, and the weights |δ_n/λ| and dinv(T) are the area and diagonal inversion statistics. The paper records this as an immediate consequence of its main conjecture.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 4.6, fermionic formula for ⟨D_n,e_η h_µ⟩
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let σ=σ_1...σ_n be a permutation with descents at r_1<...<r_{k-1}, set r_0=0 and r_k=n, and let A_j={σ_l : r_{j-1}<l≤r_j} be its j-th increasing run. Let F(σ) be the set of parking functions whose encoding tableau has the entries A_j on diagonal d(x)=n+1-j. Define H(σ;q,t)=Σ_{f in F(σ)} q^{dinv(f)} t^{a(f)}. The formula from Haglund-Loehr used here is H(σ;q,t)=t^{comaj(σ)} Π_{i=2}^n [v(σ,i)+χ(i≤r_1)]_q, where comaj(σ)=Σ_{j=1}^{k-1}(n-r_j) and v(σ,i) is the largest p, 1≤p≤i-1, such that the residues σ_{i-p}-σ_i, σ_{i-p+1}-σ_i,...,σ_{i-1}-σ_i modulo n form an increasing sequence. For sequences µ,η, define blocks B_j and C_j by B_j={M_{j-1}+E_{j-1}+1,...,M_j+E_{j-1}} and C_j={M_j+E_{j-1}+1,...,M_j+E_j}, where M_j=µ_1+...+µ_j and E_j=η_1+...+η_j. Let b_{ij}=|A_i∩B_j|, c_{ij}=|A_i∩C_j|. Let σ̃ be obtained from σ by reversing each block A_i∩C_j in σ. For nonempty A_i∩B_j, let V_{ij}=v(σ,k)+χ(k≤r_1), where σ_k is the largest element of A_i∩B_j; define W_{ij} similarly for A_i∩C_j. Then ⟨D_n(z;q,t),e_η h_µ⟩=Σ_σ t^{comaj(σ)} Π_{i,j} [V_{ij} choose b_{ij}]_q Π_{i,j} q^{binom(c_{ij},2)} [W_{ij} choose c_{ij}]_q, summed over σ for which σ̃ is a µ,η-shuffle. Empty-block factors are omitted. The special case η empty gives ⟨D_n,h_µ⟩=Σ_{σ a µ-shuffle} t^{comaj(σ)} Π_{i,j} [V_{ij} choose b_{ij}]_q.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Start from the Haglund-Loehr product formula for all parking functions with fixed diagonal run data σ. If w(f) is required to be a µ,empty-shuffle, then within each intersection A_i∩B_j the entries occur increasingly. Any parking function with the same run data is obtained uniquely by permuting these blocks, so the unrestricted generating function differs by factors [b_{ij}]_q!, leading to q-binomial factors. For negative e-blocks, the corresponding intersections A_i∩C_j are reversed; this contributes the additional q^{binom(c_{ij},2)} factors and q-binomial denominators. Summing over the permutations σ satisfying the required shuffle condition yields the displayed formulas.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 4.7, Schröder-path specialization
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A Schröder path from (n,0) to (0,n) with south, east, and diagonal steps staying below the diagonal gives a partition λ(Π)⊆δ_n by replacing each diagonal step by a south step followed by an east step. If the path has d diagonal steps, define a super tableau T(Π) of shape (λ(Π)+(1^n))/λ(Π) by placing the positive entry 1 in the d cells created by these replacements and the negative entry bar1 in all other cells. The paper observes that the dinv and area statistics of Egge-Haglund-Killpatrick-Kremer satisfy dinv(Π)=dinv(T(Π)) and area(Π)=|δ_n/λ(Π)|. Hence their conjectural formula for ⟨∇e_n,e_{n-d}h_d⟩ is exactly the special case of the superized formula with e_η h_µ=e_{n-d}h_d. The paper also records that Haglund had proved this Schröder conjecture and the h_{n-d}h_d case of the h_µ formula.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The replacement of each diagonal step identifies which cells carry positive entries; all other cells carry the negative entry. Under the definitions in [2], the area is plainly the number of cells between λ(Π) and δ_n. Checking the dinv definition shows that the path's diagonal inversions are precisely the super-tableau inversions involving equal negative entries or positive-negative order as in Theorem 3.2.1. Thus the Schröder generating function is a coefficient of the superized D_n formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.2.1, Lemmas 5.2.2-5.2.3, and Corollary 5.2.4, ribbon-tableau symmetry criterion
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let n be fixed. An n-ribbon is a connected skew shape of size n with no 2 by 2 block. For a skew shape µ tileable by n-ribbons, let SSRT_n(µ) be the semistandard n-ribbon tableaux of shape µ, where each equal-entry ribbon strip is tiled by its official horizontal-strip tiling. If s(T) is the sum over ribbons of one less than the number of rows, and sp(T)=(s(T)-s_min(µ))/2, then the spin generating function G_µ(z;q)=Σ_{T in SSRT_n(µ)} q^{sp(T)} z^T is symmetric. Now fix an n-core ν with content sequence s_0,...,s_{n-1} and n-quotient tuple µ=(µ^{(0)},...,µ^{(n-1)}). For a cell x in µ^{(i)}, set adjusted content c̃(x)=n c(x)+s_i. For a tableau S on the quotient, call a pair S(x)=a<S(y)=b an inversion if 0<c̃(x)-c̃(y)<n. Then for every standard, and also every semistandard, n-ribbon tableau T of fixed shape, sp(T)=e-inv(quot_n(T)) for a constant e depending only on the shape. Consequently, for any n-tuple of skew shapes and offsets s_i congruent to i modulo n, there is a skew shape µ and exponent e such that q^e G_µ(z;q^{-1})=Σ_{S in SSYT(tuple)} q^{inv(S)} z^S; in particular, the adjusted-content inversion generating function on the tuple is symmetric.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The symmetry of G_µ is quoted from Lascoux-Leclerc-Thibon. To relate spin to inversions, first handle standard tableaux. Any two standard tableaux of a fixed quotient shape are connected by switches of adjacent labels a,a+1. If a switch does not create an adjusted-content inversion, the two corresponding ribbon tilings have the same spin. If it does create one, the relevant skew shape consists of two overlapping ribbons and has exactly two tilings, one horizontal and one vertical; the horizontal tiling has spin larger by one, so sp changes by minus the change in inv. This proves sp=e-inv for standards. For semistandard tableaux, standardize using adjusted-content descents: equal entries are labelled so as to avoid descents. Equal entries contribute neither to inv nor to spin change, and the official tiling condition ensures standardization keeps the same ribbon tiling. Finally, arbitrary offsets can be shifted by a common multiple of n to become the content sequence of some n-core, without changing the inequalities 0<c̃(x)-c̃(y)<n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.3.1, Schur positivity via parabolic Kazhdan-Lusztig polynomials
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let µ be a partition and let ν=core_n(µ) be its n-core. For every partition λ, the Schur coefficient ⟨q^{s_min(µ/ν)} G_{µ/ν}(z;q^2), s_λ(z)⟩ equals the parabolic Kazhdan-Lusztig polynomial P^-_{µ+ρ,ν+nλ+ρ}(q) in the notation of Leclerc-Thibon. Therefore these coefficients are nonnegative polynomials in q.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Leclerc and Thibon work in a q-Fock space F_r with natural basis |α⟩ and Kazhdan-Lusztig basis G^-_α; the relevant Kazhdan-Lusztig polynomial is the coefficient of the natural basis vector in G^-. In that setting, the Schur coefficient of the ribbon spin function is the coefficient of |µ+ρ⟩ in s_λ·|ν+ρ⟩. Their theorem says s_λ·G^-_{ν+ρ}=G^-_{ν+nλ+ρ} when ν is n-restricted. The paper notes that every n-core is n-restricted. It also proves G^-_{ν+ρ}=|ν+ρ⟩ for n-cores: straightening |w_0(ν+ρ)⟩ can only produce lower terms with the same size and the same residues modulo n, hence the same n-core; since the size is already |ν|, no lower partition can occur. Thus the desired coefficient is exactly P^-_{µ+ρ,ν+nλ+ρ}(q). Nonnegativity follows from Kashiwara-Tanisaki's geometric interpretation of these parabolic Kazhdan-Lusztig polynomials as decomposition multiplicities for perverse sheaves.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 6.1.1, representation-theoretic meaning of higher powers ∇^m e_n
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let I be the ideal in C[x_1,y_1,...,x_n,y_n] generated by diagonal S_n-invariant polynomials without constant term, and let J be the ideal generated by all S_n-antisymmetric polynomials. Let ε be the sign representation. For every positive integer m, ∇^m e_n(z) is the bigraded Frobenius series of the S_n-module ε^{m-1}⊗J^{m-1}/I J^{m-1}. In symbols, ∇^m e_n(z)=F_{ε^{m-1}⊗J^{m-1}/I J^{m-1}}(z;q,t).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper derives this from Haiman's Hilbert-scheme character formula [13]. In [13, Theorem 3.3], replacing the factor s_ν[B_µ(q,t)] by e_n[B_µ(q,t)]^{m-1}=t^{(m-1)n(µ)}q^{(m-1)n(µ')} gives ∇^m e_n. The resulting module is identified with the antisymmetric component of a polygraph module R(n,(m-1)n)/mR(n,(m-1)n). Haiman's earlier identification of that antisymmetric component with ε^{m-1}⊗J^{m-1} turns the quotient by the maximal invariant ideal into ε^{m-1}⊗J^{m-1}/I J^{m-1}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Conjecture 6.2.2, higher-power analogue of the main formula, not proved in this paper
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix a positive integer m and set mδ_n=(m(n-1),m(n-2),...,0). For a cell x=(i,j), define d_m(x)=m i+j and reverse diagonal order x<d y if d_m(x)>d_m(y), or d_m(x)=d_m(y) and the column of x is smaller. For λ⊆mδ_n and a semistandard tableau T of (λ+(1^n))/λ, a pair of entries T(x)=a<T(y)=b contributes max(0,m-|d_m(y)-d_m(x)|) d-inversions if the column of x is larger than the column of y, and contributes max(0,m-|d_m(y)-d_m(x)-1|) if the column of x is smaller. Equal positive entries contribute as though they were unequal entries ordered with x<d y. Let dinv_m(T) be the total contribution and define D_n^{(m)}(z;q,t)=Σ_{λ⊆mδ_n} Σ_T t^{|mδ_n/λ|} q^{dinv_m(T)} z^T. The conjecture is ∇^m e_n(z)=D_n^{(m)}(z;q,t), equivalently the scalar products with h_µ are given by the corresponding content-µ tableau sums.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper introduces this as a direct Fuss or higher-power analogue of Conjecture 3.1.2. It is not proved, but the authors prove symmetry and Schur positivity of the right side, verify specializations, and show compatibility with known and conjectured higher q,t-Catalan and higher Hilbert-series formulas.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorems 6.2.3 and 6.2.4, symmetry, Schur positivity, and superization for D_n^{(m)}
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every positive integer m, D_n^{(m)}(z;q,t) is symmetric in z and Schur-positive; indeed each fixed-λ summand D_n^{(m),λ}(z;q)=Σ_T q^{dinv_m(T)} z^T is symmetric and Schur-positive. The super version is obtained by letting equal negative entries contribute by the opposite equality rule from equal positive entries. Then ω_W D_n^{(m)}[Z+W;q,t]=Σ_{λ⊆mδ_n} Σ_{T in SSYT±((λ+(1^n))/λ)} t^{|mδ_n/λ|} q^{dinv_m(T)} z^T. Thus, under Conjecture 6.2.2, ⟨∇^m e_n,e_η h_µ⟩ is the corresponding weighted sum over super tableaux of signed content (µ,η).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof first replaces dinv_m by a reduced statistic. If u,v are in different columns with v left of u, Lemma 6.4.1 says: when 1-m≤d_m(u)-d_m(v)≤0, the ordering T(u)<T(v) contributes exactly one more d-inversion than the reverse ordering; when 1≤d_m(u)-d_m(v)≤m, the reverse ordering contributes one more; otherwise neither ordering contributes. Hence dinv_m(T)=e(ν)+dinv'_m(T) for a constant depending only on the shape ν=(λ+(1^n))/λ, where reduced inversions are exactly pairs with 0≤d_m(y)-d_m(x)≤m-1 and right-to-left geometry, or 1≤d_m(y)-d_m(x)≤m and left-to-right geometry. Next write λ=(0^{α_0},..., (mn)^{α_{mn}}), choose the permutation β of {0,...,mn} defined by β(j)≡-n j modulo mn+1, and map column j of the strip to the one-column component µ^{(β(j))}=(1^{α_j}) of an (mn+1)-tuple. With offsets s_{β(j)}=-n j-(mn+1)λ'_{j+1}, the adjusted content of the image of x=(i,j) is c̃(x')=-i-n d_m(x). The inequality 0<c̃(x')-c̃(y')<mn+1 is then equivalent to the reduced d-inversion condition. Corollary 5.2.4 gives symmetry, and Proposition 5.3.1 gives Schur positivity. The superization proof is the same standardization argument as Theorem 3.2.1, using the equality rules built into dinv_m. The paper's displayed scalar-product equation uses the superized generating function; context indicates the t-exponent is |mδ_n/λ|.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Propositions 6.3.1, 6.3.2 and Lemma 6.3.3, higher-power specializations and higher Catalan statistic
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The higher-power formula has the following verified specializations. At q=1, D_n^{(m)}(z;1,t)=∇^m e_n(z)|_{q=1}=Σ_{λ⊆mδ_n} t^{|mδ_n/λ|} e_α(z), where λ=(0^{α_0},1^{α_1},...) and Σα_i=n. At t=0, D_n^{(m)}(z;q,0)=∇^m e_n(z)|_{t=0}=q^{(m-1)binom(n,2)}(q;q)_n h_n[Z/(1-q)]. At q=0, D_n^{(m)}(z;0,t)=∇^m e_n(z)|_{q=0}. For the higher q,t-Catalan specialization, define b_m(λ) as the number of cells x in λ⊆mδ_n whose arm and leg satisfy m l(x)≤a(x)≤m l(x)+m. If T is the all-negative super tableau of shape (λ+(1^n))/λ, then dinv_m(T)=b_m(λ).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The q=1 proof is the same as for m=1: the statistic disappears and the skew Schur function is e_α. For t=0, only λ=mδ_n contributes; in that shape dinv_m is ordinary word inversion number plus (m-1)binom(n,2), yielding the stated scalar factor times the classical coinvariant Frobenius series. For q=0, the dinv_m=0 criterion is the same as the m=1 criterion, while |mδ_n/λ|=|δ_n/λ|+(m-1)binom(n,2), matching the q-t exchanged t=0 formula. For Lemma 6.3.3, draw the boundary path of λ under the line i+j/m=n. From a cell x in λ, take u just beyond its arm and project the unit segment beyond its leg along diagonals of slope -1/m to a vertical segment on the boundary lying beside a strip cell v. The inequality m l(x)≤a(x)≤m l(x)+m becomes a bounded inequality involving d_m(u) and the projected segment. Counting all possible projected segments for a fixed pair u,v gives exactly the number of d-inversions contributed by the equal negative entries in u and v. This construction is reversible.

proof_techniques.name: Superization plus standardization to compute e_η h_µ scalar products
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly uses the identity that the coefficient of z^µ w^η in ω_W f[Z+W] is ⟨f,e_η h_µ⟩. It then proves explicit super-tableau formulas by standardizing super tableaux: equal positive letters standardize with no descents inside their block, while equal negative letters standardize with all descents inside their block. Once an ordinary generating function is known to be symmetric and has a quasisymmetric expansion, Corollary 2.4.3 allows every Q_{n,D} to be replaced by its super analogue Q̃_{n,D}.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Hook Schur coefficients are often accessible from scalar products with h_a e_b or differences of such scalar products. This technique suggests proving hook coefficient formulas by first giving a combinatorial interpretation for the relevant e/h scalar products and then isolating the hook contribution.

proof_techniques.name: Parking functions as vertical-strip tableaux with shuffle restrictions
proof_techniques.description: A parking function f is encoded by a Dyck-path partition λ⊆δ_n and a standard tableau of the vertical strip (λ+(1^n))/λ, with the column recording f(a)-1. Reading the tableau in reverse diagonal order gives a permutation w(f), and d-descents translate into descents of w(f)^{-1}. Positive and negative blocks in the superization impose increasing and decreasing runs in w(f)^{-1}, producing the µ,η-shuffle condition.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a hook coefficient can be written through e/h scalar products, the corresponding objects should be parking functions with a small number of prescribed increasing/decreasing shuffle blocks. This can give a direct candidate combinatorial interpretation.

proof_techniques.name: Embedding inversion statistics into LLT ribbon-tableau spin functions
proof_techniques.description: To prove symmetry of a non-obviously symmetric inversion enumerator, the paper maps the tableau shape into an n-quotient tuple and chooses adjusted-content offsets so that the desired inversion condition is exactly 0<c̃(x)-c̃(y)<n. Lascoux-Leclerc-Thibon ribbon-tableau spin functions are symmetric, and the paper proves spin equals a constant minus this adjusted-content inversion number. The same method works for the higher-power statistic after replacing n by mn+1 and using a nontrivial residue permutation β.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For any proposed hook-coefficient or multigraded Hilbert formula involving diagonal inversions, try to realize its statistic as an adjusted-content inversion statistic on a quotient tuple. This can bypass the obstacle of proving symmetry or Schur positivity directly.

proof_techniques.name: Schur positivity via parabolic Kazhdan-Lusztig polynomials
proof_techniques.description: After identifying an inversion generating function with a ribbon spin function, the paper invokes Leclerc-Thibon's Fock-space interpretation: certain Schur coefficients of ribbon spin functions are parabolic Kazhdan-Lusztig polynomials. For n-core base shapes, the needed Fock-space vector is the natural basis vector, and Kashiwara-Tanisaki positivity gives nonnegative coefficients.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the original hook coefficients arise from Schur expansion of a symmetric function built from LLT-type inversion statistics, this gives a route to positivity even when no direct positive Schur expansion is visible.

proof_techniques.name: Fermionic run decomposition
proof_techniques.description: The paper refines the parking-function count by fixing the run decomposition of the diagonal reading permutation σ. Haglund-Loehr's formula gives a product over positions involving q-integers; imposing shuffle constraints divides by q-factorials inside intersections of runs with prescribed blocks, producing q-binomial factors. Negative blocks add reversal factors q^{binom(c,2)}.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For hook-related e/h scalar products, a fermionic formula may give an explicit finite q,t expression that can be subtracted or specialized to isolate hook coefficients.

proof_techniques.name: Boundary-specialization checks
proof_techniques.description: The paper verifies the candidate formula at q=1, t=0, and q=0. At q=1 the statistic disappears and the answer is a sum of elementary symmetric functions indexed by column multiplicities. At t=0 only the staircase shape survives and the statistic becomes ordinary word inversion. At q=0, dinv-free tableaux are characterized and then summed using RSK, evacuation, and major-index generating functions.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Any proposed hook interpretation for multigraded coinvariants should be checked at analogous one-variable or boundary specializations. These checks may also determine the correct statistic or reveal a simpler hook formula at the boundary.

proof_techniques.name: Arm-leg projection for Catalan-type specializations
proof_techniques.description: For the all-negative super tableau, the paper proves that dinv is the same as an arm-leg count over cells under the Dyck path. In the higher-power case, the projection is along slope -1/m diagonals and the condition becomes m l(x)≤a(x)≤m l(x)+m. This converts pairwise strip inversions into local cell statistics on the underlying partition.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If hook coefficients reduce to cases with many negative or exterior algebra factors, an arm-leg statistic may provide a compact interpretation and connect the answer to Catalan/Fuss-Catalan objects.

proof_techniques.name: Higher-power reduced dinv and residue permutation β
proof_techniques.description: For ∇^m e_n, raw dinv_m can contribute more than one inversion per pair. The paper subtracts a shape-dependent constant to obtain a reduced 0-1 inversion statistic, then maps column j to residue β(j)≡-n j mod mn+1 so that adjusted-content inequalities reproduce exactly the reduced conditions. This is the key non-obvious construction in the proof of symmetry for D_n^{(m)}.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For coinvariant algebras with more variable sets or higher Fuss analogues, a residue permutation and constant shift may be needed before the statistics fit LLT theory. This is a useful template for generalizing diagonal-inversion formulas beyond the two-variable case.

other_useful_info: The paper explicitly says it cannot provide a direct combinatorial interpretation of the Schur expansion coefficients of D_n, despite proving Schur positivity via Kazhdan-Lusztig theory; this is important if one seeks hook Schur coefficients, because the paper's direct combinatorics is for h/e scalar products rather than Schur coefficients. The main formulas Conjecture 3.1.2 and Conjecture 6.2.2 are conjectural in this paper, although many consequences and specializations are proved or matched with known results. Conjecture 4.7.1 refines the main conjecture using Garsia-Haglund functions E_{n,k}, defined by e_n[Z(1-u)/(1-q)]=Σ_{k=1}^n (u;q)_k/(q;q)_k E_{n,k}, and predicts ∇E_{n,k} is obtained by summing D_n^λ over λ⊆δ_n with exactly k contacts λ_i=n-i; Conjecture 6.3.4 gives the analogous ∇^m E_{n,k} statement. The open problems listed include proving the main conjectures, proving q,t symmetry D_n(z;q,t)=D_n(z;t,q) and its higher-power analogue, proving the q=1 symmetry specialization D_n^{(m)}(z;q,1)=D_n^{(m)}(z;1,q), and proving the specialization D_n^{(m)}(z;q,q^{-1})=q^{-m binom(n,2)} e_n[Z[mn+1]_q]/[mn+1]_q. Related works cited by the paper that may be relevant include Garsia-Haglund on q,t-Catalan positivity, Haglund-Loehr on the diagonal-harmonics Hilbert series, Egge-Haglund-Killpatrick-Kremer on Schröder paths, and Loehr/Remmel on higher harmonics modules.
why_relevant: Gives the shuffle-conjecture combinatorial formula for the bigraded Frobenius characteristic of diagonal coinvariants and proves key Schur-positivity properties. Its parking-function methods are part of the standard combinatorial toolbox for coinvariant Hilbert and Frobenius series.

title: Conjectures on the Quotient Ring by Diagonal Invariants
overall_summary: This paper introduces and studies the diagonal coinvariant ring R_n = Q[x_1,...,x_n,y_1,...,y_n]/I for the diagonal action of S_n, where I is generated by all positive-degree diagonal invariants. It formulates foundational Hilbert-series and Frobenius-series conjectures relating R_n to trees, parking functions, q-Lagrange inversion, q-Catalan numbers, and the permutation module on Z_{n+1}^n/Z_{n+1}. The main proven tools are the identification of R_n with diagonal harmonics via the apolar form, GL_2/sl_2 symmetry of the bigrading, straightening relations giving top-degree bounds, an explicit description of bidegrees linear in one variable set, and q-Lagrange identities matching the conjectural one-variable specialization. For the original problem, the paper is most useful as the foundational source for diagonal coinvariants, for the parking-function and q-Lagrange combinatorics expected in Hilbert/Frobenius specializations, and for the use of harmonic differential operators and GL_m symmetry to isolate Schur components.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Core definitions 1.1-1.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X = {x_1,...,x_n} and Y = {y_1,...,y_n}. The symmetric group S_n acts diagonally on Q[X,Y] by sigma(x_i)=x_{sigma(i)} and sigma(y_i)=y_{sigma(i)}. Let I be the ideal generated by all S_n-invariant polynomials in Q[X,Y] with zero constant term, and define the diagonal coinvariant ring R_n = Q[X,Y]/I. Since I is bihomogeneous, R_n has components (R_n)_{i,j}. The Hilbert series is H_n(t,q)=sum_{i,j} t^i q^j dim (R_n)_{i,j}. If phi sends an S_n-character to its Frobenius symmetric function, the Frobenius series is F_n(t,q)=sum_{i,j} t^i q^j phi((R_n)_{i,j}).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: These are definitions. The paper observes that diagonal invariance and bihomogeneity are preserved by taking homogeneous and bihomogeneous components, so the quotient inherits a bigrading and an S_n-action in each bidegree. Frobenius series record the full S_n-character refinement of the Hilbert series; applying the inner product with p_1^n recovers dimensions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.2.1, Weyl generators for diagonal invariants
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the diagonal action of S_n on Q[X,Y], the invariant ring Q[X,Y]^{S_n} is generated by the polarized power sums p_{r,s}(X,Y)=sum_{i=1}^n x_i^r y_i^s, for nonnegative integers r,s. Consequently the ideal I defining R_n is generated by the positive-degree p_{r,s}, equivalently by any homogeneous generating set for Q[X,Y]^{S_n} with constants omitted.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites Weyl's theorem on multisymmetric invariants. Since the ideal I is generated by all positive-degree invariants, any algebra generating set of the invariant ring generates I as an ideal. The same observation is stated to hold for any finite group action: homogeneous generators of the invariant ring generate the ideal of positive-degree invariants, and conversely.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.2.2, uniqueness of the trivial representation in R_n
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The bidegree (0,0) component of R_n affords the trivial S_n-representation, and this is the only occurrence of the trivial representation in R_n. Equivalently, if (i,j) is not (0,0), then (R_n)_{i,j} has no S_n-invariant vectors.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The class of 1 spans (R_n)_{0,0} and is fixed by S_n. If a nonconstant bihomogeneous class in bidegree (i,j) with (i,j) not (0,0) were S_n-invariant, it would be represented by an invariant polynomial of positive total degree. By definition every such invariant lies in I, hence maps to zero in the quotient. Therefore no other invariant vectors survive.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.3.1, apolar form and diagonal harmonics
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Define the apolar bilinear form on Q[X,Y] by <f,g> = f(partial_{x_1},...,partial_{x_n},partial_{y_1},...,partial_{y_n}) g evaluated at X=Y=0. If I is any homogeneous ideal, then H=I^perp is a homogeneous space closed under all partial derivatives, and H = {h : f(partial_X,partial_Y) h = 0 for every f in I}. Conversely, if H is a homogeneous space closed under partial derivatives, then H^perp is a homogeneous ideal and H=(H^perp)^perp. For the ideal I defining R_n, the diagonal harmonics H_n=I^perp are S_n-stable and have the same bidegree S_n-characters as R_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The key identity is adjointness: <f, partial_x g> = <x f, g> for every variable x. If h is orthogonal to an ideal I, then <f, partial_x h>=<x f,h>=0 for all f in I, hence partial_x h is also orthogonal to I. The differential-equation characterization follows because f(partial)h has all derivatives vanishing at the origin when h is orthogonal to all multiples of f. Conversely, if H is derivative-stable, then for f in H^perp one has <x f,h>=<f,partial_x h>=0 for all h in H, so H^perp is an ideal. Homogeneity permits taking double orthogonal complements degree by degree. Since the apolar form is S_n-invariant and nondegenerate on each bidegree, H_n and R_n are isomorphic as bigraded S_n-modules.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 1.5, classical one-set coinvariant formulas
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the ordinary S_n-action on Q[X] and the quotient by the ideal generated by positive-degree symmetric polynomials, the harmonic/coinvariant space has Frobenius series F(q) satisfying F(q)/((1-q)(1-q^2)...(1-q^n)) = sum_{lambda partition of n} s_lambda(1,q,q^2,...) s_lambda. Hence the multiplicity polynomial of the irreducible character indexed by lambda is (1-q)(1-q^2)...(1-q^n) s_lambda(1,q,q^2,...) = q^{n(lambda)} [n]_q [n-1]_q ... [1]_q / product_{cells x in lambda} [h(x)]_q = sum_{T in SYT(lambda)} q^{maj(T)}. The Hilbert series is [n]_q! = [n]_q [n-1]_q ... [1]_q, and the ungraded S_n-module is the regular representation.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The fundamental symmetric functions form a homogeneous system of parameters in Q[X], and Q[X] is free over its invariant subring. The harmonic space maps isomorphically to the quotient and gives the free basis, so the Frobenius series of Q[X] factors as the product of the invariant-ring Hilbert series and the coinvariant Frobenius series. The displayed Schur-principal-specialization formula then gives the irreducible multiplicities; the q-hook and major-index forms are standard symmetric-function identities. The Hilbert series follows by summing dimensions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.2.1, one-variable edge specialization
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the diagonal coinvariant ring R_n in two variable sets, H_n(q,0)=H_n(0,q)=[n]_q!. The analogous Frobenius specialization F_n(q,0), or F_n(0,q), is the ordinary one-set coinvariant Frobenius series from Section 1.5.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Taking degree zero in Y leaves only polynomials in X. The generators of I in this subring are exactly the positive-degree symmetric polynomials in X, so the degree-zero-in-Y part of R_n is the ordinary coinvariant algebra. The same argument applies with X and Y interchanged.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.5.2, polynomiality criterion for principal Schur quotients
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix positive integers n and p. The following are equivalent: for every partition lambda of n, s_lambda(1,q,...,q^{p-1})/(1+q+...+q^{p-1}) is a polynomial; the same quotient is a polynomial for lambda=(n); and p is relatively prime to n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof replaces the Schur basis by the complete homogeneous basis h_lambda. One has h_r(1,q,...,q^{p-1}) = [p+r-1 choose r]_q = [p]_q [p+1]_q ... [p+r-1]_q / [r]_q!. The roots of [p]_q=1+q+...+q^{p-1} are the nontrivial p-th roots of unity. If such a root is primitive of order d dividing p, then it appears in h_r(1,q,...,q^{p-1}) with multiplicity 1 precisely when d does not divide r. Thus [p]_q divides h_lambda iff for every d>1 dividing p, at least one part of lambda is not divisible by d. This holds for all lambda partitioning n iff no d>1 divides both p and n, i.e. gcd(p,n)=1. The case lambda=(n) gives the same condition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.5.3, Koszul interpretation of the principal Schur quotient
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Suppose Q[X]=Q[x_1,...,x_n] contains a homogeneous system of parameters theta_1,...,theta_n such that theta_1 has degree 1, theta_2,...,theta_n have degree p, theta_1 is S_n-invariant, and theta_2,...,theta_n span the irreducible reflection representation of S_n. Then the Frobenius series of Q[X]/(theta_1,...,theta_n) is sum_{lambda partition of n} s_lambda(1,q,...,q^{p-1})/(1+q+...+q^{p-1}) s_lambda.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the Koszul resolution of Q[X]/(theta_1,...,theta_n). Each free module in the resolution has a graded S_n-basis indexed by subsets of the parameters, and the maps are homogeneous and S_n-equivariant. Exactness implies that the quotient Frobenius series is the alternating sum of the Frobenius series of the free terms. This alternating sum is the internal product of the Frobenius series of Q[X] with the character-valued exterior generating function of the parameter representation. The latter is computed from one invariant parameter of degree 1 and an (n-1)-dimensional reflection representation in degree p; simplifying the internal product gives the displayed Schur-principal-specialization quotient. Since it is a Frobenius series of an actual quotient, the coefficients are nonnegative integers.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.5.4, existence of the parameters when gcd(p,n)=1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A homogeneous system of parameters satisfying Proposition 2.5.3 exists whenever p is relatively prime to n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Take theta_1=x_1+...+x_n. For p prime not dividing n, choose theta_i=x_i^p-x_{i-1}^p for 2≤i≤n. If theta_i=0 for all i and theta_1=0, then all x_i^p are equal. A nonzero solution may be rescaled so x_1=1 and every x_i is a p-th root of unity. Then sum_i x_i=0 gives an integer polynomial f with f(xi_p)=0 at a primitive p-th root xi_p and f(1)=n; since the minimal polynomial is 1+x+...+x^{p-1}, evaluating at 1 forces p to divide n, contradiction. For composite p relatively prime to n, the paper shows closure under multiplying degrees: after modding out theta_1, a suitable system corresponds to an S_n-equivariant homogeneous polynomial map from the reflection representation to itself whose only preimage of 0 is 0. Composing such maps for degrees p and q gives one of degree pq. Factoring p into primes not dividing n yields the result.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.6.1, parking functions and Z_{n+1}^n/Z_{n+1}
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A parking function is a function f:{1,...,n}->{1,...,n} such that for every 1≤k≤n, the set f^{-1}({1,...,k}) has cardinality at least k. The number of parking functions is (n+1)^{n-1}. Under the action of S_n by permuting the domain, the permutation representation on parking functions is isomorphic to the permutation representation on Z_{n+1}^n/Z_{n+1}, where S_n permutes coordinates and the quotient is by the diagonal subgroup generated by (1,1,...,1).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Consider the parking process on a circular one-way street with n+1 spaces. For every preference function f:{1,...,n}->{1,...,n+1}, all cars park and exactly one space remains empty. Among the n+1 cyclic rotations of f, exactly one has the empty space equal to n+1, and that rotated preference function is a parking function in the usual n-space sense. Thus all functions to n+1 decompose into n+1 copies of the set of parking functions, equivariantly for the S_n-action on cars. Functions to n+1 form the permutation representation on Z_{n+1}^n, and quotienting by simultaneous cyclic rotation gives Z_{n+1}^n/Z_{n+1}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.6.2, parking-function weight and tree inversions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a parking function f:{1,...,n}->{1,...,n}, define its weight by w(f)=n(n+1)/2 - sum_i f(i). Then sum over parking functions f of q^{w(f)} equals the inversion enumerator sum_T q^{inv(T)} over spanning trees T on vertices {0,1,...,n}, rooted at 0, where an inversion is a pair i<j in {1,...,n} such that j is an ancestor of i.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites Kreweras for a bijective proof. It also later derives the identity via q-Lagrange inversion: after specializing elementary symmetric functions e_k to 1/k! in the explicit q-Lagrange solution h_n^*(q), the resulting exponential generating function agrees with Gessel's q-Lagrange formula for the Mallows-Riordan inversion enumerator for labelled trees.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.1.1, GL_2 and sl_2 decomposition
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The natural GL_2-action mixing the two rows X and Y commutes with S_n and preserves R_n and H_n.
