With sl_2 operators E=sum_i y_i partial_{x_i}, F=sum_i x_i partial_{y_i}, and H=sum_i y_i partial_{y_i} - x_i partial_{x_i}, each bidegree (H_n)_{i,j} has H-weight j-i. Consequently F_n(q^{-1},q) is a sum of irreducible SL_2 x S_n characters of the form (q^{-m}+q^{-m+2}+...+q^{m-2}+q^m) s_lambda. The same holds in each fixed total degree, so along each total-degree diagonal the coefficient of each S_n-irreducible is symmetric and unimodal; the same symmetric-unimodal conclusion holds for the Hilbert-series diagonals.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The GL_2-action is left multiplication on the two-row matrix of variables and commutes with column permutations by S_n. Since it sends invariant polynomials to invariant polynomials, it preserves I and hence R_n; via the apolar form it also preserves H_n. Finite-dimensional SL_2-modules decompose into irreducibles V_m with weights -m,-m+2,...,m. Because the S_n-action commutes with SL_2, H_n decomposes into sums V_m tensor W_lambda. Substituting t=q^{-1} records exactly the H-weight, giving the displayed character form and unimodality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.2.1, straightening relations from complete symmetric functions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For 1≤k≤n and r+s=k, the polynomial obtained as the coefficient of alpha^r beta^s in h_k(alpha x_k+beta y_k, ..., alpha x_n+beta y_n) belongs to I, where h_k is the complete homogeneous symmetric function of degree k.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: It suffices first to prove h_k(x_k,...,x_n) in I. Since product_{i=1}^n (1+t x_i)=sum_j e_j(X)t^j is congruent to 1 modulo I, one has product_{i=1}^{k-1}(1+t x_i) congruent to product_{i=k}^n (1+t x_i)^{-1}=sum_j (-t)^j h_j(x_k,...,x_n) modulo I. The coefficient of t^k on the left is zero because the left product has degree at most k-1, so h_k(x_k,...,x_n) is in I. The stated alpha,beta-polarizations are obtained from h_k(x_k,...,x_n) by applying the operator E=sum_i y_i partial_{x_i} repeatedly, and I is stable under this GL_2 action.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.2.2, monomial spanning and top total degree bound
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The quotient R_n is spanned by monomials m(X,Y) such that for each k, the total degree of m in x_k and y_k is strictly less than k. In particular, (R_n)_{i,j}=0 whenever i+j>binom(n,2).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Order monomials lexicographically by total degree in the pairs (x_1,y_1), then (x_2,y_2), and so on, with any tie-breaking. In Lemma 3.2.1 the leading term of the relation with r+s=k is x_k^r y_k^s. Thus any monomial containing a factor x_k^r y_k^s of total degree k can be reduced modulo I to a linear combination of lexicographically smaller monomials. Iterating terminates and leaves only monomials with pair-degree less than k for every k. The maximum total degree of such monomials is sum_{k=1}^n (k-1)=binom(n,2).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.2.3, tail-degree vanishing criterion for monomials
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If a monomial m(X,Y) has total degree in the variables x_k,y_k,...,x_n,y_n exceeding (k-1)+k+...+(n-1), then m(X,Y) is congruent to 0 modulo I.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Such a monomial must be subject to one of the straightening relations of Corollary 3.2.2 within the tail variables. Applying a straightening step preserves the property that the tail degree is too large, while lowering the chosen monomial order. Iteration must terminate; no admissible monomial can have tail degree exceeding the stated maximum, so the result is zero in the quotient.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.2.4, one-dimensional top total degree
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every bidegree (i,j) with i+j=binom(n,2), the component (R_n)_{i,j} is one-dimensional.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Corollary 3.2.2, any surviving monomial in total degree binom(n,2) must have pair-degree exactly k-1 in x_k,y_k for every k. Thus monomials have the form product_k x_k^{r_k} y_k^{s_k} with r_k+s_k=k-1. The proof compares two adjacent pairs k,k+1. Applying the transposition (k,k+1) to a monomial and straightening via Lemma 3.2.1 gives a relation expressing the transposed monomial in terms of two nearby exponent redistributions. Applying the same relation twice yields a tridiagonal linear relation among monomials m_a indexed by the number of x_k's assigned to the kth pair while keeping the total x-degree across pairs k,k+1 fixed. The tridiagonal matrix has positive entries on its three diagonals and each column sums to 1, so by elementary Markov-chain theory its eigenvalue-1 eigenspace is one-dimensional, forcing all m_a to be congruent. Repeating this adjacent redistribution moves all x-exponents before all y-exponents and reduces every admissible monomial of bidegree (i,j) to the unique ordered monomial of that bidegree.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.3.1, diagonal harmonics linear in Y
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (H_n)_{d,1} be the component of diagonal harmonics of X-degree d and Y-degree 1. Then sum_d t^d phi((H_n)_{d,1}) = (1+t+...+t^{n-2}) sum_{lambda partition of n} t^{binom(n-1,2)} K_{lambda,mu}(t^{-1}) s_lambda, where mu=(2,1^{n-2}) and K_{lambda,mu}(t) are the Hall-Littlewood t-Kostka coefficients.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof, due to Alfano and Wallach, uses harmonic-preserving operators. Besides E=sum_i y_i partial_{x_i}, use D=sum_i y_i partial_{y_i} partial_{x_i}. Starting from the Vandermonde Delta(X), the polynomials D^k E Delta(X), for 0≤k≤n-2, and all their X-derivatives are diagonal harmonic and linear in Y. The top element D^{n-2}E Delta(X) is, up to scalar, sum_i (-1)^i y_i Delta(x_1,...,x_{i-1},x_{i+1},...,x_n). A result of Garsia-Procesi and Bergeron-Garsia gives the Frobenius series of its X-derivative span as sum_lambda t^{binom(n-1,2)} K_{lambda,mu}(t^{-1}) s_lambda. Let A_k be the derivative span of D^k E Delta(X). The operator D induces S_n-equivariant degree-lowering surjections A_k/(A_k cap A_{k+1}) -> A_{k+1}/(A_{k+1} cap A_{k+2}), so the sum A=sum_k A_k has Frobenius series at least the displayed product, with equality if dim A≤(n-1)n!/2. To get the bound, place A inside the space T of all sums_i y_i f_i with f_i ordinary X-harmonic and sum_i f_i=0, which has dimension (n-1)n!. Compare the apolar orthogonal complement B of A in T with the diagonal-harmonic equations: writing f_i=g_i(partial_X)Delta(X), the equations defining B and those defining (H_n)_{-,1} are dual copies of the same family sum_i partial_{x_i}^k g_i(partial_X)Delta(X)=0. This gives dim B=dim(H_n)_{-,1} and B+(H_n)_{-,1}=T, forcing dim A≤(n-1)n!/2 and hence equality throughout.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.4.1, explicit solution of the Andrews-Gessel-Garsia q-Lagrange coefficients
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let H(z)=sum_{k≥0} h_k z^k with h_0=1, and define e_k by 1/H(-z)=sum_{k≥0} e_k z^k. Let h_n^*(q) be the symmetric functions determined by the q-Lagrange inverse relation z = sum_{k≥0} q^{-binom(k,2)} h_k^*(q) z^{k+1} H(-z) H(-q^{-1}z) ... H(-q^{-k}z). Then h_n^*(q)=sum_{mu subset delta_n} q^{binom(n,2)-|mu|} e_{lambda(mu)}, where delta_n=(n-1,n-2,...,1,0), the sum is over partitions mu contained in delta_n, and lambda(mu) is the partition of n whose parts are the multiplicities m_i(mu) of the parts i of mu, including m_0 so that the total number of parts is n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof rewrites the q-inverse relation using q-functional composition. Dividing the defining equation by zH(-z), replacing z by qz, and applying the basic inverse identity for q-composition yields eH^*(z;q) = (1/H(-qz)) composed_q with (z eH^*(z;q)), where eH^*(z;q)=sum q^k h_k^*(q) z^k. Comparing coefficients gives the recurrence h_n^*(q)=sum_{k=1}^n q^{binom(k,2)} e_k sum_{n_1+...+n_k=n-k} product_{i=1}^k q^{(i-1)n_i} h_{n_i}^*(q). To verify the closed formula, decompose each partition mu contained in delta_n by k, the number of zero parts. For 1≤i≤k, let p_i be the largest index whose row of mu does not fit in delta_{n-i}, and set p_i=n_1+...+n_i. The rows between p_{i-1} and p_i contribute the factor q^{(i-1)n_i} h_{n_i}^*(q), while the k zero parts contribute q^{binom(k,2)} e_k. This exactly matches the recurrence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.5.1, Frobenius series of modules in a conjectural exact sequence
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix n and 0≤k≤n. Let R_{n-k} be graded by complement of X-degree, so its Frobenius series is denoted F_{n-k}(q). Tensor this S_{n-k}-module with the S_k-module Q[x_1,...,x_k]/(x_1^{n+1-k},...,x_k^{n+1-k}), graded in the usual way, and induce the resulting S_k x S_{n-k}-module to S_n. If H(z)=sum_{r≥0} h_r z^r is the complete symmetric-function generating series, the Frobenius series of this induced module is F_{n-k}(q) times the coefficient of z^k in H(z)H(qz)...H(q^{n-k}z).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: It remains only to compute the Frobenius series of Q[x_1,...,x_k]/(x_i^{n+1-k}). The sequence x_1^{n+1-k},...,x_k^{n+1-k} is a homogeneous system of parameters, so the Koszul resolution gives the quotient character as the internal product of the polynomial-ring Frobenius series with the exterior alternating contribution of the parameters. The calculation simplifies to the coefficient of z^k in H(z)H(qz)...H(q^{n-k}z). Induction from S_k x S_{n-k} to S_n corresponds to multiplication of Frobenius characteristics, giving the displayed product with F_{n-k}(q).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 4.5.2, exact sequence implication for the parking-function Frobenius conjecture
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If for each n there exists an exact sequence 0 -> R_n^{(n)} -> R_n^{(n-1)} -> ... -> R_n^{(0)}=R_n -> 0 of graded S_n-modules, where R_n^{(k)} is the module described in Proposition 4.5.1, then the conjectural formula F_n(1,q)=sum_{mu subset delta_n} q^{binom(n,2)-|mu|} e_{lambda(mu)} holds.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The q-Lagrange identity in Proposition 4.4.1, after rewriting with F_n(q)=q^{binom(n,2)}F_n(1,q^{-1}), gives an alternating sum identity among the Frobenius series appearing in Proposition 4.5.1. An exact sequence with those terms would imply the alternating sum of Frobenius characteristics is zero. Solving the resulting recurrence is exactly the q-Lagrange solution, giving the stated formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.2.1, enlarging the operator algebra does not enlarge the generated harmonic space
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Delta(X)=product_{i<j}(x_i-x_j). Define E_p=sum_i y_i partial_{x_i}^p for p>0. Consider also all S_n-invariant differential operators linear in the variables x_i,y_i and homogeneous of nonpositive total degree, of the form sum_i y_i partial_{x_i}^h partial_{y_i}^k f(partials in all variables except x_i,y_i), and the analogous operators with x and y interchanged, where f is diagonally S_{n-1}-invariant. The smallest space containing Delta(X) and closed under all partial derivatives and all such operators is the same as the space generated from Delta(X) using only the E_p and the partial derivatives partial_{x_i}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper first notes that the large operator algebra is generated by pure invariant differential operators together with E^0_{h,k}=sum_i y_i partial_{x_i}^h partial_{y_i}^k and E^1_{h,k}=sum_i x_i partial_{x_i}^h partial_{y_i}^k. Since pure invariant negative-degree differential operators kill harmonics, only products of the E^a_{h,k} and ordinary derivatives matter. Their span is a Lie algebra, and the Poincare-Birkhoff-Witt theorem lets one order products so that all factors with k>0, h>n-1, or of type E^1_{h,0} act last. Such factors either kill Delta(X) or act by scalar/eigenvalue behavior after the preceding reductions. Therefore only the operators E^0_{p,0}=E_p with 1≤p≤n-1 and the X-derivatives are needed.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 5.2.2, symmetry of X- and Y-generated operator spaces
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The space generated from Delta(X) by the operators E_p=sum_i y_i partial_{x_i}^p and the derivatives partial_{x_i} is equal to the space generated from Delta(Y) by the operators F_p=sum_i x_i partial_{y_i}^p and the derivatives partial_{y_i}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The first generated space contains Delta(Y), because repeated application of E_1 to Delta(X) produces Delta(Y) up to a scalar. By Proposition 5.2.1 this first space is closed under the larger symmetric operator algebra, including the Y-version operators F_p and Y-derivatives, so it contains the second generated space. The reverse inclusion follows by interchanging X and Y.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.3.1, substitution criterion for the orthogonal ideal of the operator-generated space
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let J be the ideal orthogonal, under the apolar form, to the space generated from Delta(X) by E_p=sum_i y_i partial_{x_i}^p and partial_{x_i}. Then J consists exactly of those f(X,Y) such that f(x_1,...,x_n, phi_a(x_1),...,phi_a(x_n)) lies in the classical one-set coinvariant ideal generated by positive-degree symmetric polynomials in X, for every polynomial phi_a(x)=a_1 x+a_2 x^2+...+a_{n-1}x^{n-1}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The adjoint of E_p is E_p^*=sum_i x_i^p partial_{y_i}. Thus f is in J iff for every sequence of exponents, (E_1^*)^{e_1}...(E_{n-1}^*)^{e_{n-1}} f, evaluated at Y=0, is orthogonal to all X-derivatives of Delta(X), equivalently belongs to the ordinary coinvariant ideal in Q[X]. The exponential exp(a_1E_1^*+...+a_{n-1}E_{n-1}^*) translates each y_i to y_i+phi_a(x_i). Setting Y=0 gives exactly the family of substitutions in the statement, and taking all coefficients in the a_i is equivalent to requiring all iterated adjoint conditions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.5.2, operator rings as subrings of an ordinary coinvariant quotient
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let J be the ideal in Q[x_1,...,x_n,e_1,...,e_{n-1}] consisting of all polynomials p such that the differential operator p(partial_{x_1},...,partial_{x_n},E_1,...,E_{n-1}) kills Delta(X), and define T_n=Q[x_1,...,x_n,e_1,...,e_{n-1}]/J and U_n=Q[e_1,...,e_{n-1}]/(J cap Q[e_1,...,e_{n-1}]). Let N=Q[X,Y]/(p_1(X),...,p_n(X)), where p_i(X) are fundamental symmetric power sums in X. Then T_n is isomorphic to the subring of N generated by the x_i and by e_k=sum_i y_i x_i^k, and U_n is isomorphic to the subring generated by the e_k alone.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: A differential operator polynomial in y_i and partial_{x_i} kills Delta(X) precisely when each coefficient, after replacing partial_{x_i} by x_i, lies in the classical ideal generated by the symmetric functions p_1(X),...,p_n(X). Under this replacement, the operator E_k=sum_i y_i partial_{x_i}^k corresponds to the polynomial sum_i y_i x_i^k. Therefore the quotient by operators annihilating Delta(X) is exactly the indicated subring of N.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Propositions 5.5.3 and 5.5.4, complete-intersection models for the q=t^{-1} predictions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If eU_n=Q[e_1,...,e_{n-1}]/(theta_1,...,theta_{n-1}) with deg(e_k)=k+1 and theta_i a homogeneous system of parameters of degrees n+i+1, then the Hilbert series of eU_n is the conjectural Hilbert series for U_n, namely (1-q^{n+2})...(1-q^{2n})/((1-q^2)...(1-q^n)). If eT_n=Q[x_1,...,x_n,e_1,...,e_{n-1}]/(theta_1,...,theta_{2n-1}), with deg(x_i)=1, deg(e_k)=k+1, theta_1,...,theta_n S_n-invariants of degrees 1,...,n, and theta_{n+1},...,theta_{2n-1} of degree n+1 spanning the irreducible reflection representation, then the Frobenius series of eT_n is the conjectural Frobenius series for T_n, obtained by applying the involution omega to the specialization in Conjecture 2.5.1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For eU_n the formula is the standard Hilbert series of a complete intersection: multiply the numerator factors 1-q^{deg theta_i} and divide by the variable-degree factors 1-q^{deg e_k}. For eT_n, the invariant parameters theta_1,...,theta_n cancel the ordinary polynomial-ring invariant contributions, while the degree n+1 reflection-representation parameters are handled by the Koszul calculation of Proposition 2.5.3. The remaining Frobenius series is exactly the predicted q=t^{-1} expression, with omega accounting for the sign twist.

proof_techniques.name: Apolar duality and diagonal harmonics
proof_techniques.description: The paper systematically replaces the quotient R_n=Q[X,Y]/I by the orthogonal complement H_n=I^perp under the apolar form. Multiplication by a variable is adjoint to partial differentiation, so ideals correspond to derivative-stable spaces of harmonic polynomials. This converts statements about quotient classes into statements about polynomial solutions of invariant differential equations p(partial_X,partial_Y)h=0.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For Schur coefficients in the multigraded Hilbert series, one can try to identify the relevant GL_m highest-weight spaces inside the harmonic model. Hook Schur components are often easier to isolate by differential equations and raising/lowering operators than directly in the quotient.

proof_techniques.name: GL_2 and sl_2 weight decomposition
proof_techniques.description: Because the two variable sets form the rows of a 2 by n matrix, GL_2 acts by row operations and commutes with S_n. The sl_2 generators E=sum y_i partial_{x_i}, F=sum x_i partial_{y_i}, and H=sum y_i partial_{y_i}-x_i partial_{x_i} make bidegrees into weight spaces. Finite-dimensional sl_2 representation theory forces the q=t^{-1} specialization and each total-degree diagonal to decompose into symmetric strings.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem is phrased in terms of Schur polynomials in the grading variables, i.e. GL_m characters. This paper gives the m=2 prototype: Schur coefficients can be attacked by decomposing the multigrading under GL_m, and hook shapes should correspond to explicit highest-weight or exterior-power conditions.

proof_techniques.name: Polarization and straightening using invariant complete symmetric functions
proof_techniques.description: The relations h_k(alpha x_k+beta y_k,...,alpha x_n+beta y_n)|_{alpha^r beta^s} in I provide leading terms x_k^r y_k^s with r+s=k. With a lexicographic monomial order, these relations straighten any monomial whose kth variable-pair degree is too large. The method yields spanning monomials, vanishing bounds above total degree binom(n,2), tail-degree vanishing, and one-dimensionality in top total degree.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For hook Schur coefficients, especially if a combinatorial interpretation comes from monomial or lattice-path data, analogous straightening relations may identify allowed exponent arrays and force finite combinatorial models.

proof_techniques.name: Parking-function model for one-variable Frobenius specialization
proof_techniques.description: The paper relates the conjectural specialization F_n(1,q) to the S_n-module on parking functions, with grading by weight w(f)=n(n+1)/2-sum f(i). Orbits of parking functions correspond to partitions mu contained in the staircase delta_n, and this gives the elementary-symmetric expansion sum_{mu subset delta_n} q^{binom(n,2)-|mu|} e_{lambda(mu)}.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Hook Schur coefficients in a stable GL_m Hilbert series may refine or extend parking-function statistics. This paper identifies the parking-function/staircase-partition data that any hook interpretation should specialize to in small numbers of variable sets.

proof_techniques.name: q-Lagrange inversion as a structural recurrence
proof_techniques.description: The Andrews-Gessel-Garsia q-Lagrange inverse relation produces coefficients h_n^*(q) satisfying a recurrence that matches a decomposition of partitions inside the staircase. Haiman shows explicitly that h_n^*(q)=sum_{mu subset delta_n} q^{binom(n,2)-|mu|} e_{lambda(mu)}, and explains how specializations recover parking-function weights, tree inversion enumerators, and q-Catalan numbers.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If hook coefficients satisfy a recursive decomposition by the first column or leg of a hook, the q-Lagrange recurrence gives a template for converting algebraic identities into combinatorial objects such as staircase partitions, trees, or parking functions.

proof_techniques.name: Koszul resolution for character-valued Hilbert series
proof_techniques.description: Several formulas are obtained by resolving a quotient by a homogeneous system of parameters with the Koszul complex. The alternating sum of the graded S_n-characters of the free terms is computed via internal products of Frobenius characteristics, yielding principal Schur-specialization quotients and proving positivity when the quotient exists.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For m-set diagonal coinvariants, hook components may be expressible through quotients by parameter sequences carrying simple GL_m or S_n representations. Koszul computations can turn such presentations into explicit Schur-coefficient formulas.

proof_techniques.name: Operator generation from the Vandermonde
proof_techniques.description: The paper proposes that all diagonal harmonics are generated from Delta(X) by E_p=sum y_i partial_{x_i}^p and ordinary derivatives, and proves that allowing a much larger natural Lie algebra of invariant differential operators does not enlarge the generated space. The adjoint formulation converts operator-generated spaces into substitution criteria f(X,phi_a(X)) in the classical coinvariant ideal.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Hook components often arise by applying a controlled number of variable-set raising operators. The operator-generation viewpoint suggests constructing hook-shaped GL_m highest-weight vectors from Vandermonde derivatives and proving their span by adjoint substitution tests.

proof_techniques.name: Top-degree and low-Y-degree isolation
proof_techniques.description: The top total degree is shown to be extremely small: every bidegree on total degree binom(n,2) is one-dimensional. Separately, the whole component linear in Y is computed using operators E and D and known q-Kostka modules. This isolates a thin slice of the diagonal harmonics where exact Frobenius formulas are accessible.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Hook Schur shapes include cases with a small exterior leg, which can correspond to being linear or low-degree in some auxiliary variable sets. The proof of Theorem 3.3.1 is a model for deriving exact hook or near-hook components from a small number of operator-generated alternating polynomials.

proof_techniques.name: Exact-sequence interpretation of q-Lagrange identities
proof_techniques.description: Haiman rewrites the q-Lagrange recurrence as an alternating sum of Frobenius series of explicit induced modules and observes that an exact sequence with these terms would prove the parking-function Frobenius conjecture. The terms combine smaller diagonal coinvariant rings with truncated polynomial rings and induction from Young subgroups.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A hook-coefficient formula might be proved by building a complex whose terms have known Schur expansions and whose Euler characteristic is the desired hook component. The induced-module construction gives a concrete pattern for such complexes.

other_useful_info: Important conjectural content formulated in the paper, not proved there: Conjecture 2.1.1 states dim R_n=(n+1)^{n-1}. Conjecture 2.2.2 states q^{binom(n,2)} H_n(q^{-1},q)=(1+q+...+q^n)^{n-1}. Conjecture 2.3.1 states H_n(1,q) is the inversion enumerator of labelled trees on {0,1,...,n}. Conjectures 2.4.1-2.4.2 identify the ungraded S_n-module R_n with the sign twist of the permutation module on Z_{n+1}^n/Z_{n+1}, equivalently F_n(1,1)=sum_{lambda partition of n} (1/(n+1)) multinomial(n+1; m_0(lambda),...,m_n(lambda)) e_lambda = sum_{lambda partition of n} s_{lambda'}(1^{n+1})/(n+1) s_lambda. Conjecture 2.5.1 gives the q=t^{-1} Frobenius specialization q^{binom(n,2)}F_n(q^{-1},q)=sum_{lambda partition of n} s_{lambda'}(1,q,...,q^n)/(1+q+...+q^n) s_lambda. Conjectures 2.6.3-2.6.4 identify R_n graded by X-degree with the sign twist of the parking-function module graded by weight, equivalently F_n(1,q)=sum_{mu subset delta_n} q^{binom(n,2)-|mu|} e_{lambda(mu)}. Section 2.7 defines the t,q-Catalan polynomial C_n(t,q)=<s_{1^n},F_n(t,q)> as the sign-isotypic multiplicity and records specializations to the Carlitz-Riordan q-Catalan numbers and the q-binomial Catalan 1/[n+1]_q [2n choose n]_q. Section 2.8 reports computational evidence for three variable sets: for n≤5, dim R_n(X,Y,Z)=2n(n+1)^{n-2}, and the sign multiplicity is ((3n+3)(3n+4)...(4n+1))/(3·4·...·(n+1)); it also lists dimensions for four sets for n≤4. Conjecture 5.1.1, the operator conjecture, says H_n is generated from Delta(X) by E_p=sum_i y_i partial_{x_i}^p and partial_{x_i}; it is verified by computer for n≤6 and proved in the paper only in degrees (-,0) and (-,1). Consequences proposed in Section 5.4 include that the socle of R_n consists exactly of alternating polynomials and that a monomial lies in I iff for some k more than n-k indices have combined x_i,y_i exponent at least k; the special case product_i (alpha_i x_i+beta_i y_i) in I is stated to have been proved by Alfano. Related references emphasized by the paper include Andrews, Gessel, and Garsia for q-Lagrange inversion; Kreweras for parking-function/tree bijections; Garsia-Procesi and Bergeron-Garsia for q-Kostka harmonic modules; and Weyl for generators of multisymmetric invariants.
why_relevant: Introduces the diagonal coinvariant ring for the symmetric group and formulates the basic Hilbert-series and representation-theoretic conjectures. It is the foundational source for the algebraic object R_n and its diagonal-invariant quotient construction.

title: Bosonic-Fermionic Diagonal Coinvariants and Theta Operators
overall_summary: This paper is primarily a conjectural and expository note connecting multivariate diagonal coinvariant modules with bosonic and fermionic variable sets, Macdonald Delta and Theta operators, and combinatorial models such as parking functions, multilabeled Dyck paths, and tiered trees. Its most useful structural contribution for the original problem is the stable GL_k × GL_j × S_n character framework: the Schur expansion in grading variables is independent of the number of bosonic or fermionic variable sets once enough variables are present, and the Hilbert series is obtained from the Frobenius characteristic by pairing with p_1^n. The paper formulates the central boson-fermion plethystic conjecture C_n(q;u;z)=E_n(q−εu;z), where E_n is the pure bosonic stable Frobenius characteristic; this is especially relevant to hook Schur coefficients because the GL(1|1) specialization q−εu is supported on hook shapes. It also records a one-boson/one-fermion standard-tableau formula involving descents, major index, and q-binomial coefficients, which is a plausible route to a combinatorial interpretation of hook coefficients after extracting ordinary hook Schur terms. Further potentially useful material includes the skew-operator link between E_n and Delta operators, and t=1 combinatorial expansions of super nabla operators by multilabeled Dyck paths.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Character framework for coefficient-stable G-modules, Equations 3.1-3.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let B=(β_ab) be a k by n matrix of commuting bosonic variables, let F=(φ_cd) be a j by n matrix of anticommuting fermionic variables, and let R_{k,j;n}=Q[B;F]. The group G=GL_k × GL_j × S_n acts by f(B;F) ↦ f(PBσ;QFσ), where P∈GL_k, Q∈GL_j, and σ is an n by n permutation matrix acting diagonally on columns. If V is a G-stable submodule or stable quotient of R_{k,j;n}, then its multigraded Frobenius characteristic has the form V(q;u;z)=sum over μ⊢n of (sum over λ,ρ of v^{λρ}_μ s_λ(q)s_ρ(u)) s_μ(z), where q=(q_1,...,q_k), u=(u_1,...,u_j), s_λ and s_ρ are GL characters, and s_μ(z) is the Frobenius image of the irreducible S_n-module indexed by μ. The multigraded Hilbert series, or GL_k × GL_j character obtained by forgetting the S_n action, is V(q;u)=⟨V(q;u;z),p_1(z)^n⟩=sum over μ⊢n of (sum over λ,ρ of v^{λρ}_μ s_λ(q)s_ρ(u)) f^μ, where f^μ=⟨s_μ,p_1^n⟩ is the number of standard tableaux of shape μ. For the diagonal coinvariant modules C_{k,j;n}, the paper writes C_n(q;u;z)=sum over μ⊢n c_μ(q;u)s_μ(z), with c_μ(q;u)=sum over λ,ρ c^{λρ}_μ s_λ(q)s_ρ(u), and emphasizes that these coefficients are stable, i.e. independent of k and j once enough variables are present.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is the standard character decomposition for commuting actions. Because the actions of GL_k, GL_j, and S_n commute, and the relevant finite multihomogeneous pieces are semisimple, V decomposes into irreducibles of the form S_λ(C^k) ⊗ S_ρ(C^j) ⊗ S^μ with multiplicities v^{λρ}_μ. The characters of S_λ(C^k) and S_ρ(C^j) are Schur polynomials s_λ(q) and s_ρ(u), while the Frobenius characteristic sends S^μ to s_μ(z). Pairing with p_1(z)^n replaces each irreducible S_n-character by its dimension, since ⟨s_μ,p_1^n⟩=f^μ. Coefficient stability is taken as part of the setup for the modules considered: when k or j is too small, Schur functions with too many parts vanish, but the stable multiplicities themselves do not depend on k,j.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Haiman theorem for two bosonic sets, Equation 1.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let C_{2;n}=C[x_1,...,x_n,y_1,...,y_n]/I_n(x,y), where I_n(x,y) is generated by all nonconstant S_n-invariant polynomials under the diagonal action permuting x_i and y_i simultaneously. Let C_{2;n}(q,t;z) be its bigraded Frobenius characteristic, with q tracking x-degree and t tracking y-degree. Let ∇ be the Bergeron-Garsia nabla operator on modified Macdonald polynomials, defined by ∇ H̃_μ(q,t;z)=T_μ H̃_μ(q,t;z), where T_μ=product over cells (i,j) in μ of q^i t^j, using the paper's cell coordinates. Then Haiman's theorem states that C_{2;n}(q,t;z)=∇ e_n(z).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites Haiman's result and does not reproduce the proof. The proof in the cited work uses Hilbert schemes, the n! theorem, and the geometry of the isospectral Hilbert scheme to identify the bigraded Frobenius characteristic of diagonal coinvariants with the Macdonald-eigenoperator expression ∇e_n. In the present paper this theorem is used as the two-bosonic-set specialization of the general stable bosonic character E_n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: With Ω(q)=product over i=1,...,k of (1−q_i)^{-1}=sum_{r≥0} h_r(q) and Ω'(u)=product over i=1,...,j of (1+u_i)=sum_{r≥0} e_r(u), the Frobenius characteristic of the full boson-fermion polynomial ring R_n=R_{k,j;n}=Q[B;F] is R_n(q;u;z)=h_n[Ω(q) Ω'(u) z] in plethystic notation.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For each column indexed by 1,...,n, the commuting variables contribute the symmetric algebra character Ω(q), while the anticommuting variables contribute the exterior algebra character Ω'(u). The symmetric group S_n permutes the n columns. The Frobenius characteristic of an n-fold set of such weighted letters is obtained by the cycle-index/plethystic construction h_n[A z], with A=Ω(q)Ω'(u). This gives h_n[Ω(q)Ω'(u)z]. The paper states this proposition without a detailed proof, relying on standard plethystic character calculus.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Plethystic identity for bosonic and fermionic alphabets, Equation 3.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the paper's plethystic notation, ε is defined by p_i[ε]=(-1)^i, so that p_i[−ε u]=ω p_i(u). The identity Ω[q−εu]=sum over n≥0 and k+j=n of h_k(q)e_j(u)=Ω(q)Ω'(u) holds. More generally, the skew Cauchy expansion gives s_λ[q−εu]=sum over ν⊆λ of s_ν(q)s_{λ'/ν'}(u), where λ' and ν' are conjugate partitions and s_{λ'/ν'} is a skew Schur function.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The first identity is the plethystic translation of multiplying the generating series for complete symmetric functions in bosonic variables by the generating series for elementary symmetric functions in fermionic variables. The sign alphabet −εu converts complete symmetric functions into elementary symmetric functions via the involution ω. The Schur expansion follows from the standard skew Cauchy identity applied to the difference of alphabets q−εu. This identity is explicitly recalled in the paper and is the algebraic mechanism behind the boson-fermion substitution.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the purely bosonic polynomial ring case j=0, let r_μ(q)=⟨R_n(q;0;z),s_μ(z)⟩ be the coefficient of s_μ(z) in the Frobenius characteristic of the full polynomial ring R_{k,0;n}. Then r_μ(q)=s_μ[Ω(q)]=s_μ[sum_{i≥0} h_i(q)].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Set u=0 in Proposition 1 to get R_n(q;0;z)=h_n[Ω(q)z]. The Cauchy identity in plethystic form expands h_n[Xz] as sum over μ⊢n of s_μ[X]s_μ(z). Taking X=Ω(q), the coefficient of s_μ(z) is s_μ[Ω(q)]. The paper notes that the Schur expansion of this expression is connected with the restriction problem for GL_k characters restricted to permutation matrices.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Stable pure bosonic expansion cited from Bergeron, Section 4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let E_n denote the stable Frobenius characteristic of the pure bosonic multivariate diagonal coinvariant module. The paper recalls that there is a coefficient-stable expression E_n=sum over μ⊢n of a_μ ⊗ s_μ, where a_μ=sum over λ of a^λ_μ s_λ, equivalently E_n(q;z)=sum over μ⊢n (sum over λ a^λ_μ s_λ(q)) s_μ(z). The integers a^λ_μ vanish unless λ has size at most binom(n,2)−η(μ') and has at most n−μ_1 parts, where η(α)=sum_i α_i(i−1) and μ' is the conjugate partition.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this stable expression from Bergeron's work and does not prove it. The underlying idea is that the pure bosonic diagonal coinvariant module carries commuting GL_k and S_n actions, so its stable character decomposes into Schur functors for GL_k and irreducibles for S_n. The stated degree and length bounds are imported from the cited theory of multivariate diagonal harmonics and ensure that only finitely many Schur functions s_λ(q) occur for each μ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Conjecture 2, Boson-Fermion Frobenius
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let C_n(q;u;z) be the stable Frobenius characteristic of the boson-fermion diagonal coinvariant module C_{k,j;n}, and let E_n(q;z)=sum over μ⊢n a_μ(q)s_μ(z) be the stable pure bosonic Frobenius characteristic. The conjecture states that C_n(q;u;z)=E_n(q−εu;z)=sum over μ⊢n a_μ[q−εu]s_μ(z)=sum over μ⊢n sum over λ a^λ_μ s_λ[q−εu]s_μ(z). Using the skew expansion, this is equivalently C_n=sum over μ⊢n sum over λ a^λ_μ (sum over ν⊆λ s_ν ⊗ s_{λ'/ν'}) ⊗ s_μ. The paper lists several specializations: with no fermions, C_n(q_1,...,q_k;0;z)=E_n(q_1,...,q_k;z); with two bosonic variables, C_n(q+t;0;z)=∇e_n(z); with one fermionic variable and no bosons, C_n(0;u;z)=sum_{a=0}^{n−1} u^a s_{(n−a,1^a)}(z); with several fermionic variables and no bosons, C_n(0;u_1,...,u_j;z)=sum over μ⊢n (sum over λ a^λ_μ s_{λ'}(u_1,...,u_j))s_μ(z); and for a single bosonic variable q, C_n(q;0;z)=hat h_n(z)/hat h_n(1), where hat f(z)=f[z/(1−q)].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is a conjecture; the paper gives no proof. The evidence described is compatibility with known and conjectured special cases, including Haiman's two-boson theorem, the pure fermionic specializations, and formulas involving Delta and Theta operators. Section 6 explains one mechanism behind the conjecture: the identity sum_a u^a e_a^⊥ f(q)=f[q−εu] and the conjectural Delta-via-skew formula would imply the one-fermion, two-boson specialization C_n(q+t;u;z)=E_n[q+t−εu;z].

key_theorems_lemmas.label: One-boson/one-fermion standard-tableau formula, Equation 5.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For one bosonic variable q and one fermionic variable u, N. Bergeron, Machacek, and Zabrocki conjectured that C_n(q;u;z)=sum_{r=0}^{n−1} sum over λ⊢n sum over τ∈SYT(λ) of q^{α_r(τ)} [des(τ) choose r]_q u^r s_λ(z), where des(τ) is the number of descents of the standard tableau τ, maj(τ) is its major index, α_r(τ)=maj(τ)−r des(τ)+binom(r,2), and [des(τ) choose r]_q is the Gaussian binomial coefficient, interpreted as zero if r>des(τ).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper presents this as a conjecture and does not prove it. A footnote states that it follows from Zabrocki's conjectural formula C_n(q+t;u;z)=sum_{a=0}^{n−1} u^a Δ'_{e_{n−a−1}}e_n(z) together with a formula of Haglund, Rhoades, and Shimozono. The paper also rewrites it as C_n(q;u;z)=sum_{a=0}^{n−1} u^a Δ'_{e_{n−a−1}}e_n(z) evaluated at t=0, again conditional on the conjectural Delta-coinvariant connection.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Kim-Rhoades pure fermionic two-variable formula, Equations 5.3-5.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For two fermionic variables u and v and no bosonic variables, Kim and Rhoades prove the formula C_n(0;u+v;z)=sum over a,b≥0 with a+b≤n−1 of u^a v^b (s_{(n−a,1^a)} ⋆ s_{(n−b,1^b)} − s_{(n−a+1,1^{a−1})} ⋆ s_{(n−b+1,1^{b−1})})(z), with the convention that invalid hook partitions contribute zero. Here ⋆ is the Kronecker product of Schur functions. Equivalently, using Frobenius notation (a|b)=(a+1,1^b) and Kronecker coefficients g^μ_{α,β}=⟨s_α⋆s_β,s_μ⟩, the coefficient of s_μ(z) is a sum of differences g^μ_{(a|b),(c|d)}−g^μ_{(a+1|b−1),(c+1|d−1)} over b+d≤n−1 with a+b=c+d=n−1. The paper notes that these differences are known to be positive, citing Remmel.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites Kim and Rhoades for the proof and does not reproduce it. The cited result comes from Lefschetz theory for exterior algebras and fermionic diagonal coinvariants. The reformulation in terms of Kronecker coefficients is obtained by expanding each Kronecker product in the Schur basis and collecting the coefficient of s_μ(z).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Set all bosonic parameters q_i equal to 1 for i=1,...,k and all fermionic parameters u_i equal to 1 for i=1,...,j, and write C_n(k;j;z) for the resulting Frobenius characteristic. Then the coefficient of each s_μ(z) in the Schur expansion of C_n(k;j;z) is a polynomial in k and j with rational coefficients. Consequently, the total dimension C_n(k;j), obtained by replacing each s_μ(z) by f^μ, is also a polynomial in k and j with rational coefficients.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Using coefficient stability, the coefficient of s_μ(z) is a finite sum of stable multiplicities times specializations s_λ(1^k)s_ρ(1^j). Each specialization s_λ(1^k) is a polynomial in k with rational coefficients by the hook-content formula, and similarly s_ρ(1^j) is polynomial in j. Finite sums and the final dimension-weighted sum by f^μ preserve polynomiality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Conjecture 3, Delta via skew
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every a, the paper recalls the conjecture ((e_a^⊥ ⊗ Id)E_n)(q+t;z)=sum over μ⊢n (e_a^⊥ a_μ)(q+t)s_μ(z)=Δ'_{e_{n−a−1}}(e_n(z)). Here e_a^⊥ is the Hall-inner-product adjoint to multiplication by e_a, E_n=sum_μ a_μ⊗s_μ is the stable pure bosonic Frobenius characteristic, q+t means evaluation of the GL-character part in two variables q,t, and Δ'_f is the Macdonald Delta operator defined by Δ'_f H̃_μ=f[B_μ(q,t)−1]H̃_μ with B_μ(q,t)=sum over cells (i,j) in μ of q^i t^j.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is stated as a conjecture from the cited GL_k × S_n diagonal harmonics work, not proved in this paper. The paper explains its role: for any symmetric function f(q), sum_a u^a e_a^⊥ f(q)=f[q−εu]. Therefore Conjecture 3 would transform the stable bosonic character into the one-fermion specialization and yield C_n(q+t;u;z)=E_n[q+t−εu;z], matching Zabrocki's Delta-operator conjecture.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Hook component identity for the super nabla operator, Section 8.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let ∇_v be the super nabla operator defined on modified Macdonald polynomials by ∇_v H̃_μ(q,t;z)=H̃_μ(q,t;v)H̃_μ(q,t;z). The paper recalls the well-known identity ⟨H̃_μ(q,t;v),s_{n−k,1^k}(v)⟩=e_k[B_μ(q,t)−1] for 0≤k≤n−1. Consequently, the coefficient of the hook Schur function s_{n−k,1^k}(v) in ∇_v e_n(z) is Δ'_{e_k}e_n(z). At t=1, under the multilabeled path model for ∇_v e_n, extracting this hook component corresponds to restricting to paths whose reading word in the v-labels is a shuffle of the increasing sequence k+1,k+2,...,n and the decreasing sequence k,k−1,...,1; the paper explains that such paths map naturally to valley-decorated labeled Dyck paths.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The Macdonald hook scalar-product formula gives the coefficient of s_{n−k,1^k}(v) in each H̃_μ(v). Applying it termwise in the Macdonald expansion of ∇_v e_n(z) produces exactly the eigenvalue e_k[B_μ−1], which is the defining eigenvalue of Δ'_{e_k}; hence the hook coefficient is Δ'_{e_k}e_n. The t=1 path restriction uses a standard construction from the shuffle-theorem literature: Schur-hook extraction is encoded by reading words that are shuffles of the specified increasing and decreasing sequences. The nonascent condition in the multilabeled Dyck path then forces the corresponding rows to be movable valleys.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3, t=1 multilabeled Dyck path formula for super nabla
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For positive integers n and k, let MD_{kn} be the set of multilabeled kn-Dyck paths defined as follows. A path D goes from (0,0) to (kn,n), uses North and East steps, stays weakly above the relevant diagonal, and has area equal to the number of complete cells between D and the diagonal. A multilabeling assigns to the i-th North step a word w_i=(w_i^1,...,w_i^{k+1}) of positive integers, subject to the condition that the number of East steps of D along row y=i is at least the number of components j for which w_i^j≥w_{i+1}^j. For P=(D,w), define X^P=product over i=1,...,n of v_{1,w_i^1}⋯v_{k,w_i^k} z_{w_i^{k+1}}. Then the t=1 specialization of iterated super nabla is ∇_{v_1}⋯∇_{v_k} e_n(z) evaluated at t=1 equals sum over P∈MD_{kn} of q^{area(P)} X^P.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proposition is quoted from Bergeron-Haglund-Iraci-Romero and is not proved in this paper. Conceptually, at t=1 the super nabla operator admits a monomial expansion indexed by Dyck paths with multiple label sets; the q-power records the area statistic, the labels record the monomial in the v_i and z alphabets, and the row-wise nonascent condition is precisely the compatibility condition ensuring the labels define an allowed multilabeled path.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5, Schur expansion of t=1 multilabeled path formulas
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A labeling w=w_1⋯w_n of a multilabeled path is called lattice if, for every component j, the sequence (w_1^j,w_2^j,...,w_n^j) is a lattice word. For a lattice labeling, let λ_j(P) be the partition associated with the j-th component lattice word. Let LMD_{kn} be the set of lattice multilabeled kn-Dyck paths. Then ∇_{v_1}⋯∇_{v_k}e_n(z) evaluated at t=1 equals sum over P∈LMD_{kn} of q^{area(P)} s_{λ_1(P)}(v_1)⋯s_{λ_k(P)}(v_k)s_{λ_{k+1}(P)}(z). More generally, for a nonempty partition γ, the analogous marked γ,kn-Dyck paths RLMD_{γ,kn} give Δ_{m_γ}∇_{v_1}⋯∇_{v_k}((-1)^{n−1}p_n(z)) evaluated at t=1 as the corresponding sum over P∈RLMD_{γ,kn} of q^{area(P)} s_{λ_1(P)}(v_1)⋯s_{λ_k(P)}(v_k)s_{λ_{k+1}(P)}(z).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proposition is quoted from Bergeron-Haglund-Iraci-Romero. It is obtained from the monomial formula by applying the standard highest-weight or lattice-word extraction rule for Schur expansions: in a symmetric function, the coefficient of s_λ can be read from monomials whose words are lattice words of shape λ. Applying this independently in each variable set v_1,...,v_k,z converts the monomial sum over all multilabeled paths into a simultaneous Schur-positive sum over lattice multilabeled paths.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Xi-operator identities, Section 9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M=(1−q)(1−t), let f^*(z)=f[z/M], and let Π be the Macdonald eigenoperator with eigenvalue product over nonzero cells (i,j) of μ of (1−q^i t^j) on H̃_μ. The paper defines ∇_M=MΔ_{e_1}Π and ΞF(z)=∇_M F^*(z). Using Macdonald orthogonality, it obtains Ξe_λ(z)=⟨∇_v e_n(z),h_λ(v)⟩ and equivalently ∇_v e_n(z)=sum over λ⊢n of m_λ(v) Ξe_λ(z). At t=1, if MD^1_{1n}(λ) denotes the set of one-v-label Dyck paths whose v-label multiset has content λ, then Ξe_λ(z) evaluated at t=1 equals sum over P∈MD^1_{1n}(λ) of q^{area(P)} e_{η(P)}(z), where η(P) is the composition extracted from the path by subtracting label nonascents from horizontal run lengths as defined in the paper.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper starts from the Macdonald expansion e_λ^*=sum_μ H̃_μ/w_μ · ⟨H̃_μ,e_λ^*⟩_* and uses the relation ⟨H̃_μ,e_λ^*⟩_*=⟨H̃_μ,h_λ⟩. Applying ∇_M and comparing with the definition of super nabla gives Ξe_λ=⟨∇_v e_n,h_λ(v)⟩. Expanding in the monomial basis in the v variables gives ∇_v e_n=sum_λ m_λ(v)Ξe_λ. The t=1 path formula follows by specializing the multilabeled Dyck path model and grouping the z-label contributions into elementary symmetric functions indexed by η(P).

proof_techniques.name: Stable GL-character packaging of multigraded Hilbert series
proof_techniques.description: The paper treats diagonal coinvariant spaces as modules for GL_k × GL_j × S_n, with GL_k acting on bosonic variable sets and GL_j on fermionic variable sets. This upgrades a multigraded Hilbert series into a Schur expansion in grading alphabets q and u, together with an S_n Frobenius characteristic in z. The Hilbert series is then recovered by pairing the z-Frobenius characteristic with p_1^n, which simply weights each S_n-isotypic coefficient by the dimension f^μ of the corresponding irreducible.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the original pure bosonic algebra R_n^{(m)}, this says that the universal Hilbert coefficient c_λ(n) is obtained from the stable pure bosonic Frobenius coefficients by c_λ(n)=sum_μ f^μ a^λ_μ. Thus one can first seek a combinatorial interpretation of the refined coefficients a^λ_μ, or use the p_1^n pairing to turn tableau formulas in μ into Hilbert-series formulas.

proof_techniques.name: Supersymmetric specialization q−εu to access hook Schur coefficients
proof_techniques.description: The central plethystic device is replacing the bosonic alphabet q by the virtual alphabet q−εu, with s_λ[q−εu]=sum_{ν⊆λ}s_ν(q)s_{λ'/ν'}(u). This transforms a pure bosonic stable character into a conjectural boson-fermion character. In the especially important GL(1|1) case, where q and u are single variables, this specialization is supported exactly on partitions fitting the (1,1)-hook, i.e. ordinary hook shapes. An immediate consequence of the displayed skew formula is that for λ=(r,1^s), one has s_λ[q−εu]=q^r u^s+q^{r−1}u^{s+1}.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is the paper's most direct route to hook coefficients: compare the q^a u^b monomial coefficients in the one-boson/one-fermion character with the two-term expansions of hook Schur functions. Combined with the tableau formula in Equation 5.2 and the Hilbert pairing with p_1^n, this suggests an explicit inversion giving c_{(r,1^s)}(n) from standard-tableau or permutation statistics.

proof_techniques.name: Skewing by e_a^⊥ and Delta-operator extraction
proof_techniques.description: The paper emphasizes the identity sum_a u^a e_a^⊥ f(q)=f[q−εu]. This means that the fermionic variable u can be interpreted as recording the effect of skewing the bosonic Schur expansion by elementary symmetric functions. Conjecture 3 identifies the evaluation of these skewed pieces at q+t with Macdonald Delta operators: (e_a^⊥⊗Id)E_n evaluated at q+t is conjecturally Δ'_{e_{n−a−1}}e_n.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Hook Schur functions are controlled by adding or removing vertical strips, exactly the operation governed by e_a^⊥. A proof strategy for hook coefficients could translate the desired hook coefficient into a finite difference of Delta-specialized coefficients, then use known Delta formulas at t=0 or t=1, or parking-function models, to give a count.

proof_techniques.name: Standard-tableau q-binomial expansion for the GL(1|1) specialization
proof_techniques.description: The one-boson/one-fermion formula expresses C_n(q;u;z) as a sum over standard tableaux τ, with weight q^{maj(τ)−r des(τ)+binom(r,2)} [des(τ) choose r]_q u^r. The Gaussian binomial coefficient can be interpreted combinatorially as choosing a subset or subpartition within a des(τ)-by-r rectangle, depending on the desired model. This formula packages the fermionic degree r and the bosonic degree into classical descent and major-index statistics.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: After extracting hook Schur terms through the GL(1|1) specialization and then applying the Hilbert pairing, this formula should convert hook coefficients c_λ(n) into counts of tableaux, or via RSK into counts of permutations, with descent-set choices and major-index shifts.

proof_techniques.name: Macdonald hook scalar product
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses the identity ⟨H̃_μ,s_{n−k,1^k}⟩=e_k[B_μ−1] to identify hook Schur coefficients in the super-nabla variable set with Delta operators. This is a powerful way to replace a hook-extraction problem by an operator eigenvalue problem on the modified Macdonald basis.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the original hook coefficient problem can be embedded into a super-nabla or Delta-operator specialization, this identity gives a canonical hook-extraction mechanism and connects the coefficient to the Delta Conjecture combinatorics.

proof_techniques.name: Highest-weight or lattice-word extraction of Schur coefficients from path monomials
proof_techniques.description: At t=1, the paper has monomial expansions of super nabla operators over multilabeled Dyck paths. It then converts these monomial sums into Schur-positive expansions by retaining only lattice labelings, where each component word is a lattice word and hence determines a partition λ_j(P). This is the standard crystal/highest-weight method for reading Schur coefficients from a symmetric monomial generating function.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A path model for the Hilbert coefficients c_λ(n) could be obtained by imposing that the grading-label word has hook shape λ, or by using the GL(1|1) specialization where only hook highest weights survive. This may yield a direct Dyck-path interpretation for hook coefficients in specializations such as t=1.

proof_techniques.name: Fermionic hook-Kronecker reduction
proof_techniques.description: The pure two-fermion specialization is expressed by Kim and Rhoades as positive differences of Kronecker products of hook Schur functions. This translates fermionic diagonal coinvariant coefficients into known hook-Kronecker coefficient formulas, for which Remmel-type combinatorics exists.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Via the boson-fermion conjecture, pure bosonic Schur coefficients are conjugated into pure fermionic coefficients. For hook or near-hook shapes, known combinatorics of hook Kronecker coefficients may provide an alternate route to explicit interpretations or consistency checks.

other_useful_info: The paper repeatedly stresses that the main boson-fermion formula C_n(q;u;z)=E_n(q−εu;z), Zabrocki's formula C_n(q+t;u;z)=sum_a u^a Δ'_{e_{n−a−1}}e_n, and the two-fermion Theta formula are conjectural in the generality stated, although several specializations are known. The Rise version of the Delta Conjecture is known, while the Valley version remains open; the paper notes that the Valley version has a schedules formula and is naturally connected to the valley-decorated interpretation of hook components of ∇_v e_n. Equation 5.9 conjectures C_n(q+t;u+v;z)=sum_{k=0}^{n−1} Θ_{e_k[(u+v)z]}(∇e_{n−k}), and the paper says only the q=t=0 specialization is known to match the Kim-Rhoades formula. Section 6 records a low-degree approximation C_n(q;0;z)=(n) h_n[Ω(q)z]/h_n[Ω(q)], meaning equality through total q-degree n; this could be useful for checking small hook shapes of bounded degree. Section 7 gives the explicit stable example E_3=1⊗s_3+(s_1+s_2)⊗s_21+(s_11+s_3)⊗s_111, hence C_3(q;0;z)=s_3(z)+(s_1(q)+s_2(q))s_21(z)+(s_11(q)+s_3(q))s_111(z) and C_3(0;u;z)=s_3(z)+(s_1(u)+s_11(u))s_21(z)+(s_2(u)+s_111(u))s_111(z); these are useful test cases for any proposed hook-coefficient interpretation. The paper also points out that the full-polynomial-ring coefficient r_μ(q)=s_μ[Ω(q)] is tied to the classical restriction problem of decomposing GL_k characters when restricted to permutation matrices in S_k.
why_relevant: Develops conjectural and computational links between bosonic-fermionic diagonal coinvariant modules and Theta/Macdonald operators. It contains many of the operator identities and special cases relevant to interpreting mixed bosonic-fermionic Hilbert and Frobenius series.

title: A polynomial expression for the Hilbert series of the quotient ring of diagonal coinvariants
overall_summary: The paper starts from Haiman's Macdonald-polynomial formula for the Frobenius series of the two-variable diagonal coinvariant ring and transforms the resulting rational expression for its bigraded Hilbert series into an explicit polynomial formula. The main technical device is a Garsia-Zabrocki summation identity for Macdonald Pieri coefficients, combined with repeated application of e_1^⊥ to remove corners of partitions. The resulting polynomial is expressed as a weighted sum over upper-triangular Tesler matrices and equivalently as a constant term or coefficient extraction from a universal Laurent product. The paper also extends the same formulas to Haiman's ∇^m e_n modules by changing the Tesler hook-sum vector from (1,1,...,1) to (1,m,m,...,m). For the original problem, the most relevant content is the Tesler/constant-term technology for Hilbert-series extraction and the paper's Section 5.3 discussion of Schur-positive refinements and Bergeron's conjectural Schur/h-basis description for diagonal coinvariants in arbitrarily many grading variables.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Haiman character formula and Macdonald rational Hilbert-series expansion, cited in Sections 1, 2, and 4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let DR_n = C[x_1,...,x_n,y_1,...,y_n] modulo the ideal generated by all diagonal power sums ∑_{i=1}^n x_i^h y_i^k with h,k ≥ 0 and h+k > 0, with S_n acting diagonally by simultaneous permutation of the x and y variables. Let H̃_µ(X;q,t) be the modified Macdonald polynomial indexed by a partition µ of n, and define ∇ by ∇ H̃_µ = T_µ H̃_µ, where T_µ = t^{n(µ)} q^{n(µ′)} and n(µ) = ∑_i (i−1)µ_i. Haiman's theorem, as used in the paper, says that the Frobenius series of DR_n is ∇ e_n, hence its bigraded Hilbert series is Hilb(DR_n) = ⟨∇ e_n, h_1^n⟩ with respect to the Hall scalar product. For a Ferrers square s of µ, let l(s), a(s), l′(s), a′(s) be its leg, arm, coleg, and coarm. Put M = (1−q)(1−t), B_µ = ∑_{s in µ} t^{l′(s)} q^{a′(s)}, Π_µ = ∏_{s in µ, s ≠ (0,0)} (1−t^{l′(s)} q^{a′(s)}), w_µ = ∏_{s in µ} (q^{a(s)}−t^{l(s)+1})(t^{l(s)}−q^{a(s)+1}), and F_µ = ⟨H̃_µ,h_1^n⟩. The known expansion e_n = ∑_{µ⊢n} H̃_µ M Π_µ B_µ / w_µ implies Hilb(DR_n) = ∑_{µ⊢n} T_µ F_µ M Π_µ B_µ / w_µ. More generally, for every positive integer r, Haiman's result gives an S_n-module DR_n^(r) whose Frobenius series is ∇^r e_n, and therefore Hilb(DR_n^(r)) = ⟨∇^r e_n,h_1^n⟩ = ∑_{µ⊢n} T_µ^r F_µ M Π_µ B_µ / w_µ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The Haiman Frobenius statement is quoted rather than proved. The derivation of the displayed rational Hilbert-series formula is immediate from the modified Macdonald expansion of e_n: apply ∇, or ∇^r in the higher case, which multiplies H̃_µ by T_µ or T_µ^r, then take the Hall scalar product with h_1^n. The scalar product contributes F_µ = ⟨H̃_µ,h_1^n⟩ and leaves the coefficients M Π_µ B_µ / w_µ unchanged.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Macdonald Pieri adjunction and Garsia-Zabrocki summation formula, Section 2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f be a symmetric function and let f^⊥ denote the operator adjoint to multiplication by f under the Hall scalar product. If ν is obtained from µ by removing one corner square, write ν → µ, equivalently µ ← ν. Define generalized skew Pieri coefficients c^{f^⊥}_{µ,ν}(q,t) and Pieri coefficients d^f_{µ,ν}(q,t) by f^⊥ H̃_µ = ∑_{ν:ν→µ} c^{f^⊥}_{µ,ν} H̃_ν and f H̃_ν = ∑_{µ:µ←ν} d^f_{µ,ν} H̃_µ. The paper uses the relation c^{f^⊥}_{µ,ν} w_ν = d^{ω f[X/M]}_{µ,ν} w_µ, where ω is the standard involution on symmetric functions and M=(1−q)(1−t). For f=e_1=h_1, abbreviate c_{µ,ν}=c^{h_1^⊥}_{µ,ν} and d_{µ,ν}=d^{h_1[X/M]}_{µ,ν}. For a fixed partition ν and for each addable-corner extension µ←ν, let T = T_µ/T_ν. Then the Garsia-Zabrocki summation identity used in the paper is ∑_{µ:µ←ν} d_{µ,ν} T^k = 1/M for k=0, and ∑_{µ:µ←ν} d_{µ,ν} T^k = (−1)^{k−1} e_{k−1}[M B_ν − 1]/M for k ≥ 1, where e_j[ ] denotes plethystic substitution.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The adjunction relation is cited from Garsia-Haglund, and the summation identity is cited from Garsia-Zabrocki, Bergeron-Garsia-Haiman-Tesler, and Garsia. The paper's subsequent arguments use these identities as black boxes. The key role of the adjunction relation is that, after replacing F_µ by a sum over F_ν via e_1^⊥, it converts c_{µ,ν}/w_µ into d_{µ,ν}/w_ν, allowing all dependence on the newly added corner to be summed by the Garsia-Zabrocki formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 1, summation with the factor 1−T
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix a partition ν. With M=(1−q)(1−t), B_ν = ∑_{s in ν} t^{l′(s)}q^{a′(s)}, d_{µ,ν}=d^{h_1[X/M]}_{µ,ν}, and T=T_µ/T_ν for each µ obtained by adding one corner to ν, one has ∑_{µ:µ←ν} d_{µ,ν}(1−T)T^k = 0 if k=0, and ∑_{µ:µ←ν} d_{µ,ν}(1−T)T^k = (−1)^{k−1} e_k[M B_ν]/M if k ≥ 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For k=0, subtract the k=1 case of the Garsia-Zabrocki identity from the k=0 case: both sums equal 1/M. For k≥1, use the plethystic addition formula e_r[X−Y] = ∑_{j=0}^r e_j[X] e_{r−j}[−Y] and the identity f[−X] = (−1)^{deg f} ω f[X] for homogeneous f. This gives e_{k−1}[M B_ν−1] = ∑_{j=0}^{k−1} e_j[M B_ν](−1)^{k−1−j}. Then compute M∑ d_{µ,ν}(1−T)T^k as M∑ d_{µ,ν}T^k − M∑ d_{µ,ν}T^{k+1}. Substituting the Garsia-Zabrocki formulas for the two power sums causes all terms in the two finite alternating sums to cancel except the final e_k[M B_ν] term, yielding (−1)^{k−1} e_k[M B_ν].

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2, evaluation of e_k[M] as a q,t-integer
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every integer k ≥ 1 and M=(1−q)(1−t), the plethystic elementary symmetric function satisfies (−1)^{k−1} e_k[M]/M = (t^k−q^k)/(t−q). The right side is the q,t-analogue [k]_{q,t} = t^{k−1}+t^{k−2}q+...+q^{k−1}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply the Cauchy identity e_k[XY] = ∑_{λ⊢k} s_λ[X] s_{λ′}[Y] with X=1−q and Y=1−t. The evaluation s_λ[1−z] vanishes unless λ is a hook λ=(k−r,1^r), in which case it equals (−z)^r(1−z). Therefore e_k[M] = ∑_{r=0}^{k−1} (−q)^r(1−q)(−t)^{k−1−r}(1−t). Dividing by M=(1−q)(1−t) and multiplying by (−1)^{k−1} gives ∑_{r=0}^{k−1} q^r t^{k−1−r} = (t^k−q^k)/(t−q).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1, recursive polynomial formula for Hilb(DR_n)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a partition ρ, use T_ρ, B_ρ, Π_ρ, w_ρ, and F_ρ as in the Haiman-Macdonald expansion above. Define a_k = (−1)^{k−1} e_k[M]/M and, for each partition ρ, b_k(ρ) = (−1)^{k−1} e_k[M B_ρ]/M. Then for every integer p with 1 ≤ p ≤ n there is a polynomial A_p(ρ) in the symbols a_i and b_i(ρ) such that Hilb(DR_n) = ∑_{ρ⊢n−p+1} T_ρ F_ρ M Π_ρ A_p(ρ) / w_ρ. The initial polynomial is A_1=b_1. To compute A_p from A_{p−1}, replace every occurrence of b_k in A_{p−1} by b_k + T^k a_k − ∑_{j=1}^{k−1} M b_j T^{k−j} a_{k−j}, expand the result as a polynomial ∑_j c_j T^j in the auxiliary variable T, and then replace each T^j by b_{j+1}. For example A_1=b_1, A_2=b_1^2+b_2 a_1, and the paper lists A_3=b_1^3+2b_1a_1b_2+a_1^2b_3+a_1b_2b_1−M b_1a_1^2b_2+a_1a_2b_3.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Start from Hilb(DR_n)=∑_{µ⊢n} T_µ F_µ MΠ_µB_µ/w_µ. Since e_1^⊥ H̃_µ = ∑_{ν→µ} c_{µ,ν} H̃_ν, pairing with h_1^{n−1} gives F_µ=∑_{ν→µ} c_{µ,ν} F_ν. Substitute this into the Hilbert-series sum and reverse the order of summation, obtaining a sum over ν⊢n−1 and addable corners µ←ν. For such µ, B_µ=B_ν+T and Π_µ=Π_ν(1−T), where T=T_µ/T_ν. The Pieri adjunction c_{µ,ν}w_ν=d_{µ,ν}w_µ converts the inner sum to ∑_{µ←ν} d_{µ,ν}(B_ν+T)(1−T)T, and Lemma 1 evaluates this as b_1(ν)^2+b_2(ν)a_1, giving the p=2 case. Iterating the same argument requires rewriting b_k(µ) in terms of B_µ=B_ν+T. The plethystic addition formula gives (−1)^{k−1}e_k[M(B_ν+T)]/M = b_k(ν)+T^k a_k−∑_{j=1}^{k−1} M b_j(ν)T^{k−j}a_{k−j}. After this substitution into A_{p−1}, the remaining addable-corner sum has the common factor d_{µ,ν}(1−T)T; Lemma 1 then converts each monomial T^j into b_{j+1}. This proves the recursion and the displayed formula by induction on p.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2, Tesler-matrix expression for the recursive polynomials A_p
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For N ≥ 1, let Q_N be the set of N by N upper-triangular matrices C=(c_{ij}) with nonnegative integer entries satisfying the hook-sum equations ∑_{i=j}^N c_{j i} − ∑_{i=1}^{j−1} c_{i j} = 1 for every j=1,...,N. Let Pos(C) be the multiset of positive entries of C and pos(C)=|Pos(C)|. With a_k=(−1)^{k−1}e_k[M]/M and b_k=b_k(ρ)=(−1)^{k−1}e_k[M B_ρ]/M for a fixed partition ρ, the polynomial A_N(ρ) from Theorem 1 is A_N(ρ)=∑_{C in Q_N} (−M)^{pos(C)−N} ∏_{i: c_{ii}>0} b_{c_{ii}} ∏_{i<j: c_{ij}>0} a_{c_{ij}}. Equivalently, for A_p one uses matrices of size p and the exponent pos(C)−p; the paper's proof denotes the matrix size by n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by induction on the matrix size. For C in Q_N, every row must contain at least one positive entry, so pos(C)−N counts the number of adjacent pairs of positive entries in rows, with no positive entry between them. Map C to a matrix C′ in Q_{N−1} by adding each entry in the last column to the corresponding diagonal entry and then deleting the last row and last column: c′_{ij}=c_{ij} for j≠i and c′_{ii}=c_{ii}+c_{iN}. Conversely, given W in Q_{N−1}, choose for each i<N an integer k_i with 0≤k_i≤w_{ii}, set c_{ii}=w_{ii}−k_i and c_{iN}=k_i, keep c_{ij}=w_{ij} for i<j<N, and set c_{NN}=1+∑_{j=1}^{N−1} c_{jN}. If 0<k_i<w_{ii}, row i gains one additional positive entry, producing one extra factor −M. These choices exactly mirror the recursive replacement b_k ↦ b_k+T^k a_k−∑_{j=1}^{k−1}M b_jT^{k−j}a_{k−j}: keeping k_i=0 corresponds to a diagonal b_k, taking k_i=w_{ii} corresponds to an off-diagonal a_k, and splitting into two positive parts corresponds to the −M b_j a_{k−j} terms. The final diagonal entry c_{NN} records the power of T that Lemma 1 converts into the final b factor.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 1, explicit Tesler-matrix polynomial for Hilb(DR_n)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Q_n be the set of upper-triangular nonnegative integer n by n matrices satisfying ∑_{i=j}^n c_{j i} − ∑_{i=1}^{j−1} c_{i j} = 1 for all j=1,...,n. Then Hilb(DR_n) = ∑_{C in Q_n} (−M)^{pos(C)−n} ∏_{1≤i≤j≤n, c_{ij}>0} [c_{ij}]_{q,t}, where M=(1−q)(1−t), pos(C) is the number of positive entries of C, and [k]_{q,t}=(t^k−q^k)/(t−q).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Take p=n in Theorem 1, so the outer sum over partitions contains only the single partition (1). For µ=(1), one has B_(1)=1, Π_(1)=1, w_(1)=M, and F_(1)=1. Lemma 2 gives a_k=[k]_{q,t}, and since B_(1)=1 it also gives b_k=[k]_{q,t}. Substituting these evaluations into the Tesler expression for A_n from Theorem 2 yields exactly the displayed sum.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 2 and Remark 2, constant-term formula for Hilb(DR_n) and for A_p
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For n ≥ 1, Hilb(DR_n) is the coefficient of z_1 z_2 ... z_n in the Laurent series 1/(−M)^n ∏_{i=1}^n ((1−z_i)(1−qt z_i))/((1−q z_i)(1−t z_i)) ∏_{1≤i<j≤n} ((1−z_i/z_j)(1−qt z_i/z_j))/((1−q z_i/z_j)(1−t z_i/z_j)), where M=(1−q)(1−t). Equivalently, with Ω(E)=∑_{r≥0} h_r[E] in plethystic notation and Z_p denoting the set of variables {z_1,...,z_p}, the intermediate polynomial A_p(µ) satisfies A_p(µ) = coefficient of z_1...z_p in 1/(−M)^p Ω[−M B_µ Z_p] ∏_{1≤i<j≤p} ((1−z_i/z_j)(1−qt z_i/z_j))/((1−q z_i/z_j)(1−t z_i/z_j)).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Set f(x)=((1−x)(1−qt x))/((1−q x)(1−t x)). Its Taylor expansion at x=0 is f(x)=1−M∑_{k≥1} x^k [k]_{q,t}. In the product, choosing the term −M z_i^k[k]_{q,t} from f(z_i) records a diagonal matrix entry c_{ii}=k, while choosing −M(z_i/z_j)^k[k]_{q,t} from f(z_i/z_j) records an off-diagonal entry c_{ij}=k. The exponent of z_j in the resulting monomial is ∑_{i=j}^n c_{j i}−∑_{i=1}^{j−1}c_{i j}, precisely the Tesler hook sum. Thus extracting the coefficient of z_1...z_n imposes the Q_n hook-sum equations, and the prefactor 1/(−M)^n converts the product of selected −M factors into (−M)^{pos(C)−n}. The formula for A_p(µ) is the same encoding before specializing B_µ=1; Ω[−M B_µ Z_p] supplies the diagonal choices with weights b_k(µ) instead of [k]_{q,t}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 3, operator version using repeated e_1^⊥
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every integer p with 0≤p≤n−1, the same recursively defined polynomials A_r give an identity in symmetric functions: (e_1^⊥)^p ∇ e_n = ∑_{ν⊢n−p} T_ν H̃_ν M Π_ν A_{p+1}(ν) / w_ν. In particular, e_1^⊥ ∇ e_n = ∑_{ν⊢n−1} T_ν H̃_ν MΠ_ν/w_ν · (e_1[M B_ν]/M · e_1[M B_ν]/M − e_2[M B_ν]/M).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is the same as the proof of Theorem 1, but one does not take the final scalar product with h_1^{n−p}. Applying e_1^⊥ to the Macdonald expansion lowers the partition size by one through the coefficients c_{µ,ν}; the adjunction relation changes c_{µ,ν}/w_µ to d_{µ,ν}/w_ν; B_µ=B_ν+T and Π_µ=Π_ν(1−T); and Lemma 1 performs the sum over addable corners. Iterating p times gives the stated formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3 and Corollary 3, the ∇^r or m-parameter extension
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let r be a positive integer. For the S_n-module DR_n^(r) with Frobenius series ∇^r e_n, the Hilbert series is Hilb(DR_n^(r))=∑_{µ⊢n} T_µ^r F_µ MΠ_µB_µ/w_µ. Define A_1^(r)=b_1, where b_k(ρ)=(−1)^{k−1}e_k[M B_ρ]/M, and for p>1 compute A_p^(r) from A_{p−1}^(r) by the same substitution b_k ↦ b_k+T^k a_k−∑_{j=1}^{k−1}M b_jT^{k−j}a_{k−j}, with a_k=(−1)^{k−1}e_k[M]/M, followed by expansion ∑ c_jT^j and replacement of T^j by b_{j+r}. Then Hilb(DR_n^(r)) = ∑_{ρ⊢n−p+1} T_ρ^r F_ρ MΠ_ρ A_p^(r)(ρ)/w_ρ for 1≤p≤n. Let Q_N^(r) be the set of upper-triangular nonnegative integer matrices C=(c_{ij}) satisfying ∑_{i=j}^N c_{j i}−∑_{i=1}^{j−1}c_{i j}=1 for j=1 and =r for 2≤j≤N. Then A_p^(r)(ρ)=∑_{C in Q_p^(r)}(−M)^{pos(C)−p} ∏_{i:c_{ii}>0} b_{c_{ii}}(ρ) ∏_{i<j:c_{ij}>0} a_{c_{ij}}, and the specialization p=n gives Hilb(DR_n^(r))=∑_{C in Q_n^(r)}(−M)^{pos(C)−n} ∏_{1≤i≤j≤n, c_{ij}>0}[c_{ij}]_{q,t}. Equivalently, Hilb(DR_n^(r)) is the coefficient of z_1 z_2^r z_3^r ... z_n^r in the same Laurent product used in Corollary 2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof repeats the derivation of Theorem 1 with one change: because ∇^r contributes T_µ^r, after writing T_µ=T_ν T, the addable-corner inner sum contains (1−T)T^r instead of (1−T)T. Therefore, after the substitution expressing b_k(µ) in terms of b_j(ν), a_j, and powers of T, a monomial T^j is evaluated by Lemma 1 as b_{j+r}, not b_{j+1}. This proves the modified recursion. The Tesler-matrix proof is identical to Theorem 2 except that the final diagonal entry created at each extension records hook sum r in rows 2 through N, yielding hook-sum vector (1,r,r,...,r). The constant-term form follows exactly as in Corollary 2: the exponent of z_j is the j-th Tesler hook sum, so extracting z_1 z_2^r...z_n^r imposes the defining equations of Q_n^(r).

proof_techniques.name: Recursive corner removal via e_1^⊥
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly replaces the difficult scalar F_µ=⟨H̃_µ,h_1^n⟩ by a sum over one-smaller partitions using e_1^⊥ H̃_µ=∑_{ν→µ} c_{µ,ν}H̃_ν. Pairing with h_1^{n−1} gives F_µ=∑_{ν→µ}c_{µ,ν}F_ν. This turns the original sum over partitions of n into a sum over partitions of n−1 and addable corners, and iteration lowers the partition size until only the partition (1) remains.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Hook Schur coefficients in multigraded Hilbert series are often accessible through adjoint symmetric-function operators or through repeated removal operations. This paper gives a model example where repeated e_1^⊥ converts a Hilbert-series scalar product into finite combinatorial data.

proof_techniques.name: Macdonald Pieri summation eliminates rational addable-corner sums
proof_techniques.description: After corner removal, the inner sum over all ways to add a corner to ν contains Macdonald Pieri coefficients. The adjunction identity c_{µ,ν}w_ν=d_{µ,ν}w_µ converts skew Pieri coefficients into ordinary Pieri coefficients, and the Garsia-Zabrocki summation identity evaluates ∑ d_{µ,ν}T^k explicitly. Lemma 1 packages the version actually needed in the recursion: sums with an extra factor (1−T) become elementary symmetric functions e_k[M B_ν]/M.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the target hook coefficients can be expressed through scalar products involving ∇ or modified Macdonald polynomials, analogous Pieri summations may collapse otherwise complicated sums over partitions. The paper also indicates that extensions beyond h_1-type removals would require corresponding summation formulas for higher Pieri operators.

proof_techniques.name: Plethystic addition to track a newly added corner
proof_techniques.description: A central computational step is the identity B_µ=B_ν+T when µ is obtained from ν by adding one square, together with the plethystic expansion of b_k(µ)=(−1)^{k−1}e_k[M(B_ν+T)]/M. This gives b_k(µ)=b_k(ν)+T^k a_k−∑_{j=1}^{k−1}M b_j(ν)T^{k−j}a_{k−j}. Thus each recursive step has an explicit algebraic replacement rule on the variables b_k.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For multigraded Schur coefficient extraction, one can look for similar replacement rules when adding a grading variable, adding a corner, or applying an adjoint operator. The hook case may be especially compatible with one-row/one-column plethystic decompositions.

proof_techniques.name: Tesler matrices as histories of recursive substitutions
proof_techniques.description: The nonrecursive formula encodes all possible histories of the A_p recursion by upper-triangular matrices. Diagonal positive entries correspond to b-factors, off-diagonal positive entries correspond to a-factors, and every split of a positive diagonal contribution into two positive pieces contributes a factor −M. The hook-sum equations of the Tesler matrix record the powers of T that are eventually converted into b-factors by Lemma 1.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This suggests trying to interpret universal Schur coefficients by refining Tesler matrices, for example by decorating entries or imposing additional conditions corresponding to hook-shaped Schur functions. The terminology hook sum in the paper is unrelated to hook-shaped partitions but may still provide a useful matrix constraint framework.

proof_techniques.name: Constant-term encoding of matrix hook sums
proof_techniques.description: The Tesler sum is converted to a coefficient extraction using the single rational function f(x)=((1−x)(1−qtx))/((1−qx)(1−tx))=1−M∑_{k≥1}x^k[k]_{q,t}. Choosing a term from f(z_i/z_j) creates an off-diagonal entry c_{ij}; choosing a term from f(z_i) creates a diagonal entry c_{ii}. The exponent of z_j in the resulting monomial is exactly the j-th matrix hook sum, so coefficient extraction enforces the desired hook-sum vector.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Schur coefficients can often be extracted by pairing with symmetric functions or by constant-term identities. The kernel in this paper may be a starting point for deriving coefficient formulas in the grading variables, especially after symmetrizing or applying Cauchy identities.

proof_techniques.name: Schur reinterpretation of the Tesler weights
proof_techniques.description: The paper observes that the positive factor [k]_{q,t} in the Tesler formula is the Schur function s_{k−1} evaluated in the two variables {q,t}, while −M=t+q−1−qt equals s_1−1−s_{1,1} in those same variables. Therefore the entire Tesler expression can be expanded in the Schur basis in the grading variables, with cancellations of Schur functions having more than two parts when only {q,t} are present.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem is explicitly about Schur coefficients c_λ(n) in arbitrarily many grading variables. This observation is one of the paper's direct bridges from the q,t Hilbert series to Schur-basis questions and may be useful for isolating hook-shaped Schur coefficients.

proof_techniques.name: The ∇^r parameter as a hook-sum shift
proof_techniques.description: In the paper's higher ∇^r setting, no new Laurent kernel is needed. The only changes are that the recursive replacement T^j ↦ b_{j+1} becomes T^j ↦ b_{j+r}, and the Tesler hook-sum vector changes from (1,1,...,1) to (1,r,r,...,r). The constant-term formula changes only in the target monomial, from z_1...z_n to z_1 z_2^r...z_n^r.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Although the paper's r is the power in ∇^r rather than automatically the number of sets of variables in the original problem, this shows how a parameter in a diagonal-coinvariant Hilbert series can sometimes be represented purely by changing a coefficient-extraction vector. A similar mechanism might exist for the number of grading variables or for hook Schur coefficient extraction.

other_useful_info: The paper records the Haglund-Loehr parking-function conjecture Hilb(DR_n)=∑_π q^{dinv(π)}t^{area(π)}, where π ranges over parking functions, and notes possible correspondences between restricted Tesler sums and parking functions: matrices with c_11=1 reproduce Hilb(DR_{n−1}), and Maple computations suggest that matrices with c_{1i}=1 correspond to parking functions whose bottom-row car is n for i=1 and i−1 for i>1. Section 5.1 conjectures a broader positivity statement for the same constant-term kernel: if α=(α_1,...,α_n) is positive and nondecreasing, then the coefficient of z_1^{α_1}...z_n^{α_n} is in N[q,t], equivalently weighted Tesler matrices with hook sums α have a positive q,t sum; the paper proves the q=0 specialization after reversing α to a partition µ, obtaining Q_µ[−1;t]/(t−1)^n = [n]!_t t^{n(µ)+|µ|−binom(n+1,2)} via a Hall-Littlewood evaluation. Remark 4 says that when α_1=1, the coefficient with hook sums α can be generated operatorially by starting with ∇^{α_2}e_n, applying e_1^⊥, then applying ∇^{α_3−α_2}, applying e_1^⊥ again, and continuing. Section 5.3 is especially relevant to Schur expansions: the paper observes [k]_{q,t}=s_{k−1}({q,t}) and −M=s_1−1−s_{1,1}; after substituting these into the Tesler formula and cancelling Schur functions with more than two parts, computations suggest Schur-positivity. The same section cites F. Bergeron's stronger conjecture that Hilb(DR_n)=∑_{σ∈S_n} h_{λ(σ)}({q,t}) for suitable partitions λ(σ), and that evaluating this same h-basis sum in variables {q_1,...,q_k} gives the Hilbert series of diagonal coinvariants in k sets of variables for every k≥1. The paper notes that extending its integrality method from ⟨∇e_n,h_1^n⟩ to general ⟨∇e_n,h_λ⟩ would require analogues of the Pieri summation formula for coefficients d^{e_k[X/M]}_{µ,ν}.
why_relevant: Transforms Haiman's rational-function Hilbert-series formula for diagonal coinvariants into an explicit polynomial and gives constant-term formulations. The paper provides useful algebraic and symmetric-function techniques for extracting Hilbert-series coefficients.
